## Endometriosis Molecular Pathology and Cancer: Synthesis of Current Research

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The assembled literature converges on several interconnected themes in endometriosis molecular pathology: dysregulated apoptosis and cell survival, aberrant immune infiltration, angiogenesis, metabolic reprogramming, epigenetic regulation, and the malignant transformation potential of ectopic lesions. A recurrent conceptual framework positions endometriosis as sharing fundamental oncogenic mechanisms with gynecological cancers, including endometrial and ovarian carcinomas, rather than representing an entirely distinct pathological entity.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Apoptotic resistance emerges as a central mechanistic theme, with downregulation of FAS-FASL signaling enabling survival of ectopic endometrial cells (PMC12480475). This resistance is reinforced by metabolic reprogramming toward aerobic glycolysis—analogous to the Warburg effect in malignancy—mediated through HSP90B1 upregulation of GLUT1 and lactate dehydrogenase (PMC12574043). Angiogenesis, driven by FZD4/WNT/β-catenin signaling, SRPX2/FAK-integrin cascades, and COL8A1-mediated VEGF secretion, is consistently identified as an obligate step in lesion establishment (PMC12558289). Iron dysregulation, particularly elevated BMP6 and SLC48A1 with associated ferroptotic signaling, links peritoneal iron accumulation to disease severity (PMC12564322).

Immune dysregulation is reported across multiple studies. Altered infiltration patterns—including elevated gamma-delta T cells, monocytes, and natural killer cells alongside reduced activated B cells—characterize the endometriotic microenvironment (PMC12480475, PMC12558289). Endothelial-mesenchymal transition, governed by ITGB1, VIM, FGF2, and SMAD1 through PI3K-Akt and MAPK pathways, is identified as a shared mechanism linking endometriosis to recurrent miscarriage (PMC12540761).

Epigenetic dysregulation of HOXA10 and HOXA11 through promoter methylation consistently impairs endometrial receptivity in endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and uterine fibroids (PMC12563246). ESR1 occupies a central regulatory role, controlling decidualization programs through FOXO1 and progesterone receptor while simultaneously activating oncogenic targets including LINC01016 and ERRFI1, mechanistically connecting estrogen signaling to both endometrial homeostasis and pathological progression (PMC12582374). The CSF2RB-JAK/STAT-MAPK axis and dysregulated steroid receptor balance—specifically elevated ESR-α relative to ESR-β and reduced PGR-B—further consolidate immune and hormonal dysregulation as convergent drivers (PMC12480475, PMC12530196).

The molecular overlap between endometriosis and endometrioid ovarian cancer is substantiated by shared genomic alterations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, and CTNNB1, with whole-genome sequencing demonstrating that ovarian and uterine endometrioid carcinomas are genomically nearly indistinguishable (PMC12586982). Lynch syndrome studies reinforce this continuum, identifying stepwise progression from normal endometrium through atypical hyperplasia to carcinoma via MMR deficiency and somatic accumulation of mutations in ARID1A, PTEN, and PIK3CA (PMC12496004). Archaic introgression analyses further indicate that chromosome 2 loci derived from Neanderthal ancestry contribute to endometriosis susceptibility at the population genetic level (PMC12474892).

Mendelian randomization studies identify multiple putative causal biomarkers, including downregulated ADK, EEFSEC, and INO80E exerting protective effects, and upregulated HCG22, RAP1GAP, and RSPRY1 promoting disease (PMC12500265). Iron metabolism genes BMP6 and SLC48A1 show Men

## Endometriosis Molecular Pathology and Cancer: Synthesis of Research Themes

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers assembled in this cluster span gynecological pathology, cancer biology, reproductive immunology, and epigenetic regulation, with endometriosis serving as a recurring disease model connecting molecular mechanisms of inflammation, aberrant hormone signaling, epigenetic dysregulation, and malignant transformation. Several overarching mechanistic themes emerge consistently across these studies.

Dysregulation of epigenetic machinery represents a dominant axis. Aberrant DNA methylation mediated by upregulated DNMT1, DNMT3A, and DNMT3B silences critical regulatory genes including ESR2, PR-B, HOXA10, and aromatase in endometriotic lesions, directly impairing steroid hormone responsiveness. Complementary to this, m6A RNA methylation via METTL3 modulates inflammatory responses and ectopic implantation in pelvic inflammatory disease sequelae through PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway regulation, while long noncoding RNA networks—including ceRNA axes involving Dio3os, LINC00449, and LINC01871—broadly reshape gene expression in cancer and immune contexts. MicroRNA-mediated regulation appears equally pervasive: miR-424 and miR-503 govern endometrial decidualization through FOXO1-SCARA5 suppression, miR-193b targets PI3K-AKT and WNT/β-catenin signaling across multiple malignancies, and dysregulated miR-155, miR-223, miR-21, and miR-146a orchestrate NF-κB, JAK-STAT, and Toll-like receptor inflammatory cascades in inflammatory bowel disease with documented relevance to colitis-associated cancer.

Immune microenvironment remodeling constitutes a second dominant theme. Across endometriosis, recurrent pregnancy loss, premature ovarian insufficiency, and gynecological cancers, aberrant natural killer cell polarization, macrophage phenotype shifts, and dysregulated regulatory T cell function are consistently implicated. In chronic endometritis, elevated uNK2/uNK3 ratios driven by EOMES/ELF4 and ELK4/IRF1 transcription factor networks create a pro-inflammatory milieu hostile to implantation. Vitamin D metabolism genes CYP2R1, CYP27B1, and VDR normally suppress NK cell hyperactivation and inflammatory interferon-gamma and TNF-α/NF-κB signaling, and their deficiency promotes recurrent pregnancy loss through immune dysregulation. FOXP3 polymorphisms (rs3761548, rs3761549) that impair regulatory T cell suppressive function further compromise fetal-maternal tolerance. In ovarian and endometrial cancers, tumor-associated macrophage polarity—indexed by CXCL9:SPP1 ratios—stratifies prognosis, with immunosuppressive M0 macrophage expansion and regulatory T cell infiltration characterizing aggressive disease.

Oncogenic signaling pathway convergence emerges as a third major theme. PI3K/AKT/mTOR, WNT/β-catenin, MAPK/ERK, and TGF-β signaling are dysregulated across endometriosis, endometrial cancer, ovarian cancer, and related gynecological conditions. Somatic mutations in KRAS, ARID1A, and PTEN are repeatedly identified as drivers linking endometriosis to malignant transformation; KRAS activates SIRT1 and BCL6 to suppress p53 and impair LIF expression critical for implantation, while ARID1A loss—a component of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex—creates synthetic lethality with PI3K/AKT inhibitors and PARP inhibitors across cholangiocarcinoma and ovarian clear cell carcinoma. The GWAS of endometriosis further implicates Wnt signaling, ESR1 upregulation, and GREB1 as consistently dysregulated molecular nodes, with multi-omics integration identifying KDR and RSPO3 as pleiotropic regulators supported across transcriptomic, methylation

## Endometriosis Molecular Pathology and Cancer: Synthesis of Research Findings

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers assembled in this cluster address molecular pathogenesis across endometriosis, endometriosis-associated malignancies, and related gynecological conditions, with recurring emphasis on aberrant signaling, epigenetic dysregulation, immune microenvironment remodeling, and metabolic reprogramming. Several studies extend these themes to related reproductive pathologies including recurrent implantation failure, recurrent spontaneous abortion, and ovarian and endometrial cancers, reflecting the recognized oncogenic continuum from ectopic endometrial tissue to frank malignancy.

### Consistently Reported Findings

PI3K/AKT signaling emerges as a dominant oncogenic pathway, implicated in endometriosis-recurrent implantation failure overlap (PMC12748698), CCNE1-amplified gynecological cancers (PMC12748378), Met-amplified ovarian clear cell adenocarcinoma (PMC12912569), and endometrial cancer progression (PMC12911402). Co-occurring dysregulation of Wnt/β-catenin signaling and TGF-β pathways is repeatedly observed across benign and malignant endometrial contexts.

Immune microenvironment dysfunction is consistently reported. Studies identify characteristic shifts toward immunosuppression, including reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity (GNLY, PRF1 downregulation; PMC12866336), elevated M2 macrophage polarization, diminished CD8+ T cell infiltration, and upregulation of adenosine-mediated suppressive signaling through ENTPD1. Epigenetic regulators—notably HDAC9, YAF2, and CDC6—are elevated in endometriosis tissues and linked to estrogen/progesterone receptor modulation, focal adhesion, and oxidative phosphorylation pathways (PMC12880091).

Environmental endocrine disruptors, particularly DEHP, are mechanistically connected to endometriosis through lipid metabolism reprogramming involving LXR/SREBP-1c and PPAR-α/γ activation and dysregulation of enzymes including HMGCR and ELOVL6 (PMC12751832). The Met proto-oncogene is identified as the most frequently amplified driver in endometriosis-associated ovarian clear cell adenocarcinoma, with preferential AKT2 activation distinguishing this histotype (PMC12912569). CCNE1 amplification characterizes aggressive gynecological subtypes, co-occurring with TP53 and PIK3CA alterations and conferring replication stress and immune exclusion, with WEE1 inhibition emerging as a viable therapeutic strategy (PMC12748378).

Multi-omics analyses linking endometriosis to recurrent implantation failure identified downregulated hub genes SRPRB, RBM3, INSIG2, GYG1, and FBXW2, with shared hyperactivation of PI3K/AKT, mitochondrial oxidative stress, and elevated IL-1β and TNF-α (PMC12748698). BCL6, proposed as an endometriosis biomarker in infertility contexts, showed no significant specificity advantage over controls or other endometrial pathologies, representing a notable contradictory finding relative to prior clinical advocacy for its diagnostic use (PMC12905616).

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Beyond the BCL6 specificity question, tension exists between studies emphasizing cell-intrinsic oncogenic drivers (Met amplification, CCNE1 amplification, epigenetic silencing) and those foregrounding microenvironmental and immune mechanisms as primary disease determinants. The relative contribution of systemic environmental exposures versus somatic genetic events to endometriosis-to-malignancy transformation remains unresolved across these papers. Additionally, several studies outside the core endometriosis focus—addressing ulcerative colitis, osteosarcoma, osteoarthritis, and pulmonary pathology—were included in this cluster, suggesting imprecise thematic curation that limits the coherence of cross-paper synthesis.

## Endometriosis Molecular Pathology and Cancer: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes linking endometriosis molecular biology to malignant transformation, hormonal signaling, immune dysregulation, and epigenetic reprogramming. A central motif is the progression from benign endometriotic lesions to endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers (EAOCs), particularly endometrioid and clear cell histotypes, through accumulation of somatic mutations and chronic inflammatory conditioning. Across multiple studies, dysregulation of chromatin remodeling, non-coding RNA networks, and growth factor signaling emerges as mechanistically fundamental to both lesion establishment and oncogenic transformation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

ARID1A loss-of-function mutations appear repeatedly as a defining molecular event in EAOC pathogenesis, consistently co-occurring with PIK3CA alterations and implicating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis as a convergent oncogenic pathway. This PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling network is independently corroborated across studies examining progesterone resistance in endometrial cancer, autophagy dysregulation in adenomyosis, and ibrutinib sensitivity in ovarian clear cell carcinoma, suggesting it represents a pan-gynecological oncogenic hub. KRAS, PTEN, and ERBB2 mutations are similarly recurrent across EAOC-focused analyses.

Chronic inflammation mediated by NF-κB, TGF-β, and inflammasome components—particularly NLRP3, AIM2, and PYCARD—is consistently implicated in driving the endometriosis-to-cancer transition. Elevated expression of inflammasome genes and toll-like receptor pathway components correlates with poor survival in EAOC patients, reinforcing the concept that sustained immune activation constitutes a carcinogenic microenvironment. Complementarily, immune checkpoint modulation and tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte composition, exemplified by B4GALNT3's association with anti-tumor immune enrichment in EAOC, highlight the immune microenvironment as a determinant of prognosis.

Hormonal dysregulation, particularly estrogen-dependent signaling through ESR1, GREB1, and downstream targets including HOXA10 and HOXA11, is repeatedly identified as a driver of both endometriosis progression and malignant transformation. GREB1 rearrangements in uterine tumors and ESR1 fusions in sex cord-like neoplasms demonstrate how estrogen-responsive transcriptional programs can be hijacked by structural genomic alterations. The WNT signaling axis, encompassing both canonical β-catenin/CTNNB1 and non-canonical WNT5A-mediated pathways, emerges as a recurrent regulator of reproductive tract development and tumor suppression, with its downregulation noted in endometriosis-associated ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma.

Epigenetic mechanisms—including aberrant DNA methylation, histone modification, and non-coding RNA dysregulation—are consistently highlighted as early and sustained contributors to disease pathogenesis. MicroRNA dysregulation, specifically downregulation of miR-449 and miR-200, promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis in endometriosis, while lncRNA networks modulate chromatin accessibility at disease-relevant loci. In adenomyosis, promoter hypermethylation silencing PIEZO1 disrupts mechanotransduction and nitric oxide signaling, demonstrating that epigenetic silencing of functionally diverse genes contributes to symptom generation as well as structural pathology.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Some tension exists regarding the prognostic implications of endometriosis concurrent with ovarian cancer. One study reports paradoxically improved clinical outcomes in ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma patients with concurrent endometriosis despite molecular evidence of enhanced NFκB and RAS signaling activation, a finding that is difficult to reconcile with the broader body of evidence linking these pathways to aggressive behavior. Additionally, while ARID1A loss is broadly established as tumor-suppressive across gynecological cancers, its role in determining ibrutinib sensitivity in clear cell carcinoma showed a biphasic, cell

# Endometriosis: Clinical Presentations and Imaging

## Overview

The central research questions addressed by this literature cluster concern the full anatomical scope of endometriosis, the diagnostic performance of available imaging modalities across diverse disease manifestations, and the mechanistic basis for the remarkable clinical heterogeneity that defines this condition. Endometriosis is increasingly recognized not as a localized pelvic disorder but as a systemic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease capable of infiltrating virtually any anatomical compartment through retrograde menstruation, lymphovascular dissemination, coelomic metaplasia, and iatrogenic implantation during surgical procedures. The literature converges on a core paradox: despite affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and causing substantial morbidity, endometriosis continues to carry a diagnostic delay of 3–10 years, driven by nonspecific symptomatology, imaging limitations at certain anatomical sites, and a broad differential diagnosis that includes malignant, infectious, and structural mimics.

## Consensus Findings

Several findings emerge with high consistency across the synthesized literature. Extrapelvic endometriosis is far more prevalent and clinically significant than traditionally appreciated, with documented involvement of the ureter, bladder detrusor, rectosigmoid colon, appendix, abdominal wall, umbilicus, diaphragm, pleural cavity, inguinal canal, canal of Nuck, fallopian tubes, and, rarely, the pancreas and liver. Ureteral endometriosis is particularly notable for its clinically silent progression to obstructive uropathy and irreversible renal dysfunction, underscoring the need for routine upper urinary tract surveillance in patients with known deeply infiltrating disease. Bowel endometriosis localizes predominantly to the rectosigmoid in approximately 90% of cases, with lesions invading serosa-inward and capable of precipitating fibrosis, luminal stenosis, and, in rare instances, intussusception or perforation. Thoracic endometriosis syndrome manifests with consistent right-sided predominance in approximately 90% of catamenial pneumothorax cases, attributable to peritoneal fluid dynamics, diaphragmatic fenestrations, and high estrogen receptor expression in diaphragmatic tissue. Abdominal wall endometriosis following cesarean section accounts for approximately 80% of extrapelvic scar-related cases, typically presenting as hypoechoic nodules within the rectus abdominis on ultrasound. Obstructive Müllerian anomalies—including OHVIRA syndrome, imperforate hymen, unicornuate uterus with noncommunicating rudimentary horn, and Robert's uterus—are consistently identified as structural predisposing factors through the mechanism of retrograde menstrual seeding, with endometriosis developing in 13–60% of affected patients depending on anomaly type and diagnostic timing.

Regarding imaging, MRI with targeted endometriosis protocols is broadly endorsed as the reference standard for characterizing deep infiltrating endometriosis, with characteristic T1-hyperintense, T2-shading foci in endometriomas, T2-hypointense nodular lesions with hyperintense glandular inclusions in deep disease, and the "mushroom cap" sign for rectosigmoid involvement. For bladder endometriosis specifically, MRI achieves sensitivity up to 88% and specificity 99%. Transvaginal ultrasonography achieves sensitivity and specificity exceeding 98% for ovarian endometriomas and performs comparably to MRI for rectosigmoid disease, while demonstrating substantially lower sensitivity (4–43.5%) for superficial peritoneal implants. Meta-analytic data indicate statistically equivalent sensitivity and specificity between TVS (0.85/0.92) and MRI (0.83/0.95) for rectosigmoid deep infiltrating endometriosis, with MRI demonstrating superiority for rectovaginal septum, round ligament, and ileal involvement. Histopathological confirmation demonstrating endometrial glands, stroma, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and immunopositivity for CD10, estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, CK7, PAX8, and vimentin remains the diagnostic gold standard across all anatomical sites. CA-125 elevation serves as a corroborating but non-specific biomarker, elevated across diverse inflammatory, infectious, and malignant conditions including peritoneal tuberculosis and ovarian carcinoma.

Regarding treatment, surgical excision with clear margins combined with postoperative hormonal suppression using GnRH agonists followed by dienogest or progestins represents the most consistently endorsed paradigm for deep infiltrating, bowel, and extrapelvic disease. Fallopian tube endometriosis, present in 72.3% of women with biopsy-proven pelvic disease, correlates strongly with stage IV disease and contributes independently to tubal occlusion, hydrosalpinx, and reduced fecundity. Endometriosis-associated malignant transformation—to endometrioid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and carcinosarcoma—is documented at multiple extrapelvic sites including abdominal wall scars and ovaries, with scar endometriosis carrying an approximately 15% recurrence risk following resection.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Substantive conflicts exist across several domains. Hormonal therapy demonstrates inconsistent efficacy: GnRH agonists achieve satisfactory symptom control in select ureteral and thoracic cases, particularly when combined with surgery, yet fibrotic deeply infiltrating disease is repeatedly characterized as poorly responsive to hormonal suppression alone, and recurrence rates following combined surgical and medical therapy for thoracic endometriosis range widely from 8% to 55% across reports, reflecting heterogeneity in surgical completeness, hormonal regimens, and follow-up duration rather than a coherent evidence base. For abdominal wall endometriosis, cryoablation and ultrasound-guided microwave ablation demonstrate outcomes comparable to formal surgical excision, yet prospective long-term comparative data are absent, preventing definitive equivalence claims. One study of 161 patients found no histologically confirmed cases of true rectovaginal septum involvement, challenging established anatomical teaching and attributing historical prevalence data to misclassification of lower rectal or peritoneal lesions.

## Endometriosis: Clinical Presentations and Imaging

The papers reviewed collectively emphasize the remarkable anatomical diversity of endometriosis and the substantial diagnostic challenges this diversity creates. Dominant themes include extrapelvic and deeply infiltrating manifestations, imaging limitations requiring multimodal approaches, and the critical role of histopathological confirmation when imaging proves equivocal.

The most consistently reported finding across papers is that endometriosis extends well beyond the pelvis, with documented involvement of the ureter, bladder, bowel, abdominal wall, diaphragm, pleura, and inguinal canal. Deeply infiltrating disease—particularly ureteral endometriosis—was repeatedly highlighted as clinically silent until advanced obstructive uropathy or irreversible renal dysfunction develops, underscoring the need for routine renal surveillance. Ureteral endometriosis (PMC10882363, PMC11833833, PMC11869247) consistently presented with nonspecific symptoms such as renal colic and recurrent urinary tract infections, with CT urography and MRI identified as the preferred imaging combination. Thoracic endometriosis, manifesting as catamenial pneumothorax and hemothorax, was uniformly right-sided in approximately 90% of cases and associated with diaphragmatic defects expressing high estrogen receptor levels (PMC10806970, PMC11832986, PMC11839223). Abdominal wall endometriosis following cesarean section represented another recurrent theme, with ultrasound identifying hypoechoic lesions in the rectus abdominis region and wide surgical excision with clear margins consistently endorsed as definitive treatment (PMC11803579, PMC11851086, PMC11896734, PMC11910310).

Several papers highlight endometriosis as a predisposing factor for serious complications including spontaneous hemoperitoneum in pregnancy (PMC10916107, PMC11869271), malignant transformation to endometrioid carcinoma and carcinosarcoma (PMC11695655, PMC11879078), pelvic abscess following assisted reproduction (PMC11867230), and intestinal perforation (PMC11847643). Scar endometriosis following obstetric procedures, including episiotomy and cesarean section, was repeatedly noted to carry approximately 15% recurrence risk and rare malignant transformation potential (PMC11573930, PMC11851086).

Certain contradictions merit attention. Papers addressing hormonal therapy report conflicting efficacy: gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists achieved satisfactory symptom control in some ureteral and thoracic cases when combined with surgery, yet fibrotic deep infiltrating disease was frequently characterized as unresponsive to hormonal therapy alone (PMC11833833, PMC11869247). Similarly, while combined surgical and medical management was consistently superior for thoracic endometriosis (PMC11832986, PMC11893213), cryoablation for abdominal wall endometriosis demonstrated outcomes comparable to surgical excision (PMC11803579), suggesting treatment equivalence that warrants prospective validation. MRI was broadly endorsed as the gold standard for characterizing complex endometriosis, yet umbilical endometriosis papers noted that all imaging modalities showed limited preoperative sensitivity, necessitating histological confirmation (PMC11800267). Additionally, several non-endometriosis entities—including splenosis (PMC11896003), bladder leiomyoma (PMC10961557), leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (PMC11801444), and nevus lipomatosus superficialis (PMC11813040)—produced clinical and imaging phenotypes indistinguishable from endometriosis, reinforcing that broad differential diagnosis remains essential in women presenting with cyclic pelvic pain or pelvic masses.

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging of Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across these papers converges on several interrelated themes: the anatomical diversity of endometriosis beyond classical pelvic sites, the diagnostic challenges posed by its mimicry of other conditions, and the complementary roles of imaging modalities in disease characterization. Endometriosis emerges as a multifocal, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder driven by retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and lymphovascular dissemination, with iatrogenic implantation representing an additional mechanism in abdominal wall and splenic cases following cesarean section or prior surgery. The disease's hormonal dependency is underscored by its persistence in postmenopausal women through peripheral estrogen production in adipose tissue, unopposed hormone replacement therapy, and tamoxifen use—mechanisms that collectively sustain ectopic endometrial viability beyond the reproductive years.

Obstructive Müllerian anomalies, including unicornuate uterus with non-communicating rudimentary horns, OHVIRA syndrome, and imperforate hymen, represent a distinct but recurrent mechanistic theme: retrograde menstruation secondary to outflow obstruction predisposes to endometriosis development, particularly in adolescent patients. This association reinforces the centrality of retrograde flow in disease pathogenesis and highlights the importance of early surgical correction to prevent endometriotic sequelae.

Extrapelvic endometriosis constitutes a prominent subtheme, with documented involvement of the thoracic cavity, appendix, terminal ileum, ureter, bladder including urachal involvement, spleen, umbilicus, round ligaments, canal of Nuck, and abdominal wall. Thoracic endometriosis syndrome manifests characteristically as catamenial pneumothorax—reported in 50–73% of thoracic cases—alongside hemothorax, hemoptysis, and chest pain, with diaphragmatic perforations producing pathognomonic "Swiss cheese" defects. Gastrointestinal involvement, while predominantly rectosigmoid, extends to the appendix and terminal ileum, where endometrial implants expressing estrogen and progesterone receptors infiltrate the muscularis propria and trigger fibrosis, adhesion formation, and mechanical complications including intussusception and small bowel obstruction. Appendiceal endometriosis, present in less than 1% of cases, recurrently emerges as a diagnostic mimic of acute appendicitis, resolvable only through histopathological confirmation demonstrating endometrial glands, stroma, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and cytokeratin 7 and estrogen receptor positivity.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several findings appear with high consistency across papers. First, the diagnostic performance of transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is repeatedly compared, with a meta-analytic synthesis demonstrating statistically equivalent sensitivity and specificity for rectosigmoid deep infiltrating endometriosis—TVS achieving sensitivity 0.85 and specificity 0.92, MRI achieving sensitivity 0.83 and specificity 0.95. However, MRI demonstrates superiority for lesions in the rectovaginal septum, round ligaments, and ileal involvement, while TVS more effectively characterizes rectosigmoid disease due to intestinal gas artefact degrading MRI quality in this location. MRI with targeted endometriosis protocols consistently identifies characteristic T2-hypointense nodules with hyperintense glandular components and T1-hypointense lesions with intense contrast enhancement, patterns reproducibly described in round ligament endometriosis in monozygotic twins and bladder endometriosis. Pelvic MRI for bladder endometriosis specifically demonstrates sensitivity up to 88% and specificity 99%, with immunohistochemical confirmation showing estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and vimentin expression.

Second, CA-125 elevation is consistently reported as a supportive but non-specific diagnostic marker across pelvic endometriosis, thoracic endometriosis syndrome, peritoneal tuberculosis, and moss-like endometriosis with hemorrhagic ascites, repeatedly cautioned against use as a standalone diagnostic criterion given its elevation across diverse conditions including ovarian malignancy and granulomatous

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging of Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across these 50 articles, several overarching themes emerge. Endometriosis manifests across a remarkably broad anatomical spectrum—from canonical pelvic locations to extrapelvic sites including the thorax, gastrointestinal tract, abdominal wall, biliary system, inguinal canal, and vulvar skin—underscoring the systemic nature of what is often conceptualized as a localized pelvic disorder. The pathogenic mechanisms most consistently invoked include retrograde menstruation with ectopic implantation, hematogenous and lymphatic dissemination, coelomic metaplasia, and iatrogenic transplantation during surgical procedures, particularly cesarean section. Estrogen dependence and TGF-β–mediated fibrosis are repeatedly cited as drivers of lesion establishment, progressive tissue infiltration, and adhesion formation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Cyclical symptomatology synchronized with menstruation is the most consistently reported diagnostic clue across diverse anatomical presentations, including catamenial pneumothorax, thoracic endometriosis syndrome, inguinal endometriosis, abdominal wall endometriosis, and cutaneous endometriosis. Histopathological confirmation—demonstrating endometrial glands, stroma, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and estrogen receptor or CD10 positivity—remains the diagnostic gold standard regardless of lesion location. MRI is repeatedly identified as the superior imaging modality for characterizing deep, complex, or extrapelvic disease, while ultrasound serves reliably as first-line evaluation for adnexal and abdominal wall lesions. Surgical excision with adequate margins is consistently reported as definitive treatment, with postoperative hormonal suppression using GnRH agonists, progestins, or dienogest reducing recurrence risk. Endometriosis frequently coexists with anatomical anomalies—including MRKH syndrome, imperforate hymen, and Müllerian duct malformations—where obstructed menstrual outflow potentiates retrograde seeding. CA-125 elevation appears across multiple contexts, including spontaneous endometrioma rupture and thoracic endometriosis, though its specificity remains limited given overlap with malignancy and tuberculosis.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several tensions are apparent. Although laparoscopic approaches are broadly endorsed for fertility preservation and reduced adhesion formation, some cases—particularly those involving large or deeply infiltrating lesions—required conversion to open surgery, suggesting that procedure selection remains individualized rather than uniformly standardized. The recurrence rates following combined surgical and hormonal therapy for thoracic endometriosis vary substantially across reports (8–55%), reflecting heterogeneity in follow-up duration, hormonal regimens, and surgical completeness rather than a coherent evidence base. Additionally, while ultrasound and MRI achieved equivalent diagnostic accuracy under O-RADS guidance in one comparative study, other reports emphasize MRI's superiority for extrapelvic and deep infiltrating lesions, creating some inconsistency regarding when imaging escalation is warranted. The distinction between appendiceal deciduosis and appendiceal endometriosis—both capable of precipitating acute appendicitis—is noted as clinically important yet histologically challenging, with no consensus on preoperative differentiation. Collectively, these papers highlight that endometriosis remains a diagnostically heterogeneous condition requiring individualized multimodal evaluation and flexible surgical planning.

# Clinical Presentations and Imaging in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

## Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster collectively emphasize endometriosis as a multisystem disease extending well beyond its classical pelvic boundaries, with clinical presentations spanning gastrointestinal, urological, thoracic, cutaneous, and even pancreatic compartments. Retrograde menstruation remains the most consistently invoked pathogenic mechanism across papers, supplemented by coelomic metaplasia and lymphovascular dissemination to explain extrapelvic manifestations such as diaphragmatic, thoracic, and pancreatic involvement. Proinflammatory mediators including TNF-α, IL-1β, VEGF, and IGF, alongside immune dysregulation involving reduced natural killer cell activity, are repeatedly implicated in disease establishment and progression. Estrogen dependence is a recurrent mechanistic theme, underscored by rat model data demonstrating that elevated estrogen-to-progesterone ratios drive endometrial malignant transformation.

## Consistently Reported Findings

Delayed and misdiagnosis emerges as the most consistently documented clinical problem, with gastrointestinal endometriosis frequently misattributed to irritable bowel syndrome or inflammatory bowel disease, and appendiceal or bladder endometriosis mimicking appendicitis or neoplasia, respectively. Multimodal imaging—combining transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI, and endoscopic ultrasound—is consistently recommended over any single modality, with MRI demonstrating particular utility for deep infiltrating endometriosis, bladder involvement (T1-hyperintense foci, wall thickening), and diaphragmatic disease. Bowel endometriosis predominantly affects the rectosigmoid colon in approximately 90% of cases across multiple independent reports, producing cyclic gastrointestinal symptoms. Catamenial pneumothorax, reported in two papers, consistently demonstrates right-sided predominance and responds to combined surgical and hormonal management. Immunohistochemical markers CK7, PAX8, CD10, and estrogen receptor positivity reliably confirm ectopic endometrial tissue across diverse anatomical sites. Congenital Müllerian anomalies, particularly OHVIRA syndrome, are repeatedly identified as predisposing to secondary endometriosis through obstructed menstrual outflow.

## Contradictions and Conflicting Findings

Notable tensions exist regarding optimal treatment sequencing: several papers advocate hormonal suppression as primary management while others emphasize surgical resection as definitively superior, particularly for deep infiltrating and bowel disease. Regarding diaphragmatic repair in catamenial pneumothorax, one report favors biological mesh with zero recurrence, while another suggests GnRH agonists alone can achieve prolonged control, reflecting unresolved questions about when surgical intervention is strictly necessary. Additionally, endoscopic ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration is claimed to exceed pelvic MRI in diagnostic accuracy for intestinal endometriosis in one paper, contradicting the broader literature consensus positioning MRI as the reference standard for deep disease characterization.

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging of Endometriosis: A Synthesis

The collected literature collectively highlights the remarkable anatomical versatility of endometriosis and the persistent diagnostic challenges this versatility creates across clinical practice. The dominant theme is ectopic implantation of endometrial glands and stroma at sites far removed from the uterine cavity, with mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, lymphovascular dissemination, and direct iatrogenic transplantation during obstetric or surgical procedures. Cesarean section emerges as the most consistently cited precipitating event for extrapelvic disease, accounting for approximately 80% of abdominal wall endometriosis cases, while episiotomy, appendectomy, and other pelvic surgeries contribute to scar-related presentations.

Across extragonadal sites—appendix, cecum, rectum, umbilicus, abdominal wall, cervix, liver, thoracic cavity, and inguinal structures including the canal of Nuck—cyclical pain synchronized with menstruation and a palpable mass constitute the hallmark presentation. Immunohistochemical confirmation with estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and CD10 expression is consistently reported as essential for definitive diagnosis, particularly when tissue architecture is distorted by fibrosis, hemorrhage, or degeneration. MRI demonstrates superior diagnostic performance over transvaginal ultrasound for endometriomas, with reported sensitivity of 95.9% versus 78.3%, and T1 hyperintensity with T2 shading reliably identifies chronic hemorrhagic content.

A consistent finding across gastrointestinal presentations is the capacity to mimic malignancy or acute surgical emergencies, including appendicitis, colorectal carcinoma, and cecal neoplasm, necessitating histopathological confirmation in all cases. Thoracic endometriosis syndrome, though rare, reproducibly presents with right-sided catamenial pneumothorax attributable to peritoneal fluid dynamics and diaphragmatic fenestrations.

Minor contradictions exist regarding management: surgical excision with clear margins is uniformly endorsed as the gold standard, yet several reports document sustained symptomatic improvement with progestin-based hormonal therapy, dienogest, or GnRH analogues, suggesting comparable outcomes in selected patients unwilling or unable to undergo resection. Additionally, minimally invasive approaches such as cryoablation show promising results for abdominal wall lesions, though long-term comparative data against formal excision remain limited. The potential for malignant transformation—to endometrioid, clear cell, or signet ring cell carcinoma—is mentioned across multiple sites but remains incompletely quantified, representing an area requiring prospective study.

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging of Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across these papers converges on several interrelated themes: the extraordinary anatomical diversity of endometriosis and its mimics, the central role of advanced imaging in diagnosis, and the challenge of distinguishing endometriotic lesions from malignant or infectious pathology. Endometriosis emerges as a systemic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease capable of infiltrating virtually any anatomical compartment, including the bladder detrusor muscle, sigmoid colon, appendix, iliopsoas muscle, femoral lymph nodes, thoracic cavity, umbilicus, and abdominal wall. The pathophysiological mechanisms consistently invoked include retrograde menstruation, lymphovascular dissemination, and coelomic metaplasia, with lesion establishment driven by pro-inflammatory cytokines, prostaglandins, and immune cell infiltration. Outflow obstruction—as in OHVIRA syndrome and noncommunicating rudimentary uterine horns—represents an additional mechanistic pathway specifically implicated in retrograde endometrial seeding.

### Consistently Reported Findings

MRI is repeatedly identified as the cornerstone imaging modality, with specific diagnostic signatures reported across multiple anatomical sites: T1-hyperintense foci indicating subacute hemorrhage, T2 shading reflecting chronic recurrent bleeding in endometriomas, T2 dark spots from chronic hemorrhage, and the "mushroom cap" sign in deep infiltrating endometriosis involving the rectosigmoid. Transvaginal ultrasonography achieves sensitivity and specificity exceeding 98% for ovarian endometriomas and demonstrates high accuracy for pouch of Douglas obliteration, but performs poorly for superficial peritoneal lesions (sensitivity 4–43.5%). Deep infiltrating endometriosis most consistently localizes to the uterosacral ligaments, posterior cul-de-sac, rectosigmoid colon, and torus uterinus. Surgical resection—laparoscopic where feasible—combined with postoperative hormonal suppression using GnRH agonists followed by progestogens such as dienogest is the most consistently recommended treatment paradigm across bladder, bowel, thoracic, and abdominal wall manifestations. Appendiceal endometriosis, identified in approximately 0.61–10% of female appendectomy specimens, recurrently presents as a mimic of acute appendicitis or appendiceal neoplasm, requiring histopathological confirmation.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several points of tension emerge. Regarding catamenial pneumothorax, one report documents the condition in the complete absence of histologically confirmed thoracic endometriotic implants, attributing the mechanism to systemic prostaglandin F2α-mediated bronchoconstriction and alveolar rupture, while other papers treat thoracic endometriotic tissue as a necessary prerequisite—a contradiction with implications for diagnostic criteria. Concerning MRI contrast utilization, one study demonstrates that intravenous gadolinium significantly improves visualization of deep lesion penetration in the rectosigmoid and posterior uterine wall, whereas ovarian endometriomas are detected equally well on non-contrast sequences, suggesting contrast protocols should be site-specific rather than uniformly applied. Additionally, while multiple papers affirm that TVUS diagnostic accuracy is highest when patients with ovarian endometriosis and pouch of Douglas obliteration are excluded, this creates circularity in clinical application, since these conditions are frequently co-present with the superficial disease that TVUS performs least adequately in detecting. Finally, the literature on abdominal wall endometriosis recurrence identifies the number of cesarean sections as the strongest independent predictor, yet the broader surgical literature treats cesarean-associated implantation as a discrete rather than dose-dependent phenomenon—a distinction warranting prospective validation.

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging of Endometriosis: A Synthesis

The papers in this cluster collectively illustrate that endometriosis is a systemic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition extending well beyond its classical pelvic boundaries, with clinical presentations ranging from typical dysmenorrhea and infertility to life-threatening emergencies and rare extrapelvic manifestations. A dominant theme is the diagnostic challenge posed by endometriosis mimicking unrelated pathologies—acute appendicitis (appendiceal endometriosis), nerve sheath tumors (pubic symphysis lesions), ovarian malignancy (hydatid cysts, peritoneal tuberculosis), and soft tissue sarcomas (abdominal wall endometriosis)—underscoring the necessity of maintaining broad differential diagnoses in women of reproductive age.

The most consistently reported finding across studies is the estrogen-dependence of ectopic endometrial tissue, with hormonal responsiveness confirmed through immunohistochemical estrogen receptor positivity in lesions at diverse anatomical sites including the appendix, vagina, umbilicus, bladder, abdominal wall scars, diaphragm, and parametrium. Histopathological confirmation of endometrial glands and stroma with CD10 and estrogen receptor positivity remains the diagnostic gold standard across virtually all reports. Serum CA-125 elevation appears repeatedly as a corroborating biomarker, though it lacks specificity, being elevated also in peritoneal tuberculosis, ovarian neoplasms, and other inflammatory conditions. Regarding imaging, transvaginal ultrasound and MRI emerge as complementary modalities, with MRI preferred for deep infiltrating and parametrial disease, while standardized ultrasound protocols demonstrate diagnostic performance approaching MRI for parametrial assessment. Intraoperative adjuncts, including the Aqua Blue Contrast Technique and indocyanine green fluorescence imaging, substantially improve lesion detection and ureteral identification during surgical resection.

Surgical excision with clear margins represents the definitive treatment across all anatomical variants, with postoperative hormonal suppression using GnRH analogues, progestins, or combined regimens consistently reported to reduce recurrence risk. Several papers document the iatrogenic dimension of endometriosis, particularly cesarean section as a primary risk factor for scar and abdominal wall endometriosis through mechanical implantation, and laparoscopic morcellation as a mechanism for parasitic leiomyoma dissemination.

Notable contradictions exist regarding the anatomical distribution of deep endometriosis: one study of 161 patients found no cases of true rectovaginal septum involvement, challenging established teaching and attributing prior diagnoses to misclassified lower rectal or peritoneal lesions—a finding that conflicts with textbook descriptions and historical prevalence data. Additionally, some papers emphasize conservative hormonal management as effective first-line therapy, while others document high recurrence rates following cessation, suggesting that the evidence base for non-surgical approaches remains inconsistent. Collectively, these papers support a paradigm of endometriosis as a multisystem condition requiring multimodal imaging, precise surgical mapping, and individualized hormonal management to address both visible and occult disease burden.

## Clinical Presentations and Imaging in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature in this cluster converges on three dominant themes: (1) the diverse anatomical distribution of endometriosis beyond the pelvis, encompassing extrapelvic and iatrogenic sites; (2) the pathogenic role of obstructive Müllerian anomalies in driving secondary endometriosis through retrograde menstruation; and (3) the diagnostic challenges posed by endometriosis mimicking or coexisting with malignant, infectious, and structural pathologies. Iatrogenic implantation emerges as the predominant mechanism for abdominal wall and umbilical endometriosis, particularly following cesarean section and laparoscopic procedures, with estrogen and progesterone receptor-positive ectopic endometrial cells surviving and proliferating at surgical incision sites. Separately, obstructive anomalies—including imperforate hymen, OHVIRA syndrome, distal vaginal atresia, cervical hypoplasia, and rudimentary uterine horns—consistently precipitate hematometrocolpos and retrograde menstruation, establishing endometriosis as a secondary complication in 13–60% of such cases depending on the specific anomaly and diagnostic timing.

### Consistently Reported Findings

MRI is uniformly identified as the gold standard imaging modality across obstructive Müllerian anomalies, deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE), and thoracic endometriosis syndrome, with jelly-enhanced MRI specifically endorsed for preoperative mapping of rectosigmoid involvement. Ultrasound retains value as a first-line screening tool, with characteristic hypoechoic nodules with hyperechoic rims described in AWE. Histopathological confirmation—demonstrating endometrial glands, stroma, and CD10 immunopositivity—remains the diagnostic standard across anatomical sites. Fallopian tube endometriosis, present in 72.3% of women with biopsy-proven pelvic disease and correlating strongly with stage IV disease, is identified as a significant contributor to tubal occlusion, hydrosalpinx, and reduced fecundity. Intestinal endometriosis, predominantly affecting the rectosigmoid, characteristically invades from the serosa inward and is associated with obliteration of the pouch of Douglas; complete intestinal stenosis, though rare (<1% of intestinal cases), represents a severe complication requiring segmental resection. Elevated CA-125 is reported across multiple manifestations—intestinal, thoracic, and fallopian tube disease—though its diagnostic specificity is limited by elevation in inflammatory and other benign conditions. Dienogest and GnRH analogs feature consistently as postoperative hormonal suppression strategies to reduce recurrence following surgical management of DIE and bowel endometriosis.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Evidence

A notable conceptual tension exists regarding the etiology of endometriosis in Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome. While coelomic metaplasia and embryonic cell remnant theories have historically been invoked to explain endometriosis in the absence of a functional uterus, one study provides imaging-based evidence that endometriosis in MRKH patients occurs significantly more frequently when functional uterine remnants are identifiable, thereby supporting the implantation theory as the primary mechanism. This finding does not entirely exclude alternative pathways but shifts the evidential weight toward retrograde implantation as a necessary initiating factor. Additionally, while complete surgical excision is universally endorsed as the gold standard for AWE and extrapelvic disease, some reports document high recurrence rates following medical therapy alone, yet emerging minimally invasive thermal ablation techniques (HIFU, cryoablation, microwave ablation) are proposed as alternatives without comparative long-term recurrence data. The diagnostic utility of FDG-PET in endometrioma surveillance is complicated by documented false-positive uptake in cholesterol granulomas, creating potential overestimation of malignant transformation rates and underscoring the inadequacy of metabolic imaging alone in this context.

## Clinical Presentations, Imaging Modalities, and Diagnostic Challenges in Endometriosis and Related Gynecological Conditions

The papers within this cluster collectively address the protean clinical manifestations of endometriosis and closely related gynecological conditions, emphasizing the diagnostic challenges posed by ectopic endometrial implants across anatomical compartments and the central role of advanced imaging in guiding management.

A dominant theme is the capacity of endometrial tissue to establish functionally active implants far beyond the pelvic cavity. Extrapelvic manifestations are prominently documented, encompassing pulmonary parenchyma causing cyclical hemoptysis, the diaphragm producing catamenial pneumothorax and hepatic herniation, the bladder dome causing cyclical voiding dysfunction, the ureter causing silent hydronephrosis, the appendix precipitating intussusception, abdominal wall scars following cesarean delivery, umbilical port sites, and, rarely, ascitic fluid mimicking ovarian malignancy. Across these reports, a unifying pathogenic mechanism is retrograde or hematogenous/lymphatic dissemination of endometrial tissue, with iatrogenic seeding via surgical trauma representing a recurrent contributing factor. Vascular dissemination is further supported by intravascular adenomyosis observations in nonhuman primates, with CD31/CD34 immunohistochemistry confirming endothelial-lined containment of endometrial tissue within myometrial veins.

A second dominant theme concerns Müllerian anomalies as structural risk amplifiers for endometriosis. Multiple independent reports—covering Robert's uterus, OHVIRA syndrome, rudimentary uterine horn with ipsilateral renal agenesis, imperforate hymen, and bicorporeal septate uterus—converge on the mechanism of obstructed menstrual outflow driving hematometra, retrograde menstruation, and secondary endometriosis. A retrospective cohort study applying the Enzian classification confirmed that obstruction-associated uterine morphologies carry the highest endometriosis prevalence, while aplastic uteri lacking functional endometrium demonstrated markedly lower rates, directly implicating functional endometrial tissue combined with outflow obstruction as necessary co-determinants of disease development. A predictive nomogram study further identified preoperative hematometra volume exceeding 5 cm³ and elevated rASRM stage as independent predictors of postoperative endometrioma recurrence (5-year AUC = 0.862), reinforcing the mechanistic link between obstruction severity and disease burden.

Imaging modalities receive consistent emphasis across papers. MRI is repeatedly identified as the gold standard for characterizing lesion depth, extent, and tissue involvement across pelvic and extrapelvic endometriosis, with specific mention of T1/T2 signal characteristics, the "black garland sign," and O-RADS risk stratification for ovarian masses. Transvaginal ultrasound is endorsed for initial evaluation and intraoperative guidance, including ultrasound-guided microwave ablation of abdominal wall endometriosis. Several papers note MRI's superiority over CT and ultrasound for diagnosing Müllerian anomalies, characterizing ureteral involvement, and mapping deep infiltrating disease involving the uterosacral ligaments and bladder. Proteomics-based biomarker discovery in cervicovaginal fluid identified legumain (LGMN), NAMPT, OLFM4, and SPARCL1 as candidate diagnostic proteins quantified by SWATH-MS, representing a minimally invasive alternative to imaging and surgery for reducing the documented 3–10-year diagnostic delay.

Immunohistochemical confirmation of endometrial origin is consistently reported across cases, with CD10 positivity for endometrial stroma, estrogen and progesterone receptor expression, and PAX-8 epithelial marking serving as standard diagnostic markers. Thoracic endometriosis lesions additionally demonstrate matrix metalloproteinase activity and epithelial-mesenchymal transition as mechanisms enabling diaphragmatic invasion, distinguishing the pathophysiology of thoracic disease from conventional pelvic endometriosis.

# Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: An Integrated Review

## Overview

A compelling convergence of evidence has reframed endometriosis not as an isolated pelvic disorder but as a systemic inflammatory and metabolic condition embedded within a broader pathophysiological continuum that includes insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, oxidative stress, and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis dysregulation. The core research question animating this literature is whether—and through what mechanisms—metabolic dysfunction causally contributes to endometriosis susceptibility, disease progression, ovarian reserve depletion, and infertility. Answering this question requires disentangling the reciprocal relationships among visceral adiposity, chronic low-grade inflammation, hormonal disruption, and reproductive failure across diverse study designs spanning Mendelian randomization, metabolomics, epigenomics, and large population-based cohort analyses.

## Consensus Findings

The most reproducible finding across this literature is the independent association of composite metabolic indices—including the triglyceride-glucose (TyG) index, cardiometabolic index (CMI), lipid accumulation product (LAP), and non-HDL-to-HDL cholesterol ratio (NHHR)—with both endometriosis risk and female infertility. Women in the highest LAP quartile demonstrate approximately a 93% increased endometriosis risk, while each unit increase in NHHR confers a 14–17% elevated odds of disease. Mendelian randomization analyses establish causal contributions of elevated triglycerides, BMI, and fasting insulin, with visceral adipose tissue promoting ectopic endometrial cell survival through interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and MCP-1 signaling. These same cytokines disrupt steroidogenesis, suppress gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility, and impair endometrial receptivity, linking adipose-driven inflammation mechanistically to both conditions simultaneously.

Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) functions as the most consistently validated intermediate biomarker across the literature. Endometriosis is reproducibly associated with reduced AMH independent of patient age, reflecting accelerated follicular depletion through cortical fibrosis, granulosa cell apoptosis, and ischemia surrounding endometriomas. Laparoscopic cystectomy further compounds this deficit, with postoperative AMH declining approximately 38–57% depending on laterality and hemostatic technique; bipolar electrocoagulation causes significantly greater collateral follicular damage than suture or oxidized cellulose polymer hemostasis—a finding corroborated across multiple independent cohorts. Systemic inflammatory indices, including the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), inversely predict ovarian reserve parameters and postoperative spontaneous conception rates in endometriosis, establishing peripheral inflammation as a quantifiable, non-invasive prognostic tool.

Oxidative stress constitutes a third consensus mechanism. Elevated reactive oxygen species impair oocyte maturation, compromise embryonic development, and promote sperm DNA fragmentation across male and female reproductive compartments alike. Follicular fluid metabolomic profiling in diminished ovarian reserve reveals perturbations in glycerophospholipid metabolism, steroid hormone biosynthesis, and pentose phosphate pathways, with reduced estrone-3-sulfate and 5α-pregnane-3,20-dione serving as candidate biomarkers of compromised follicular microenvironments. Dietary antioxidant intake—operationalized through the oxidative balance score integrating zinc, magnesium, carotenoids, and vitamins—reduces infertility odds by approximately 43%, while adherence to anti-inflammatory dietary patterns reduces risk by up to 70%, partially mediated through reductions in BMI and serum uric acid.

Epigenetic mechanisms are implicated through aberrant DNA methylation at HOXA10 and progesterone receptor-B promoters, contributing to progesterone resistance as a hallmark of endometriosis-associated implantation failure. Epigenetic aging at loci including ELOVL2 and KLF14 independently predicts IVF live birth outcomes, reducing success likelihood by approximately 9% per additional epigenetic year, positioning biological aging mediated through oxidative and inflammatory processes as a determinant of reproductive competence that transcends conventional ovarian reserve metrics.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several genuine conflicts persist within this literature. The relationship between CMI and endometriosis is paradoxically inverse in some cross-sectional datasets, with severe disease (AAGL stages III–IV) associated with lower BMI and body fat percentage than mild disease—an apparent contradiction that likely reflects competing mechanistic pathways, wherein intermediate adiposity drives aromatase activity and retrograde menstruation susceptibility while extreme leanness may exacerbate neurogenic pain sensitization without reducing lesion burden. Similarly, while AMH robustly predicts oocyte yield and early pregnancy loss risk in younger women, at least one study reports that supraphysiological AMH may paradoxically reduce oocyte developmental competence, and another demonstrates that cumulative live birth rates following IVF in women with latent tuberculosis-related AMH suppression are not significantly compromised—dissociating ovarian reserve depletion from implantation potential and challenging the assumption that AMH decline invariably predicts ART failure. The reproductive consequences of obesity present a related ambiguity: while maternal obesity consistently impairs ovulatory function, oocyte quality, and steroidogenesis, clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in IVF were not significantly different across BMI groups in at least one large cohort, suggesting that laboratory-assisted fertilization may partially compensate for upstream metabolic impairments while downstream gestational complications persist. These discordances collectively indicate that metabolic dysfunction exerts stage-specific rather than uniformly adverse effects across the reproductive continuum.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Several critical mechanistic questions remain inadequately addressed. The directionality of the endometriosis-metabolic dysfunction relationship is not established; it remains unclear whether metabolic dysfunction precedes and promotes endometriosis development, whether the chronic inflammatory burden of established disease secondarily induces systemic metabolic disturbance, or whether both processes are driven by shared upstream genetic or epigenetic determinants. The role of gut microbiota in mediating estrogen metabolism through the estrobolome—and thereby modulating both metabolic

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: Synthesis of Research Themes

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on three interconnected domains: metabolic dysfunction as a driver of reproductive failure, ovarian reserve as a measurable intermediate phenotype linking systemic disease to infertility, and the inflammatory microenvironment as a common mechanistic substrate. A consistent overarching narrative emerges in which insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, visceral adiposity, and chronic low-grade inflammation collectively impair the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, folliculogenesis, and endometrial receptivity.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Metabolic indices incorporating both lipid and adiposity parameters—including the triglyceride-glucose (TyG) index, atherogenic index of plasma (AIP), and cardiometabolic index (CMI)—are repeatedly associated with infertility risk and endometriosis susceptibility. The TyG-derived indices link insulin resistance mechanistically to endometriosis through upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17α) and oxidative stress pathways, while CMI elevation confers a 34% increased infertility risk per unit through visceral adipose-mediated cytokine secretion and ovarian hyperandrogenism. Elevated BMI independently reduces oocyte and embryo quality, increases gonadotropin requirements, and raises miscarriage risk across diverse populations.

Ovarian reserve, indexed by AMH and AFC, emerges as a critical intermediate biomarker. Endometriosis is consistently associated with reduced AMH levels, consistent with accelerated follicular depletion, while polycystic ovary syndrome demonstrates the opposite—elevated AMH reflecting follicular arrest. Systemic inflammation, measured by C-reactive protein, further suppresses inhibin B and FSH-regulated follicular dynamics. Inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and Crohn's disease share a common trajectory of diminished ovarian reserve. Oncostatin M signaling, PI3K-Akt, and IL-10 anti-inflammatory pathways are identified as regulators of follicular development, with OSM correlating positively with AFC and retrieved oocyte numbers.

Oxidative stress represents another consistently implicated mechanism: elevated reactive oxygen species impair oocyte maturation, embryonic development, and implantation, while a higher oxidative balance score—integrating dietary antioxidants and lifestyle behaviors—reduces infertility odds by approximately 43%. This protective effect operates through antioxidant defense pathways involving zinc, magnesium, and dietary vitamins, which are deficient in infertile women and those with inflammatory comorbidities such as celiac disease. Celiac disease itself demonstrates a distinct mechanistic pathway to reduced ovarian reserve, combining intestinal inflammation with nutritional malabsorption of iron, folate, zinc, and selenium.

Endometriosis-specific findings are reinforced across multiple papers. The ACSAP score for endometrioma recurrence identifies younger age, larger cyst size, prior surgery, and advanced disease stage as independent recurrence predictors, while postoperative pregnancy is protective—likely through anovulation-induced lesion necrosis. Extended medical suppression with GnRH agonists or levonorgestrel intrauterine systems significantly reduces recurrence in intermediate- and high-risk groups. At the molecular level, endometrial receptivity failure in repeated implantation failure is linked to elevated miR-31 expression, which correlates inversely with endometrial thickness and blood flow parameters, potentially through disruption of steroid hormone metabolism and implantation window regulation.

Vitamin D sufficiency is positively associated with live birth rates in fertility treatment, with sufficient 25(OH)D conferring a 28% higher likelihood of live birth, particularly in normal-BMI couples—suggesting that obesity-related chronic inflammation attenuates vitamin D's reproductive benefits. Male factors are not neglected: bicycling with soft seats reduces fecundability, androgen receptor GGN polymorphisms associate with AFC but not stimulation response, and paternal age independently predicts miscarriage risk alongside maternal age.

Epigenetic mechanisms are implicated through correlated regions of systemic interindividual variation (CoRSIVs), which are enriched 53.5-fold in endocrine disorders

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across these 50 articles, three interconnected themes emerge consistently: the role of metabolic dysregulation in reproductive failure, the centrality of ovarian reserve markers—particularly anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH)—in predicting fertility outcomes, and the pathophysiological overlap between systemic inflammatory states and endometriosis-associated reproductive dysfunction. A unifying mechanistic framework positions chronic low-grade inflammation, oxidative stress, and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis disruption as convergent pathways through which metabolic conditions impair fertility. Endometriosis appears repeatedly not as an isolated gynecological condition but as a disease embedded within broader metabolic and inflammatory contexts, sharing pathogenic mechanisms with conditions including metabolic syndrome, NAFLD, PCOS, and type 2 diabetes.

### Consistently Reported Findings

AMH emerges as the most consistently validated biomarker across studies, with multiple independent investigations confirming its superiority over age and antral follicle count for predicting ovarian response, oocyte yield, and IVF outcomes. Endometriosis is specifically and repeatedly associated with reduced AMH and diminished ovarian reserve independent of age, with surgical resection of endometriomas further compromising reserve through damage to healthy ovarian tissue and follicular vasculature. Studies examining the IVF-before-surgery sequence demonstrate that preserving ovarian reserve by delaying endometrioma resection significantly improves clinical pregnancy rates, fertilization success, and embryo quality, consistent with the mechanistic evidence that endometrioma surgery reduces GDF-9 and BMP-15 expression critical for oocyte maturation.

Metabolic indices—including the cardiometabolic index (CMI), triglyceride-to-HDL ratio, uric acid-to-HDL ratio (UHR), and the dietary inflammatory index—are independently and reproducibly associated with female infertility risk across multiple NHANES-based cross-sectional analyses. The shared mechanistic pathway involves central obesity driving insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogens, which collectively disrupt HPO axis signaling and impair ovulatory function. Importantly, endometriosis itself is cited across several studies as a condition mechanistically linked to chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, positioning it within the same pathogenic framework as metabolic syndrome components. CMI association with endometriosis is specifically noted, and NAFLD severity is shown to escalate ovarian cancer risk in young women who disproportionately carry comorbid endometriosis diagnoses.

Gut microbiota composition and dietary quality emerge as consistently protective factors, with higher Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota scores associated with substantially reduced female infertility risk. The proposed mechanisms involve flavonoids and dietary fiber reducing reactive oxygen species that compromise oocyte quality, while healthy microbiota regulate estrogen metabolism and steroid hormone levels through the AhR-IL-22 signaling axis—a pathway implicated specifically in both PCOS and endometriosis pathogenesis. Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns additionally delay menopause onset, suggesting diet modulates the reproductive lifespan through estrogen-inflammatory interactions.

Epigenetic mechanisms are highlighted in the context of endometriosis, with aberrant DNA methylation at HOXA10 and progesterone receptor-B promoters contributing to progesterone resistance—a hallmark feature of the disease. Environmental exposures including pollutants, nutritional status, and socioeconomic factors substantially shape these methylation patterns independent of genetic inheritance, with combined polygenic and methylation risk scores achieving modest but improved predictive accuracy (AUC up to 0.73). Mitochondrial genetic variation, specifically haplogroup R conferring resistance to age-related oxidative stress and telomere shortening, is identified as an additional layer of biological regulation influencing ovarian aging and gynecological pathologies including endometriosis.

Genetic architecture studies reveal that dizygotic twinning polygenic scores show a positive genetic correlation with endometriosis while negatively correlating with anovulatory infertility, suggesting that endometriosis occupies a distinct position on the female fertility genetic spectrum—associated with hyper-ovulatory rather than anovulatory biological mechanisms.
