## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on a central framework linking metabolic dysfunction, oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and hormonal dysregulation as interconnected drivers of endometriosis-associated infertility. Across multiple study designs—including cross-sectional analyses of NHANES data, prospective cohort studies, systematic reviews, and metabolomic investigations—insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and adipokine dysregulation emerge as mechanistically central to both endometriosis pathogenesis and broader reproductive failure. Visceral adiposity, operationalized through composite indices such as the lipid accumulation product (LAP), cardiometabolic index (CMI), and non-HDL/HDL cholesterol ratio (NHHR), consistently associates with endometriosis prevalence, with women in the highest LAP quartile demonstrating a 93% increased risk and each unit increase in NHHR conferring a 17% elevated risk. These indices capture shared pathophysiology involving pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-6, TNF-α, MCP-1), reactive oxygen species generation, and impaired nitric oxide-mediated signaling that collectively promote ectopic endometrial tissue survival and progression.

Oxidative stress constitutes a second dominant theme, manifesting across multiple reproductive compartments. Elevated total oxidant status and oxidative stress index in follicular fluid correlate with poor embryo quality and unexplained infertility, while thiol/disulfide redox imbalance in follicular fluid predicts poor ovarian response. Endometriosis amplifies this oxidative burden through chronic local inflammation surrounding endometriomas, which triggers granulosa and cumulus cell apoptosis, progressively depletes anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), and impairs oocyte maturation. Metabolomic profiling of follicular fluid in poor ovarian response patients reveals perturbations in glycerophospholipid metabolism, choline metabolism, and autophagy pathways, with downregulated estrone-3-sulfate and phosphatidylinositol serving as candidate biomarkers of compromised follicular microenvironments.

Anti-Müllerian hormone functions as a unifying biomarker across this cluster, reflecting ovarian reserve status in endometriosis, poor ovarian response, ovarian cyst disease, FMR1 genetic variation, thyroid autoimmunity, and post-surgical states. Ovarian endometriomas reduce AMH through cortical fibrosis and follicular atresia, with stage III/IV disease and bilateral involvement producing the most pronounced decrements. Surgical cystectomy further reduces AMH by 39.5–57.0% depending on laterality and hemostatic technique, with bipolar electrocoagulation causing significantly greater damage than suture hemostasis—a finding corroborated across multiple independent studies. AMH additionally predicts long-term skeletal health, cumulative live birth rates in IVF, and medically assisted reproduction outcomes in women over 35, underscoring its broad clinical utility.

Immune dysregulation represents a third dominant mechanism, operating through multiple axes. Thyroid autoimmunity, even in euthyroid patients, impairs folliculogenesis and reduces retrieved oocyte numbers by targeting thyroid peroxidase expressed on granulosa cells and elevating follicular CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 concentrations. Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody positivity independently predicts recurrent miscarriage and implantation failure, with endometriosis itself identified as an independent risk factor within this immunological framework. Sleep disturbances elevate platelet-lymphocyte ratio, C-reactive protein, and neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio, impairing oocyte quality, endometrial decidualization, and implantation—with circadian rhythm disruption specifically compromising melatonin-mediated anti-inflammatory protection. The systemic immune inflammation index (SII) demonstrates a dose-dependent relationship with infertility risk, escalating from 4.5% to 22.1% across increasing tertiles, establishing systemic inflammation as a quantifiable, independent infertility risk factor.

Insulin resistance pervades the metabolic-reproductive interface with particular consistency. Composite insulin resistance indices—METS-IR, TyG

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes linking reproductive dysfunction, metabolic disturbance, and systemic inflammation. A dominant motif is the role of visceral adiposity, insulin resistance, and chronic low-grade inflammation as shared mechanistic drivers of both infertility and endometriosis. Multiple studies frame these conditions not as isolated reproductive disorders but as manifestations of broader metabolic dysregulation, with obesity-related pathways—including gut microbiota dysbiosis, estrogen-gut axis disruption, hyperinsulinemia, and oxidative stress—repeatedly implicated across diverse study designs and populations.

Ovarian reserve assessment emerges as a second major theme, with anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) functioning as the most consistently cited biomarker across studies. AMH not only predicts oocyte quantity and IVF stimulation response but also associates with endometriosis severity, early pregnancy loss risk, and blastocyst outcomes, underscoring its multifunctional prognostic utility beyond simple reserve quantification.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Endometriosis is specifically and repeatedly linked to reduced AMH levels, diminished ovarian reserve, and impaired fertility outcomes. Women treated in endometriosis-specialized units demonstrated significantly lower median AMH (1.6 ng/mL) compared to general infertility populations, and endometriosis appeared as a comorbidity in studies of ovarian reserve, IVF prediction models, and machine learning-based infertility risk assessment. The lipid accumulation product and visceral adiposity index independently predicted endometriosis risk (OR approximately 1.54–1.56), with associations strengthening in women aged 35 and older, suggesting age-dependent metabolic contributions to disease pathogenesis.

Oxidative stress and antioxidant pathways constitute another consistently reported mechanistic axis. Vitamin B2 intake, dietary antioxidant scores (oxidative balance score), carotenoid levels, and vitamin D status all demonstrated protective associations across reproductive and metabolic outcomes, operating through reduction of reactive oxygen species, mitigation of inflammatory cascades, and preservation of cellular integrity in reproductive tissues. Sperm DNA fragmentation, itself driven by reactive oxygen species and abortive apoptosis, correlated inversely with vitamin D levels and negatively predicted ART outcomes, connecting male reproductive dysfunction to the same oxidative framework.

Insulin resistance and glycemic dysregulation appeared as cross-cutting mechanisms linking dietary patterns, metabolic phenotype, and reproductive impairment. High dietary glycemic index associated with diminished ovarian reserve through hyperinsulinemia, hyperandrogenism, and elevated insulin-like growth factor I. Metabolic health status, independent of BMI category, predicted IVF pregnancy rates and miscarriage risk, with elevated blood pressure, triglycerides, and testosterone levels representing the relevant metabolic parameters. Body composition measures beyond BMI—including waist-to-height ratio, body roundness index, and relative fat mass—demonstrated superior predictive accuracy for infertility compared to BMI alone, reflecting the primacy of fat distribution and metabolic activity over simple weight classification.

Epigenetic aging, assessed through DNA methylation at loci including ELOVL2, KLF14, and TRIM59, predicted IVF live birth outcomes independently of AMH and antral follicle count, with each additional year of epigenetic age reducing live birth likelihood by approximately 9%. This finding positions systemic biological aging—mediated through inflammation and oxidative stress—as a determinant of reproductive competence that transcends conventional ovarian reserve metrics.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several tensions are apparent across the literature. Regarding AMH, one study reported that paradoxically high AMH levels may reduce oocyte developmental competence and that supraphysiological estradiol in high-reserve cases impairs endometrial receptivity and oocyte quality—a finding in apparent conflict with the general assumption that higher reserve confers superior outcomes. Similarly, while AMH strongly predicted early pregnancy loss in women under 35 undergoing euploid blastocyst transfer, another study found that embryo developmental quality was independent of ovarian reserve status once fertilization was achieved, suggesting a decoupling between reserve quantity and intrinsic embryo competence.

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: Synthesis of Emerging Evidence

Research across this cluster converges on several dominant themes: the metabolic underpinnings of reproductive failure, inflammatory pathway dysregulation as a shared mechanistic driver, and the utility of composite biomarkers for assessing fertility-related pathology.

**Metabolic dysfunction emerges as a central mechanism linking systemic health to reproductive outcomes.** Multiple studies demonstrate that insulin resistance, visceral adiposity, dyslipidemia, and oxidative stress collectively impair fertility across sexes. Elevated triglyceride-glucose index, cardiometabolic index, and uric acid-to-HDL ratio each independently associate with infertility and reproductive hormone dysregulation. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis is particularly vulnerable: excess adipose tissue upregulates aromatase activity, converts testosterone to estradiol, suppresses gonadotropin-releasing hormone, and reduces luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone production, driving both male hypogonadism and female ovulatory dysfunction. Pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6—elaborated by dysfunctional adipose tissue—further suppress steroidogenesis and impair endometrial receptivity. Pre-conception weight reduction through interventions such as very-low calorie ketogenic diets demonstrably improves insulin sensitivity and pregnancy rates, reinforcing metabolic optimization as a therapeutic target.

**Ovarian reserve depletion represents a consistently reported consequence** of both endometriosis and surgical intervention. Endometriotic cyst size negatively correlates with antral follicle count, while anti-Müllerian hormone reflects global rather than localized ovarian damage, making AFC the more sensitive lateralized marker. Fibroblast activation protein—driven by TGF-β, IL-1β, and TNF-α—emerges as a complementary biomarker capturing stromal fibrotic burden independent of AMH. Laparoscopic endometrioma stripping persistently reduces follicle counts and cumulative live birth rates despite two decades of technical refinement, with electrosurgical hemostasis and vascular injury identified as primary mechanisms of collateral damage. Shear wave elastography similarly documents that adenomyosis-associated fibrosis and microvascular remodeling alter myometrial biomechanics across all layers, with junctional zone stiffness providing the most diagnostically sensitive parameter.

**Inflammatory diet and lifestyle factors modulate infertility risk** through overlapping pathways. Elevated dietary inflammatory index scores increase infertility odds by 61–77%, operating through elevated interleukins, CRP, endometriosis promotion, and gut dysbiosis-mediated endotoxemia. Conversely, omega-3 fatty acids, zinc, selenium, and Mediterranean-pattern diets mitigate these effects. In endometriosis specifically, PTGS2 and ACSL4 are identified as hub genes at the intersection of fatty acid metabolism and inflammation, directly implicating lipid dysregulation in disease pathogenesis.

**Genetic and epigenetic contributions** further contextualize reproductive dysfunction. Mendelian randomization studies identify FSHB, GNRH1, and FSHR as causal loci governing the ovulation spectrum from anovulatory infertility through dizygotic twinning, with strong negative genetic correlations between hyper-ovulation propensity and PCOS or endometriosis susceptibility. PAX1 and SF-1 methylation serve as diagnostically and prognostically valuable epigenetic markers in endometrial carcinoma. AZFc structural variants—particularly b2/b4 deletions and primary duplications—impair spermatogenesis and ICSI outcomes through nonallelic homologous recombination destabilizing palindromic Y-chromosome regions.

**Endometrial receptivity emerges as a distinct but related axis of infertility.** Intrauterine adhesions, abnormal endometrial peristalsis, and diminished vascular perfusion indices consistently predict implantation failure. Morphokinetic time-lapse analysis reveals that biochemical pregnancy losses share embryo-quality profiles with successful clinical pregnancies, implicating endometrial rather than embryonic factors in early loss—specifically impaired decidualization and supraphysiological hormonal environments during stimulated cycles.

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interconnected themes: the central role of systemic inflammation and oxidative stress in reproductive failure, the bidirectional relationship between metabolic dysfunction and fertility, and the utility of surrogate biomarkers—particularly anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH)—for assessing reproductive capacity across diverse clinical contexts. A recurring mechanistic framework positions chronic low-grade inflammation, insulin resistance, and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis dysregulation as converging pathways through which metabolic burden impairs fertility at multiple levels, from folliculogenesis and oocyte quality to endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Elevated inflammatory markers—including neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, monocyte-to-HDL ratio, and immature granulocyte percentage—are consistently associated with infertility and poor assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes, implicating cytokines such as interleukin-6 and TNF-α in disrupting endometrial immune tolerance and trophoblast invasion. Obesity and visceral adiposity emerge as independent and dose-dependent contributors to reproductive dysfunction across male and female populations, operating through oxidative stress, sperm DNA fragmentation, aromatase-driven estrogen excess, and impaired oocyte developmental competence. AMH is the most consistently cited biomarker across studies, reliably reflecting primordial follicle pool size and predicting ovarian stimulation response, aneuploidy risk, and premature ovarian insufficiency susceptibility across conditions including cancer treatment, autoimmune thyroid disease, latent tuberculosis, and psychological stress. Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies independently reduce AMH and antral follicle count in euthyroid women, implicating immune-mediated follicular damage independent of overt thyroid dysfunction. Dietary and lifestyle factors demonstrate reproducible associations with fertility outcomes: pro-inflammatory dietary patterns, elevated uric acid, poor antioxidant intake, and sugar-sweetened beverage consumption are each independently linked to increased infertility risk, while adherence to healthy lifestyle composites reduces risk by up to 70%, partially mediated through reductions in BMI and serum uric acid. Endometriosis is referenced across multiple studies as a condition exacerbated by or contributing to systemic inflammatory burden, ovarian reserve depletion, tubal dysfunction, false-positive diagnostic findings, and increased stroke risk, reinforcing its classification as a systemic inflammatory disorder with broad metabolic and vascular ramifications.

### Contradictions and Conflicts

Several areas of genuine conflict emerge. The relationship between infertility treatments and stroke risk remains unresolved: studies report hazard ratios spanning 0.82 to 1.66, with one large cohort paradoxically suggesting infertility medications may reduce ischemic stroke risk, while others report significantly elevated thrombotic risk through gonadotropin-induced hypercoagulability. In ovarian clear cell carcinoma—strongly associated with endometriosis—adjuvant chemotherapy improved survival in women over 70 but paradoxically worsened outcomes in those aged 50 or younger, suggesting that age-dependent tumor biology or immune competency fundamentally alters treatment response. Regarding AMH assay performance, studies highlight that epitope-specific antibody selection critically determines clinical accuracy, with linear-epitope assays outperforming conformational-epitope and automated platforms, introducing inter-study comparability concerns. Furthermore, although latent tuberculosis significantly reduces AMH and antral follicle count, it does not appear to compromise cumulative live birth rates following IVF, dissociating ovarian reserve markers from implantation potential in this context and challenging the assumption that AMH decline necessarily predicts ART failure. Finally, while elevated BMI consistently impairs reproductive outcomes, studies differ regarding which anthropometric index—traditional BMI, waist circumference, or novel indices such as the Body Shape Index—provides superior predictive value, with no consensus on optimal clinical measurement strategy.

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of studies, three interrelated themes emerge consistently: the contribution of systemic metabolic dysfunction to reproductive failure, the centrality of chronic low-grade inflammation as a mechanistic intermediary, and the utility of composite biomarkers for predicting fertility-related outcomes. A striking number of papers converge on the concept that lipid dysregulation, insulin resistance, and obesity collectively impair reproductive function through partially overlapping pathways. Abdominal adiposity, as indexed by body roundness index and BMI, disrupts hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis signaling, impairs steroidogenesis, and reduces sex hormone-binding globulin, thereby elevating bioavailable estrogen and promoting conditions such as endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and endometrial cancer. Mendelian randomization analyses reinforced causal relationships between obesity-related metabolic traits—including elevated BMI, waist circumference, fasting insulin, and type 2 diabetes—and gynecological pathology, while higher HDL cholesterol and SHBG were consistently protective across multiple conditions.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Inflammatory pathway dysregulation emerges as the most uniformly reported mechanistic theme. Pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-18 were implicated across endometriosis, uterine fibroids, adenomyosis, recurrent implantation failure, and infertility more broadly, disrupting folliculogenesis, oocyte competence, endometrial receptivity, and implantation. The non-HDL-C to HDL-C ratio (NHHR) independently emerged in multiple studies as a biomarker linking atherogenic lipid profiles to endometriosis risk and female infertility, with each unit increase in NHHR associated with a 14.3% greater odds of endometriosis and significant mediation of obesity-related infertility risk. The monocyte-to-HDL ratio similarly appeared as a composite inflammatory-metabolic index distinguishing infertile from fertile women. Dietary inflammatory index scores were positively and significantly associated with infertility risk across two independent population-based studies using distinct cohorts, with anti-inflammatory nutrients—particularly omega-3 fatty acids, antioxidants, and fiber—consistently protective for ovarian reserve markers including AMH and antral follicle count. Sperm DNA fragmentation index emerged as a robust independent predictor of blastocyst formation and embryo quality, with HDL cholesterol identified as the sole male metabolic parameter associated with sperm DNA integrity, suggesting a conserved lipid-oxidative stress axis affecting gamete quality in both sexes.

Ovarian reserve assessment remained a central preoccupation across multiple IVF-focused studies. AMH, antral follicle count, and basal FSH consistently predicted oocyte retrieval outcomes, with endometriosis specifically linked to follicular fibrosis, reduced AMH levels, and lower oocyte yield. Latent tuberculosis infection further impaired granulosa cell function and reduced AMH independently of structural damage, highlighting infectious etiologies as underappreciated contributors to ovarian reserve depletion. Age retained its dominant role as the strongest predictor of IVF outcomes, with live birth rates declining sharply after age 35.5 years through mechanisms involving increased oocyte aneuploidy and chromosomal instability.

Vitamin D deficiency was linked to endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and premature ovarian insufficiency, with vitamin D receptor expression found upregulated in endometrial polyps as a putative compensatory response to estrogen-driven proliferation. The metabolite 24,25(OH)₂D₃ demonstrated superior storage stability and stronger correlation with follicular fluid vitamin D status compared to conventional 25(OH)D₃, with implications for the reliability of prior reproductive vitamin D research. Immune dysregulation at the maternal-fetal interface—characterized by elevated Th1/Th2 ratio, autoantibodies including anti-TPO, anti-dsDNA, and anti-phospholipid antibodies, and altered regulatory T cell and natural killer cell populations—was identified as a significant independent contributor to recurrent implantation failure, with immunomodulatory interventions including tacrolimus,

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of studies, three interconnected themes emerge with striking consistency: the centrality of chronic systemic inflammation as a mediator of reproductive failure, the convergence of metabolic dysregulation with endometriosis pathophysiology, and the progressive impairment of ovarian reserve through both disease-intrinsic and iatrogenic mechanisms. Endometriosis operates at the intersection of these processes, functioning not merely as a pelvic anatomical disorder but as a systemic inflammatory and metabolic condition with broad reproductive consequences.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Ovarian reserve impairment, indexed primarily by anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC), is the most pervasively reported determinant of IVF outcomes across these papers. Low AMH correlates with reduced oocyte yield, diminished blastocyst formation, and elevated miscarriage risk, though Mendelian randomization evidence suggests these associations are mediated through oocyte quality rather than direct AMH causality. Endometriosis itself reduces AMH, and laparoscopic cystectomy compounds this effect, with bipolar coagulation causing substantially greater follicular damage than oxidised cellulose polymer hemostasis. The paradox of surgical intervention—providing symptomatic relief while accelerating ovarian aging—is consistently noted, with postoperative AMH declines of approximately 38% and twofold increases in premature ovarian insufficiency risk reported across multiple cohorts.

Systemic inflammatory markers constitute a second convergent finding. Elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), and the Systemic Inflammatory Response Index (SIRI) are reproducibly associated with diminished ovarian reserve, reduced AFC, poorer oocyte retrieval, and increased infertility risk across independent study populations. In endometriosis specifically, preoperative SII and NLR levels inversely predict postoperative spontaneous conception rates, establishing peripheral inflammatory indices as non-invasive prognostic biomarkers. The mechanistic basis involves activated neutrophils and macrophages impairing folliculogenesis, disrupting endometrial receptivity through epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and altering extracellular matrix dynamics via fibronectin and collagen IV modifications.

Metabolic dysfunction emerges as a third unifying axis. Insulin resistance—quantified through METS-IR and the cardiometabolic index (CMI)—demonstrates dose-dependent associations with infertility risk, with Mendelian randomization confirming causal contributions of elevated triglycerides and BMI. Notably, the CMI-endometriosis relationship is paradoxically inverse in cross-sectional data, with women carrying endometriosis showing higher metabolic risk profiles despite lower BMI, consistent with the separately reported finding that severe endometriosis (AAGL stages III–IV) is associated with lower body fat percentage and BMI than mild disease. This apparent contradiction likely reflects opposing mechanistic pathways: adipose-derived aromatase and adipokine-driven inflammation increase retrograde menstruation susceptibility at intermediate adiposity levels, while extreme leanness may exacerbate neurogenic pain sensitization without reducing disease burden.

Adenomyosis, frequently comorbid with endometriosis, independently impairs IVF outcomes through junctional zone disruption, progesterone resistance, and defective spiral artery remodeling, reducing live birth rates to approximately 24.5% versus 45% in unaffected controls. Machine learning analyses across multiple papers consistently identify AMH and embryo developmental stage as the strongest predictors of live birth, superseding morphological adenomyosis features, reinforcing that ovarian reserve and embryo quality are dominant determinants of ART success irrespective of uterine pathology.

At the molecular level, convergent evidence implicates oxidative stress as a proximate mediator linking inflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and reproductive failure. Follicular fluid metabolomic profiling in diminished ovarian reserve patients identifies dysregulation of steroid hormone biosynthesis, pentose phosphate, and purine metabolism pathways, with reduced 5α-pregnane-3,20-dione and estrone glucuronide reflecting compromised

## Endometriosis, Infertility, and Metabolic Dysfunction: A Synthesis

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes: the mechanistic links between metabolic dysfunction and reproductive failure, the role of systemic inflammation and oxidative stress in impairing fertility, and the identification of novel biomarkers for reproductive disorders.

A dominant and recurrent finding across multiple studies is that metabolic dysregulation directly compromises reproductive function through inflammatory and endocrine pathways. Visceral adiposity, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance emerge as particularly consistent mechanistic drivers. Elevated triglycerides and the lipid accumulation product index are causally associated with endometriosis risk via Mendelian randomization, with visceral fat promoting ectopic endometrial cell invasion through interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha signaling. Obesity independently disrupts steroidogenesis, granulosa cell responsiveness, and ovulatory function in IVF populations, while the RAR composite biomarker links systemic inflammation and nutritional insufficiency to impaired folliculogenesis and oocyte quality. Oxidative stress appears as a unifying downstream mechanism, with sperm DNA fragmentation, mitochondrial injury, and apoptosis reported across male and female infertility contexts alike.

Vitamin D deficiency represents another consistently reported modifiable risk factor, with protective effects documented across endometriosis, uterine myomas, dysmenorrhea, and cervical pathology through anti-inflammatory and prostaglandin-suppressive mechanisms. Similarly, CYP2C18 variants rs12248560 and rs4917623 were specifically associated with endometriosis and female infertility, implicating progesterone metabolism as a pharmacogenomic contributor to disease susceptibility.

Hormonal dysregulation—elevated FSH, reduced AMH, and disrupted hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis signaling—is consistently observed in diminished ovarian reserve, with sleep architecture and circadian rhythm disruption identified as underappreciated modifiable contributors. TORCH infections, particularly Toxoplasma gondii, emerged as a notably strong independent risk factor for diminished ovarian reserve, especially in younger women, representing a finding warranting further mechanistic investigation.

Some tension exists regarding the relative independence of metabolic factors from embryological outcomes: while maternal obesity impairs ovulatory function and steroidogenesis, clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in IVF were not significantly different across BMI groups in one large cohort, suggesting that laboratory-assisted fertilization may partially compensate for oocyte quality deficits while metabolic complications in pregnancy persist. Similarly, elevated sperm DNA fragmentation index reduced fertilization rates but did not compromise live birth outcomes, potentially reflecting embryo-level repair mechanisms or selection pressures during culture. These discordances highlight that metabolic dysfunction may exert stage-specific rather than uniformly adverse effects across the reproductive continuum, a distinction with practical implications for patient counseling and intervention timing.

# Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: An Integrated Review

## Overview

The central research questions unifying this domain concern how structural uterine pathologies—principally adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, endometriosis, intrauterine adhesions, and congenital Müllerian anomalies—disrupt the hormonal, immunological, and architectural prerequisites for normal reproductive function, and how these disruptions can be reversed or mitigated through medical, surgical, or combined interventions. A unifying mechanistic principle is that estrogen dominance relative to progesterone creates a permissive environment for aberrant tissue proliferation, while chronic inflammation amplifies and sustains pathological remodeling across multiple compartments simultaneously. These processes are not condition-specific but represent shared molecular cascades operating with varying spatial distributions and clinical expressions across the uterus and pelvis.

## Consensus Findings

The literature consistently implicates estrogen dependence, progesterone resistance, and NF-κB–mediated chronic inflammation as the primary pathological drivers across adenomyosis, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and endometrial hyperplasia. Intrinsic aromatase overexpression within ectopic lesions enables autonomous estrogen biosynthesis, sustaining disease independently of systemic hormonal fluctuations. Downstream inflammatory cascades—characterized by elevated TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8—drive epithelial-mesenchymal transition, neoangiogenesis via VEGF and matrix metalloproteinases, impaired fibrinolysis through elevated PAI-1, and fibroblast-mediated collagen overproduction, collectively producing adhesion formation, junctional zone disruption, and compromised myometrial integrity.

Endometrial receptivity impairment is a consistently reported mechanistic consequence of this milieu. Adenomyosis reduces implantation success through suppression of HOXA10 and HOXA11 expression—the latter additionally mediated by TGF-β3 overproduction from submucosal fibroids—alongside abnormal uterine peristalsis and junctional zone architectural disruption. Adenomyosis is independently associated with a 41% reduction in live birth rates, elevated miscarriage risk, and adverse obstetric outcomes including preterm delivery and preeclampsia. Uterine fibroids driven by somatic mutations in MED12, HMGA2, and collagen genes COL4A5/COL4A6, acting through Wnt/β-catenin and AKT/mTOR signaling, impair fertility most severely in submucosal subtypes while producing obstructive complications including bilateral hydronephrosis in larger lesions.

Endometrial dysbiosis and chronic endometritis represent a consistently validated mechanistic link to implantation failure. CD138-positive plasma cell infiltration remains the diagnostic gold standard for chronic endometritis, with antibiotic therapy—particularly azithromycin and doxycycline—reproducibly improving conception rates from approximately 20–44% to 53–85% in treated populations, a finding replicated across independent cohorts. Causative organisms including Escherichia coli, Streptococcus, Enterococcus faecalis, Mycoplasma, and Ureaplasma disrupt endometrial immune homeostasis through CXCL13/CXCL1-mediated B-cell recruitment and bacterial lipopolysaccharide-induced E-selectin upregulation. Congenital Müllerian anomalies, including OHVIRA syndrome and unicornuate uterus with rudimentary horn, are uniformly associated with hematometra, retrograde menstruation, and secondary endometriosis, with endometriosis developing in up to 56% of patients when surgical correction is delayed. Iatrogenic uterine injury from cesarean delivery produces isthmocele in 24–70% of cases and is mechanistically linked to placenta accreta spectrum, fibrotic endometrial remodeling, and impaired estrogen sensitivity through ERK-2 signaling pathways.

Across surgical management, minimally invasive approaches—laparoscopic excision, robotic-assisted adenomyomectomy, hysteroscopic procedures, and uterine artery embolization—consistently demonstrate efficacy comparable or superior to open surgery, with reduced blood loss, shorter hospitalization, and preserved ovarian reserve as measured by anti-Müllerian hormone levels. GnRH antagonists including elagolix and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems emerge as the most consistently endorsed pharmacological agents across estrogen-driven conditions.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several areas of genuine evidentiary conflict persist. The relationship between chronic endometritis and assisted reproductive technology outcomes remains contested: while the majority of literature supports antibiotic treatment to optimize implantation, at least one propensity-weighted study found untreated chronic endometritis was not associated with inferior live birth rates and was unexpectedly associated with reduced miscarriage risk, directly contradicting the prevailing treatment paradigm. Similarly, while combined doxycycline and probiotic therapy achieved clinical pregnancy in 88.9% of patients in one cohort, conventional antibiotic therapy alone was reported not to improve pregnancy outcomes in another, suggesting that microbial ecosystem restoration rather than pathogen elimination alone may be the relevant therapeutic target. Furthermore, endometrial microbiome sequencing has been reported as superior to hysteroscopy and CD138 immunostaining for predicting implantation success, challenging established diagnostic hierarchies without yet altering clinical practice standards.

Adenomyosis presents paradoxical associations in the oncological context: while sharing etiological pathways with endometrial cancer through estrogen and inflammation, concurrent adenomyosis paradoxically associates with less aggressive endometrial cancer phenotypes and reduced myometrial invasion, possibly through mechanical barrier effects and altered cytokine profiles. Adenomyomectomy fertility outcomes are inconsistently framed; clinical pregnancy rates ranging from 25% to 61.5% are reported across studies, and residual myometrial thickness below 7 mm significantly elevates uterine rupture risk, creating unresolved tension between fertility restoration and obstetric safety. For uterine artery embolization, although broadly endorsed, fibroid size and incomplete devascularization are identified as significant complication predictors in some series while unacknowledged in others, suggesting publication bias or patient selection heterogeneity. Adiposity paradoxically associates with earlier-stage endometrial cancer at presentation despite being

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The dominant themes across these 50 articles encompass structural uterine pathologies, hormonal dysregulation, vascular complications, congenital anomalies, and the reproductive consequences of uterine dysfunction. A recurring mechanistic thread involves estrogen dependence as a driver of pathological tissue growth, implicated consistently in adenomyosis, endometriosis, leiomyomas, and endometrial cancer, with downstream effects on endometrial receptivity, myometrial integrity, and systemic vascular function.

Adenomyosis emerges as the most extensively discussed individual condition, reported across multiple studies as causing infertility through reduced endometrial receptivity, junctional zone disruption, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Treatment convergence around minimally invasive uterus-sparing approaches—particularly transcervical radiofrequency ablation, uterine artery embolization, and high-intensity focused ultrasound—was consistently reported, with hormonal agents including dienogest, levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, and GnRH modulators serving as first-line medical management. Notably, delayed treatment exceeding 24 months correlated with increased hysterectomy rates, underscoring the clinical importance of early intervention. Intrauterine endometriosis-like mechanisms were also implicated in cesarean scar defects, hematometra formation, and abdominal wall endometriosis, reinforcing the broad pathophysiological reach of ectopic endometrial tissue.

Vascular complications constituted another dominant cluster, with uterine arteriovenous malformations, pseudoaneurysms, spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy secondary to endometriosis-associated neovascularization, and pelvic congestion syndrome all reported as causes of significant hemorrhagic morbidity. Uterine artery embolization was consistently identified as a first-line uterus-sparing intervention across these vascular pathologies. Separately, estrogen deficiency in postmenopausal models was shown to impair nitric oxide bioavailability and endothelium-dependent vasodilation, while abnormal menstrual patterns linked to adenomyosis and leiomyomas were associated with systemic hypercoagulability and elevated stroke risk, establishing bidirectional relationships between uterine pathology and systemic cardiovascular health.

Congenital Müllerian anomalies, particularly OHVIRA/Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome, were reported across multiple case reports with consistent emphasis that delayed diagnosis increases endometriosis risk in approximately 14% of affected patients. Minimally invasive vaginoscopic septectomy with intraoperative ultrasound guidance was uniformly endorsed as the preferred surgical approach, particularly for preserving hymenal integrity in younger patients.

Regarding reproductive outcomes, adenomyosis patients demonstrated 41% reduced live birth rates compared to unaffected women, and endometrial dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species was identified as an independent predictor of implantation failure, with combined doxycycline and probiotic therapy achieving clinical pregnancy in 88.9% of treated patients. Importantly, endometrial microbiome testing proved superior to hysteroscopy and CD138 immunostaining for predicting clinical success, a finding that challenges current diagnostic hierarchies. Endometriosis similarly impaired fertility through macrophage-mediated inflammation, elevated cytokines and prostaglandins, and ovulation and implantation disorders, with genetic contributions from estrogen and progesterone receptor polymorphisms noted.

Several contradictions merit attention. For chronic endometritis, antibiotic treatment alone was reported not to improve pregnancy outcomes despite its standard clinical use, contrasting with the microbiome-targeted approach demonstrating significant benefit. Additionally, while uterine artery embolization is broadly endorsed for adenomyosis and fibroids, one study identified fibroid size and incomplete devascularization as major complication predictors, suggesting patient selection limitations that are not uniformly acknowledged across the literature. The rare but documented case of adenomyosis causing uterine rupture in an unscarred gravid uterus challenges the conventional risk stratification framework that attributes rupture primarily to prior surgical scarring. Finally, endometrial cancer literature documents persistent racial mortality disparities in Black women despite similar incidence rates, attributed to the disproportionate preval

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The 50 articles reviewed converge on several dominant themes: the structural and functional consequences of uterine pathology on fertility and pregnancy outcomes, the expanding role of minimally invasive and imaging-guided interventions, and the complex interplay between inflammatory, hormonal, and vascular mechanisms in gynecological disease.

**Adenomyosis and endometriosis** emerged as recurrently implicated conditions across multiple papers. Adenomyosis was consistently identified as a risk factor for uterine surgery complications, isthmocele formation, fibroid recurrence, and obstetric morbidity, while paradoxically appearing protective against aggressive behavior in endometrioid endometrial cancer—a notable finding attributed to mechanical barrier effects and altered inflammatory cytokine profiles. Endometriosis was independently associated with adenomyosis development, post-cesarean wound healing impairment, and endometrioma-related infection risk during oocyte retrieval. Both conditions share pathophysiological underpinnings involving retrograde menstruation, chronic inflammation, estrogen dependence, and disrupted immune regulation.

**Intrauterine adhesions and endometrial damage** represented a second major theme, with dilation and curettage, cesarean section, and hysteroscopic procedures consistently identified as primary iatrogenic causes. Asherman's syndrome, isthmocele, and uterine niches were each mechanistically linked to impaired endometrial receptivity through overlapping pathways: inflammatory cytokine elevation (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), compromised blood supply, fibrosis, and microbiota disruption. Platelet activation and coagulation cascades were specifically implicated in adhesion recurrence.

**Chronic endometritis** was repeatedly associated with recurrent implantation failure, with causative organisms including *E. coli*, *Streptococci*, *Staphylococci*, and *E. faecalis* disrupting the uterine microbiome and narrowing the implantation window through proinflammatory cytokine elevation.

**Pelvic vascular pathology**, particularly ovarian vein incompetence and pelvic congestion syndrome, was identified as an underrecognized etiology of chronic pelvic pain, with left ovarian vein reflux carrying an odds ratio of 9.1 for pain association. Transcatheter embolization consistently achieved symptom relief exceeding 85%, positioning vascular correction as a primary therapeutic target alongside hormonal management.

**Uterine fibroids** featured prominently, with recurrence risk following myomectomy linked to obesity-driven leptin signaling, elevated estrogen, and endometrial hyperplasia rather than fibroid size alone—challenging size-centric surgical decision-making. Laparoscopic hemostatic techniques demonstrated equivalent preservation of ovarian reserve as measured by anti-Müllerian hormone levels.

Across multiple papers, **minimally invasive and imaging-guided approaches**—including laparoscopic repair of isthmocele, MRI-guided oocyte retrieval, robotic-assisted niche correction with near-infrared fluorescence, and deep learning-enhanced hysteroscopic assessment—consistently demonstrated superior or equivalent outcomes compared to conventional methods, with reduced morbidity and improved reproductive results.

**Contradictions and conflicts** are present within this literature. Fibroid diameter was not significantly associated with recurrence in one laparoscopic myomectomy study, contrasting with conventional clinical assumptions. Adenomyosis appeared both as a pathological risk factor for vascular rupture and obstetric complications and as a protective factor in endometrial cancer staging—findings that reflect condition-specific rather than universally consistent effects. Diagnostic accuracy for adenomyosis on transvaginal ultrasound was highlighted as substantially underperforming in routine practice despite established sonographic criteria, creating tension with the widespread clinical reliance on this modality. Additionally, while sentinel lymph node biopsy was supported as an effective staging tool in endometrial cancer, multiple patient and tumor factors significantly limited bilateral detection success, underscoring ongoing technical limitations.

Collectively, these papers reinforce that uterine structural integrity, endometrial inflammatory status, and pelvic vascular competence are interconnected determinants of reproductive health, requiring multimodal diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for optimal patient management.

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Key Themes and Findings

The 50 articles in this cluster collectively address a broad spectrum of uterine and reproductive pathologies, with several dominant mechanistic themes emerging across conditions. Estrogen dominance and chronic inflammation represent the most consistently invoked pathological drivers, appearing in adenomyosis, endometriosis, chronic endometritis, uterine fibroids, and their interactions with endometrial cancer. Disrupted endometrial receptivity—mediated through altered expression of implantation markers such as HOXA10 and HOXA11, impaired vascularization, and aberrant immune activation—constitutes a unifying mechanism linking adenomyosis, chronic endometritis, isthmocele, and intrauterine adhesions to infertility and recurrent implantation failure.

Adenomyosis emerges as a particularly prominent entity, addressed across numerous papers examining its epidemiology, comorbidities, treatment responses, and surgical outcomes. Consistently reported findings include its frequent coexistence with uterine leiomyomas (approximately 49%), endometriosis, and endometrial polyps, alongside its independent impairment of intrauterine insemination success, elevated miscarriage rates, and progesterone resistance in extrinsic subtypes linked to IL-6/STAT-3 pathway activation. Multiple iatrogenic uterine injuries—cesarean section scarring, hysteroscopic procedures, and curettage—recurrently appear as predisposing factors for conditions including placenta accreta spectrum, isthmocele, and vesicouterine fistula, with residual myometrial thickness emerging as a critical prognostic determinant across these sequelae.

Chronic endometritis, characterized by CD138-positive plasma cell infiltration, is consistently associated with recurrent implantation failure and adverse pregnancy outcomes, with antibiotic therapy—particularly doxycycline—reliably restoring endometrial receptivity. The CXCL13/CXCL1-mediated B-cell recruitment pathway and bacterial lipopolysaccharide-induced E-selectin upregulation represent the most mechanistically detailed inflammatory cascade described across these studies.

Notable contradictions arise regarding adenomyosis and endometrial cancer: while shared etiological pathways involving estrogen and inflammation are acknowledged, concurrent adenomyosis paradoxically associates with less aggressive tumor phenotypes and reduced myometrial invasion, challenging a straightforward oncogenic interpretation. Similarly, parity data present conflicting implications—higher parity reduces early menopause risk yet increases hysterectomy risk after age 40, likely reflecting confounding by underlying gynecological pathology. Anti-Müllerian hormone appears as a consistently valuable biomarker of ovarian reserve across diverse clinical contexts, including obesity, post-COVID-19 reproductive dysfunction, and infertility workup, though its predictive utility varies by clinical setting.

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Dominant Themes and Findings

The 50 articles in this cluster converge on several dominant themes: the pathophysiology and clinical management of benign uterine conditions (principally adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, and endometriosis), complications of uterine instrumentation and cesarean delivery, Müllerian developmental anomalies, and the reproductive consequences of chronic endometrial dysfunction.

**Adenomyosis** emerges as a consistently prominent condition, with multiple studies reporting its core mechanisms—ectopic endometrial gland and stromal invasion of the myometrium causing myometrial fibrosis, smooth muscle hypertrophy, and junctional zone disruption—that collectively impair implantation, elevate miscarriage risk, and worsen obstetric outcomes including preeclampsia and preterm delivery. Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, GnRH agonist protocols, and uterine artery embolization were each reported as effective management strategies, with hysterectomy retaining superior symptom relief in direct comparative studies. Minimally invasive ablative techniques including cryoablation and high-intensity focused ultrasound were identified as emerging alternatives, while adenomyomectomy timing within the secretory phase was independently associated with reduced recurrence.

**Uterine fibroids** similarly received extensive coverage, with driver mutations in *MED12*, *HMGA2*, and *COL4A6* linked to abnormal uterine bleeding via dysregulated TGF-β signaling and aberrant RNA splicing. Submucosal location remained the strongest fertility-impairing subtype, though larger subserosal fibroids (≥5 cm combined diameter) also significantly reduced conception rates. Racial disparities were consistently noted, with Black women experiencing disproportionate disease burden and higher rates of surgical intervention. Organ-axial T2-weighted MRI was demonstrated to substantially outperform conventional body-axial imaging in FIGO classification accuracy (92.9% versus 58.9%), with direct implications for surgical planning.

Complications of **cesarean delivery and intrauterine instrumentation** represented another major theme. Cesarean scar defects (isthmoceles), postoperative adhesions, uterine artery pseudoaneurysm, gravid uterine incarceration, and IUD migration with vesicouterine fistula formation were documented across multiple case reports, collectively underscoring cesarean delivery as a significant source of downstream reproductive morbidity. Postoperative adhesions affecting up to 90% of gynecologic surgery patients were mechanistically linked to fibroblast activation, myofibroblast differentiation, and ERK-2 signaling, with emerging biomaterial barriers and laparoscopic technique modifications offering partial mitigation.

**Müllerian anomalies**—including unicornuate uterus with rudimentary horn, OHVIRA syndrome, cervical atresia, and vaginal aplasia—were consistently associated with hematometra, secondary endometriosis, and infertility, with early surgical intervention uniformly emphasized as critical to preventing progressive pelvic damage. The frequent co-occurrence of ipsilateral renal agenesis reinforced the mesonephric-Müllerian developmental axis as a unifying pathogenic mechanism across these conditions.

**Chronic endometritis** was identified as an underrecognized contributor to recurrent implantation failure and infertility, with CD138-positive plasma cell infiltration serving as the diagnostic hallmark and antibiotic treatment combined with hysteroscopic intervention demonstrating restoration of fertility in affected patients. Placental site nodule and plaque represented a related endometrial lesion similarly associated with secondary infertility through inflammatory mechanisms.

Several **contradictions and tensions** merit acknowledgment. Studies comparing uterine artery embolization and hysterectomy for adenomyosis reported comparable overall quality-of-life improvements, yet hysterectomy demonstrated superior outcomes in specific symptom domains, leaving the optimal choice for individual patients unresolved. For uterine fibroids, myomectomy demonstrated superior mental health outcomes versus UAE in one study, while overall quality-of-life improvements were comparable—a discrepancy likely reflecting differential patient populations and outcome measurement heterogeneity. ## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The 50 articles in this cluster collectively address a broad spectrum of uterine and reproductive pathologies, with recurring emphasis on inflammatory mechanisms, endometrial integrity, hormonal dysregulation, and iatrogenic injury as central drivers of reproductive dysfunction.

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Chronic inflammation emerges as the most pervasive mechanistic theme across these papers. Chronic endometritis, adenomyosis, and endometriosis are repeatedly linked through shared pathological cascades involving toll-like receptor signaling, NF-κB-dependent sterile inflammation, and dysregulated pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8. The tissue injury and repair (TIAR) cascade is specifically implicated in the pathogenesis of adenomyosis, wherein recurrent basal endometrial damage triggers epithelial-mesenchymal transition, neoangiogenesis, and fibrosis, ultimately promoting myometrial infiltration. Endometrial dysbiosis—characterized by colonization with Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, Mycoplasma, Ureaplasma, Gardnerella, and Ralstonia, alongside depletion of protective Bifidobacterium and lactic acid-producing organisms—is consistently reported as a precipitating factor in chronic endometritis and associated reproductive failure.

Iatrogenic uterine injury represents a second dominant theme. Curettage and uterine surgery are the most consistently identified risk factors across multiple conditions, including intrauterine adhesions (uterine synechiae), cesarean scar disorder with niche formation, hematometra, intramural pregnancy, and retained products of conception-associated sepsis. Cesarean section specifically is linked to isthmocele formation in 24–70% of cases, placenta accreta spectrum disorder, cesarean scar pregnancy, and long-term impairment of endometrial receptivity through fibrotic remodeling and reduced estrogen sensitivity. Collectively, these findings underscore a reproducible pattern whereby endometrial trauma initiates inflammatory and fibrotic cascades that compromise both uterine structural integrity and reproductive potential.

Hormonal dysregulation, particularly estrogen excess relative to progesterone, is consistently implicated in the pathogenesis of uterine fibroids, endometrial polyps, endometrial hyperplasia, and adenomyosis. Thyroid dysfunction, polycystic ovary syndrome, obesity, and insulin resistance are repeatedly identified as comorbid conditions amplifying estrogen-driven endometrial proliferation. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system is among the most consistently effective pharmacological interventions reported for estrogen-driven conditions, reducing polyp recurrence by inducing endometrial atrophy.

Ovarian reserve preservation constitutes a third major theme, particularly in the context of endometrioma surgery and myomectomy. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) is uniformly endorsed as the primary marker of ovarian reserve, with laparoscopic cystectomy and open myomectomy both demonstrated to reduce AMH levels, the latter more significantly. Alternative ablative techniques including CO₂ laser ablation, plasma energy, and transcervical radiofrequency ablation are proposed as tissue-sparing approaches with comparable recurrence rates but reduced ovarian damage.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several findings recur with notable consistency across independent studies. First, CD138 immunohistochemical staining for endometrial plasma cells is repeatedly identified as the diagnostic gold standard for chronic endometritis, with conventional histology and hysteroscopy alone demonstrating insufficient sensitivity. Second, antibiotic treatment of chronic endometritis—particularly with azithromycin—consistently improves conception rates, with two independent studies reporting improvement from approximately 20–21% to 53% in treated versus untreated patients (p=0.022), a finding that strengthens the causal relationship between endometrial microbial dysbiosis and implantation failure. Third, endometriosis appears across multiple clinical contexts not only as a primary pathology but as a contributory or complicating factor in tubal factor infertility, uterine rupture risk, piriformis syndrome, ovarian malignant transformation, ectopic pregnancy, and uretero-fallopian fistula formation

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The assembled literature reflects a broad but interconnected body of work centered on structural uterine pathology, reproductive tract anomalies, and their systemic consequences for fertility, pregnancy, and quality of life. Several dominant themes emerge consistently across these papers.

### Hormonal Dysregulation and Molecular Pathogenesis

Estrogen and progesterone signaling appear as central pathogenic drivers across multiple conditions. Uterine fibroids, adenomyosis, endometriosis, and endometrial hyperplasia all demonstrate hormone dependency, with somatic mutations—most prominently in *MED12*, *HMGA2*, fumarate hydratase (*FH*), and collagen genes (*COL4A5/COL4A6*)—further amplifying dysregulated proliferative signaling through the Wnt/β-catenin and AKT/mTOR pathways. Elevated transforming growth factor-β3 (TGF-β3) produced by submucosal fibroids is consistently reported to suppress *HOXA10* and *HOXA11* expression, directly impairing endometrial receptivity and contributing to implantation failure. Obesity-driven aromatization amplifies estrogenic exposure and is independently associated with fibroid growth, endometrial hyperplasia, and malignancy risk, with structured weight reduction demonstrably improving treatment response in fertility-sparing management of early endometrial cancer. GnRH antagonists and agonists, including elagolix and GnRH-a, recur as effective pharmacological strategies across fibroid, endometriosis, and adenomyosis management, with add-back hormonal therapy necessary to mitigate hypoestrogenic sequelae including bone mineral density loss.

### Structural Uterine Pathology and Fertility Impairment

Impaired endometrial receptivity and compromised uterine architecture represent the most consistently reported mechanisms linking structural pathology to infertility and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Adenomyosis disrupts endometrial thinning, abnormal uterine peristalsis, and myometrial integrity, increasing miscarriage risk and complicating assisted reproduction, with adenomyomectomy using reconstructive flap techniques improving postoperative endometrial thickness and pregnancy rates. Uterine fibroids, particularly submucosal variants, impair sperm transport and embryo implantation through both mechanical and molecular mechanisms, while also elevating risk for preterm birth, placental abruption, fetal malpresentation, and labor dystocia. Congenital uterine anomalies, including dysmorphic uterus and Müllerian duct malformations, similarly compromise reproductive outcomes through reduced cavity volume and obstructed outflow, the latter predisposing to retrograde menstruation and secondary endometriosis or adenomyosis. Thin endometrium, whether arising from iatrogenic trauma, infection, or associated conditions such as fibroids and endometriosis, further reduces implantation success, with current pharmacological interventions including low-dose aspirin and estrogen therapy demonstrating only modest efficacy.

The literature converges on endometriosis as a condition of particular complexity and chronicity, operating through three recognized pain mechanisms—nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic—and involving immune dysregulation that paradoxically inhibits apoptosis of ectopic implants, promoting persistent inflammation, fibrosis, and adhesion formation. Central sensitization and overlap with fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, and migraine are consistently noted comorbidities, complicating both diagnosis and management. Adenomyosis co-occurs with endometriosis in up to 90% of cases across multiple papers, and the two conditions share overlapping hormonal, inflammatory, and structural pathogenic mechanisms.

### Surgical Management and Minimally Invasive Approaches

Surgical intervention recurs as essential across fibroid, endometriosis, adenomyosis, and Müllerian anomaly management, with a consistent emphasis on minimally invasive and fertility-preserving techniques. Robotic-assisted surgery demonstrated safety and efficacy across 106 benign gynecological cases without conversion to open surgery, and hysteroscopic metroplasty achieved 100% cavity normalization in dysmorphic uterus with subsequent pregnancy

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Key Themes and Findings

The fifty articles in this cluster collectively illuminate the interconnected burden of benign uterine pathologies—principally adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, endometriosis, and structural defects such as isthmoceles and Müllerian anomalies—on reproductive health, fertility, and systemic wellbeing. A dominant theme across papers is the mechanistic overlap between these conditions, particularly their shared reliance on estrogen-driven proliferation, chronic inflammation, and aberrant tissue remodeling. Endometriosis and adenomyosis are repeatedly implicated in dysregulated angiogenesis (via VEGF and matrix metalloproteinases), impaired fibrinolysis (elevated PAI-1, reduced tPA activity), and fibroblast-mediated collagen overproduction, collectively driving adhesion formation, pelvic pain, and infertility. The junctional zone emerges as a consistently reported site of pathological disruption in adenomyosis, with myometrial smooth muscle hyperplasia, abnormal contractility, and collagen deposition compromising implantation and peristalsis. Estrogen dependence is reinforced across multiple papers through findings that aromatase overexpression, progesterone resistance, and GnRH-responsive pathways underpin both adenomyosis and low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma biology.

The most consistently reported clinical associations include the co-occurrence of adenomyosis and endometriosis (in 65–70% of cases), their shared contributions to heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, and their adverse impact on obstetric outcomes including preterm birth, placental complications, and uterine rupture. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system is uniformly endorsed as first-line medical management for adenomyosis and heavy menstrual bleeding, with hysteroscopic fixation improving outcomes in high-expulsion-risk patients. Surgical approaches—laparoscopic excision for endometriosis, myomectomy or hysterectomy for fibroids, and adenomyomectomy for focal disease—demonstrate efficacy but carry condition-specific recurrence and obstetric risks.

Several contradictions warrant acknowledgment. Ethanol sclerotherapy achieved superior pain resolution compared to laparoscopic cystectomy for endometriomas, yet recurrence rates were comparable, challenging assumptions of surgical superiority. Similarly, obesity paradoxically associated with earlier-stage endometrial cancer despite being a primary etiological risk factor, suggesting that hormonal and metabolic pathways produce non-linear disease trajectories. The relationship between adenomyomectomy and fertility improvement is inconsistently framed: while clinical pregnancy rates of 25–61.5% are reported, residual myometrial thickness below 7 mm substantially elevates uterine rupture risk, creating tension between fertility restoration and obstetric safety. Additionally, bariatric surgery improved fecal incontinence scores yet paradoxically increased urgency, illustrating that symptomatic improvement does not uniformly indicate physiological normalization. Together, these findings underscore that uterine pathologies operate within a complex hormonal, immune, and structural milieu requiring individualized, multidisciplinary management strategies.

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The collective body of literature in this cluster addresses three intersecting domains: structural uterine pathology and its systemic sequelae, reproductive failure and fertility restoration, and minimally invasive diagnostic and therapeutic innovation. Adenomyosis and uterine fibroids emerge as the most extensively examined conditions, with multiple papers converging on shared pathophysiological mechanisms involving chronic inflammation, hormonal dysregulation, and disruption of the endometrial-myometrial interface. Endometriosis appears recurrently as both a primary pathology and a complicating comorbidity across diverse clinical contexts, including uterine rupture, ovarian clear cell carcinoma, Müllerian anomalies, and assisted reproduction.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Estrogen dependence and progesterone resistance are consistently implicated across adenomyosis and endometriosis, with intrinsic aromatase activity enabling autonomous estrogen production within ectopic lesions. Multiple papers identify downstream inflammatory cascades—particularly NF-κB activation, elevated IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β—as drivers of pain, abnormal bleeding, and impaired implantation. Junctional zone disruption and aberrant expression of implantation markers (HOXA10, HOXA11, LIF) are reported as mechanisms through which adenomyosis reduces frozen embryo transfer success. Uterine fibroids are consistently associated with obstructive complications extending beyond the reproductive tract, including bilateral hydronephrosis and acute renal impairment, with complete symptom and organ function resolution following surgical intervention. Minimally invasive ablative strategies—microwave ablation and uterine artery embolization—demonstrate comparable efficacy for adenomyosis symptom control, with preservation of anti-Müllerian hormone levels confirming ovarian reserve maintenance across multiple studies.

Diagnostically, shear-wave elastography, transvaginal ultrasound, and preoperative sonographic sliding sign assessment are repeatedly validated as accurate, non-invasive adjuncts. The sliding sign demonstrates particularly strong performance, with transabdominal sensitivity reaching 96% for adhesion detection, while cervical elastography stiffness thresholds above 17.5 kPa show independent association with adenomyosis.

Endometriosis is specifically identified as an independent risk factor for uterine rupture during labor, with deeply infiltrating lesions on the posterior uterine wall, prior laparoscopic resection, adenomyotic co-involvement, and uterotonic augmentation forming a convergent risk profile. Separately, the literature consistently documents endometriosis development as a secondary complication of obstructed reproductive tract anomalies, including Müllerian duct malformations and UGTOIRA syndrome, with rates approaching 56% when surgical correction is delayed.

Chromosomal abnormalities—particularly Klinefelter syndrome, Y-chromosome microdeletions, and balanced translocations—are uniformly reported as significant contributors to infertility and ART failure, reinforcing the role of routine karyotyping in evaluation protocols. The OPRM1 A118G polymorphism is identified as a pharmacogenomic determinant of postoperative opioid requirements in gynecological surgery patients, with the G allele associated with reduced μ-opioid receptor availability and elevated analgesic demand.

Iron deficiency in adenomyosis merits specific attention: transferrin saturation is reported as more sensitive than ferritin for detecting functional iron deficiency in this population, as ferritin elevation driven by IL-6-induced hepcidin production via JAK/STAT3 signaling obscures underlying iron depletion despite normal or elevated ferritin values.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable tensions exist within this literature. Regarding chronic endometritis and ART outcomes, one propensity-weighted study found that untreated CE was not associated with inferior live birth rates and was unexpectedly linked to reduced miscarriage risk, directly contradicting the prevailing assumption that CE uniformly impairs reproductive outcomes and that antibiotic treatment is warranted. This conflicts with the broader emphasis across multiple papers on treating intrauterine inflammation to optimize implantation. Similarly, prophylactic antibiotic administration in endometriosis surgery did not reduce postoperative infection rates; rather, infections occurred exclusively in the antibi

## Uterine Pathology and Reproductive Health: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The assembled literature addresses a broad spectrum of uterine and gynecological pathologies, with several dominant themes emerging across papers: the pathophysiological consequences of ectopic or aberrant endometrial tissue, hormonal regulation of uterine and reproductive function, surgical management strategies, and postoperative complications.

Adenomyosis and endometriosis represent the most consistently examined conditions. Multiple studies independently document their frequent co-occurrence—reported in approximately 10.7% of adenomyosis cases—alongside shared risk factors including early menarche, elevated BMI, increased parity, and prior uterine surgery. Mechanistically, dysregulated estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor signaling, elevated matrix metalloproteinases, and mutations in PTEN, KRAS, and BCL2 are recurrently implicated in disease progression and malignant transformation toward endometrial carcinoma. Adenomyosis is increasingly positioned as a precancerous condition, warranting heightened surveillance, particularly when concurrent endometrial polyps or atypical hyperplasia are present. Endometriosis additionally contributes to serious systemic complications including bowel perforation, uterine rupture risk, and postoperative thromboembolic events, underscoring its multi-organ impact.

Surgical management themes are prominent throughout, with consistent evidence favoring minimally invasive approaches. Robot-assisted and laparoscopic techniques demonstrate superior outcomes across adenomyomectomy, hysterectomy, and hysteroscopic procedures, including reduced blood loss, shorter hospitalization, and improved reproductive results compared to open surgery. The COVID-19 pandemic data corroborate this trajectory, revealing preferential adoption of less invasive alternatives during healthcare disruptions without apparent compromise of care quality.

Regarding postoperative complications, venous thromboembolism, urinary retention, and systemic infection emerge as the most consistently reported adverse events, with diabetes mellitus identified as an independent predictor of poor outcomes across multiple surgical contexts through autonomic neuropathy and impaired immune response.

Chronic endometritis represents a notably consistent finding in reproductive failure literature, with targeted antibiotic therapy improving live birth rates from approximately 44% to 85%, implicating endometrial dysbiosis as a modifiable contributor to recurrent pregnancy loss.

No direct contradictions are evident, though studies differ in emphasizing hormonal versus structural versus infectious etiologies for overlapping presentations such as abnormal uterine bleeding and infertility, reflecting genuine pathophysiological heterogeneity rather than conflicting evidence.

# Inflammatory and Immune Mechanisms in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

## Overview

Endometriosis has been increasingly reframed not merely as an anatomical disorder of ectopic endometrial tissue but as a systemic condition of chronic, self-perpetuating immune dysregulation. The central research question driving this literature is how the peritoneal and lesion microenvironments sustain immune tolerance toward ectopic endometrial implants while simultaneously maintaining a chronically inflamed state. These seemingly paradoxical objectives—inflammation without effective clearance—define the immunological paradox of endometriosis and have motivated extensive investigation into macrophage polarization dynamics, cytokine network architecture, innate lymphocyte dysfunction, and adaptive immune imbalances that collectively permit lesion establishment and progression.

## Consensus Findings

The most robustly replicated finding across this body of literature is the dysregulation of a core pro-inflammatory cytokine triad—IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α—within ectopic lesions, peritoneal fluid, and systemic circulation. These mediators converge on NF-κB signaling as the dominant downstream effector, with co-activation of JAK/STAT3, PI3K/AKT, and MAPK/ERK cascades collectively upregulating COX-2, prostaglandin E2 synthesis, and aromatase expression, thereby establishing a feedforward loop between local inflammation and estrogen dominance that sustains lesion viability. IL-8/CXCL8, acting through CXCR1/CXCR2, is specifically and repeatedly implicated as a chemotactic and proangiogenic mediator that promotes endometrial stromal cell adhesion, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and neovascularization, with plasma IL-8 levels correlating with peritoneal concentrations independent of disease stage.

Macrophage polarization imbalance constitutes the second pillar of consensus. Multiple independent studies demonstrate that the peritoneal environment in endometriosis is characterized by a pathological shift away from cytotoxic M1 macrophages toward immunosuppressive M2 phenotypes that secrete VEGF, TGF-β, IL-10, and matrix metalloproteinases, actively promoting lesion angiogenesis and fibrosis while suppressing immune clearance. Upstream drivers of this polarization shift include cholesterol-driven STAT6/PPARγ signaling, TET3-mediated epigenetic reprogramming, and VEGFR1 tyrosine kinase activity in peritoneal macrophages. Mathematical modeling corroborates that impaired M1-mediated clearance creates bistable dynamics permitting irreversible lesion persistence.

Natural killer cell dysfunction represents a third area of strong consensus. Endometriosis is consistently associated with reduced NK cytotoxicity attributable to upregulation of inhibitory receptors—including KIR2DL1, KIR3DL1, NKG2A, and PD-1—and downregulation of activating receptors NKG2D, NKp46, and NKp30. Ectopic lesions actively enforce this suppression through expression of immune checkpoint ligands HLA-E and HLA-G, secretion of immunosuppressive exosomes bearing FasL and MICA/B, and CXCL8-mediated direct impairment of NK cytotoxic machinery. This NK cell exhaustion is mechanistically analogous to tumor immune evasion and is proposed as a primary driver of impaired ectopic tissue clearance. Complementing this, the Th17/regulatory T cell imbalance—elevated Th17 activity alongside diminished Treg function—is consistently reported as a contributor to peritoneal inflammation, nociceptor sensitization, and autoimmune comorbidity susceptibility, with overlapping genetic susceptibility loci including TNF-α rs1800629 and FOXP3 rs3761549 linking endometriosis to psoriasis and other immune-mediated conditions. Progesterone resistance is additionally identified as a critical permissive factor, preventing secretory-phase suppression of M1 macrophage activation and perpetuating the pro-inflammatory uterine environment.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive contradictions permeate this literature. The role of M1 macrophages is perhaps the most prominent source of tension: while M1 phenotypes are consistently described as protective through cytotoxic clearance of ectopic cells, sustained M1 activation in established disease is simultaneously reported to drive pain sensitization, tissue damage, and neuroinflammation, suggesting that the temporal context of macrophage activation critically determines whether inflammatory signaling is pathogenic or beneficial. This temporal heterogeneity is further compounded by conflicting reports regarding disease-stage dependency, with some studies finding M1 dominance in early endometriosis that transitions to M2 dominance in advanced disease, while others emphasize M2 macrophages as primary pathogenic drivers regardless of stage.

IL-6 presents a conspicuous interpretive contradiction: it is described both as a central pro-inflammatory driver of lesion growth and disease severity and as a potential therapeutic or physiological mediator in contexts of endometrial repair and implantation support. Similarly, IFN-γ is reported as both a pro-inflammatory promoter of endometriotic cell proliferation and a necessary co-factor for antiproliferative and pro-apoptotic effects in certain experimental models. TGF-β signaling exhibits isoform-dependent opposing effects, with TGF-β3 favoring tissue healing while TGF-β1 and TGF-β2 drive fibrosis and adhesion formation, complicating any monolithic therapeutic targeting of this pathway. Whether elevated regulatory T cell and IL-24-producing Treg populations represent protective immune compensation or pathological immunosuppression enabling lesion persistence similarly remains unresolved.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Despite substantial progress, critical mechanistic gaps persist. The precise temporal sequence governing how the immune microenvironment transitions from initial pro-inflammatory lesion-permissive states to chronic immunosuppressive equilibria remains poorly characterized in humans, given the constraints of studying early-stage disease. The upstream triggers that initiate macrophage polarization toward M2 phenotypes in susceptible individuals—as opposed to effective M1-mediated clearance in unaffected women—are incompletely understood, with epigenetic, metabolic, and microbiotic contributors requiring integration into coherent mechanistic models.
