## Inflammatory and Immune Mechanisms in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of papers, several interconnected themes emerge with particular relevance to endometriosis pathophysiology. Macrophage polarization is the most recurrent mechanism, with the M1/M2 balance consistently implicated in driving or resolving inflammation. Multiple papers establish that a shift toward M1-dominant, pro-inflammatory macrophage activation characterizes endometriosis, impairing implantation, tissue repair, and immune tolerance. This polarization is tightly coupled to metabolic reprogramming, with M1 macrophages relying on glycolysis and succinate-mediated HIF-1α stabilization to sustain cytokine production, while M2 macrophages depend on oxidative phosphorylation for their anti-inflammatory and tissue-remodeling functions.

Cytokine dysregulation constitutes a second dominant theme. TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 are the most consistently reported pro-inflammatory mediators across conditions mechanistically analogous to endometriosis, including rheumatoid arthritis, acute pancreatitis, and postoperative adhesion formation. In endometriosis specifically, these cytokines activate NF-κB signaling pathways to drive neuroinflammation, nociceptive sensitization, and lesion proliferation. Estrogen synergizes with TNF-α to amplify IL-8 expression, linking hormonal dysregulation directly to the inflammatory cascade. IL-8 receives particular emphasis as a chemotactic and proangiogenic mediator, with anti-IL-8 treatment reducing both postoperative adhesions and endometriosis-related pathology in primate models.

Immune cell compositional shifts represent a third dominant theme. Endometriosis is associated with elevated M1 macrophage density, altered natural killer cell and T cell populations, and impaired regulatory T cell function. The thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP)/Th2 axis is specifically implicated in endometriosis progression, with TSLP—elevated in lesions, peritoneal fluid, and serum—activating JAK-STAT signaling to promote stromal proliferation while inducing Th2 responses (IL-4, IL-5, IL-9, IL-13) that form positive feedback loops sustaining lesion growth. This contrasts with but does not contradict the simultaneous evidence for Th1-mediated tissue damage, suggesting a complex mixed inflammatory phenotype rather than a purely Th1 or Th2 disease.

Fibrosis and tissue remodeling emerge as downstream consequences of sustained inflammation, driven by TGF-β, SPP1+ macrophages, and cancer-associated fibroblast-like stromal cells. TGF-β, produced by regulatory T cells and M2 macrophages, paradoxically suppresses anti-tumor and anti-lesion immunity while promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and extracellular matrix deposition—mechanisms directly relevant to endometriotic lesion fibrosis and adhesion formation.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

The NF-κB signaling pathway is the most consistently identified convergence point for inflammatory stimuli across papers, activated downstream of TLR4/LPS signaling, IL-1β, TNF-α, and damage-associated molecular patterns. Progesterone resistance is identified as critical to endometriosis-related inflammation, preventing the normal secretory-phase suppression of M1 macrophages and perpetuating a pro-inflammatory uterine environment. The gut microbiota–estrogen axis, operating through altered β-glucuronidase activity and lipopolysaccharide-TLR4 signaling, is reported as an upstream perpetuator of systemic and local inflammatory states. IL-33/ST2 signaling is highlighted as a broad damage-response pathway that activates innate immune cells including mast cells, ILC2, and macrophages, and whose release during necrosis amplifies NF-κB-dependent inflammation—a mechanism with direct relevance to endometriotic lesion microenvironments. Mendelian randomization evidence implicates specific immune mediators including IL-17 and the chemokine MIP1β in causal pathways to female infertility, lending genetic support to observat

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Findings

The literature across this cluster converges on chronic, dysregulated inflammation as a central mechanism in endometriosis pathogenesis, characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, aberrant immune cell polarization, and disrupted apoptotic signaling. The most consistently reported finding is the upregulation of IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and IL-1β within ectopic lesions, peritoneal fluid, and systemic circulation, where these mediators collectively promote endometriotic cell survival, proliferation, and adhesion while suppressing apoptosis. Single-cell analyses reveal a characteristic immune cell dysregulation in ectopic tissue, including reduced NK and B cell populations alongside elevated macrophages, T cells, and mast cells, with macrophages predominantly polarized toward an immunosuppressive M2 phenotype that sustains lesion viability through IL-10 and TGF-β secretion.

A recurring mechanistic theme involves impaired immune clearance of ectopic endometrial cells. Endometriotic lesions actively evade cytotoxic surveillance by downregulating pro-apoptotic TRAIL receptors DR4/DR5, upregulating decoy receptors DcR1/DcR2, and releasing immunosuppressive exosomes expressing MICA/B and FasL that inhibit NK cell cytotoxicity. Neutrophils are also consistently implicated, with elevated peritoneal neutrophil numbers, increased neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios correlating with disease severity, and NET formation contributing to chronic pelvic inflammation. Osteopontin signaling through RhoA/ROS and the NOD/NF-κB pathway emerge as additional mechanistic nodes driving inflammatory amplification.

Some contradictions are apparent regarding cytokine directionality. IFN-γ, for instance, is reported both as a pro-inflammatory driver promoting endometriotic cell proliferation and, paradoxically, as a necessary co-factor for NAC-mediated antiproliferative and pro-apoptotic effects in endometriotic cells, demonstrating context-dependent outcomes. Similarly, IL-10 appears both as a marker of M2-driven immunosuppression facilitating lesion persistence and as a diagnostic biomarker elevated in menstrual blood, complicating its interpretive utility. These contradictions likely reflect differences in cellular context, disease stage, and experimental models across studies, underscoring the need for standardized experimental frameworks in endometriosis immune research.

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Findings

The assembled literature converges on several dominant themes: dysregulated cytokine networks, impaired innate and adaptive immune cell function, chemokine-mediated recruitment deficits, and macrophage polarization imbalances as central mechanisms perpetuating endometriosis pathogenesis.

The most consistently reported finding across these papers is the elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines—particularly IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8—within endometriotic compartments. These mediators, predominantly produced by peritoneal macrophages and ectopic stromal cells, drive ectopic lesion survival through multiple converging mechanisms: IL-6 suppresses apoptosis of implants, TNF-α upregulates COX-2 and MMP-9 while promoting M2 macrophage polarization, and IL-8 directly stimulates endometrial cell proliferation in a dose-dependent manner. IL-8 emerges as a particularly promising biomarker, with plasma levels correlating with local peritoneal concentrations regardless of disease stage. Downstream signaling cascades implicated recurrently include NF-κB, JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and MAPK pathways, which collectively sustain the chronic inflammatory milieu.

Immune cell dysfunction constitutes a second dominant theme. Natural killer cells display elevated inhibitory receptor expression (KIR2DL1, KIR3DL1, NKG2A, PD-1) alongside reduced activating receptor expression (NKp46, NKp30, NKG2D), consistent with functional impairment. The CCL5-CCR5 axis is specifically disrupted in ectopic lesions, where decreased CCL5 production reduces recruitment of cytotoxic CCR5+CD4+ T cells and limits interferon-gamma secretion, thereby permitting immune tolerance of ectopic implants. Concurrently, ILC populations are dysregulated, with reduced protective ILC3 cells and elevated ILC2 cells driven by the alarmin IL-33, promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis through the IL-33/ST2 axis.

Macrophage biology features prominently, with the TREM1/TLR4/NF-κB cascade identified as a critical driver of M1 macrophage polarization and post-surgical recurrence. Regulatory immune populations—including IL-24-producing Tregs and plasmablasts—are elevated, potentially reflecting compensatory immunosuppression, while the pyroptotic release of IL-16 via gasdermin E adds a mechanistically distinct inflammatory amplification pathway.

Notable contradictions emerge regarding the net functional consequence of elevated regulatory cells: whether increased IL-24-producing Tregs represent protective compensation or pathological immune suppression enabling lesion persistence remains unresolved. Similarly, TGF-β signaling exerts isoform-dependent opposing effects, with TGF-β3 favoring healing while TGF-β1/β2 promote fibrosis and adhesion formation, complicating therapeutic targeting. The precise causal role of Chlamydia trachomatis-driven miRNA dysregulation in disease initiation versus progression also requires further clarification, as simultaneous activation of pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive responses creates interpretive ambiguity. Collectively, these findings support a model in which endometriosis exploits multiple converging immune evasion strategies, with cytokine networks, innate lymphoid dysfunction, and macrophage reprogramming as priority therapeutic targets.

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: Synthesis

Research across this cluster converges on a unified framework of endometriosis as a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease sustained by pervasive dysregulation of both innate and adaptive immune compartments. The dominant mechanistic theme is the failure of immune surveillance alongside active immunosuppressive remodeling of the peritoneal microenvironment, collectively permitting ectopic endometrial cell survival, lesion establishment, and progression.

**Macrophage polarization** emerges as the most consistently reported mechanism. Multiple studies independently demonstrate that peritoneal macrophages shift from cytotoxic M1 phenotypes toward alternatively activated M2 states that secrete pro-angiogenic and pro-fibrotic mediators including VEGF, TGF-β1, and IL-10. The VEGFR1 tyrosine kinase pathway in peritoneal macrophages specifically drives this polarization shift and promotes neovascularization and lymphangiogenesis essential for lesion establishment. Mathematical modeling corroborates that impaired M1-mediated clearance creates bistable dynamics permitting irreversible lesion persistence.

**Natural killer cell dysfunction** is consistently reported across multiple papers, with reduced cytotoxicity attributed to upregulation of inhibitory receptors and immunosuppressive ligands including HLA-E and HLA-G. NK cell depletion experiments demonstrate that loss of cytotoxic surveillance directly facilitates ectopic tissue immune evasion, paralleled by observations in lymphangioleiomyomatosis models where uterine NK cell loss promotes metastatic spread through analogous mechanisms.

**Cytokine dysregulation** represents another dominant theme, with elevated IL-17A, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and MCP-1 reported across studies as drivers of peritoneal inflammation, angiogenesis, and nociceptor sensitization underlying chronic pelvic pain. The Th17/regulatory T cell imbalance—with elevated Th17 activity and diminished Treg function—appears across endometriosis, recurrent pregnancy loss, and autoimmune comorbidity literature, reinforcing the concept of shared immunological architecture. Genetic association studies identify overlapping susceptibility loci including TNF-α rs1800629 and FOXP3 rs3761549 linking endometriosis to psoriasis and other autoimmune conditions.

Novel innate immune contributors identified include **neutrophil extracellular traps**, whose circulating markers correlate with pain severity and infertility, and **eosinophils**, which accumulate in ovarian endometriomas and drive fibrosis through a GM-CSF–PAI-1 axis resistant to standard hormonal therapies. These findings expand the inflammatory cell repertoire beyond the classical macrophage-NK cell paradigm.

Regarding contradictions, one notable tension exists in macrophage biology: while M1 macrophages are consistently described as protective through cytotoxic clearance of ectopic cells, several papers simultaneously report that sustained M1 activation and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines in established disease contribute to pain sensitization and tissue damage, suggesting temporal context critically determines whether inflammatory activation is beneficial or pathogenic. Additionally, eosinophil-mediated fibrosis appears unresponsive to progestin and GnRH analog therapies that broadly suppress inflammation, indicating mechanistic heterogeneity within the inflammatory response that current hormonal treatments fail to address comprehensively.

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

The literature across this cluster converges on a multifaceted inflammatory immune landscape in endometriosis, characterized by dysregulated innate and adaptive immune cell populations, aberrant cytokine networks, and failure of immune surveillance mechanisms that collectively facilitate ectopic lesion establishment and persistence.

**Dominant Themes and Mechanisms**

Macrophage polarization emerges as a consistently central mechanism. Multiple papers document a pathological shift toward M2 macrophage dominance in the peritoneal environment of endometriosis, where M2 cells promote lesion vascularization, tissue remodeling, and immune suppression through secretion of TGF-β, IL-10, VEGF, and matrix metalloproteinases. Conversely, M1 macrophages appear to suppress disease progression, and experimental restoration of M1/M2 balance—for instance, through andrographolide—produces lesion reduction comparable to standard pharmacological therapies such as dienogest. Peritoneal macrophages additionally drive coagulation, fibrin deposition, and adhesion formation through coagulation factor production and TGF-β1/VEGF-α secretion, linking immune dysregulation directly to structural pathological sequelae.

Natural killer cell dysfunction constitutes a second major axis. Endometriosis patients exhibit impaired NK cytotoxicity attributable to reduced NKG2D expression, upregulation of inhibitory receptors NKG2A and PD-1 driven by IL-10, soluble ICAM-1-mediated synapse disruption, and ectopic tissue upregulation of HLA-G and HLA-E that engage inhibitory checkpoint pathways analogous to tumor immune evasion. This NK cell exhaustion is mechanistically similar to cancer immune escape and is proposed as a central driver of the high rates of infertility and chronic pelvic pain associated with the condition.

Pro-inflammatory cytokine dysregulation is ubiquitously reported. Elevated IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8 appear across multiple contexts—peritoneal fluid, ectopic lesions, and systemic circulation. IL-8, acting through CXCR1/CXCR2, promotes endometrial stromal cell adhesion to fibronectin, endothelial migration, and angiogenesis, with estradiol and progesterone directly stimulating its production from endometrial endothelial cells and thereby linking hormonal dysregulation to inflammatory amplification. This IL-8 axis has been targeted therapeutically using AMY109, a long-acting monoclonal antibody demonstrating efficacy in primate models. Ghrelin treatment in endometrial stromal cells produced marked dose-dependent reductions in TNF-α and IL-6 while enhancing IL-10, implicating GHSR-mediated inhibition of NF-κB and MAPK as plausible anti-inflammatory mechanisms.

Estrogen's immunomodulatory role is highlighted by evidence of sex-dependent effects on macrophage signaling: in female macrophages, estrogen suppresses TNF-α and IL-6 through NFκB inhibition and enhances mitochondrial Sirt3 expression, while paradoxically promoting pro-inflammatory NFκB-p65 and IL-1β production in male macrophages. These findings provide mechanistic grounding for the female-predominant inflammatory phenotype characteristic of endometriosis and related conditions.

The PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint axis and regulatory T cell populations are identified as critical modulators of the endometriotic immune microenvironment, with reduced Treg frequencies and dysregulated checkpoint signaling contributing to the failure of maternal-type immune tolerance that would otherwise suppress ectopic implantation. Decidualization-associated extracellular vesicles suppress NK cytotoxicity through cAMP-mediated HLA-G upregulation, and their dysfunction in endometriosis may impair this protective mechanism. NLRP3 inflammasome activation, triggered by endoplasmic reticulum stress, oxidative stress, and damage-associated molecular patterns including HMGB1, drives IL-1β-mediated endometrial dysfunction and is implicated in chronic endometritis, a condition strongly associated with endometriosis.

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of studies, dysregulated cytokine signaling, macrophage polarization imbalance, and senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) emerge as the dominant mechanistic themes in endometriosis pathophysiology, though many articles situate these mechanisms within broader inflammatory disease contexts. The peritoneal and lesion microenvironments in endometriosis are consistently portrayed as chronically inflamed states maintained by reciprocal interactions among immune cells, stromal cells, and ectopic endometrial tissue.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

Macrophage biology features prominently across multiple papers. M2-polarized macrophages are repeatedly identified as central to endometriosis lesion persistence, with cholesterol-driven STAT6/PPARγ signaling and TET3-mediated epigenetic reprogramming identified as upstream drivers of this immunosuppressive phenotype. M2 macrophages produce IL-1A and IL-1B, which activate NF-κB signaling in endometrial epithelial and stromal cells, and anakinra-mediated IL-1 receptor blockade consistently reduces lesion burden and angiogenesis in experimental models. The pro-inflammatory cytokine triad of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 is reported across virtually every study addressing endometriosis mechanisms directly, with NF-κB pathway activation serving as a convergent downstream node. LPS-driven TNF-α/NF-κB signaling dysregulates both pro-inflammatory and proangiogenic microRNA expression in endometrial stromal cells, reinforcing bacterial endotoxin as a potential environmental trigger. Senescent fibroblast accumulation, evidenced by elevated p16INK4a and senescence-associated β-galactosidase, sustains chronic inflammation through SASP components including IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and matrix metalloproteinases MMP-1, MMP-2, and MMP-3, with oxidative stress and MAPK dysregulation identified as upstream activators.

β-NGF emerges as a causally implicated mediator via Mendelian randomization, promoting hyperinnervation, mast cell and macrophage activation, and further cytokine release, linking neurogenic and immune mechanisms of endometriosis-associated pain. The tryptophan-kynurenine pathway, initiated by IDO upregulated through IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-6 via STAT1/STAT3, drives immune tolerance through regulatory T cell induction while sustaining IL-33-mediated inflammation in ectopic tissue. CXCL8 secretion from endometrial stromal cells impairs natural killer cell cytotoxic activity, providing a mechanistic explanation for deficient immune surveillance of ectopic implants. Macrophage migration inhibitory factor further contributes by activating ERK, PI3K-Akt, and NF-κB cascades, suppressing apoptosis and promoting angiogenesis through VEGF upregulation.

Immunomodulatory interventions demonstrate efficacy in experimental models: *Lactobacillus gasseri* OLL2809 postbiotics suppress IL-12 and IL-6 while maintaining IL-10 signaling in dendritic cells; combined plasma-treated solutions with steroid hormones synergistically reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines; and senolytic agents including quercetin and metformin target SASP-driven inflammation through AMPK activation and mTOR inhibition respectively. Macrophage-targeted strategies, including CD47-SIRPα axis disruption and pharmacological TET3 degradation via Bobcat339, attenuate disease progression across models encompassing endometriosis alongside other chronic inflammatory diseases.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Some tension exists regarding the balance of M1 versus M2 macrophage involvement. One paper reports that early-stage endometriosis is dominated by M1-like pro-inflammatory macrophages that transition toward M2 in advanced disease, whereas other studies emphasize M2 macrophages as primary pathogenic drivers regardless of disease stage, suggesting that macrophage polarization dynamics may be more complex and temporally heterogeneous than current models capture.

## Inflammatory and Immune Mechanisms in Endometriosis: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanistic Frameworks

Across this body of literature, endometriosis emerges consistently as a condition of profound, multidimensional immune dysregulation rather than a purely anatomical or hormonal disorder. The dominant conceptual framework positions inadequate immune surveillance and active immune evasion as the permissive foundation upon which ectopic lesions establish and persist. Several interlocking mechanisms recur throughout: aberrant macrophage polarization, dysregulated cytokine networks, impaired natural killer cell cytotoxicity, disrupted T-cell subset balance, and a self-amplifying estrogen–inflammation feedforward loop.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The cytokine triad of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α appears with exceptional consistency as central pro-inflammatory drivers across studies examining endometriosis, adenomyosis, and related gynecological pathologies. These mediators activate canonical signaling cascades—NF-κB, JAK/STAT3, PI3K/AKT, and MAPK/ERK—that collectively upregulate COX-2, prostaglandin E2 synthesis, and aromatase expression, thereby sustaining local estrogen dominance and lesion survival (PMC12842206, PMC12814728, PMC12756115). The COX-2/PGE2/aromatase axis represents perhaps the most robustly supported feedforward mechanism in this literature.

Macrophage polarization is another dominant and repeatedly documented theme. Multiple studies report a progressive shift from cytotoxic M1 macrophages toward immunosuppressive M2 phenotypes within endometriotic and adenomyotic microenvironments, with M2-derived IL-10 and TGF-β reinforcing immunosuppression while M1 macrophage depletion reduces protective inflammatory clearance (PMC12756115, PMC12714385, PMC12801971, PMC12813101). Notably, large peritoneal macrophages are identified as functionally distinct reparative cells whose estrogen-enhanced recruitment prevents pathological fibrosis following endometrial injury, suggesting that not all macrophage reduction is equivalent in its consequences (PMC12722460). Chemokine-mediated recruitment of monocytes to ectopic lesions—particularly via MCP-1/CCL2, IL-8/CXCL8, and RANTES/CCL5—is described as an active mechanism by which the lesion microenvironment perpetuates its own inflammatory infiltrate (PMC12756115, PMC12801971).

Natural killer cell dysfunction is consistently implicated, with reduced cytotoxicity attributed partly to ectopic lesion-derived estrogen suppressing perforin expression in CD56dim NK cells (PMC12814728). Concurrently, Treg expansion within the peritoneal and endometrial compartments suppresses effector immune responses, and the Th17/Treg imbalance—characterized by elevated IL-17 family members alongside reduced regulatory populations—is repeatedly linked to lesion progression and infertility (PMC12782778, PMC12756115). IL-17C specifically has been identified as a driver of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and malignant transformation toward endometriosis-associated ovarian carcinoma via IL-17RE/PI3K signaling, representing a mechanistic bridge between chronic inflammation and oncogenesis (PMC12765842).

The complement system, neutrophil-derived NETs and reactive oxygen species, and dysbiotic microbiota further amplify peritoneal inflammation, with reduced Lactobacillus dominance elevating systemic estrogen through enhanced β-glucuronidase activity (PMC12814728, PMC12858905). The CXCL12/CXCR4 axis emerges across reproductive immunology studies as a critical regulator of immune cell trafficking, decidualization, and vascular remodeling whose dysregulation links directly to endometriosis pathogenesis (PMC12730366).

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster collectively reveal that endometriosis and related gynecological conditions are sustained by a chronic, self-reinforcing inflammatory microenvironment orchestrated through convergent innate and adaptive immune dysregulation. Several dominant mechanistic themes emerge. First, macrophage polarization imbalance—specifically the skewing toward pro-inflammatory M1 or dysfunctional M2 phenotypes—appears as a unifying mechanism across endometriosis, adenomyosis, intrauterine adhesion, and PCOS. Second, cytokine-mediated signaling networks, particularly involving IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, TGF-β, and IL-8/CXCL8, drive ectopic lesion establishment, angiogenesis, and fibrosis through downstream activation of NF-κB, JAK/STAT, and MAPK pathways. Third, intercellular communication via extracellular vesicles, exosomal microRNAs, and paracrine secretomes substantially amplifies and propagates inflammatory signals across the peritoneal cavity and systemic circulation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

IL-6 emerges as the most consistently implicated mediator, appearing across endometriosis, adenomyosis, chronic endometritis, and associated comorbidities, uniformly activating JAK2/STAT3 signaling to suppress endometrial receptivity markers including HOXA10 and HOXA11. TNF-α and IL-1β similarly appear with high frequency as drivers of NF-κB activation and macrophage M1 polarization. The IL-8/CXCR1/CXCR2 axis is specifically highlighted in both endometriosis pathogenesis—where follicular fluid IL-8 promotes endometrial cell migration and epithelial-mesenchymal transition—and in PCOS, where neutrophil recruitment via this axis perpetuates ovarian inflammation. TGF-β signaling through Smad pathways consistently links immune dysregulation to fibrosis, EMT, and regulatory T cell expansion, reported across endometriosis, adenomyosis, intrauterine adhesion, and Chlamydia trachomatis-associated endometrial pathology. Dysregulation of the M1/M2 macrophage balance is reported in virtually every endometriosis-relevant paper: andrographolide restores M1 activity to suppress peritoneal lesions, while IL-37 and scaffold-based co-delivery systems promote M2 polarization to resolve fibrosis and inflammation, reflecting context-dependent therapeutic requirements. Uterine natural killer cells and regulatory T cells are consistently identified as critical modulators of implantation and feto-maternal tolerance, with their dysfunction linked to recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss. Exosome-mediated transfer of immunomodulatory molecules—including miR-199a-3p in PCOS follicular fluid—emerges as a reproducible mechanism by which local inflammatory signals are disseminated to macrophages and other immune effectors.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable contradictions exist within this literature. Macrophage polarization presents the most prominent tension: andrographolide's therapeutic benefit in endometriosis is attributed to promoting M1 macrophage activity and suppressing M2-driven angiogenesis, whereas scaffold-based endometrial repair, uterine regeneration, and intrauterine adhesion treatment all depend on M2 polarization for tissue healing. This reflects a fundamental context-dependency—M2 macrophages are pathological in sustaining ectopic endometriosis but essential for endometrial repair—yet the literature does not consistently reconcile how these opposing requirements are distinguished therapeutically. Similarly, IL-17A is reported as driving renal fibrosis through TGF-β/Smad pathway activation, whereas in endometrial and periodontal contexts IL-17 primarily drives pro-inflammatory Th17 responses; the degree to which IL-17 contributes to or protects against endometriosis-associated fibrosis remains unresolved. TGF-β presents analogous duality: it simultaneously promotes immunosuppressive Treg differentiation beneficial for implantation tolerance and drives EMT and fibrotic

## Inflammatory Immune Response in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

The papers reviewed collectively illuminate a multifaceted inflammatory immune landscape in endometriosis and related gynecological conditions, converging on several dominant mechanistic themes.

**Dominant Themes and Core Mechanisms**

The most pervasive theme is progressive immune dysfunction enabling ectopic tissue survival. Multiple papers describe a failure of cytotoxic immune surveillance, wherein NK cells exhibit reduced expression of activating receptors (NKG2D, NKP46) and upregulated inhibitory receptors (KIR2DL1, NKG2A), alongside diminished CD8+ T cell activation and immature dendritic cell function. This immunosuppressive milieu is actively maintained by ectopic endometrial cells secreting IL-6, TGF-β, PGE2, and IL-15. NF-κB signaling emerges as a central inflammatory amplifier across multiple papers, activated downstream of LPS-exosome-lncRNA circuits, macrophage polarization cascades, and prostaglandin-driven pathways. Macrophage polarization—specifically the M1/M2 balance—represents another consistently reported mechanism, with pro-inflammatory M1 states driven by NF-κB and Notch2 signaling, and immunosuppressive M2 phenotypes sustained by arginase-1 activity and TGF-β/IL-10.

**Consistently Reported Findings**

IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-8/CXCL8 are repeatedly identified as central pro-inflammatory mediators across endometriosis, endometritis, and dysmenorrhea contexts. The OTUD6B-AS1/miR-128/Notch2 ceRNA axis and the ILC2-MDSC-arginase-1 axis represent novel mechanistic contributions supported by clinical validation. Exosome-mediated intercellular communication and prostaglandin-NF-κB signaling appear consistently as druggable pathways.

**Contradictions and Caveats**

Notable tensions exist regarding the net inflammatory versus immunosuppressive character of the peritoneal environment—some papers emphasize excessive pro-inflammatory cytokine production driving pathology, while others foreground immune exhaustion and tolerance as primary disease drivers. Additionally, several included papers (perineural invasion in pancreatic cancer, diabetes-tuberculosis co-infection, spinal cord injury) are tangentially related to endometriosis at best, potentially reflecting cluster contamination that limits synthesis coherence.

# Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: Mechanisms, Evidence, and Translational Prospects

## Overview

The core research question driving this literature is whether plant-derived polyphenols and related natural compounds can therapeutically modulate the pathophysiological processes that sustain endometriosis—specifically ectopic lesion establishment, inflammatory amplification, aberrant angiogenesis, hormone-driven proliferation, and oxidative tissue injury. Endometriosis is uniquely suited to investigation with multi-target natural compounds because its pathogenesis involves simultaneously dysregulated inflammatory signaling, estrogen hypersensitivity, reactive oxygen species accumulation, and immunological tolerance of ectopic implants—mechanisms that polyphenols characteristically address through pleiotropic pharmacology rather than singular pathway inhibition. Across the 373 articles synthesized here, an extensive experimental literature spanning in vitro cellular models, rodent endometriosis models, and a smaller number of clinical studies converges on defining both the therapeutic potential and the translational limitations of this compound class.

## Consensus Findings

The most consistently reported finding across all groups is that NF-κB represents the central transcriptional hub targeted by natural compounds in endometriosis-relevant contexts. Curcumin, quercetin, resveratrol, EGCG, luteolin, baicalein, astragaloside IV, parthenolide, and numerous traditional Chinese medicine formulations uniformly suppress NF-κB activation, producing downstream reductions in the cytokine triad of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6—mediators that sustain peritoneal inflammation, recruit macrophages, and promote lesion neovascularization. Parallel activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant axis is equally pervasive, reported for curcumin, astaxanthin, fisetin, polydatin, and α-cyperone, counteracting the elevated reactive oxygen species that drive endometrial stromal cell survival in ectopic sites. Complementing these anti-inflammatory and antioxidant mechanisms, VEGF suppression and inhibition of matrix metalloproteinase activity—demonstrated prominently for EGCG and Pro-EGCG through TGF-β/SMAD pathway interference—consistently emerges as a therapeutically relevant anti-angiogenic mechanism, targeting the neovascularization indispensable for lesion maintenance.

The PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis represents a second major convergence point. Resveratrol, naringenin, baicalin, EGCG, and quercetin all modulate this pathway, with quercetin's senolytic activity—reducing ectopic lesion volume through AKT/ERK suppression and p53 upregulation—among the most directly endometriosis-specific mechanistic findings. The NLRP3 inflammasome has emerged more recently as an important target, with parthenolide, quercetin, and vitamin D₃ demonstrating its suppression while reducing downstream IL-1β and caspase-1 activity; notably, parthenolide mechanistically couples NLRP3 inhibition to AMPK/ULK1-mediated autophagy activation, establishing an elegant regulatory intersection relevant to lesion biology. Phytoestrogens including genistein, daidzein, kaempferol, and resveratrol demonstrate preferential estrogen receptor beta agonism, which opposes the ERα-driven proliferative signaling central to endometriosis pathogenesis, providing a mechanistic basis for hormonal modulation without the systemic estrogenic risks of conventional therapy. Vitamin D₃ similarly inhibits NF-κB through VDR-mediated IκBα stabilization, with consistent evidence of ectopic lesion reduction in rodent models.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive contradictions complicate interpretation of this literature. The most explicit clinical discordance involves omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids: despite mechanistically credible inhibition of NF-κB and reduction of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in preclinical models, omega-3 supplementation has failed to produce statistically significant improvements in endometriosis-related pain across multiple randomized controlled trials, directly challenging the assumption that inflammatory biomarker reduction constitutes a valid surrogate endpoint for symptom relief. This disconnect between biochemical efficacy and clinical outcome represents the most consequential translational gap in the field.

Dose-dependency introduces additional complexity. Quercetin exhibits a narrow therapeutic window in which high concentrations paradoxically impair granulosa cell function and embryonic development despite beneficial anti-inflammatory and senolytic effects at lower doses—a phenomenon mirrored by B vitamins, where adequate intake reduces cancer risk while excess supplementation accelerates tumor growth. CBD similarly demonstrates anti-inflammatory activity through NF-κB and Nrf2 modulation at therapeutic concentrations while inducing hepatotoxicity dose-dependently. Gossypol, active against endometriosis through PIK3R2/MAPK1 suppression, induces hepatotoxicity via elevation of the same mTOR and TNF-α mediators that other compounds suppress. These findings collectively establish that the therapeutic-to-toxic ratio for polyphenols is neither uniformly favorable nor predictable from pathway-level mechanistic data alone.

Context-dependent pathway activity generates further interpretive tensions. Endoplasmic reticulum stress is therapeutically exploited by myrrh to induce selective apoptosis in endometriotic stromal cells through ATF4/ATF6/DDIT3 upregulation, yet the same ER stress process requires suppression in granulosa cells to prevent follicular dysfunction. IL-6, overwhelmingly characterized as a pro-inflammatory driver of lesion maintenance, was paradoxically elevated as a beneficial reparative response in resveratrol-treated equine endometritis. Nitric oxide presents a fundamental pharmacological paradox, exhibiting pro-proliferative activity at low concentrations and cytotoxicity at high concentrations within the same endometrial context. These contradictions underscore that pathway-level interventions cannot be interpreted outside their cellular and concentration-specific contexts.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Despite substantial mechanistic characterization, critical knowledge gaps persist. The interaction between polyphenols and the gut microbiome represents an underinvestigated but potentially decisive variable; unabsorbed polyphenols undergo extensive colonic microbial biotransformation into bioactive metabolites with superior systemic bioavailability, and

## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Findings

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 articles, several dominant mechanistic themes emerge. First, oxidative stress attenuation through reactive oxygen species scavenging and upregulation of endogenous antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase, Nrf2-regulated pathways) represents the most pervasive mechanism by which natural compounds exert protective effects. Second, suppression of pro-inflammatory signaling—particularly through NF-κB, STAT3, NLRP3 inflammasome, and JAK-STAT pathways—is consistently implicated across diverse compound classes including polyphenols, flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids. Third, modulation of hormonal and steroidogenic pathways, relevant to estrogen-dependent conditions like endometriosis, appears repeatedly through phytoestrogen activity (daidzein, formononetin, diosgenin) and regulation of aromatase and steroidogenic gene expression.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The cytokine triad of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 is uniformly identified as a central inflammatory mediator targeted by natural compounds across virtually all relevant studies, including resveratrol (mitochondrial ROS/NLRP3 axis), EGCG (NF-κB/MAPK/STAT1), curcumin, quercetin, and formononetin. NF-κB emerges as the most consistently implicated transcriptional hub. Several papers specifically relevant to endometriosis pathophysiology highlight STAT3 phosphorylation as a driver of ectopic lesion development and macrophage polarization toward pro-inflammatory M1 phenotypes, with therapeutic interventions—notably nanoceria and AMPK activators—demonstrating reversal toward anti-inflammatory M2 states. Dietary factors modulating endometriosis risk converge on antioxidant deficiency and estrogen-promoting adipose aromatase activity as mechanistic links between nutrition and disease progression.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable contradictions emerge. Nitric oxide demonstrates concentration-dependent opposing roles in endometrial pathology—pro-tumorigenic at low concentrations and cytotoxic at high concentrations—representing a fundamental pharmacological paradox. JAK/STAT3 inhibition with tofacitinib suppressed ectopic lesions but caused complete implantation failure, contrasting with nanoceria's ability to reduce lesions while preserving fertility, highlighting that pathway-level selectivity critically determines reproductive safety. CBD exhibits anti-inflammatory properties through NF-κB and Nrf2 modulation at therapeutic concentrations, yet induces hepatotoxicity dose-dependently, paralleling findings with C-DIM12 and Euodiae Fructus alkaloids. B vitamin supplementation paradoxically reduces cancer risk at adequate levels but accelerates tumor growth when excessive, suggesting that natural compound dosing thresholds fundamentally alter therapeutic versus adverse outcomes—a principle broadly applicable to polyphenol-based endometriosis therapeutics requiring rigorous dose optimization.

## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature surveyed across this cluster converges on several recurring themes: suppression of pro-inflammatory signaling, attenuation of oxidative stress, modulation of angiogenesis, and regulation of cell proliferation and apoptosis. Natural polyphenolic compounds—including quercetin, resveratrol, myricetin, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein, naringenin, hesperidin, and gossypol—emerge as mechanistically diverse agents applicable to endometriosis and related reproductive pathologies. A dominant mechanistic axis involves inhibition of NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways, consistently reported across compounds including myricetin, astragaloside IV, neferine, and octyl gallate, leading to downregulation of TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8—cytokines centrally implicated in endometriotic lesion establishment and maintenance. Parallel activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway represents a second conserved mechanism, reducing reactive oxygen species accumulation and lipid peroxidation that drive oxidative tissue damage characteristic of endometriosis.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several findings recur with notable consistency. First, suppression of the TLR4/NF-κB axis by compounds such as astragaloside IV directly attenuates endometriosis-associated inflammatory lesions, reducing key mediators including TNF-α, CCL-2, IL-1β, and IL-6. Second, inhibition of VEGF-mediated angiogenesis—demonstrated prominently for EGCG and Pro-EGCG via TGF-β/SMAD pathway modulation—is consistently identified as therapeutically relevant, given that pathological neovascularization sustains ectopic endometrial tissue. Third, phytotherapeutic compounds including hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein, and naringenin reduce cyst diameter and fibrosis while selectively modulating estrogen receptor activity, addressing the estrogen-dependent proliferative component of endometriosis. Quercetin recurrently demonstrates pleiotropic reproductive benefits, improving ovarian follicular integrity, reducing oxidative stress-induced granulosa cell apoptosis, and modulating hormonal balance through NF-κB, AP-1, and TLR inhibition. The PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway represents another consistently implicated signaling node, targeted by resveratrol, curcumin, and baicalin across multiple reproductive disease contexts.

Gossypol and cotton phenol merit specific mention as polyphenolic compounds with documented activity against endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and dysfunctional uterine bleeding, though their mechanisms differ substantially: gossypol inhibits viral adsorption and induces mitochondrial dysfunction, while cotton phenol carries nephrotoxic risks at occupational exposure levels, highlighting important safety distinctions within this compound class.

Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, despite mechanistic evidence for reducing TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β via NF-κB inhibition, failed to produce statistically significant improvements in endometriosis-related pain across multiple randomized controlled trials—a finding that stands in notable contrast to the consistently positive preclinical results reported for polyphenolic compounds in cellular and animal models. This constitutes the most explicit contradiction within the cluster, raising questions about the translational validity of inflammatory biomarker reductions as surrogate endpoints for symptom relief.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Beyond the omega-3 discordance, several internal tensions merit attention. Quercetin studies report a narrow therapeutic window, with high doses paradoxically impairing embryonic development despite beneficial effects at lower concentrations, suggesting dose-dependency that complicates therapeutic translation. The broader polyphenol literature also reveals compound-specific heterogeneity in mechanisms: resveratrol's anticancer activity depends critically on VDAC1 upregulation, whereas structurally related analogs that bind VDAC1 without inducing its overexpression fail to trigger apoptosis, illustrating that structural similarity does not guarantee mechanistic equivalence. ## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The dominant themes across this cluster center on the anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, pro-apoptotic, and antiangiogenic properties of polyphenolic and other natural compounds in endometriosis and related gynecological conditions. Mechanistically, these compounds converge on a core set of signaling pathways: PI3K/AKT/mTOR, NF-κB, MAPK/ERK, TGF-β/Smad, and Wnt/β-catenin. A recurring mechanistic axis involves suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines—particularly TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8—alongside modulation of apoptotic regulators including Bcl-2/Bax ratios and caspase cascades. Estrogen receptor signaling emerges as an additional relevant target, given endometriosis's hormone-dependent pathogenesis.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

Quercetin, EGCG, naringenin, curcumin, resveratrol, and luteolin are the most frequently cited compounds with demonstrated activity against endometriosis-relevant pathways. Quercetin's senolytic activity—reducing lesion size and correcting impaired decidualization through AKT/ERK suppression and p53 upregulation—is among the most directly endometriosis-specific findings reported. EGCG consistently inhibits endometrial cell proliferation, suppresses TGFβ1-mediated fibrosis, and reduces angiogenesis by downregulating VEGF and matrix metalloproteinases. Naringenin, alone and synergistically with β-catenin inhibitors, suppresses PI3K/AKT and Wnt/β-catenin signaling in endometrial mesenchymal stem cells. Vitamin D₃ and its active metabolite 1,25(OH)₂D₃ are consistently reported to inhibit NF-κB signaling through VDR-mediated IκBα stabilization, reducing ectopic lesion growth in both cellular and rodent models. The PI3K/AKT pathway is the single most consistently implicated therapeutic target across studies. Pterostilbene's restoration of endometrial receptivity via ERK pathway downregulation and normalization of implantation factors (ITGB3, VEGF, LIF) represents another specific mechanistic finding. Angiogenesis suppression, mediated through VEGF and MMP inhibition, is reported across multiple compounds including EGCG, polyphenols from traditional Chinese formulas (Ge Xia-Zhu Yu decoction constituents: quercetin, kaempferol, baicalin, luteolin), and ferulic acid.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Some tensions are apparent. Quercetin is reported as both pro-apoptotic in ectopic endometrial cells and broadly suppressive of normal ovarian granulosa cell functions including proliferation and hormone secretion, raising concerns about systemic reproductive effects. Resveratrol demonstrates clear benefits for pain and bone metabolism markers in meta-analytic data but shows inconsistent or null effects across cognitive, metabolic, and quality-of-life outcomes, indicating pathway-specific rather than broadly pleiotropic efficacy. Additionally, while observational evidence links tea polyphenol consumption to reduced cancer risk, Mendelian randomization analysis found no causal relationship for glioblastoma, underscoring the broader methodological challenge of translating epidemiological associations into causal mechanisms—a caveat equally applicable to dietary polyphenol studies in endometriosis.

## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Themes

The dominant theme across this cluster is the multi-targeted anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity of plant-derived polyphenols and natural compounds, with consistent mechanistic convergence on a core set of shared pathways. NF-κB inhibition emerges as the most universally reported mechanism, cited across studies examining resveratrol, curcumin, EGCG, epicatechin, ε-viniferin, chrysin, quercetin, and astaxanthin, among others. Downstream of NF-κB suppression, reductions in TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and COX-2 expression are consistently reported across diverse disease models, suggesting that polyphenol-mediated modulation of this pathway represents a common therapeutic denominator applicable to endometriosis pathophysiology. A second consistently reported axis involves activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway, documented for astaxanthin, curcumin, citrus polyphenols, and α-cyperone, which counteracts reactive oxygen species accumulation central to endometrial lesion progression.

Several papers specifically address endometriosis-relevant mechanisms. ε-Viniferin directly suppresses migration and invasion of human endometrial stromal cells while downregulating HPSE and FGFR4, and reducing TGF-β and TNF-α. Resveratrol modulates estrogen receptor activity and sirtuin-mediated pathways including AMPK and PGC-1α, addressing the hormonal dysregulation driving endometriosis. Tetrahydropalmatine reduces endometriosis-associated pain by suppressing TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. EGCG demonstrates anti-angiogenic activity through AMPK activation and suppression of angiogenic gene expression, targeting a critical feature of lesion establishment. Oligo-fucoidan suppresses TGF-β3/SMAD2 signaling relevant to fibrotic remodeling in related uterine pathology.

Beyond direct endometriosis models, papers examining reproductive and inflammatory conditions highlight repeatedly relevant pathways. The PI3K/AKT/mTOR cascade, NLRP3 inflammasome activation, and MAPK signaling are modulated by multiple compounds including berberine, resveratrol, and epicatechin, pathways known to participate in endometrial cell survival and inflammatory amplification. JAK-STAT inhibition by natural compounds including formononetin, ellagic acid, and naringenin adds another therapeutically relevant dimension given STAT3's established role in endometriosis lesion growth.

A notable mechanistic subdivision exists regarding estrogenic activity. Phytoestrogens including genistein, daidzein, kaempferol, and resveratrol preferentially activate estrogen receptor beta, which suppresses proliferative signaling, in contrast to ER-α-mediated protumoral effects. This selectivity carries direct implications for endometriosis, where estrogen-driven proliferation is a central pathogenic feature. However, a degree of tension exists within the literature regarding estrogenic compounds: while phytoestrogenic activity is generally framed as therapeutically beneficial through ER-β selectivity, the review of diethylstilbestrol highlights how estrogenic disruption can impair endometrial receptivity and immune homeostasis, underscoring that the net effect of estrogen receptor modulation depends critically on receptor subtype specificity and compound concentration.

Bioavailability represents a cross-cutting limitation. Curcumin, chrysin, and resveratrol all demonstrate poor oral bioavailability due to rapid phase II metabolism, necessitating formulation strategies including piperine co-administration, nanoencapsulation, and polymeric carriers. Nanotechnology approaches, including mesoporous silica and cerium oxide nanoparticles, are explicitly proposed for endometrial drug delivery, addressing both bioavailability and tissue-targeted delivery simultaneously.

## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature in this cluster converges on several interconnected themes: the use of polyphenolic and other natural compounds to suppress inflammatory signaling, modulate oxidative stress, and restore reproductive homeostasis in conditions with direct relevance to endometriosis pathogenesis. A substantial proportion of studies examine compounds—including curcumin, quercetin, resveratrol, rutin, baicalein, forsythiaside A, and various traditional Chinese medicine formulations—that act on overlapping molecular targets central to endometriosis biology. Parallel mechanistic themes emerge from studies addressing environmental endocrine disruptors (DEHP, PCB126, DBP) as disease-promoting agents, with natural compounds proposed as countermeasures.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The NF-κB signaling pathway is the most uniformly implicated target across these studies. Curcumin, quercetin, alpinumisoflavone, rutin, baicalein, and multiple herbal formulations (Shenghua Decoction, Jiedu Prescription, traditional Chinese medicine formula from *Viola yedoensis* and *Leonurus japonicus*) all suppress NF-κB activation and downstream pro-inflammatory cytokine production, particularly IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. This pathway convergence is noteworthy given its centrality to endometriotic lesion maintenance, immune dysregulation, and pain sensitization.

The PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis represents a second consistently targeted pathway. Quercetin activates PI3K/AKT to protect granulosa cells from apoptosis in premature ovarian failure models, while resveratrol inhibits mTOR to restore autophagy in endometrial cancer. Rutin, black garlic extracts, and beta-sitosterol similarly modulate this pathway in cancer and inflammatory contexts. Notably, DEHP-induced endometriosis progression also operates through PI3K/Akt/mTOR via m6A-mediated IGF-1 upregulation, establishing this axis as both a disease-promoting and therapeutically targetable node.

Oxidative stress suppression is a third dominant theme. The HMGB1-TLR4-NF-κB axis linking oxidative stress to granulosa cell dysfunction, impaired steroidogenesis, and follicular apoptosis is described in two independent studies (PMC12470860 and PMC12608728), with curcumin identified as an inhibitor of this cascade. Selenium nanoparticles, ginsenoside Rg3, and Saussurea lappa extract similarly reduce ROS-mediated reproductive damage, with the latter specifically reducing F2-isoprostane levels and TNF-α in endometriosis mouse models to preserve folliculogenesis.

Apoptotic pathway modulation—specifically the BCL-2/BAX/caspase axis—is reported across diverse compound classes and disease models, consistently showing that natural polyphenols shift the balance toward pro-apoptotic signaling in cancer contexts while paradoxically suppressing apoptosis in healthy reproductive cells, reflecting the context-dependent nature of these compounds.

Environmental toxicant-mediated epigenetic reprogramming emerges as a mechanistically distinct but thematically integrated finding. PCB126 promotes endometriosis via AXL/ESR2/DNMT3A-mediated epigenetic alterations and immune dysregulation, while DEHP disrupts the gut microbiota–butyrate axis to enhance METTL3-dependent m6A methylation of IGF-1, driving epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition. Butyrate supplementation reverses these effects, connecting microbiome-derived metabolites to endometriosis therapeutics and complementing findings on cervicovaginal microbiome composition influencing endometriosis risk through NF-κB-mediated epithelial inflammation.

Bioavailability enhancement through nanoformulation is a recurring practical theme: liposomal resveratrol, rutin phytosomes, and chitosan nanoparticles consistently demonstrate superior efficacy compared to unconjugated compounds, addressing a well-recognized limitation of polyphenol therapeutics.

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on a central paradigm: plant-derived polyphenols and natural compounds exert therapeutic effects in endometriosis and related gynecological conditions through coordinated suppression of oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and aberrant cellular proliferation. The most consistently invoked mechanisms span inhibition of pro-inflammatory signaling cascades, restoration of antioxidant defense systems, and modulation of apoptotic pathways. Several papers address bioavailability limitations of key polyphenols and propose nanoformulation or complexation strategies as solutions.

Resveratrol similarly inhibits NF-κB and TLR4/MyD88 signaling, reduces prostaglandin E2 and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and upregulates antioxidant enzymes including glutathione peroxidase and catalase. The COX-2/PGE2/aromatase positive feedback loop is identified as a central driver of endometriosis pathophysiology, with multiple compounds—including curcumin, shogaol, and quercetin—interrupting this cycle at different nodes. Bioavailability remains a recurrent challenge: resveratrol's poor aqueous solubility is addressed through hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin complexation and PEG-coated stealth liposomal encapsulation, with freeze-drying identified as superior to spray drying for preserving the bioactive trans-isomer. Curcumin nanoformulations including PLGA nanoparticles and folic acid-conjugated nanomicelles similarly enhance cellular uptake and therapeutic efficacy.

## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Themes

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature assembled in this cluster reveals a broad investigation of polyphenolic and natural compounds across gynecological and inflammatory disease contexts, with recurrent emphasis on multi-target pharmacology. Although many papers address conditions beyond endometriosis strictly defined—including ovarian cancer, diminished ovarian reserve, polycystic ovary syndrome, and general reproductive dysfunction—several mechanistic themes converge consistently. The predominant framework involves simultaneous modulation of pro-inflammatory signaling and oxidative stress, with compounds acting on interconnected molecular networks rather than single targets.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The NF-κB signaling pathway emerges as the most universally reported therapeutic target, suppressed by quercetin, resveratrol, luteolin, fisetin, curcumin, astaxanthin, leonurine, and parthenolide across multiple experimental systems. The NLRP3 inflammasome represents a third convergent target, with its suppression reported for parthenolide, α-cyperone, vitamin D3, and metformin, typically reducing downstream IL-1β and caspase-1 activity.

Within endometriosis-specific studies, quercetin was shown to target PDGFRB and inhibit the downstream RAS/RAF1/ERK1/2 MAPK cascade to reduce ectopic lesion volume and suppress cellular invasion. Parthenolide reduced endometriotic lesion burden through dual AMPK/ULK1-mediated autophagy activation and NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition, with autophagy functioning as a negative regulator of inflammasome activity—a mechanistically elegant intersection. Myrrh extract selectively induced endoplasmic reticulum stress-dependent apoptosis in endometriotic stromal cells via upregulation of ATF4, ATF6, DDIT3, and ERN1, activating mitochondrial apoptosis through Bax/Bcl-2 modulation and caspase-3/9 cleavage. Luteolin and fisetin addressed endometriosis through suppression of NF-κB/MAPK inflammatory cascades while additionally targeting angiogenesis and the senescence-associated secretory phenotype, respectively. Resveratrol demonstrated efficacy in a mare model of endometritis by reducing IL-1β and IL-8 while increasing IL-6, shifting the uterine cytokine environment toward inflammatory resolution.

The PI3K/AKT pathway was recurrently implicated in protective mechanisms across reproductive contexts, including ginseng polysaccharide protection of granulosa cells from ER stress-induced necroptosis and ginsenoside Rg1 preservation of stem cell function, both dependent on PI3K/AKT activation. Steroidogenic gene expression—including StAR, CYP11A1, and CYP19A1—was identified as a downstream functional output of ER stress in granulosa cells, with relevance to follicular atresia and impaired estrogen synthesis in conditions affecting ovarian reserve.

The cluster reveals certain tensions worth noting. ER stress is reported as a pathogenic mechanism exploited therapeutically by myrrh (inducing apoptosis in endometriotic cells) but simultaneously as a harmful process requiring suppression by ginseng polysaccharides in granulosa cells, illustrating that the same cellular pathway can be either targeted for activation or inhibition depending on cell type and therapeutic intent. Similarly, IL-6 is consistently described as pro-inflammatory across most papers, yet the resveratrol/endometritis study characterizes its elevation as a beneficial anti-inflammatory and reparative response in uterine tissue, highlighting context-dependency in cytokine interpretation. ## Polyphenols and Natural Compounds in Endometriosis and Related Gynecological Conditions

The papers in this cluster collectively examine how naturally occurring polyphenols, flavonoids, alkaloids, and related bioactive compounds modulate shared inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways across a spectrum of gynecological, oncological, and systemic conditions. While not all papers address endometriosis directly, they converge on mechanistic themes highly relevant to its pathophysiology.

**Dominant Themes and Mechanisms**

The most pervasive theme across these studies is the dual regulation of oxidative stress and inflammation through the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant axis and the NF-κB/PI3K/AKT signaling network. Compounds including piperine, boswellic acid, carnosic acid, cashew polyphenols, abrine, and taxifolin consistently activate Nrf2-mediated cytoprotection while simultaneously suppressing NF-κB-driven pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β). This mechanistic duality appears central to the therapeutic rationale for polyphenol use across multiple disease contexts. A second prominent theme is modulation of apoptotic and proliferative signaling; flavonoids such as morin, quercetin, and fisetin, as well as ginsenosides and imperatorin, converge on BCL-2 family proteins, caspase-3/9 activation, and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway suppression to promote cancer cell death while preserving normal tissue integrity. Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) suppression, particularly through E-cadherin upregulation and vimentin/N-cadherin downregulation, is reported for ginsenosides, abrine, and imperatorin, suggesting broad anti-metastatic relevance.

Of particular relevance to endometriosis, the ACS14@BSA nanoparticle study directly demonstrates that PI3K/Akt and NF-κB suppression reduces ectopic lesion proliferation and IL-6 expression in vivo, while Alchemilla alpina extract inhibits COX-1/COX-2 by over 70%, suppresses AKR1C3-mediated androgen synthesis, and binds estrogen receptor α—collectively addressing the hormonal and inflammatory drivers of endometriosis. Icariin similarly restores ovarian tissue architecture by downregulating PI3K/Akt, while gossypol demonstrates direct activity against gynecological disorders including endometriosis through PIK3R2/MAPK1 suppression. Coffee-derived polyphenols (resveratrol, quercetin, kaempferol) are explicitly noted to inhibit endometriosis progression through NR4A1-dependent mechanisms, and the nutritional review confirms that vitamins C, A, and E, alongside dietary fiber and minerals, suppress NF-κB and prostaglandin pathways implicated in pelvic inflammatory disease—a recognized endometriosis comorbidity.

**Consistently Reported Findings**

The PI3K/AKT signaling pathway emerges as the single most consistently implicated target across this cluster, appearing in studies of boswellic acid, icariin, gossypol, imperatorin, ACS14@BSA, resveratrol combinations, and matrine. NF-κB inhibition is equally recurrent, reported for PEA, taxifolin, piperine, matrine, and nutritional interventions. Upregulation of antioxidant enzymes SOD and CAT, alongside MDA reduction as an oxidative damage marker, is consistently reported as a readout of therapeutic efficacy. Caspase-3 activation and BCL-2 downregulation as indicators of pro-apoptotic activity appear across multiple compound classes. COX-2 suppression and prostaglandin reduction are noted for PEA and Alchemilla alpina, directly relevant to the prostaglandin-driven pain and inflammation characteristic of endometriosis.

**Contradictions and Conflicting Results**

Notable tensions exist within this literature. Gossypol demonstrates promising anti-gynecological cancer and anti-endometriosis activity, yet induces hepatotoxicity through mTOR and TNF-α elevation—the same inflammatory mediators other compounds

# Endometrial Dysfunction in Reproductive Failure: Mechanisms, Controversies, and Translational Opportunities

## Overview

A fundamental question in reproductive medicine concerns how the endometrium achieves and maintains the precise molecular, cellular, and structural state required for successful embryo implantation, and how disruption of this state precipitates reproductive failure across clinically heterogeneous conditions including endometriosis, recurrent implantation failure (RIF), recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), intrauterine adhesions, and adenomyosis. The literature reviewed here addresses this question through converging lines of evidence implicating impaired decidualization, progesterone resistance, immune dysregulation, extracellular matrix pathology, and disrupted epithelial-stromal crosstalk as the dominant mechanistic axes. Critically, these pathways do not operate independently; rather, they form an integrated pathological network in which hormonal insufficiency, epigenetic reprogramming, and structural remodeling mutually reinforce one another to generate a uterine environment hostile to implantation and early pregnancy maintenance.

## Consensus Findings

Progesterone resistance and the consequent failure of decidualization emerge as the most consistently reported mechanisms across the entire body of literature. At the transcriptional level, deficient nuclear localization or activity of FOXO1—mediated through AKT1/NEK2 phosphorylation, Notch pathway downregulation, Meflin-dependent attenuation, or TGF-β1-driven ciliary dysfunction suppressing EP4-PGE2 signaling—prevents adequate induction of canonical decidualization markers including IGFBP1, prolactin, and WNT4. Complementary transcription factors, including HOXA10, HAND2, FOXA2, and RUNX1, are similarly suppressed in endometriosis and RIF endometria through mechanisms ranging from H3K27ac erosion and polycomb repressive complex 2 dysfunction to WT1 downregulation and sumoylation of the progesterone receptor itself. The BMP2-FOXO1 axis, GRB2-mediated EGFR/ERK1/2 and PI3K/AKT signaling, and ZIP10-dependent zinc homeostasis governing GLI1 nuclear localization each provide mechanistically distinct but functionally convergent routes to decidualization failure, underscoring the redundancy and robustness of the pathological state. Importantly, decidualization defects are not confined to endometriosis; they are documented in severe preeclampsia, placenta accreta spectrum, and RPL, implicating a shared pathogenic mechanism across reproductive complications of diverse clinical presentation.

Immune dysregulation constitutes a second axis of strong consensus. Uterine natural killer cells, when appropriately recruited, support spiral artery remodeling and trophoblast invasion through VEGF secretion and matrix metalloprotease activity; conversely, reduced CD56bright uterine NK populations and elevated cytotoxic CD56dimCD16+ fractions are reproducibly associated with implantation failure and pregnancy loss. Progesterone-impaired IL-15-driven NK cell proliferation provides a direct mechanistic link between hormonal insufficiency and immune intolerance. Beyond NK cells, the literature consistently identifies skewed Th1/Th17-to-Treg ratios, CD138+ plasma cell infiltration in chronic endometritis with consequent LIF and HOXA10 suppression and NF-κB overactivation, and dysregulated macrophage polarization as contributors to a pro-inflammatory decidual environment. In endometriosis specifically, single-cell transcriptomics identifies a senescent stromal population secreting CXCL1, CXCL8, and IL-6, alongside impaired macrophage phagocytosis, collectively sustaining lesion persistence through paracrine inflammatory amplification. Spatial transcriptomics further identifies complement and MIF pathway signaling from lesion epithelium to macrophages as an active, spatially organized driver of local immune dysregulation.

Pathological extracellular matrix remodeling and fibrosis represent a third consistent finding. TGF-β superfamily signaling drives fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition and collagen accumulation in endometriosis, adenomyosis, and intrauterine adhesions, with activin A/STAT3, CGRP/RAMP1/ERK, and Rho/ROCK/YAP-TAZ pathways identified as context-specific effectors. Macrophage-derived CCL5, SPP1, and S100A8 promote stromal scarring, while telocyte depletion releases fibroblast inhibitory control. The mechanobiological dimension of this process is increasingly appreciated, as matrix stiffness itself modulates progesterone responsiveness and decidualization competence.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several significant areas of conflict emerge. The relative contribution of macrophage polarization states to endometrial pathology remains contested: whereas hydrosalpinx studies identify M1 macrophage dominance as pathological, platelet-rich plasma therapy increases M1 infiltration while improving outcomes, suggesting that M1 activity is context- and stage-dependent rather than uniformly detrimental. The cellular origin of endometriotic lesions similarly remains debated, with transmesothelial invasion studies emphasizing inherent stromal invasiveness while mesenchymal-epithelial transition observations in co-culture models suggest bidirectional plasticity requiring host-derived cellular contributions. Evidence regarding PANX1 function in decidualization illustrates a further complexity: both knockdown and overexpression impair stromal transformation, indicating that therapeutic intervention must target a precise activity range rather than simple loss-of-function correction. Finally, the two molecularly distinct RIF subtypes—immune-driven (RIF-I) and metabolic-driven (RIF-M)—suggest that studies treating RIF as a homogeneous entity may generate contradictory findings due to patient population differences, an important interpretive caveat for the broader literature.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Despite substantial progress, important mechanistic questions remain unresolved. The spatial organization of senescent cell populations and their paracrine interactions with immune subsets within the implantation window are incompletely characterized, and whether targeted senolysis at specific endometrial niches can restore receptivity without disrupting physiological decidual remodeling is unknown. The contribution of the endometrial microbiome, beyond Lactobacillus dominance metrics, to epigenetic programming
