## Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of papers, estrogen-driven tissue proliferation and progesterone resistance emerge as the most pervasive mechanistic themes. Estrogen, acting primarily through estrogen receptor alpha (ERα), consistently drives aberrant cellular proliferation, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, and inflammatory signaling in ectopic and eutopic endometrial tissue. This is most explicitly demonstrated in adenomyosis, where elevated ERα expression creates local estrogen dominance that overrides normal progesterone-mediated suppression, partly through reduced progesterone receptor-B isoform expression. A parallel theme is the disruption of progesterone responsiveness: in obese women, impaired progesterone-metallothionein signaling increases oxidative DNA damage in the endometrium, while in adenomyosis, compensatory receptor mechanisms involving ERβ and G protein-coupled estrogen receptor (GPER) are simultaneously dysregulated. These convergent findings reinforce the concept that endometriosis and related conditions represent states of estrogen dominance coupled with progesterone insufficiency at the tissue level.

The gut microbiome emerges as a second major regulatory axis, with microbial β-glucuronidase enzymes—particularly from *Lachnospiraceae*, *Ruminococcus gnavus*, and *Roseburia hominis*—reactivating conjugated estradiol and estrone in the intestinal lumen, thereby elevating systemic estrogen bioavailability. This "estrobolome" mechanism provides a microbiome-dependent pathway through which dysbiosis could amplify estrogen-dependent pathology. Complementing this, aromatase overexpression links thyroid dysfunction and other metabolic perturbations to increased local estrogen synthesis within endometrial tissue, further sustaining proliferative signaling independent of ovarian steroidogenesis.

Vascular remodeling and folliculogenesis represent additional mechanistic domains. Cortisol and progesterone exert hormone-specific effects on endometrial microvascular endothelial cell chirality and vascular network architecture, with progesterone increasing vascular branch number while reducing branch length—findings with direct implications for the aberrant neovascularization characteristic of endometriotic lesions. In the ovary, peritoneal endometriosis disrupts folliculogenesis across multiple developmental stages by suppressing KIT ligand expression and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) production in granulosa cells, activating apoptotic cascades through dysregulated BCL-2/BCL-XL ratios, and impairing mTOR and PI3K signaling pathways essential for oocyte maturation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several mechanistic entities appear with notable consistency. The ERα–estrogen axis is repeatedly implicated not only in endometriosis and adenomyosis but also in endometrial polyp formation (via obesity-driven adipose estrogen secretion), endometrial cancer prognosis (where post-diagnosis circulating estrone and estradiol independently predict worse survival outcomes), and even male reproductive function (where ESR1 polymorphisms modulate spermatogenesis). Progesterone resistance, manifested through reduced PR-B expression and impaired downstream signaling, is consistently identified as a therapeutic obstacle in adenomyosis and endometriosis alike. AMH reduction as a biomarker of diminished ovarian reserve in endometriosis is corroborated across multiple studies, with peritoneal disease demonstrated to suppress AMH through direct effects on granulosa cell KITLG and AMHR2 expression. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis—specifically GnRH-mediated coordination of LH and FSH—appears repeatedly as the central regulatory scaffold governing ovarian steroidogenesis, whose dysregulation propagates reproductive pathology downstream.

Inflammation-hormone crosstalk is another consistently reported theme. Prostaglandin E2 promotes decidualization, while prostaglandin F2α drives luteal regression through lysosomal enzyme release. IL-18, operating through NLRP3 inflammasome activation elevated in endometriosis, modulates implantation and trophoblast invasion, linking inflammatory signaling directly to the infertility phenotype. ## Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The assembled literature converges on estrogen-centric mechanisms as the dominant framework governing endometriosis pathogenesis and its broader hormonal consequences. A consistently reported finding across multiple papers is the pathological overexpression of aromatase (CYP19A1) within ectopic endometrial tissue, which drives local estrogen biosynthesis independent of ovarian steroidogenesis. This feed-forward loop—whereby aromatase-derived estradiol stimulates prostaglandin E2 production, which in turn upregulates aromatase activity—is reinforced by evidence from studies on malignant transformation of endometriotic lesions and from mechanistic reviews of steroidogenic enzyme inhibition strategies. Progesterone resistance represents a complementary and equally well-supported theme, with multiple papers documenting impaired progesterone signaling in eutopic and ectopic endometrium, disrupted decidualization markers including IGFBP1 and FOXO1, and reduced endometrial receptivity contributing to infertility.

Androgen signaling emerges as a mechanistically nuanced contributor. Elevated intratissular testosterone concentrations, enhanced 5α-reductase activity, and androgen receptor overexpression in ectopic endometrium collectively promote lesion persistence, while hyperandrogenemia in polycystic ovary syndrome—a condition sharing neuroendocrine dysregulation with endometriosis—further impairs endometrial function through downregulation of implantation-associated genes. The kisspeptin-GnRH axis is recurrently implicated, with reduced KISS1 and KISS1R expression in eutopic endometrium facilitating ectopic implantation despite paradoxically elevated circulating kisspeptin levels, highlighting compartment-specific hormonal dysregulation.

Environmental disruption of steroidogenesis is consistently reported, particularly through aryl hydrocarbon receptor-mediated suppression of CYP11A1, CYP17A1, CYP19A1, and HSD17B1 following dioxin exposure, directly causing endometriosis in exposed populations. Ovarian endometriosis additionally accelerates primordial follicle depletion through mTOR and Hippo pathway activation, reducing anti-Müllerian hormone and estradiol while elevating FSH—a hormonal signature of premature ovarian insufficiency with systemic consequences including osteoporosis and cognitive decline.

Inflammatory pathway crosstalk with hormonal signaling is prominently represented. TGF-β1-mediated fibrosis, elevated IL-6 and TNF-α, NF-κB activation, and vascular endothelial growth factor-driven neuroangiogenesis collectively amplify estrogen-dependent pain and tissue invasion, with nerve growth factor overexpression further contributing to the characteristic neurogenic inflammation underlying dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia.

Some contradictions merit acknowledgment. The role of androgens in endometrial biology is contested: while physiological androgen signaling supports decidualization and protects against apoptosis, supraphysiological levels impair receptivity and promote pathology. Similarly, estrogen's effects on pain perception are reported as both analgesic—through endogenous opioid enhancement and serotonin modulation—and pronociceptive through nociceptor upregulation and neuroinflammation amplification, with the net effect appearing context- and dose-dependent. The relationship between prolactin and reproductive immune modulation also yields conflicting signals, as breastfeeding-associated prolactin elevation protects against high-grade serous ovarian cancer but inversely associates with risk in clear cell tumors, the histotype most strongly linked to endometriosis. These contradictions likely reflect tissue-specific receptor isoform distribution, hormonal milieu interactions, and disease-stage heterogeneity rather than genuine biological inconsistency, underscoring the necessity of context-sensitive interpretation when translating hormonal research into therapeutic strategies for endometriosis.

# Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

## Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes: estrogen as a pleiotropic regulator of reproductive and systemic physiology, the consequences of estrogen deficiency or excess across multiple organ systems, and the dysregulation of steroid hormone synthesis and signaling as a shared pathophysiological basis for endometriosis and related conditions. Estrogen's centrality is reinforced across nearly every paper, whether through its cardioprotective, neuroprotective, immunomodulatory, or proliferative roles. A recurrent mechanistic thread is the CYP19A1-encoded aromatase enzyme, which governs local androgen-to-estrogen conversion in adipose tissue, endometrium, and brain, with dysregulated aromatase activity implicated in endometriosis, leiomyoma, and lipedema pathogenesis. Several papers independently highlight intracrine estrogen production—local steroidogenesis occurring outside the gonads—as a mechanism sustaining hyperestrogenic tissue microenvironments even during systemic estrogen decline, particularly relevant to endometriotic lesion persistence. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis emerges as a central regulatory hub, with GnRH, FSH, and LH dynamics governing downstream steroidogenesis, follicular development, endometrial cycling, and reproductive outcomes. Disruption of this axis, whether by glucocorticoids, viral infection, pituitary pathology, or exercise modality, produces cascading hormonal imbalances with significant reproductive and metabolic consequences.

Progesterone resistance constitutes another dominant theme, appearing across discussions of endometriosis organoids, endometrial cancer, perimenopausal bleeding, and implantation failure. Specifically, downregulation of progesterone receptor-B in endometriosis tissue is linked to epigenetic methylation changes, establishing a molecular basis for the impaired decidualization and inadequate secretory transformation characteristic of endometriotic endometrium. This progesterone insensitivity contrasts with the apparent progesterone responsiveness exploited therapeutically in endometrial cancer, where progestin-based regimens achieve complete response in up to 80% of early-stage cases by downregulating estrogen receptor signaling and modulating apoptotic pathways including p21 and Bcl-2.

Estrogen receptor subtype biology receives substantial attention, with multiple papers distinguishing the proliferative, pro-inflammatory actions of estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) from the anti-proliferative, neuroprotective, and anti-fibrotic effects of estrogen receptor beta (ERβ). This functional dichotomy has direct therapeutic implications: selective ERβ agonists and phytoestrogens such as daidzein and genistein are proposed as safer alternatives to conventional hormone replacement by preserving beneficial estrogenic effects while avoiding ERα-mediated cancer promotion. Similarly, estetrol's selective nuclear ERα activation without membrane ERα engagement is highlighted as conferring a differentiation-promoting rather than proliferation-promoting profile, with potential applications in endometriosis management.

The gut microbiome emerges as an underappreciated regulator of systemic estrogen availability. Bacterial β-glucuronidase enzymes, particularly from Steroidobacter denitrificans and Comamonas testosteroni, deconjugate excreted estrogens in the intestinal lumen, reactivating them for enterohepatic recirculation. Dysbiosis elevating this enzymatic activity amplifies circulating bioactive estrogen, potentially contributing to endometriosis disease burden—a mechanism consistent with clinical observations linking dysbiosis to endometriosis severity. The gut also engages in local steroidogenesis via expressed P450scc (Cyp11a1), 3β-HSD, and related enzymes, with immune activation through NF-κB and TNF-α upregulating steroidogenic enzyme expression via the transcription factor LRH-1, connecting inflammatory signaling to local hormone production relevant to endometriosis-associated inflammation.

## Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers assembled in this cluster address hormonal regulation across a broad spectrum of gynecological and reproductive conditions, with recurrent mechanistic emphasis on the estrogen–progesterone axis, hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis dysregulation, and the downstream inflammatory and metabolic consequences of hormonal imbalance. Although only a subset of papers addresses endometriosis directly, the collective literature converges on several principles highly relevant to endometriotic pathophysiology: unopposed estrogen signaling as a driver of aberrant endometrial proliferation, progesterone resistance and insufficiency as permissive factors for lesion persistence, and chronic inflammation mediated by prostaglandins, cytokines, and oxidative stress as amplifiers of disease.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Unopposed or excessive estrogen exposure emerges as the most uniformly reported pathogenic mechanism across conditions sharing endometriosis-relevant biology. Multiple papers document that estrogen drives endometrial cell proliferation through PI3K-AKT signaling, PTEN loss, and upregulation of aromatase activity, while progesterone normally counteracts these effects by inducing differentiation, promoting apoptosis via FOXO1 and IGFBP-1, and suppressing estrogenic transcriptional programs. Conditions that chronically disrupt this balance—obesity (through adipose aromatization of androgens), nulliparity, chronic anovulation, polycystic ovary syndrome, and perimenopause—consistently appear as risk amplifiers across endometrial cancer, hyperplasia, uterine fibroids, and endometriosis itself. Sex hormone-binding globulin emerges repeatedly as a critical modulator of bioavailable estrogen and androgen, with BMI-dependent reductions in SHBG creating a hormonal milieu permissive to estrogen-driven pathology.

The role of progesterone resistance receives particular attention in the context of endometriosis pain management. One study demonstrates that peritoneal fluid estrogen and progesterone concentrations directly correlate with lesion activity, and that suppression of ovarian steroidogenesis—via oral contraceptives, progestogen monotherapy, or GnRH agonists and antagonists—reduces pain primarily through hypoestrogenic rather than progestogenic mechanisms. Individual lesion heterogeneity in aromatase activity and progesterone receptor expression is explicitly identified as a determinant of variable therapeutic response, a finding with direct implications for personalized treatment selection. GnRH-based suppression appears consistently effective across papers examining endometriosis pain, ovarian hyperstimulation risk reduction, and progestogen hypersensitivity management, underscoring the centrality of HPO axis suppression as a therapeutic strategy.

Inflammatory amplification of hormonal dysregulation represents a second major mechanistic theme. Cyclooxygenase-2 upregulation, prostaglandin F2α excess, and pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and vascular endothelial growth factor are reported across studies examining endometriosis, primary dysmenorrhea, and adenomyosis. These mediators perpetuate a self-reinforcing cycle in which estrogen induces COX-2 expression and prostaglandin synthesis, prostaglandins stimulate further local estrogen production via aromatase induction, and the resulting inflammatory milieu promotes lesion survival, angiogenesis, and pain sensitization. One study examining Graptophyllum pictum demonstrates that simultaneous targeting of COX-2, cytokine production, and oxidative stress reduces implant volume, pain, and restores follicular development in a rodent endometriosis model, illustrating the therapeutic leverage available at this inflammatory-hormonal interface.

The HPO axis and its neuroendocrine regulation feature prominently across papers addressing ovarian reserve, ovulatory dysfunction, athletic menstrual dysfunction, and acupuncture-based interventions. Diminished ovarian reserve, luteinized unruptured follicle syndrome, and ovulatory dysfunction all converge on disrupted HPO axis signaling, with downstream reductions in estradiol, luteinizing hormone surge amplitude, and progesterone production. # Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Research Findings

## Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 articles, estrogen-dependent signaling emerges as the central organizing principle, with estrogen production, receptor expression, and downstream proliferative effects appearing as consistent mechanistic threads linking endometriosis to a broader landscape of reproductive and metabolic pathology. A second dominant theme is the bidirectional interplay between hormonal dysregulation and systemic metabolic dysfunction, with obesity, insulin resistance, and adipose tissue aromatization amplifying estrogenic stimulation of hormone-sensitive tissues including ectopic endometrium. A third recurrent theme concerns the disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis homeostasis—whether by circadian disruption, oxidative stress, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, or chronic psychological stress—as a convergent pathway impairing ovarian function and exacerbating endometriosis-associated pathology.

## Consistently Reported Findings

Estrogen's role in promoting endometrial cell proliferation, lesion establishment, and vascular recruitment is reported across multiple papers (PMC12686953, PMC12650026, PMC12648777, PMC12698538). Local estrogen biosynthesis via aromatase (CYP19A1) within ectopic lesions and adipose tissue is identified as a self-amplifying mechanism sustaining disease independent of gonadal output (PMC12575157, PMC12656771). The neuroendocrine–immune axis is consistently implicated, with elevated neuropeptides including substance P, brain-derived neurotrophic factor, and vasoactive intestinal peptide correlating with dysmenorrhea severity and lesion neurogenesis, modulated downstream by estrogen signaling (PMC12650026). Progesterone resistance, operationalized as impaired progesterone receptor responsiveness in ectopic tissue, is a recurrently implied mechanism underlying failed suppression of lesion growth despite therapeutic intervention. Anti-Müllerian hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone serve as the most consistently cited endocrine biomarkers of ovarian reserve compromise in endometriosis and associated iatrogenic conditions (PMC12609317, PMC12606964, PMC12639398, PMC12694999). The PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK signaling cascades appear repeatedly as intracellular mediators of estrogen- and growth factor-driven proliferation (PMC12587835, PMC12653611, PMC12698538). Intergenerational hormonal programming is highlighted in the finding that daughters of women with endometriosis exhibit elevated intrauterine estradiol-to-androgen ratios, potentially encoding epigenetic susceptibility through altered 11β-HSD2 activity (PMC12648777). Circadian disruption and shift work are independently associated with menstrual irregularity, reduced ovarian follicle counts, lower progesterone levels, and a 30–40% higher incidence of endometriosis, implicating melatonin-LH axis desynchronization as a contributing pathway (PMC12713819). Physical activity confers protective effects against estrogen-dependent gynecologic pathology by reducing circulating free estrogens, IGF-1, and adiposity (PMC12595962). GnRH agonists, progestins, and aromatase inhibitors are cited across multiple papers as reducing estrogen-dependent lesion activity and nerve fiber density, validating estrogen suppression as the primary therapeutic strategy (PMC12650026, PMC12686953).

## Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable tensions emerge across the corpus. Bilateral oophorectomy reduces breast cancer risk by suppressing estrogen yet simultaneously accelerates bone loss and cardiovascular disease through the same hormonal depletion (PMC12705852, PMC12691220), illustrating that estrogen's systemic role resists simple therapeutic framing. Regarding menopausal hormone therapy, transdermal estradiol with micronized progesterone is presented as having a favorable safety profile and cardiovascular benefit when initiated early (PMC12652300), whereas oral conjugated equine estrogens are associated with substantially elevated thrombotic and stroke risk (PMC12652158), underscoring that formulation-specific effects are not always distinguished in epidemiological analyses conflating hormone therapy

## Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The collected literature converges on several interconnected themes: the opposing yet context-dependent actions of estrogen and progesterone across reproductive and non-reproductive tissues; the centrality of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis in orchestrating hormonal cascades; and the intimate crosstalk between sex hormones, inflammation, and immune regulation. Estrogen consistently emerges as a proliferative and pro-inflammatory driver across multiple tissue contexts—endometrium, ovary, bone, vasculature, and immune compartments—while progesterone generally exerts antiproliferative and immunomodulatory countereffects, though its role is demonstrably context-dependent and occasionally paradoxical.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Unopposed estrogen exposure is recurrently implicated in pathological endometrial proliferation, driving hyperplasia and carcinoma risk through upregulation of mitogenic signaling including PI3K/AKT/mTOR, IGF-1, and Wnt/β-catenin pathways. Aromatase-mediated local estrogen biosynthesis, amplified by obesity-driven adipose aromatization and reduced sex hormone-binding globulin, represents a consistently reported mechanism linking metabolic dysfunction to estrogen-dependent diseases including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and endometrial carcinoma. Progesterone receptor signaling is repeatedly identified as protective against endometrial malignancy, with progestin-based therapies exploiting this pathway therapeutically. Vitamin D deficiency is independently associated with endometriosis, infertility, and polycystic ovarian syndrome across multiple papers, with VDR signaling suppressing pro-inflammatory Th1/Th17 responses and promoting regulatory T cell activity—mechanisms directly relevant to endometriosis pathogenesis. Immune dysregulation, particularly disrupted Treg/Th17 balance modulated by progesterone, recurs as a mechanistic theme in implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and endometriosis-associated infertility.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable tensions emerge across the literature. Progesterone's role in fibroids is explicitly contradictory: while traditionally considered antiproliferative, multiple papers identify progesterone as actively promoting fibroid cell proliferation, collagen deposition, and extracellular matrix accumulation, challenging the conventional estrogen-centric model of fibroid pathogenesis. Similarly, progesterone paradoxically both enhances overall sleep quality and acutely increases wakefulness during rapid luteal-phase elevation. Estrogen's immunological effects are inconsistently directional depending on receptor subtype: ERα deletion decreases pro-inflammatory Th1 responses, whereas ERβ activation increases IL-17 and IL-21 production, complicating interpretations of estrogen as uniformly immunosuppressive or immunostimulatory. These contradictions likely reflect tissue specificity, receptor subtype distribution, hormonal concentration thresholds, and interaction with local microenvironmental factors, underscoring the need for mechanistically precise, context-aware frameworks when evaluating hormonal therapies for endometriosis and related conditions.

## Hormonal Regulation in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Cluster Findings

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The 39 articles in this cluster collectively illuminate the breadth of hormonal regulatory mechanisms relevant to endometriosis, spanning estrogen-progesterone signaling, gonadotropin axis dynamics, epigenetic control, circadian coordination, and immune-endocrine crosstalk. A dominant and recurrent theme is the central role of estrogen as a pleiotropic driver of tissue proliferation, inflammation, and systemic comorbidity. Across multiple organ systems—uterus, bone, brain, airways, gastrointestinal tract, and cornea—estrogen emerges as a critical modulator whose dysregulation underlies pathological states directly relevant to endometriosis and its associated conditions.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Estrogen's pro-proliferative and pro-inflammatory effects are among the most consistently documented observations. Multiple papers describe estrogen-driven activation of downstream targets including RANKL-mediated osteoclastogenesis, cyclooxygenase-dependent AKT phosphorylation in endometrial stroma, and enhancement of TRPV1/TRPA1 sensory channel activity—mechanisms that collectively link estrogen excess or dysregulation to tissue remodeling, pain sensitization, and impaired decidualization. The latter is particularly pertinent: defective decidualization in endometriosis, driven by elevated AKT signaling, was shown to be partially correctable through cyclooxygenase inhibition, establishing a mechanistic rationale for aspirin as an adjunct therapy.

Progesterone resistance or antagonism represents a complementary and equally prominent theme. Papers addressing endometrial cancer risk, leiomyoma biology, and meningioma growth consistently describe progesterone as a counterbalance to unopposed estrogen-driven proliferation, mediated through progesterone receptor signaling and downstream transcriptional regulation of genes including HOXA10. Disruption of this balance—whether through receptor downregulation, pharmacological antagonism, or epigenetic silencing—was repeatedly associated with pathological proliferation. Notably, the mifepristone study provided reassurance that short-term progesterone receptor blockade does not induce endometrial carcinogenic molecular signatures, introducing nuance into assumptions about progesterone receptor antagonism.

The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis and its gonadotropin components—FSH, LH, and GnRH—featured prominently as regulatory nodes whose perturbation underlies both endometriosis pathophysiology and its treatment complications. GnRH agonist-mediated ovarian suppression for breast cancer management and endometriosis treatment was shown to carry risks of ovarian escape, particularly in younger, higher-BMI patients, while GnRH antagonists avoid the initial estrogen flare. The critical role of LH in follicular maturation and oocyte quality was independently confirmed across IVF studies, with both insufficient and excessive LH associated with diminished reproductive outcomes. Chronopharmacological optimization of oral contraceptives—exploiting circadian LH peaks for dosing—demonstrated that a 67% reduction in total hormone exposure is achievable through timing alignment, underscoring the physiological significance of pulsatile gonadotropin rhythms.

Epigenetic regulation, particularly DNA methylation mediated by DNMT and TET enzyme families, was identified as a fundamental mechanism controlling steroidogenesis, folliculogenesis, and uterine remodeling. Aberrant methylation patterns were consistently associated with endometriosis, PCOS, and endometrial cancer, positioning the methylome as both a pathological contributor and a potential biomarker of disease state.

Circadian rhythm disruption emerged as a mechanistically coherent contributor to reproductive dysfunction. Reduced Rev-erbα expression following shift work or circadian misalignment impaired folliculogenesis, progesterone secretion, and oocyte maturation in animal models, while pharmacological Rev-erbα activation restored fertility. Complementarily, electroacupuncture was shown to restore endometrial receptivity in controlled ovarian hyperstimulation models by activating arginine vasopressin neurons in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, re-establishing circadian coordination of the HPO axis and endometrial clock genes including BMAL1 and CLOCK.

# Artificial Intelligence Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: Current Evidence, Controversies, and Translational Prospects

## Overview

Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, yet the mean diagnostic delay persists at 6–8 years, driven by the historical requirement for laparoscopic confirmation and the limited sensitivity of conventional non-invasive modalities. The core research question animating this literature is whether artificial intelligence—applied across imaging interpretation, molecular biomarker discovery, and multimodal data integration—can meaningfully compress this diagnostic gap while maintaining or exceeding the accuracy of surgical verification. This body of work encompasses convolutional neural networks (CNNs) applied to laparoscopic video and gynecological imaging, ensemble machine learning architectures integrating clinical and molecular features, deep learning-augmented transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and emerging platforms leveraging proteomics, epigenomics, and spectroscopy as AI-compatible diagnostic substrates.

## Consensus Findings

The most consistently replicated finding across this literature is that AI-driven approaches substantially outperform conventional diagnostic methods in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for endometriosis detection. Deep learning architectures applied to MRI achieved diagnostic accuracy of 94.3% versus 63.2% for conventional radiologist interpretation in ovarian endometriosis, while CNN-based TVUS analysis produced area under the curve (AUC) values exceeding 0.99 for adnexal pathology discrimination. Object detection frameworks including FasterRCNN and Mask R-CNN, trained on laparoscopic video datasets such as GLENDA, achieved lesion detection precision of 0.98 with mean average precision of 0.82, while ResNet50 reported approximately 91% accuracy for intraoperative endometriosis identification. Self-configuring segmentation architectures such as nnU-Net achieved Dice similarity coefficients exceeding 0.96 on three-dimensional TVUS datasets, and multimodal fusion models integrating TVUS, MRI parameters including the T2 dark spot sign and T1 fat-saturation sequences, and CA-125 consistently achieved AUC values of 0.83–0.92. Ensemble and gradient boosting methods—particularly XGBoost and random forest—reproducibly outperform single-algorithm approaches when integrating heterogeneous clinical, imaging, and molecular data, with SHAP-based explainability analyses consistently identifying baseline clinical severity, hormonal markers, and patient age as dominant predictive features. Non-invasive molecular platforms exhibit parallel advances: the salivary microRNA-based Endotest® platform demonstrated diagnostic accuracy comparable to laparoscopy, plasma 5-hydroxymethylcytosine profiling achieved AUC of 0.91 with a 10-marker liquid biopsy signature surpassing CA-125 and CA-199, and plasma proteomic neural network models identified progestagen-associated endometrial protein (PAEP) as a disease-specific biomarker. Epigenetic DNA methylation panels distinguish endometriosis from malignancy with 99.9% specificity in liquid biopsy contexts, and antibody-based biosensing targeting high-mobility group box protein 1 (HMGB-1) in menstrual effluent demonstrates the diagnostic potential of molecularly specific, sample-efficient platforms.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Despite the consistency of performance metrics, substantive contradictions pervade this literature. A fundamental tension exists between reported diagnostic accuracy and methodological rigor: AI ultrasound models report mean accuracies exceeding 92%, yet reviewers across multiple groups identify single-institution training datasets, absence of prospective external validation, and overfitting as pervasive limitations undermining generalizability. The Hôtel-Dieu MRI classification study simultaneously reports AI-enhanced diagnostic improvement while identifying systematic underestimation of uterosacral ligament involvement for early-stage lesions, illustrating that algorithmic amplification of modality-inherent limitations remains unresolved. Interobserver variability in ultrasound image quality scoring—with intraclass correlation coefficients as low as 0.336 between professional groups—represents a critical bottleneck for training data integrity that contradicts high reported model performance. The salivary miRNA approach faces clinical skepticism from 84.4% of surveyed gynecologists, with legitimate concerns regarding population-level miRNA variability, overdiagnosis risk, and absence of phenotype-specific validation. Deep learning models, while demonstrating superior peak accuracy, exhibit substantial cross-site performance variability attributable to equipment heterogeneity and operator technique, in contrast to the more stable but lower-performing traditional machine learning approaches—an unresolved accuracy-generalizability trade-off. Furthermore, CNN-LSTM architectures applied to wearable pain-monitoring data underperformed simpler random forest approaches, challenging the assumption that architectural complexity invariably confers diagnostic advantage. GPT-4o demonstrated a 6.25-fold reduction in accuracy for pelvic imaging relative to other anatomical regions with identified hallucination tendencies, introducing an important caveat against uncritical deployment of large language models in gynecological imaging contexts.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Several important mechanistic and methodological unknowns persist. AI training datasets systematically underrepresent racially, socioeconomically, and geographically diverse populations, raising the prospect that algorithmic models may perpetuate rather than correct the diagnostic disparities already characterizing endometriosis care; however, empirical quantification of this bias in endometriosis-specific models is currently absent. The APP overexpression–HOXD9 upregulation axis identified through 5-hydroxymethylcytosine profiling as a regulator of endometrial cell migration and fibrosis represents a mechanistically specific finding requiring independent replication. Batch effect correction in high-throughput sequencing studies remains methodologically inconsistent, with anticlustering approaches outperforming established tools in 78–84% of variables, suggesting that reported AI performance metrics may be vulnerable to uncorrected technical confounding. The biological basis for endometriosis-specific spectroscopic signatures at wavelengths associated with lipid and amide structural features (2371, 1990, 1612, and 1095 nm) is not fully characterized at the molecular level. Digital twin technology for personalized endometriosis monitoring and multi-class machine learning architectures correcting for outcome conflation in electronic health record models are proposed but remain empirically underdeveloped. No prospective outcome data confirm that AI-assisted ultrasound training translates to improved real-world diagnostic rates in clinical practice

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

The reviewed literature collectively establishes artificial intelligence as an increasingly integral tool in endometriosis diagnosis and management, operating primarily through imaging enhancement, classification systems, and multimodal data integration. Several dominant themes emerge: the application of machine learning to ultrasound and MRI interpretation, the development of standardized disease classification frameworks, and the integration of AI with non-invasive biomarker discovery.

The most consistently reported finding across papers is the substantial diagnostic improvement achieved when AI algorithms are applied to gynecological imaging. Machine learning approaches—particularly convolutional neural networks and fuzzy/hard C-means algorithms—demonstrate markedly superior performance over conventional imaging alone for identifying endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis, with one study reporting diagnostic accuracy of 94.3% versus 63.2% for MRI-based ovarian endometriosis detection. Deep learning models applied to ultrasound similarly achieved area under the curve values exceeding 0.99 in distinguishing endometriosis-related adnexal pathology from other lesions. Complementing these imaging advances, the development of structured classification systems such as the Hôtel-Dieu MRI-based staging for uterosacral ligament involvement and the Visual-Numeric Endometriosis Scoring System (VNESS) reflects a parallel drive toward standardized, reproducible disease characterization that can serve as reliable training data for AI pipelines.

Non-invasive biomarker integration represents another recurrent theme, with proteomic, transcriptomic, and hemostatic markers—including the identification of a prothrombotic state in endometriosis patients via T-TAS functional hemostasis assay—suggesting that AI-assisted multiomics profiling could improve diagnostic specificity beyond imaging alone. Digital twin technology is additionally proposed as an emerging framework for personalized endometriosis monitoring, integrating real-time physiological data with predictive modeling.

A notable contradiction exists regarding imaging reliability: while AI substantially improves MRI diagnostic accuracy, the Hôtel-Dieu classification study simultaneously identified systematic underestimation of uterosacral ligament involvement by MRI, particularly for early-stage lesions. This tension between algorithm-enhanced performance and inherent modality limitations remains unresolved. Furthermore, interobserver variability in ultrasound image quality scoring—with poor agreement between professional groups (ICC 0.336)—represents a critical bottleneck for AI training data quality, potentially undermining model generalizability despite high reported performance metrics. Collectively, these findings support AI integration into endometriosis diagnostics while underscoring that standardization of imaging protocols, labeling practices, and classification systems must advance concurrently with algorithmic development.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis

Across this cluster of papers, a dominant and recurrent theme is the application of machine learning and deep learning architectures to improve the accuracy, non-invasiveness, and efficiency of endometriosis diagnosis and related reproductive pathology assessment. Multiple papers converge on the utility of convolutional neural networks—including ResNet50, Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN, and U-Net variants—for automated lesion detection and organ segmentation during laparoscopic imaging, with ResNet50 achieving reported accuracies of approximately 91% for endometriosis detection in surgical video datasets such as GLENDA. Complementing surgical applications, AI-assisted ultrasound analysis using deep learning models demonstrated diagnostic accuracies exceeding 95% for pelvic pathology evaluation, including Pouch of Douglas assessment and ovarian endometrioma characterization.

A consistently reported finding across multiple papers is that ensemble and gradient boosting methods—particularly random forest and XGBoost architectures—outperform single-algorithm approaches when integrating heterogeneous clinical, imaging, and molecular data. This was observed both in endometriosis-adjacent prediction tasks (IVF outcome prediction, miscarriage risk stratification) and in broader gynecological applications. Feature importance analyses repeatedly identified hormonal markers, patient age, and imaging-derived parameters as the strongest predictors across models.

Several papers highlight non-invasive biomarker discovery as a parallel diagnostic strategy. A salivary microRNA panel of 109 distinct miRNAs underpins the commercial Endotest®, achieving diagnostic accuracy comparable to laparoscopy, while single-cell transcriptomic atlasing of endometrial tissue was identified as capable of capturing molecular disease hallmarks missed by bulk profiling. Machine learning models predicting endometriosis from questionnaire responses and clinical data further reinforce the shift toward non-surgical diagnostic pathways.

Contradictions and tensions are nonetheless evident. Despite high reported technical performance, the salivary miRNA approach faces substantial clinical skepticism, with 84.4% of surveyed gynecologists doubting it can replace histological and ultrasound-based diagnosis, and significant concerns raised regarding population-level miRNA variability, overdiagnosis risk, and absence of phenotype-specific external validation. Similarly, while AI-assisted surgical guidance systems achieved inter-rater agreement with surgeons of 82–93% in cholecystectomy contexts—directly analogous to endometriosis laparoscopy—real-world implementation remains limited by dataset heterogeneity and insufficient prospective validation. A further methodological concern raised across papers is outcome conflation in single-outcome machine learning models trained on electronic health records, where shared confounders reduce disease specificity; multi-class architectures are proposed as a corrective strategy but remain underutilized in endometriosis-specific AI development. Collectively, these papers support AI as a transformative but incompletely validated adjunct to endometriosis diagnosis, with reproducibility, generalizability, and clinical integration representing the most pressing unresolved challenges.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across the 50 papers reviewed, several converging themes emerge regarding AI applications in endometriosis diagnosis and management. Deep learning architectures—particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs), object detection frameworks such as FasterRCNN and YOLOv9, and ensemble methods—constitute the predominant computational approaches applied to endometriosis-related diagnostic challenges. These methods are most consistently deployed for image-based tasks, including automated lesion detection in laparoscopic video frames, segmentation of endometriotic foci from transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) and MRI, and differentiation of endometriosis-associated pathologies such as adenomyosis and ovarian cancer. A secondary but prominent theme involves multimodal data integration, wherein AI models combine imaging features with clinical parameters, tumor markers (e.g., CA-125), and molecular signatures to improve diagnostic specificity. Machine learning techniques are additionally applied to pelvic floor dysfunction assessment via surface electromyography, plasma proteomic risk stratification identifying PAEP as a unique endometriosis biomarker, and digital health platforms supporting symptom-based self-screening in reproductive-age women.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The most consistently reported finding across these papers is that AI-driven approaches substantially outperform conventional diagnostic methods in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for detecting endometriosis and related conditions. FasterRCNN achieved precision of 0.98 and mean average precision of 0.82 for laparoscopic lesion detection, while CNN ensemble models demonstrated 91% sensitivity and 92% specificity for ovarian cancer identification in endometriosis patients. Multimodal fusion models incorporating TVUS, MRI markers (T2 dark spot sign, T1 fat-saturation sequences), and CA-125 consistently achieved area under the curve values of 0.83–0.92. AI integration with adenomyosis imaging facilitated automated measurement of junctional zone thickness and differentiation from uterine fibroids with superior speed relative to conventional analysis. Radiomics-based approaches and deep learning applied to pelvic floor sEMG data similarly demonstrated diagnostic improvements, with the AI-Diagnostician-PFD model achieving an AUC of approximately 0.81. Plasma proteomic neural network models identified PAEP as a disease-specific endometriosis biomarker while achieving C-index improvements of 0.02–0.08 over traditional linear models. Digital self-screening tools on women's health platforms demonstrated particular uptake among women aged 25–44, suggesting AI-adjacent technologies can reduce the historically reported diagnostic delay of 4–11 years.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Despite general agreement on AI superiority, notable contradictions exist across the literature. Several papers report that deep learning models, including CNN-LSTM architectures applied to wearable pain-monitoring data, underperformed simpler ensemble approaches such as Random Forest, challenging the assumption that architectural complexity invariably confers diagnostic advantage. Conflicting evidence also surrounds dataset methodology: while Copy-Paste augmentation and stratified splitting improved FasterRCNN performance, other studies highlighted that small, institution-specific training datasets cause overfitting and poor external generalizability—a limitation not uniformly addressed across the reviewed works. A further tension concerns multimodal integration; although fusion models generally improve performance, some papers caution that insufficient external validation across diverse populations limits clinical translation of even high-performing models. Finally, while TVUS is widely endorsed as the primary non-invasive modality for endometriosis-associated lesion assessment, its combination with MRI is variably recommended, with disagreement regarding which imaging sequences and AI architectures optimally complement one another in clinically heterogeneous patient populations.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis of Recent Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of articles, several intersecting themes emerge around the application of artificial intelligence and advanced computational methods to endometriosis diagnosis and characterization. A primary focus involves leveraging machine learning and deep learning architectures—particularly convolutional neural networks, graph convolutional networks, and transformer-based models—to improve diagnostic accuracy where conventional clinical tools remain insufficient. Complementary to algorithmic development, multiple studies emphasize biomarker discovery through proteomics, metabolomics, spectroscopy, and epigenomics as substrates upon which AI classification can operate. A secondary but consistent theme concerns non-invasive or minimally invasive diagnostic modalities, including transvaginal ultrasound with AI augmentation, liquid biopsy with methylation-based panels, lateral flow immunoassays, and Raman spectroscopy of biological fluids.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several findings recur with notable consistency. Multi-omics integration frameworks outperform single-modality approaches: the DRIVE-KG knowledge graph achieved superior endometriosis patient classification (AUPRC 0.738 versus 0.679 for conventional genetic risk scores) by integrating genomic, proteomic, and phenotypic data, implicating shared pathways involving body mass index, lipid metabolism, and depressive disorders. Epigenetic signatures, specifically DNA methylation panels, demonstrated high specificity for distinguishing endometriosis from malignancy in liquid biopsy contexts, correctly classifying endometriosis as non-tumoral with 99.9% accuracy. Antibody-based biosensing targeting HMGB-1 in menstrual effluent and cell surface proteomics optimized for low-input endometrial tissue both highlight the diagnostic potential of molecularly specific, sample-efficient platforms. Transvaginal ultrasound combined with AI consistently emerges as the preferred first-line imaging approach, with color Doppler and AI-enhanced interpretation improving detection of deep infiltrating and rectosigmoid lesions. GPT-4o, however, demonstrated substantially reduced accuracy for pelvic imaging specifically, performing 6.25-fold worse than for other anatomical regions and exhibiting hallucination tendencies, introducing an important caveat regarding large language model reliability in this domain.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Tensions exist between the demonstrated promise of individual AI tools and their readiness for clinical deployment. While the Gynecological AI Diagnostics model reportedly achieved 87% diagnostic accuracy for pelvic pain disorders, GPT-4o showed poor pelvic performance and susceptibility to suggestion-induced errors, suggesting that AI diagnostic capability is highly architecture- and task-dependent rather than generalizable. Furthermore, digital fertility trackers—while facilitating endometriosis symptom documentation—were found to produce false confidence and inappropriate clinical decision delegation when algorithms failed to accommodate irregular cycles characteristic of the condition, highlighting a disconnect between technological accessibility and clinical validity. Batch effect correction methods for high-throughput endometriosis sequencing studies similarly reveal that methodological rigor in upstream data handling remains inconsistent across the field, with anticlustering approaches outperforming established tools in 78–84% of variables, indicating that reported AI performance metrics may be vulnerable to uncorrected technical confounding. Collectively, these contradictions underscore that diagnostic AI in endometriosis remains heterogeneous in both methodological quality and clinical translation readiness.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

Across the reviewed literature, several convergent themes emerge regarding artificial intelligence applications in endometriosis diagnosis, though the corpus contains substantial heterogeneity reflecting the breadth of adjacent biomedical research.

**Dominant Themes and Mechanisms**

The most consistently represented theme is the application of AI-enhanced imaging for endometriosis detection and characterization. Deep learning architectures, particularly convolutional neural networks and self-configuring frameworks such as nnU-Net, enable automated segmentation of pelvic structures on transvaginal ultrasound and MRI, with nnU-Net achieving Dice scores exceeding 0.96 on three-dimensional TVUS datasets. MRI-based approaches are endorsed for detecting deep infiltrating endometriosis in anatomically challenging locations, including uterosacral ligaments and parametrial regions, where operator-dependent ultrasound has limited precision. Complementing imaging, quantitative MRI techniques including T1/T2 mapping, multiparametric elastography, and apparent diffusion coefficient measurements are emerging as tools to characterize fibrosis and inflammation central to endometriotic pathology.

A second major theme involves noninvasive molecular diagnostics. Multi-omics platforms integrating microRNA signatures, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics are being leveraged to develop blood- and tissue-based biomarkers that circumvent the diagnostic delay associated with laparoscopy. MicroRNA assays such as DotEndo and platforms like Orion demonstrate capacity for noninvasive disease subtype prediction.

**Consistently Reported Findings**

Machine learning models integrating multimodal data consistently outperform single-modality approaches. XGBoost and random forest algorithms demonstrate superior discriminative performance across gynecologic prediction tasks. Radiofrequency-based sensors and wearable continuous monitoring technologies have been specifically tested in endometriosis populations, suggesting interest in real-time physiological surveillance. The deep-pelvic endometriosis index emerged as the most externally validated radiological classification, predicting operative time, hospital stay, and postoperative complications.

**Contradictions and Limitations**

A notable tension exists between reported diagnostic accuracies and methodological rigor. AI ultrasound models for gynecologic pathology report mean accuracies exceeding 92%, yet reviewers consistently identify single-institution datasets, absence of prospective external validation, and overfitting as pervasive limitations undermining generalizability. Furthermore, AI training datasets systematically underrepresent diverse demographic groups, risking exacerbation of existing diagnostic disparities in endometriosis, a condition already characterized by prolonged diagnostic delay across racial and socioeconomic groups. Standardized validation frameworks such as TRIPOD-AI are advocated but inconsistently applied, representing a critical unresolved methodological gap requiring prospective multicenter resolution.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across the reviewed literature, a convergent theme emerges: artificial intelligence is being systematically applied to overcome longstanding diagnostic limitations in endometriosis, principally the invasiveness and delay associated with laparoscopic confirmation. The predominant diagnostic modalities being augmented by AI are transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), both of which have achieved guideline-level recognition as primary non-invasive tools. The mean diagnostic delay of 6.8 years and the historical exposure of approximately one-third of laparoscopy patients to unnecessary surgery provide the clinical imperative driving this technological transition. Alongside imaging-based approaches, AI is being applied to spectroscopic biomarker identification, histopathological classification, and multimodal clinical prediction.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Deep learning architectures, particularly convolutional neural networks and Vision Transformer-based models, consistently outperformed traditional machine learning algorithms in image-based classification tasks, achieving accuracies of 0.89–0.93 and AUC values approaching 0.90 on TVUS datasets. Traditional machine learning approaches—including Random Forest and Support Vector Machines—demonstrated AUC values of 0.75–0.80 but proved more effective for non-imaging predictive and risk-stratification tasks. Infrared spectroscopy combined with one-class genetic algorithm optimization identified endometriosis-specific spectroscopic signatures at characteristic wavelengths (2371, 1990, 1612, and 1095 nm) associated with lipid and amide structural features, establishing a biochemically grounded non-invasive diagnostic avenue. MRI quantitative parameters, including IVIM-derived diffusion values and T2 mapping, further demonstrated utility in distinguishing malignant transformation of endometriosis (endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer), with a combined model achieving AUC = 0.874. Intraoperative ultrasound was consistently identified as enhancing complete lesion identification during endometriosis excision surgery. Complementary to imaging, menstrual blood-based diagnostics—leveraging digital droplet ELISA and next-generation sequencing—and fertility preservation registries both reported endometriosis as an increasingly recognized indication warranting specialized clinical pathways.

Multimodal machine learning frameworks integrating radiomics with deep feature extraction (e.g., ResNet-50 and Vision Transformers) achieved superior segmentation performance (Dice similarity coefficient 96.2%) and prognostic prediction (concordance index 0.847) for ovarian tumors including endometriomas, suggesting that feature complementarity is a robust principle across gynecologic imaging tasks. Explainable AI approaches, including SHAP analysis applied to XGBoost models, consistently identified baseline clinical severity as the dominant predictor in pelvic pain–related outcomes, reinforcing the translational value of interpretable algorithms for clinical decision support.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several tensions exist within the evidence base. Deep learning models, while demonstrating superior accuracy in controlled validation cohorts, exhibited substantial performance variability across clinical sites due to differences in imaging equipment, patient populations, and operator technique—a limitation explicitly noted in both ultrasound and IVF embryo selection contexts. This reproducibility deficit contrasts with the more stable but lower-performing traditional machine learning approaches, creating an unresolved trade-off between peak accuracy and generalizability. Additionally, while AI integration into ultrasound training is proposed as a solution to the widely documented gap in clinician expertise—identified as a primary barrier to non-invasive diagnosis adoption—no reviewed study provided prospective outcome data confirming that AI-assisted training translates to improved real-world diagnostic rates. A further concern raised across multiple papers is the potential for gender and cultural biases present in clinical practice to become embedded in algorithmic training data, which could perpetuate rather than correct existing diagnostic disparities in endometriosis; however, empirical quantification of this algorithmic bias in endometriosis-specific models remains absent from the current literature.

## AI Applications in Endometriosis Diagnosis: A Synthesis

The 33 articles assigned to this cluster present a heterogeneous collection of biomedical research, and notably, only a minority directly address artificial intelligence applications in endometriosis diagnosis. Nonetheless, several convergent themes emerge that collectively illuminate the trajectory of AI-assisted and technology-driven approaches to endometriosis detection and management.

A dominant theme across relevant papers is the inadequacy of current non-invasive diagnostic standards and the consequent drive toward novel imaging and biomarker modalities. The persistent reliance on laparoscopic surgery for definitive endometriosis diagnosis is explicitly critiqued in multiple studies, motivating development of alternatives including photoacoustic imaging with methylene blue for intraoperative ureteral visualization (PMC12988832), gold nanorod-based theranostic platforms exploiting the enhanced permeability and retention effect for photoacoustic lesion detection (PMC13038282), and plasma 5-hydroxymethylcytosine profiling as a liquid biopsy approach achieving AUC values of 0.91 with a 10-marker signature outperforming CA125 and CA199 (PMC13018905). The 5hmC study further identifies a regulatory axis involving APP overexpression and downstream HOXD9 upregulation promoting endometrial cell migration and fibrosis, representing one of the few mechanistic contributions in this cluster.

Deep learning and multimodal imaging fusion appear as consistently reported methodological advances. A ResNet-18-based fusion model integrating grayscale, color Doppler, and three-dimensional transvaginal ultrasound achieved AUC of 0.92 for endometrial cancer discrimination, with sensitivity for malignancy detection markedly superior to radiologist assessment (91% versus 30%; PMC13049860). Similarly, AI-augmented multi-omics frameworks incorporating transcriptomics, proteomics, and automated follicle quantification are described for ovarian tissue characterization in fertility preservation contexts (PMC13066179), and large language models demonstrated comparable or superior diagnostic accuracy to gynecologists for endometriosis presenting with acute abdominal pain, though with identified limitations in reference accuracy and emotional intelligence (PMC12988381).

Several papers touch on endometriosis as a comorbidity or confounding condition rather than a primary focus. Endometriosis is noted as a risk factor complicating ureteral visualization during pelvic surgery (PMC12988832), a histological mimic in differential diagnosis of small bowel Crohn's disease (PMC13079126), a rare finding in rectal biopsy series (PMC13066892), and a condition associated with phenol occupational exposure (PMC13032468) and with cervical endometrioid adenocarcinoma (PMC12976804). Ovarian tissue cryopreservation literature specifically lists endometriosis among non-malignant indications warranting fertility preservation (PMC13076554).

Contradictions and gaps are evident. While the 5hmC liquid biopsy approach reports high diagnostic accuracy, validation remains limited to a single cohort, and the clinical reproducibility of the APP-HOXD9 axis requires independent confirmation. The large language model evaluation (PMC12988381) reports superior AI performance for endometriosis diagnosis without specifying the clinical scenario's complexity, limiting interpretability. More broadly, the cluster contains numerous papers entirely unrelated to endometriosis or AI diagnostics—including studies on preterm infant nutrition, pilonidal sinus disease, rectal cancer MRI preparation, and vagal neuromodulation—suggesting that cluster assignment may reflect bibliometric co-citation patterns rather than strict topical coherence. Consequently, definitive synthesis of AI-specific endometriosis diagnostic findings is constrained by the limited number of directly relevant studies and their methodological heterogeneity, underscoring the need for prospective, multicenter validation of the imaging and biomarker approaches described herein.

# NLRP3 Inflammasome Signaling in Endometriosis

## Overview

A defining feature of endometriosis pathophysiology is the establishment and maintenance of a pro-inflammatory peritoneal microenvironment that supports ectopic lesion survival, neovascularization, and pain sensitization. The NLRP3 inflammasome has emerged as a mechanistic nexus within this environment, integrating hormonal, metabolic, microbial, and damage-associated signals into coordinated innate immune responses. The central research question driving this field concerns how NLRP3 inflammasome activation is initiated, sustained, and dysregulated within the ectopic endometrial niche, and whether this pathway represents a tractable therapeutic target for disrupting the self-reinforcing inflammatory loops that characterize the disease.

## Consensus Findings

The literature converges on a canonical two-signal activation model as the operative framework. An NF-κB-dependent priming stage—initiated by toll-like receptor engagement (particularly TLR4/MyD88 signaling), cytokine stimulation, and estrogen receptor activation—transcriptionally upregulates NLRP3 and pro-IL-1β. A subsequent activation signal, most consistently attributed to potassium efflux, mitochondrial reactive oxygen species accumulation, or ATP-mediated P2X7 receptor stimulation, triggers NLRP3 oligomerization with the ASC adaptor protein and pro-caspase-1, culminating in caspase-1–dependent maturation of IL-1β and IL-18 alongside gasdermin D (GSDMD) cleavage and pyroptotic cell death. This mechanistic architecture is reproducibly documented across experimental systems and is directly implicated in peritoneal inflammation relevant to endometriosis.

Estrogen signaling constitutes the most consistently identified disease-specific amplification mechanism. In adenomyosis models, estrogen receptor-beta directly activates NLRP3, releasing IL-1β that subsequently drives COX-2/prostaglandin E2/aromatase upregulation, generating a self-sustaining inflammatory-estrogenic feed-forward loop. CYP19A1-mediated aromatase overexpression, sustained through prostaglandin E2–cAMP–CREB signaling, further entrenches hyperestrogenism as a permissive condition for ongoing inflammasome priming. Progesterone resistance, linked to dominant PR-A expression and impaired coactivator recruitment, removes the anti-inflammatory counterbalance that would ordinarily constrain this amplification cycle. Epigenetic regulation adds further specificity: TET3-overexpressing macrophages epigenetically activate NLRP3, IL-1β, TGF-β1, and CCL2 promoters via STAT3-dependent mechanisms, creating a self-sustaining inflammatory loop that pharmacological NLRP3 inhibition demonstrably reduces in vivo lesion burden. Complement-NLRP3 crosstalk represents an additional amplification circuit, wherein C3a, C5a, and membrane attack complex components function as both priming and activation signals, while NLRP3-derived cytokines reciprocally enhance complement synthesis. Peritoneal succinate accumulation, documented in endometriosis patients, stabilizes HIF-1α through SUCNR1 signaling and promotes IL-1β secretion in M1-polarized macrophages, linking metabolic reprogramming to inflammasome output. Downstream of NLRP3 activation, IL-1β drives angiogenesis through EPHB4-mediated SDF-1/CXCR4 signaling and mast cell-mediated TRPV1 sensitization, directly connecting inflammasome activity to lesion vascularization and pain pathophysiology.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several points of genuine tension exist within the literature. The net biological consequences of NLRP3-driven pyroptosis remain contextually ambiguous: whereas NLRP3 inhibition consistently reduces endometriotic lesion burden in experimental models, related inflammasome sensors—notably NLRP6—perform indispensable protective functions in mucosal barrier maintenance and commensal microbiota homeostasis, cautioning against non-selective inflammasome suppression. The role of estrogen in inflammasome regulation is similarly non-uniform; while estrogen broadly activates NLRP3 in endometriotic and adenomyotic tissue, evidence from implantation models indicates that excess progesterone impairs uterine inflammatory homeostasis through AhR pathway diversion rather than direct inflammasome engagement, suggesting that steroid hormone effects on inflammasome biology are not uniformly directional. A further unresolved tension concerns macrophage polarization: while M1 macrophages consistently amplify NLRP3-dependent inflammatory responses, ectopic stromal cells in some models reprogram peritoneal macrophages toward immunosuppressive phenotypes through ACOD1-mediated itaconate overproduction and NRF2 activation, suppressing immunosurveillance independently of overt inflammasome activation. This implies that NLRP3-driven inflammation and NLRP3-independent immunosuppression may coexist within the same lesion microenvironment, complicating interpretation of net immune function. Additionally, the relative contributions of canonical caspase-1–dependent versus non-canonical caspase-11/caspase-5–dependent pyroptosis in gynecological tissue contexts remain unresolved.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Critical mechanistic questions remain inadequately addressed. The cell-type specificity of NLRP3 activation within endometriotic lesions—whether dominant activation occurs in peritoneal macrophages, ectopic stromal cells, mast cells, or infiltrating neutrophils—has not been systematically characterized using single-cell resolution approaches. TET3-mediated epigenetic regulation of NLRP3 requires independent replication in patient-derived endometriotic tissue rather than disease-agnostic macrophage models. The endogenous regulatory circuits that modulate net NLRP3 output in ectopic lesions, including meteorin-β/AMPK/PPAR-mediated suppression, IL-37/IL-1R8 antagonism, and microRNA-mediated control through miR-22, miR-146a, and miR-223, have not been characterized at the level of patient tissue or disease stage. ## NLRP3 Inflammasome Signaling in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The 50 articles assigned to this cluster exhibit considerable thematic heterogeneity, with only a minority directly addressing NLRP3 inflammasome signaling in endometriosis. Nevertheless, several convergent mechanistic themes emerge that are relevant to inflammasome biology in gynecological and inflammatory contexts.

The most consistently reported finding across relevant papers is the centrality of caspase-1–mediated pyroptotic signaling downstream of inflammasome activation. Multiple studies describe the canonical NLRP3 pathway—comprising NLRP3 sensor activation, ASC adaptor recruitment, caspase-1 cleavage, and gasdermin D–mediated pore formation leading to IL-1β and IL-18 secretion—as a driver of tissue inflammation and cell death. One study demonstrated that RARγ directly binds ASC to suppress inflammasome activation across NLRP3, AIM2, and NLRC4 pathways, reducing IL-1β secretion in peritoneal macrophages, a population particularly relevant to endometriosis pathophysiology. Separately, Kir4.1 downregulation in astrocytes was shown to activate NLRP3 through NMDAR/calpain-1 signaling, amplifying IL-1β and IL-6 production. TRADD-mediated pyroptosis via the inflammasome–caspase-1–GSDMD axis was documented in cardiomyocytes, illustrating the pathway's operation across tissue types.

Estrogen-driven aromatase overexpression via CYP19A1, sustained by prostaglandin E2–cAMP–CREB feed-forward loops, represents a dominant endometriosis-specific mechanism described in two papers, linking hormonal dysregulation to the inflammatory microenvironment in which inflammasome activation may be sustained. P2X7 receptor signaling was identified as an upstream activator of NLRP3 inflammasome in neuroinflammatory contexts, consistent with purinergic regulation of inflammasome priming.

No direct contradictions regarding core NLRP3 mechanisms were identified; however, the preponderance of articles in this cluster address topics peripheral to NLRP3 inflammasome signaling in endometriosis specifically, limiting definitive synthesis of disease-specific conclusions.

## NLRP3 Inflammasome Signaling in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across the surveyed literature, the NLRP3 inflammasome emerges as a broadly operative innate immune signaling platform with consistent mechanistic features: NLRP3 oligomerizes with the adaptor protein ASC to recruit and activate caspase-1, which proteolytically processes pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature forms and cleaves gasdermin D (GSDMD) to initiate pyroptotic cell death. This canonical cascade, detailed across multiple papers (PMC12125403, PMC12206412, PMC12127367), is primed through NF-κB-dependent transcriptional upregulation and triggered by potassium efflux, reactive oxygen species accumulation, and lysosomal damage. In the context of endometriosis specifically, NLRP3 activation contributes to the pro-inflammatory peritoneal microenvironment that sustains ectopic lesion survival, with pyroptosis-derived inflammatory mediators promoting immune dysfunction and tissue remodeling characteristic of the disease.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several mechanistic nodes appear with notable regularity. First, the NLRP3–ASC–caspase-1–IL-1β/IL-18 axis is consistently identified as the core effector pathway, with GSDMD pore formation representing the terminal pyroptotic event (PMC12125403, PMC12206412, PMC12127367, PMC12185947). Upstream, NF-κB signaling functions as the dominant transcriptional priming mechanism, while ROS generation—whether from mitochondrial dysfunction, hyperglycemia, or environmental toxin exposure—serves as a recurrent activation trigger. Estrogen signaling intersects with this pathway at multiple levels: estrogen and its receptors modulate NLRP3 activity in a context-dependent manner (PMC12127367), and the hyperestrogenic milieu of endometriosis, driven by mechanisms including FSHR-induced aromatase upregulation (PMC12087307) and PCB-126-mediated ERβ/AXL signaling (PMC12136210), may constitute a permissive environment for sustained inflammasome priming. Progesterone resistance, linked to dominant PR-A expression and impaired coactivator recruitment (PMC12069617), further compromises the anti-inflammatory counterbalance normally provided by progesterone signaling. The diethylstilbestrol exposure model directly demonstrated NLRP3 upregulation alongside caspase-1-driven pyroptosis in uterine tissue, with concurrent loss of implantation-associated receptivity markers (PMC12071748), reinforcing that excessive inflammasome activation disrupts normal endometrial function. Meteorin-β was identified as an endogenous suppressor of NLRP3 activity, downregulating IL-1β and IL-18 through AMPK/PPAR-mediated pathways (PMC12110803), suggesting that deficiency of such regulatory molecules may contribute to uncontrolled inflammasome activity in endometriotic lesions.

Second, angiogenesis represents a consistently implicated downstream consequence of inflammasome-driven inflammation in endometriosis. EPHB4-mediated SDF-1/CXCR4 signaling (PMC12126875), FSHR-driven hyperestrogenism (PMC12087307), and inflammatory cytokine-mediated vascular remodeling collectively support neovasculogenesis required for ectopic lesion maintenance. IL-1β released downstream of NLRP3 activation is a well-established pro-angiogenic mediator, providing a mechanistic bridge between inflammasome activity and lesion vascularization.

Third, the TRPM2–NLRP3 axis deserves particular note as an amplification mechanism: TRPM2 channel activation by oxidative stress permits calcium influx that triggers mitochondrial ROS production, further driving NLRP3 assembly (PMC12185947). This feed-forward loop between oxidative stress and inflammasome activation is relevant to endometriosis, where elevated peritoneal oxidative stress is well-documented.
