## Endometriosis Pain Management and Quality of Life: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on three dominant themes: the inadequacy of unimodal treatment approaches for endometriosis-related pain, the neurobiological underpinnings of central sensitization as a driver of chronic pelvic pain, and the therapeutic potential of multimodal, biopsychosocial interventions. Endometriosis is consistently framed as a chronic inflammatory, estrogen-dependent condition in which ectopic endometrial lesions activate macrophages and mast cells, sustain peripheral sensitization through elevated prostaglandins and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8), and progressively recruit central sensitization mechanisms. This neurobiological cascade—spanning peripheral nociceptor activation, spinal cord sensitization at lamina I, and dysregulation of supraspinal affective circuits including the anterior cingulate cortex and insular cortex—explains both the breadth of symptom burden and the resistance of endometriosis pain to single-target pharmacotherapy.

A consistently emphasized theme is the psychosocial amplification of pain through catastrophizing, fear-avoidance behaviors, stigma, and diagnostic delay, particularly in adolescents. Multiple papers note that hyperalgesia priming from recurrent dysmenorrhea during adolescence can establish chronic pain trajectories, and that poor pelvic health literacy compounds disability by delaying care-seeking and reinforcing maladaptive pain cognitions. Pain science education (PSE) frameworks that shift patient understanding from purely biomedical to biopsychosocial models appear across several studies as a foundational component of effective management. Notably, one e-Delphi study identified a significant tension between clinician tendencies to de-emphasize pathology-pain relationships in favor of central sensitization narratives and patients' strong preference for acknowledgment of disease-specific pathological drivers—a discordance with direct implications for therapeutic alliance and treatment engagement.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Physical activity and structured exercise emerge as among the most robustly supported interventions across this corpus. Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses demonstrate that exercise reduces pain intensity, improves quality of life, and attenuates psychological comorbidities through convergent mechanisms: suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6, TNF-α), stimulation of endogenous opioid release, reduction of prostaglandin F2α levels, and activation of descending spinal pain modulation pathways. Strength training ranks highest in efficacy for primary dysmenorrhea (SUCRA 71.0%), followed by aerobic exercise, with optimal dosing parameters of at least 8 weeks duration, more than 3 sessions per week, and sessions exceeding 30 minutes. Exercise benefits extended beyond pain to encompass depression, anxiety, fatigue, and social functioning, consistent with the broader literature on physical activity as an independent predictor of mental health in chronic pelvic pain disorders.

Acupuncture and related modalities—including electroacupuncture, ear electroacupuncture (SUCRA 83.0%), moxibustion, and auricular point therapy—demonstrate consistent efficacy across network meta-analyses for both primary dysmenorrhea and symptomatic endometriosis. Proposed mechanisms include promotion of pelvic circulation, reduction of uterine smooth muscle spasm, endogenous opioid release, and modulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. When combined with conventional pharmacotherapies such as GnRH agonists, gestrinone, or dydrogesterone, acupuncture-related interventions reduce serum CA125 and inflammatory biomarkers alongside symptomatic improvement, suggesting additive rather than merely parallel therapeutic effects.

Multimodal digital and interdisciplinary programs also demonstrate consistent benefit. The Endo-App and School of Endo digital platforms both produced clinically significant improvements in pain self-efficacy, fatigue, depression, neuropathic pain, and quality-of-life domains. Cognitive behavioral therapy components—including cognitive restructuring, mindfulness, and emotion regulation training—appear across multiple successful interventions (PEEPS, School of Endo, VR-based programs) as active therapeutic ingredients targeting fear-avoidance, catastrophizing, and the affective dimension of pain. ## Pain Management and Quality of Life in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interconnected themes: the inadequacy of unimodal treatment for endometriosis-associated pain, the centrality of neuroinflammatory and hormonal mechanisms in symptom generation, and the growing evidence base for multimodal and non-pharmacological interventions. Endometriosis pain arises through multiple overlapping pathways, including chronic pelvic inflammation, central sensitization, neuropathic signaling via sympathetic and somatic nerve fibers, estrogen-driven ectopic tissue proliferation, and visceral hypersensitivity that frequently extends to gastrointestinal and bladder symptom domains. The superior hypogastric plexus, sacral nerve roots, and dorsal root ganglia emerge repeatedly as anatomical targets for interventional pain management, reflecting the visceral and somatic duality of endometriosis-associated nociception.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Neuromodulatory interventions demonstrate robust and reproducible efficacy. Sacral neuromodulation reduced median pain scores from 7.4 to 2.3 in refractory cases, with 88% achieving sustained long-term relief, while dorsal root ganglion stimulation produced 80–100% pain reductions in abdominal pain conditions including endometriosis. Superior hypogastric plexus block achieved 70% pain reduction in visceral pelvic pain by interrupting sympathetic nociceptive transmission at the L5–S1 level. Percutaneous ablative techniques, including microwave ablation and cryoablation, demonstrated effectiveness for abdominal wall endometriosis, with cryoablation achieving approximately 82% pain reduction and favorable safety profiles. Enhanced Recovery After Surgery protocols reduced postoperative hospital stay by approximately 2.88 days in deep infiltrating endometriosis surgery without increasing complication rates, reflecting the importance of perioperative pain optimization given this population's characteristically lower postoperative pain thresholds.

Dietary interventions, particularly the low-FODMAP diet, consistently improved gastrointestinal symptoms and quality of life across two independent trials, with one crossover study demonstrating a 60% response rate compared to 26% on control diet. The mechanism involves reduction of osmotic load and colonic fermentation, attenuating visceral hypersensitivity through the gut-brain axis. Improvements extended beyond gastrointestinal endpoints to encompass psychological symptoms including anxiety and depression, measured by validated instruments. The MIND diet showed a strong protective association with endometriosis risk, with high adherence corresponding to 83% reduced odds of disease, mediated through anti-inflammatory nutrient profiles and modulation of circulating estrogen concentrations via dietary fiber and fat composition.

Hormonal and pharmacological findings remained more heterogeneous. The levonorgestrel intrauterine system demonstrated a more favorable tolerability profile than subdermal etonogestrel implants, with lower rates of acne, weight gain, and device removal, though both suppressed endometriosis pain through endometrial atrophy and ovulation inhibition. Linzagolix with add-back therapy achieved 72.9% response rates for dysmenorrhea, while combination pharmacotherapy more broadly failed to demonstrate superiority over monotherapy for chronic pelvic pain, with the notable exception of specific regimens such as troxerutin combined with carbazochrome for defecation-related pain. Vitamin D supplementation showed medium-to-high effectiveness for primary dysmenorrhea through prostaglandin modulation and anti-inflammatory pathways, but evidence for endometriosis-specific pain relief remained substantially weaker, underscoring condition-specific biological differences.

Psychological and behavioral interventions featured prominently, with digital and mindfulness-based platforms demonstrating consistent improvements in patient-reported quality of life, self-efficacy, and pain acceptance. A digitalized mindfulness and acceptance-based intervention for endometriosis specifically improved pelvic pain, sleep, rumination, and disease literacy, while facilitating a shift from pain catastrophizing toward acceptance-based coping. Exercise, including high-intensity interval training, reduced both endometriosis lesion burden and cardiovascular risk markers—specifically high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, total cholesterol, and triglycerides—particularly when combined with pentoxifylline, which suppresses inflammatory cytokines and enhances end

## Endometriosis Pain Management and Quality of Life: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of studies, several converging themes emerge regarding endometriosis and related pelvic pain conditions. A predominant focus is the multidimensional nature of pain—encompassing nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic components—and the inadequacy of single-modality treatment strategies. Central sensitization is consistently invoked as a pathophysiological mechanism, wherein prolonged peripheral nociceptor activation driven by prostaglandins (particularly PGF2α and PGE2), pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and elevated CA125 progressively amplifies pain perception through neuroplastic remodeling of ascending and descending pain pathways. Several papers extend this framework by implicating the vaginal microbiome as an independent driver of pain: dysbiosis appears sufficient to generate pain independently of tissue pathology, and restoration of healthy microbiota composition via antibiotic treatment or microbiome transplantation reversed pain in experimental models, representing a mechanistically novel therapeutic target. The psychosocial burden of endometriosis—encompassing anxiety, depression, pain catastrophizing, and social withdrawal—emerges as both a consequence and amplifier of physical symptoms, with catastrophizing specifically linked to dysregulation of the anterior insula and periaqueductal gray matter circuits involved in descending pain modulation.

A secondary dominant theme is the imperative for multimodal, mechanism-stratified management. Papers consistently advocate stratifying interventions by pain subtype: hormone-dependent nociceptive pain responds to progestogens, combined estradiol-progestogen regimens, or GnRH modulators, while hormone-independent nociplastic pain requires non-hormonal approaches. The latter category encompasses aerobic exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), pain neuroscience education (PNE), pelvic floor rehabilitation, acupuncture, and anti-inflammatory dietary modification. This stratification principle aligns with evidence that diagnostic delay worsens outcomes by permitting progressive afferent hypersensitization and central sensitization.

Self-management and patient empowerment constitute a third prominent theme. Multiple studies demonstrate that structured educational interventions, mobile health applications, peer support, and health literacy programs improve pain control, reduce healthcare dependency, and strengthen patients' perceived control over their condition. Digital health technologies—including smartphone applications, chatbot-assisted tracking, and video-based exercise programs—consistently reduce dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain severity while circumventing barriers related to healthcare access, stigma, and medication side effects.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The prostaglandin synthesis pathway, specifically PGF2α and PGE2 overproduction driven by decreased progesterone and enhanced cyclooxygenase activity, is the most uniformly cited mechanistic substrate across papers addressing primary dysmenorrhea and endometriosis-related pain. NSAIDs targeting cyclooxygenase enzymes and progestogen-based hormonal suppression therefore represent the most robustly supported pharmacological interventions. Acupuncture emerges as the non-pharmacological intervention with the strongest cross-study endorsement: multiple independent investigations report significant reductions in endometriosis-associated dysmenorrhea, with proposed mechanisms including β-endorphin release, prostaglandin suppression, enhanced pelvic blood flow, and normalization of pain-processing abnormalities in the insula and anterior cingulate cortex. The combination of physical and psychological interventions—particularly PNE with exercise, or CBT with pelvic floor rehabilitation—consistently outperforms single-modality approaches across pain intensity, catastrophizing, and quality-of-life metrics.

Inflammatory biomarker reduction is reported across diverse interventions: acupuncture combined with herbal medicine lowered CRP, TNF-α, IL-6, and CA125; whole-body cryostimulation reduced prostaglandin E2 and systemic inflammatory markers; and the low-FODMAP diet modulated gut-brain axis dysfunction with secondary improvements in mood and cognition. Collectively, these findings reinforce that inflammatory pathway suppression, regardless of the modality achieving it, correlates reliably with symptomatic improvement.

## Pain Management and Quality of Life in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this body of literature, three overarching themes emerge: the neuroinflammatory basis of endometriosis-associated pain, the superiority of multimodal and multidisciplinary treatment approaches over single-modality interventions, and the profound biopsychosocial burden imposed by chronic pelvic pain on quality of life, sexual function, and psychological well-being. Pain perpetuation in endometriosis is consistently attributed to prostaglandin overproduction, pro-inflammatory cytokine cascades (particularly TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β), central sensitization, and neuroimmune alterations including elevated dendritic cell density and altered stromal nerve architecture. Vascular dysfunction—characterized by dysregulated endothelin-1, nitric oxide, thromboxane A2, and prostacyclin balance—emerges as a mechanistically important contributor to dysmenorrhea across multiple studies examining both conventional and traditional Chinese medicine interventions.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Suppression of TNF-α and IL-6 is the most consistently reported anti-inflammatory mechanism across pharmacological, herbal, and physical interventions. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists reduce inflammation and lesion vascularization, improving bowel symptoms, menstrual pain, and overall quality of life post-surgically, though dyspareunia response remains inconsistent. Traditional Chinese medicine formulations—including Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction, Chinese herbal medicine combinations, and acupuncture—repeatedly demonstrate reductions in serum CA-125, PGE2, and VAS pain scores, with combination acupuncture plus herbal medicine yielding odds ratios of 3.75 over monotherapy. Multidisciplinary approaches incorporating physiotherapy, psychological interventions (cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness, acceptance and commitment therapy), dietary modification (particularly low-FODMAP protocols), and pelvic floor rehabilitation consistently outperform single-discipline care in reducing pain intensity and improving sexual function and psychological outcomes. Digital health technologies, including virtual reality and internet-delivered cognitive behavioral interventions, show emerging efficacy as accessible adjuncts. Neuromodulation strategies, particularly dorsal root ganglion stimulation, demonstrate sustained pain relief in refractory cases, with VAS reductions from 8.7 to 3.8 over long-term follow-up.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable contradictions complicate interpretation. Acupuncture demonstrates superiority over NSAIDs as standalone therapy and significant immediate analgesic effects using dense-sparse electroacupuncture waveforms, yet shows no statistical superiority over sham acupuncture at three-month follow-up, raising unresolved questions about specific versus nonspecific mechanisms. GnRH agonist therapy improves most pain domains but fails to significantly ameliorate dyspareunia and may paradoxically complicate surgical dissection through tissue fibrosis. The relationship between endometriotic lesion burden and pain severity is explicitly questioned in one review cautioning against lesion-centered diagnostic frameworks, noting that superficial peritoneal endometriosis may respond well to hormonal suppression while noncyclic pain in the same patient may reflect central sensitization requiring entirely different management—a finding that contradicts the implicit assumption in several surgical and hormonal intervention studies that lesion treatment equates to pain resolution. Multidisciplinary treatment, while superior for pain and sexual function outcomes, did not produce statistically significant quality of life improvements over single-discipline care in one meta-analysis, suggesting that current quality of life measurement tools may be insufficiently sensitive to capture the full treatment benefit. These contradictions collectively highlight that endometriosis pain is mechanistically heterogeneous and that treatment stratification based on pain phenotype—cyclical versus noncyclic, peripheral versus centrally sensitized—remains an unmet clinical priority.

## Endometriosis Pain Management and Quality of Life: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers reviewed converge on several dominant themes in endometriosis pain management: the chronic inflammatory and estrogen-dependent nature of the disease, the multidimensionality of pain (nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic components), and the necessity of multimodal, biopsychosocial treatment approaches. A recurring mechanistic framework positions endometriosis-associated pain as arising from lesion-derived proinflammatory mediators—principally IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, prostaglandin E2, substance P, and calcitonin gene-related peptide—that drive both peripheral sensitization and central sensitization via hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation. Pelvic floor myofascial dysfunction is consistently identified as a secondary pain perpetuator that persists independent of lesion burden. Oxidative stress is highlighted as an additional pathophysiological layer, with reactive oxygen species amplifying inflammatory cascades and pain perception.

Physical rehabilitation modalities constitute the most extensively represented therapeutic category. Physiotherapy interventions including TENS, transcranial electrotherapy, laser therapy, manual therapy, pelvic floor muscle training, and multimodal exercise programs all demonstrate statistically significant reductions in pelvic pain across multiple study designs. Electrotherapy and laser-based modalities consistently outperform exercise-only approaches in pain reduction magnitude. Complementary to these, acupuncture and electroacupuncture are reported to reduce pain through endogenous opioid release, modulation of descending inhibitory pathways, and suppression of inflammatory cytokines, with one case report documenting sustained reduction of a refractory chronic pelvic pain score from 10 to 2 over 24 months following 40 electroacupuncture sessions. Acupuncture additionally demonstrates anxiolytic and antidepressant effects attributed to vagal nerve stimulation, addressing the substantial psychological comorbidity burden in endometriosis.

Dietary and nutritional interventions represent a consistently supported non-pharmacological strategy. Mediterranean diet adherence is positively correlated with reductions in dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, with mechanistic evidence implicating cyclooxygenase enzyme inhibition and reduction of free circulating estrogen via upregulated sex hormone-binding globulin. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids competitively inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, while curcumin suppresses COX-2 activity and exerts phytoestrogenic modulation of estrogen receptors. Antioxidant supplementation, particularly melatonin at 5–10 mg daily and combined vitamin C plus vitamin E, shows promise in reducing dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain through oxidative stress mitigation, although evidence quality remains low to very low. Conversely, elevated consumption of red meat, saturated fat, cholesterol, caffeine, and alcohol is negatively associated with pain outcomes, with caffeine consumption conferring a 2.51-fold increased dysmenorrhea risk in one cohort study.

Psychological and mind-body interventions constitute a third major therapeutic domain. Cognitive-behavioral therapy reduces pain, depression, and cortisol levels by targeting catastrophizing, kinesiophobia, and maladaptive coping. Yoga and progressive muscle relaxation reduce pain through prostaglandin inhibition, improved uterine perfusion, and augmented serotonin and β-endorphin production. Multidisciplinary pelvic pain programs simultaneously improve pain self-efficacy, patient activation, and social isolation. Therapeutic patient education is strongly preferred by 87.8% of patients immediately post-diagnosis, yet structured programs remain largely underdeveloped. Music therapy combined with mindfulness reduces postoperative pain, anxiety, and sleep disturbance in adenomyosis patients undergoing high-intensity focused ultrasound ablation.

Interventional and surgical approaches are less uniformly represented but include notable findings. Ethanol sclerotherapy for endometriomas preserves anti-Müllerian hormone levels and ovarian reserve while achieving comparable pain relief to laparoscopic cystectomy, with ethanol retention exceeding ten minutes reducing recurrence approximately fivefold. OnabotulinumtoxinantA injected into hypertonic pelvic floor muscles effectively reduces endometriosis-

## Endometriosis Pain Management and Quality of Life: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 38 papers, several interconnected themes emerge: the prostaglandin-mediated pathophysiology of endometriosis-related pain, the expanding role of non-pharmacological and complementary interventions, the psychological and quality-of-life burden of pelvic pain conditions, and the growing interest in gut-microbiome and nutritional influences on disease. Prostaglandin dysregulation—particularly excessive prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) driving uterine hypercontractility, vasoconstriction, and ischemia—is consistently identified as a central mechanistic driver not only of primary dysmenorrhea but also of endometriosis-associated pain, with the PGF2α/PGE2 ratio serving as an objective biomarker of therapeutic response. Neuroinflammatory sensitization, involving cytokines IL-6, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein, is repeatedly implicated in lesion persistence, neuroangiogenesis, and central pain sensitization in endometriosis specifically.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Non-pharmacological interventions—including acupuncture, physical therapy, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), yoga, and herbal medicine—demonstrate significant and reproducible efficacy across multiple study designs. Acupuncture consistently reduces pelvic pain through neuromodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and prostaglandin-modulating mechanisms, with the acupoint combination of Sanyinjiao (SP6), Guanyuan (CV4), and Diji (SP8) emerging as the most evidence-supported core formula for dysmenorrhea. CBT and yoga, evaluated in the HaPPI randomized controlled trial, significantly improved endometriosis-specific health-related quality of life (HRQoL), with reductions in pain catastrophizing and increases in pain self-efficacy identified as key mediating pathways. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid supplementation produced significantly greater reductions in IL-6, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein alongside clinically meaningful pain score improvements (visual analog scale decrease of 3.0 versus 1.5 in controls), supporting adjunctive nutritional strategies. Digital health technologies, including mobile applications and virtual reality platforms, consistently reduced pain intensity and medication utilization while improving self-efficacy. A novel gut-microbiome axis was identified through the discovery that sakuranin, a microbially-derived flavonoid metabolite requiring intestinal deglycosylation for bioavailability, alleviates endometriosis-associated pain and modulates sympathetic (TH) and sensory (SP) nerve markers in disease models, pointing to gut dysbiosis as a contributing pathophysiological feature. Hormonal contraceptives and the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) were consistently reported as effective for dysmenorrhea and adenomyosis management, though elevated LNG-IUS expulsion rates in adenomyosis (15–37.5%) necessitate risk stratification and GnRH agonist pretreatment in high-risk patients. The psychological burden of endometriosis and related pelvic pain conditions is substantial and recurrent across studies, with anxiety, depression, and impaired HRQoL documented across diverse populations and conditions including persistent genital arousal disorder (PGAD/GPD), premenstrual syndrome, and primary dysmenorrhea.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Evidence

Several contradictions and tensions are evident across the literature. For dysmenorrhea in adolescent populations, students with severe symptoms were paradoxically less likely to use oral hormonal contraceptives despite established efficacy, reflecting a gap between evidence-based recommendations and real-world practice. Regarding inflammatory bowel disease comorbidity, NSAIDs—the first-line pharmacological treatment for dysmenorrhea—are contraindicated due to risk of disease exacerbation, creating a therapeutic conflict with standard pain management guidelines that is not fully resolved. The evidence base for Chinese herbal medicine is internally consistent regarding efficacy and safety, yet the mechanistic pathways identified through network pharmacological analyses (PI3K-Akt, cAMP, AGE-RAGE cascades) have not been validated in prospective clinical trials, limiting translational conclusions. # Mental Health and Sexual Function in Endometriosis: Psychosocial Burden, Mechanistic Pathways, and Clinical Implications

## Overview

Endometriosis occupies a uniquely complex intersection of chronic pain, hormonal dysregulation, and psychosocial impairment. The central research question animating this literature asks how pelvic pathology—ectopic endometrial lesions, adhesions, deep infiltrating disease—generates cascading consequences for mental health, sexual function, and quality of life, and by what biological and psychosocial mechanisms these consequences are maintained, amplified, or potentially mitigated. A secondary but equally pressing concern is whether psychological distress functions primarily as a downstream consequence of somatic disease or whether it constitutes an upstream mechanistic driver that worsens pain perception, hormonal dysregulation, and reproductive outcomes. Across 276 articles, these questions are examined through epidemiological, clinical, neurobiological, and psychosocial frameworks, producing a rich but partially contradictory body of evidence.

## Consensus Findings

Depression and anxiety represent the most robustly and consistently replicated psychiatric comorbidities in endometriosis, documented across diverse geographic, cultural, and clinical contexts. Infertile women with endometriosis exhibit approximately 60% higher rates of psychological distress compared to fertile controls, and the relationship between pain severity and mental health outcomes appears dose-dependent, with moderate positive correlations between dysmenorrhea intensity and depression (r=0.42), anxiety (r=0.45), and stress (r=0.40) reported in cross-sectional analyses. Sexual dysfunction is pervasively documented, with rates as high as 76.9% in some endometriosis populations, encompassing impaired desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, and satisfaction as measured across all domains of the Female Sexual Function Index. Deep infiltrating endometriosis demonstrates the strongest association with dyspareunia and sexual avoidance, though pelvic floor hypertonicity—spanning pubococcygeus, iliococcygeus, and obturator muscle groups—emerges as a proximate driver of dyspareunia that may operate independently of ectopic lesion burden, suggesting peripheral sensitization as a critical intermediate mechanism.

The prostaglandin F2α–mediated pathway linking progesterone withdrawal to uterine ischemia is consistently cited as a fundamental mechanism underlying dysmenorrhea and its associated affective sequelae. Neuroimmune dysregulation represents a transdiagnostic biological substrate: pro-inflammatory cytokines and estrogen-driven signaling sustain endometrial lesion growth while simultaneously perturbing serotonin neurotransmission and activating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, thereby linking somatic and psychiatric morbidity through shared inflammatory pathways. The gut-brain axis constitutes an additional mediating pathway, with intestinal microbiome dysbiosis influencing estrogen metabolism and visceral sensitivity; women with endometriosis experience up to a threefold elevated risk of irritable bowel syndrome and report gastrointestinal symptoms in up to 90% of cases. Sleep disturbance functions as both consequence and amplifier of this pathophysiological cycle, with insomnia independently associated with increased endometriosis risk (OR=1.80) and abnormal menstruation (OR=2.37) through hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal dysregulation and suppression of gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion. Diagnostic delays averaging 7–11 years, driven by clinician normalization of menstrual pain and institutional fragmentation of reproductive and mental health services, compound psychological burden through prolonged invalidation—a phenomenon documented as medical gaslighting—that independently elevates distress and suppresses future care-seeking. Physical activity, particularly yoga and Pilates, demonstrates consistent benefits for pain reduction, pelvic floor muscle function, and psychological outcomes, with proposed mechanisms including anti-inflammatory effects, estradiol reduction, improved genital blood flow, and neuromuscular normalization.

## Controversies and Contradictions

The most substantive controversy concerns the directional primacy of psychological versus nociceptive mechanisms in sexual dysfunction. Some studies position visceral hypersensitivity—particularly bladder hypersensitivity experimentally provoked under controlled conditions—as the primary independent predictor of dyspareunia severity, with anxiety and pain catastrophizing operating as secondary modulators. Contradicting this hierarchy, other investigations identify depression and psychological distress as mechanistically upstream mediators of sexual dysfunction, causally antecedent to rather than consequent upon somatic dysfunction. This tension remains unresolved and likely reflects genuine biological heterogeneity within endometriosis populations rather than methodological artifact alone. A second contradiction concerns the anxiety–sexual performance relationship: while the majority of studies treat anxiety as uniformly detrimental to arousal and function, at least one investigation documents a nonlinear relationship wherein low-to-moderate anxiety may enhance sexual arousal through sympathetic nervous system activation, with deterioration occurring only at high anxiety levels—a nuance that challenges therapeutic models premised on uniform anxiety reduction. The relationship between body mass index and dysmenorrhea similarly diverges across populations, with adipose-driven estrogen excess framed as pain-amplifying in most Western cohorts yet paradoxically protective in some sub-Saharan adolescent samples, likely reflecting population-specific confounders rather than contradictory biology. Fertility awareness demonstrates paradoxically adverse psychological effects in certain infertility populations, suggesting that increased health literacy without commensurate psychosocial scaffolding may heighten anxiety rather than reduce it.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Significant mechanistic questions remain unresolved. The molecular pathways through which elevated endometrial cortisol concentrations—directly associated with altered expression of 182 genes governing implantation and immune response—connect psychological stress to reproductive failure warrant prospective longitudinal investigation. The precise contribution of gut microbiome composition to estrogen metabolism and central sensitization in endometriosis patients is incompletely characterized, as are the bidirectional signaling mechanisms connecting intestinal dysbiosis to pelvic nociception. The neurobiological basis for the substantial heterogeneity in psychological responses to equivalent disease burdens remains poorly understood; why some patients develop post-traumatic growth while others with comparable pathology experience entrenched shame and post-traumatic stress symptomatology is mechanistically unexplained. The independent contribution of pharmacological treatments—specifically GnRH agonists, opioids, and neuropathic pain agents—to fatigue and sexual dysfunction, above and beyond disease-related effects, is understudied in head-to-head comparative designs. ## Mental Health, Sexual Function, and Psychosocial Burden in Endometriosis and Related Conditions

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The body of literature synthesized here converges on several dominant themes: the profound psychological burden of chronic pelvic pain conditions (particularly endometriosis), the bidirectional relationship between sexual dysfunction and mental health, the role of visceral hypersensitivity and hormonal dysregulation as unifying biological mechanisms, and the critical mediating roles of shame, body image, and social support in psychological outcomes. Endometriosis emerges as a condition at the intersection of pain, hormonal dysregulation, and psychosocial impairment, with biological mechanisms including estrogen-dependent inflammation, prostaglandin-mediated sensitization, deep infiltrating lesions, and adhesion-related tissue scarring converging to produce chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and sexual avoidance. Menopausal endocrinological shifts—specifically declining estrogen, androgens, and free plasma testosterone—further amplify sexual dysfunction in affected women, particularly impairing arousal and satisfaction domains of the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI). Beyond the primary disease, insomnia has been identified as causally associated with increased endometriosis risk (OR=1.80) and abnormal menstruation (OR=2.37) through hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation and suppression of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, while endometriosis reciprocally disrupts sleep via chronic pain and hormonal perturbation, establishing a self-reinforcing negative cycle.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Across multiple independent studies, depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life are consistently and robustly associated with endometriosis and related menstrual pain conditions. Illness-related shame and negative illness perception repeatedly emerge as mediating variables connecting symptom burden to depressive outcomes, while psychological flexibility, self-compassion, and positive body image function as protective factors that attenuate depression and anxiety risk. Visceral hypersensitivity—particularly bladder hypersensitivity—is consistently implicated as a primary biological driver of dyspareunia and sexual dysfunction in women with dysmenorrhea, with experimentally provoked bladder pain being the only visceral parameter independently predictive of dyspareunia severity in at least one controlled study. Healthcare provider normalization of menstrual pain is a recurrently identified barrier to timely diagnosis across diverse geographic and cultural contexts, with diagnostic delays perpetuating psychological harm through prolonged suffering and invalidation. Physical activity, particularly yoga and Pilates, demonstrates consistent benefits across studies for reducing chronic pelvic pain, improving pelvic floor muscle function, and alleviating anxiety and depression in affected women, with mechanisms encompassing both neuromuscular and psychosocial pathways. Body image dissatisfaction and reduced self-esteem are uniformly associated with worsened psychological outcomes across endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and post-hysterectomy populations, with body appreciation independently and negatively correlating with depression and anxiety severity.

Sexual dysfunction in women with endometriosis and related pelvic conditions operates through multifactorial pathways that are consistently documented. Dyspareunia from deep infiltrating lesions and pelvic adhesions drives sexual avoidance, which in turn generates anxiety, guilt, and relationship deterioration. Infertility associated with endometriosis compounds psychological burden through shame, reduced self-esteem, and disruption of reproductive identity, while also impairing sexual function through lubrication deficits, anorgasmia, and reduced satisfaction—particularly in women with prolonged infertility duration. The prostaglandin F2α–mediated pathway linking progesterone withdrawal to uterine ischemia and pain is consistently cited as a fundamental mechanism underlying dysmenorrhea and its associated psychological sequelae, including anxiety, depression, and irritability. Occupational stress and reproductive health disorders, including endometriosis, are independently and consistently associated with reduced well-being and labor productivity, effects not offset by higher income levels.

Several inconsistencies and tensions are apparent across the literature. ## Mental Health, Sexual Function, and Quality of Life in Endometriosis and Related Conditions

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes linking gynecological and reproductive conditions to mental health impairment, sexual dysfunction, and broader quality-of-life disruption. A dominant mechanistic thread is the bidirectional relationship between chronic pain—particularly pelvic and menstrual pain—and psychological distress. Across multiple studies, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and deep infiltrative endometriosis are consistently linked to anxiety and depression through both biological and psychosocial pathways. Biologically, chronic inflammation is repeatedly implicated as a shared substrate: pro-inflammatory mediators underlying endometriosis, interstitial cystitis, inflammatory bowel disease, and rheumatoid arthritis are each independently associated with depression and fatigue, suggesting that neuroimmune dysregulation represents a transdiagnostic pathway connecting somatic and psychiatric morbidity. Hormonal dysregulation—particularly estrogen and progesterone fluctuations modulating serotonin neurotransmission and prostaglandin production—emerges as a second major mechanism linking menstrual disorders to depressive symptomatology and sexual dysfunction. A third dominant theme is the role of psychosocial mediators, including self-efficacy, health literacy, social support, and sexual self-esteem, in amplifying or attenuating the impact of physical symptoms on mental health and functional outcomes.

Sexual dysfunction features prominently across conditions including endometriosis, premature ovarian insufficiency, polycystic ovary syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and vulvodynia. The mechanistic pathways described are consistently multidimensional: physical pain reduces arousal and lubrication through direct nociceptive interference, while catastrophizing, pelvic floor hypertonicity, and fear-avoidance cognitions perpetuate dyspareunia independently of tissue-level pathology. Psychological comorbidities—anxiety and depression—are identified across numerous studies as critical mediators that worsen sexual outcomes beyond the contribution of pain alone, with GAD-7 and PHQ-9 scores showing strong associations with arousal disorder, dyspareunia, and orgasmic dysfunction in infertile women with ovarian disorders. The fear-avoidance model, described in the context of postoperative kinesiophobia, applies analogously to sexual avoidance in pelvic pain conditions, wherein pain expectancy and catastrophizing elevate pelvic floor tension and reinforce dysfunction.

Fatigue constitutes a third major symptom domain, reported in up to 87% of women with endometriosis and substantially prevalent in inflammatory bowel disease, myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis. Fatigue's impact on depression risk is quantified in rheumatoid arthritis, where each unit increase in fatigue raises depression odds by 26%, partially mediated through reduced self-efficacy. In endometriosis specifically, commonly used pharmacological treatments including GnRH agonists, opioids, and neuropathic pain agents worsen fatigue in substantial proportions of patients, whereas laparoscopic surgical excision provides the most consistent improvement, implicating neuroinflammatory mechanisms that current medical therapies inadequately address.

Structural and systemic barriers to care represent a pervasive theme. Diagnostic delays averaging 7–11 years for endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain are attributed to clinician knowledge gaps, dismissal of women's pain, and medical gaslighting—behaviors documented across endometriosis, vulval lichen sclerosus, vulvodynia, and gynecological pain in students. These delays compound psychological distress, as patients experiencing clinical gaslighting demonstrate higher overall distress and reduced likelihood of seeking future care. Fragmented, non-integrated healthcare systems that separate reproductive and mental health services are identified as a structural determinant of poor outcomes, with cross-disciplinary coordination between psychiatric, reproductive, and surgical specialists consistently advocated.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

The most robustly replicated finding across these papers is the co-occurrence of anxiety and depression with chronic gynecological and pelvic pain conditions. Dysmenorrhea, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain are each independently associated with clinically significant psychological distress, and the relationship appears dose-dependent: women experiencing multiple concurrent menstrual disorders (dysmenorrhea, premenstr

## Mental Health and Sexual Function in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of the Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature converges on several interconnected themes: the bidirectional relationship between chronic gynecological conditions and psychological distress, the multifactorial origins of sexual dysfunction in women with endometriosis and related disorders, and the systemic barriers—diagnostic, socioeconomic, and cultural—that amplify psychological burden. Across papers, endometriosis emerges as a condition whose impact extends well beyond pelvic pathology, generating cascading consequences for mental health, sexual function, fertility, and quality of life. A recurring mechanistic framework positions chronic pain as a primary driver of psychological distress, which then reciprocally worsens pain perception through sensitization of central pain pathways and dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Elevated prostaglandin biosynthesis, systemic inflammation, and hormonal dysregulation are consistently implicated as shared biological substrates linking endometriosis-associated dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia to downstream affective symptoms.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Depression and anxiety are the most uniformly reported psychiatric comorbidities across populations with endometriosis and related gynecological conditions, with several studies documenting rates substantially exceeding those in unaffected women. Infertile women with endometriosis exhibit approximately 60% higher rates of psychological distress compared to fertile controls, and endometriosis independently contributes to socio-psychological distress even after controlling for other gynecological and socioeconomic variables. Sexual dysfunction—encompassing impaired desire, arousal, orgasm, and satisfaction, as well as dyspareunia—is pervasively reported, driven by the intersection of pelvic floor dysfunction, genital pain, hormonal imbalance, and comorbid depression. The pathway from chronic pain through depression to sexual dysfunction is consistently supported, with depression identified as among the strongest predictors of sexual dysfunction across chronic condition populations. Sleep disturbance emerges as both a consequence and amplifier of this cycle: infertile women with endometriosis demonstrate substantially elevated rates of sleep disorders (approximately 65.9% in infertile women broadly), with poor sleep quality associated with diminished ovarian reserve, reduced fertilization rates, and worsened psychological outcomes. Stigma—whether internalized shame, menstrual taboo, or provider dismissal—is repeatedly identified as a structural determinant that delays diagnosis, suppresses help-seeking, and independently worsens psychological outcomes including post-traumatic stress symptoms following hysterectomy. Diagnostic delay, a near-universal finding, compounds mental health burden through prolonged invalidation and untreated pain. Social support and post-traumatic growth function as protective factors, with higher education, reduced stigma, and stronger social networks consistently associated with better psychological trajectories.

Physical exercise demonstrates consistent evidence as an intervention addressing both psychological and sexual health outcomes: regular physical activity reduces anxiety and depression scores, improves sexual function across desire, arousal, satisfaction, and orgasm domains, and is hypothesized to act through improved cardiovascular function, enhanced genital blood flow, hormone modulation, and reduction of systemic inflammation. Cognitive behavioral therapy similarly shows repeated efficacy for managing endometriosis-related psychological distress and menopausal symptoms. Among hormonal interventions, tibolone demonstrates superior benefits for sexual function compared to conventional estrogen-progestogen therapy, while local estrogen and vaginal DHEA effectively address genitourinary syndrome manifestations including dyspareunia.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several contradictions warrant attention. Fertility awareness—a construct linked to health literacy—shows paradoxically adverse psychological effects in some infertility populations, suggesting that increased knowledge without accompanying support structures may heighten anxiety rather than reduce it. Telework's effects on presenteeism among women with dysmenorrhea and endometriosis are similarly contradictory: some evidence suggests telework reduces symptom-related work absence, while other data demonstrate increased presenteeism as blurred work-life boundaries encourage working through illness. Psychological interventions in assisted reproductive technology settings effectively reduce depression and anxiety, yet neither expressive writing nor relaxation techniques translated these improvements into enhanced pregnancy rates, indicating a dissociation between psychological and clinical fertility outcomes that challenges assumptions of straightforward bidirectionality. Post-traumatic growth following hysterectomy is reported as a positive psychological trajectory in some patients, yet stigma and internalized shame in the same population produce negative growth, suggesting heterogeneity in psychological response that aggregate-

## Mental Health and Sexual Function in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Related Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The collected literature converges on several interrelated themes central to understanding mental health and sexual function in the context of endometriosis and related gynecological conditions. A primary theme is the bidirectional relationship between hormonal dysregulation and psychological distress, wherein fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone disrupt neurotransmitter systems—particularly serotonin and gamma-aminobutyric acid—while simultaneously activating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, elevating glucocorticoid levels and perpetuating chronic stress responses. A second dominant theme is the cascade from physical symptomatology to psychological impairment and onward to sexual dysfunction, with anxiety and depression consistently emerging as critical mediating variables along this pathway. Multiple papers identify chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, fatigue, and hormonal imbalance as initiating insults that trigger psychological sequelae—including diminished self-efficacy, negative body image, shame, and social withdrawal—which subsequently impair sexual desire, arousal, orgasmic function, and relationship satisfaction. A third prominent theme concerns the role of structural and social determinants, including stigma, delayed diagnosis, discriminatory healthcare experiences, and limited access to psychological support, in amplifying the burden of gynecological conditions on mental and sexual health.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Anxiety emerges as the most consistently reported psychological consequence across conditions affecting reproductive and menstrual health, functioning both as an outcome of physical symptoms and as an independent mediator of reduced well-being and sexual dysfunction. Multiple studies identify anxiety as the primary pathway through which menstrual pain, abnormal bleeding, premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), premature ovarian insufficiency, hysterectomy, and infertility impair quality of life. Depression is similarly pervasive, reported alongside anxiety in studies of infertility, hysterectomy, vulvodynia, abnormal uterine bleeding, and postoperative gynecological conditions, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) identified as effective interventions across multiple contexts. Serotonergic dysfunction is specifically implicated in premenstrual dysphoric disorder and menstrual cycle-related mood disturbances, linking gonadal steroid fluctuations to central nervous system pathophysiology.

Sexual dysfunction in gynecological conditions is consistently characterized across multiple domains of the Female Sexual Function Index, including diminished desire, impaired arousal and lubrication, orgasmic difficulties, dyspareunia, and reduced satisfaction. The sympathetic nervous system emerges as a key physiological mediator, with performance anxiety and chronic stress activating vasoconstrictive responses in genital tissues that directly inhibit lubrication and arousal. Pelvic floor hypertonicity is identified as an additional mechanism, particularly in vulvodynia and postpartum sexual dysfunction, where myofascial trigger point release combined with pelvic floor muscle training demonstrates efficacy in restoring sexual function across all FSFI domains. Orgasmic frequency is reported as a particularly robust predictor of psychological well-being, correlating with elevated self-esteem, reduced subjective stress, and improved relationship quality, underscoring the bidirectional nature of sexual health and mental health outcomes.

Psychological interventions—including CBT, mindfulness-based programs, Hatha yoga, acupuncture, and structured counseling—consistently reduce anxiety and depression across infertility, ART treatment, vulvodynia, and post-hysterectomy populations. Notably, preventive psychological support administered before ART procedures demonstrates superior efficacy compared to interventions during or after treatment. Social support, dyadic adjustment, and therapeutic alliance are reported as consistent moderators of treatment effectiveness, while stigma, fragmented care, and delayed diagnosis are uniformly identified as barriers to improvement.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several areas of conflicting evidence warrant attention. The relationship between anxiety and sexual performance is described as nonlinear by one study, with low-to-moderate anxiety potentially enhancing performance through increased arousal, while high anxiety causes deterioration—a nuance absent from most other papers that treat anxiety uniformly as detrimental to sexual function. Regarding physical activity, evidence diverges on intensity thresholds: moderate exercise is described as protective for hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis function and fertility, while high-intensity training in the context of relative energy

## Mental Health, Sexual Function, and Psychosocial Burden in Endometriosis and Related Gynecological Conditions

The papers reviewed collectively establish that endometriosis and related gynecological disorders—including chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, vulvodynia, interstitial cystitis, and dysmenorrhea—impose a substantial and multidimensional psychosocial burden that extends well beyond their primary somatic manifestations. Three dominant themes emerge across this literature: (1) bidirectional relationships between chronic pain and psychological distress, (2) the centrality of sexual dysfunction as both a consequence and perpetuator of psychological morbidity, and (3) systemic barriers to care that compound disease burden.

**Pain–Psychological Distress Bidirectionality**

The most consistently reported finding across multiple papers is the robust, dose-dependent relationship between pain severity and mental health outcomes. Studies of dysmenorrhea, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain uniformly document elevated depression, anxiety, and stress in affected women, with effect sizes increasing proportionally with pain intensity. One cross-sectional study of Saudi women with dysmenorrhea reported moderate positive correlations between pain severity and depression (r=0.42), anxiety (r=0.45), and stress (r=0.40), alongside negative correlations with quality of life (r=−0.28). Similarly, qualitative investigations of endometriosis patients identified anxiety, depression, hopelessness, suicidal ideation, catastrophic thinking, and fear-avoidance behaviors as prevalent psychological sequelae, with the unpredictable, cyclical nature of pain specifically undermining patients' sense of control and contributing to anticipatory anxiety.

Mechanistic pathways proposed across these papers include central sensitization, prostaglandin-mediated visceral hypersensitivity, hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, and estrogen-driven pro-inflammatory signaling. One review documented that elevated estradiol perpetuates a pro-inflammatory environment promoting endometrial lesion growth, while exercise-induced reductions in C-reactive protein and estradiol were associated with concurrent improvements in both pain and psychological distress, suggesting that inflammatory pathways represent a shared mechanistic substrate linking somatic and psychiatric symptoms. The gut-brain axis emerged as an additional mediating pathway, with intestinal microbiome dysbiosis influencing estrogen metabolism and visceral sensitivity in women with endometriosis, who experience up to a threefold elevated risk of irritable bowel syndrome and report chronic gastrointestinal symptoms in up to 90% of cases.

**Sexual Dysfunction as a Central Morbidity**

Sexual dysfunction was identified as a primary quality-of-life impairment in endometriosis across multiple studies. One investigation of women in a low-middle-income country reported female sexual dysfunction in 76.9% of endometriosis patients, with particular deficits in arousal and orgasm domains. Deep infiltrating endometriosis showed the strongest associations with dyspareunia and reduced sexual satisfaction, consistent with findings linking pelvic myofascial tenderness—assessed across pubococcygeus, iliococcygeus, and obturator muscle groups—to a dose-dependent restriction of sexual activity independent of endometriosis diagnosis per se. This dissociation between endometriosis diagnosis and sexual restriction when myofascial pain is controlled suggests that peripheral sensitization and pelvic floor hypertonicity may be proximate drivers of dyspareunia rather than the ectopic lesions themselves.

Psychological mechanisms amplifying sexual dysfunction were consistently reported. Anticipatory anxiety regarding pain during intercourse, intercourse avoidance, negative body image, feelings of guilt and inadequacy, and relational strain were documented across qualitative and quantitative studies. A randomized controlled trial of the Odeya digital intervention demonstrated that addressing these cognitive-behavioral and emotional dimensions—through pelvic floor training, sensate focus exercises, mindfulness, and psychoeducation—produced significant improvements in sexual distress, sexual function, anxiety, depression, and emotion regulation, supporting the efficacy of interventions targeting psychological mediators of sexual dysfunction. Complementary findings from studies of vulvodynia and interstitial cystitis reinforced that catastrophizing, hypervigilance, and fear of pain amplify symptom severity through bidirectional emotional-nociceptive interactions, while partner empathy and open communication serve as protective factors.

**Systemic Barriers, Diagnostic Delay, and Medical Gaslighting

## Mental Health, Sexual Function, and Psychosocial Burden in Endometriosis-Adjacent Conditions

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interrelated themes: the bidirectional relationship between psychological distress and reproductive/pelvic health, the role of psychosocial factors in modulating pain perception and healthcare engagement, and the systemic barriers that impede access to appropriate care across diverse populations. A pervasive mechanistic thread involves hypothalamic-pituitary-axis dysregulation—whether through cortisol elevation secondary to chronic stress, melatonin suppression from circadian disruption, or hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis impairment from low energy availability—linking psychological burden to measurable reproductive pathophysiology. Elevated endometrial cortisol concentrations, for instance, were directly associated with altered expression of 182 genes governing implantation and immune response, providing molecular evidence that psychological stress independently compromises reproductive outcomes. Similarly, artificial light at night suppresses melatonin via suprachiasmatic nucleus signaling, disrupting gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion and downstream sex steroid production, with downstream consequences including dysmenorrhea and abnormal uterine bleeding.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Depression and anxiety emerged as the most consistently reported comorbidities across conditions including dysmenorrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, infertility, recurrent implantation failure, and sexual dysfunction. Depression independently predicted female sexual dysfunction with an odds ratio exceeding three, and negative cognitions and hyperarousal symptom clusters specifically mediated gynecological examination avoidance in trauma-exposed women. Psychosocial protective factors—including self-compassion, psychological resilience, family cohesion, and social support—were repeatedly identified as mitigating psychological distress and improving treatment adherence across infertility, adolescent menstrual disorders, and chronic pelvic conditions. Physical inactivity consistently emerged as a risk factor for menstrual disturbance, dysmenorrhea, and bruxism, while regular exercise demonstrated protective effects through anti-inflammatory and hormonal regulatory mechanisms. Visceral hypersensitivity and pelvic floor dysfunction appeared as shared pathophysiological substrates linking conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome and endometriosis to sexual dysfunction.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

A notable contradiction concerns body mass index and dysmenorrhea: one study of Ugandan adolescents reported that overweight and obese girls paradoxically experienced less menstrual pain than normal-weight peers, attributed to differential prostaglandin sensitivity, while other papers consistently framed adipose-driven estrogen excess as pain-amplifying. This discrepancy likely reflects population-specific confounders including nutritional status, pubertal stage, and measurement methodology rather than a true biological contradiction, but it underscores the limitations of extrapolating BMI-pain relationships across demographic contexts.

## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes

### Overview

Endometriosis has long been conceptualized as a disorder of ectopic endometrial implantation confined to the pelvic cavity; however, an expanding body of evidence fundamentally reframes this disease as a systemic, multimorbid condition with consequences extending across virtually every organ system. The core research question animating this literature is whether the comorbidity burden observed in women with endometriosis reflects shared upstream pathophysiological mechanisms—primarily chronic immune dysregulation, hormonal disruption, and central sensitization—or whether these associations are driven by detection bias, shared risk factors, and iatrogenic sequelae of treatment. Resolving this question carries profound implications for how endometriosis is diagnosed, managed, and conceptualized within clinical practice.

### Consensus Findings

The literature converges with notable consistency on several mechanistic and epidemiological findings. Systemic inflammatory dysregulation, mediated by cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-17, constitutes the most robustly replicated pathophysiological substrate linking endometriosis to its broader comorbidity profile. This pro-inflammatory milieu underpins co-occurrence with autoimmune disorders—including Hashimoto's thyroiditis, celiac disease, and rheumatoid arthritis—at rates substantially exceeding population prevalence, and contributes to the significantly elevated cardiovascular risk documented across multiple large cohort studies, including a 50% increased myocardial infarction risk and 39% increased stroke risk, particularly in women under 40. Notably, conventional cardioprotective agents including aspirin and statins have failed to attenuate this elevated risk, suggesting that endometriosis-specific inflammatory mechanisms, potentially involving the cyclooxygenase-2/thromboxane A2 axis and endothelial nitric oxide pathway dysfunction, operate through pathways not adequately targeted by standard preventive pharmacology.

Central sensitization represents a second mechanistically coherent consensus finding. Endometriosis co-occurs with fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, migraine, temporomandibular disorders, and vulvodynia at elevated rates, unified by shared nociplastic pain mechanisms including impaired conditioned pain modulation, enhanced temporal summation, and visceral hypersensitivity mediated by ion channels TRPV1, TRPA1, and TRPV4. Calcitonin gene-related peptide signaling in peptidergic nociceptors has been identified as a female-predominant amplifier of this sensitization, and increasing comorbidity burden progressively erodes descending inhibitory pain capacity, establishing a self-reinforcing cycle of nociceptive amplification. Hormonal dysregulation provides a third consensus axis: estrogen and progesterone fluctuations modulate serotonergic and dopaminergic neurotransmission, gut motility, adrenal axis reactivity, and vascular tone, explaining associations spanning premenstrual dysphoric disorder, peripartum depression, obstructive sleep apnea-associated insulin resistance, and long-COVID menstrual disturbances. Women with endometriosis demonstrate approximately five-fold increased odds of developing long-COVID, attributed to shared macrophage activation, cytokine dysregulation, and gut microbiota alterations. Health disparities are also consistently documented: Black patients are diagnosed with uterine fibroids—which co-occur with endometriosis at an odds ratio of 7.89—at more than three times the rate of White patients, while Black, Hispanic, and Indigenous patients experience substantially longer diagnostic delays, receive inferior analgesia, and face higher surgical complication rates, driven by structural racism and implicit clinical bias.

### Controversies and Contradictions

Several areas of genuine evidential conflict emerge. The reported association between COVID-19 vaccination and elevated endometriosis incidence (odds ratio 1.63) and menstrual disruption remains unresolved, with proposed mechanisms involving spike protein-induced cytokine storms disrupting folliculogenesis and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis function, while critics attribute findings to surveillance bias and ascertainment artifacts in post-vaccination healthcare-seeking behavior. Tension also persists regarding the relative contributions of physiological versus psychological factors to pain phenotyping: while latent profile analyses identify questionnaire-derived psychological variables as superior stratifiers of pain phenotype compared to objective physiological measures, parallel literature emphasizes objectively measurable inflammatory and structural pathways as primary determinants. Additionally, the causal direction underlying the depression-endometriosis association remains contested, as treated perinatal mental health conditions appear as pre-existing comorbidities in endometriosis cohorts, yet chronic pain and diagnostic delay independently generate depressive and anxiety disorders, making bidirectional pathways difficult to disentangle. Hormonal contraceptives prescribed for endometriosis symptom management paradoxically suppress central serotonergic activity through progestin-mediated monoamine oxidase-A upregulation and may worsen oral health outcomes, creating therapeutic conflicts not yet reconciled in clinical guidelines.

### Mechanistic Gaps

Substantial mechanistic lacunae persist. The precise biological pathways through which maternal endometriosis-associated immune activation elevates offspring risk of neurodevelopmental disorders including Tourette's disorder remain incompletely characterized, with neuroinflammatory disruption of fetal neurodevelopment proposed but insufficiently studied in human cohorts. The contribution of the gut–brain axis—encompassing microbiota composition, intestinal barrier integrity, and HLA-DQ2/DQ8-mediated immune vulnerability—to endometriosis pathogenesis and comorbidity generation has been identified but not mechanistically resolved. The sexually dimorphic metabolomic consequences of UGT2B17 and UGT2B28 glucuronidation pathway dysfunction, with female-specific alterations in androgen and fatty acid profiles, represent an understudied hormonal pathway potentially relevant to endometriosis-associated metabolic comorbidity. Furthermore, the long-term neurological consequences of chronic estrogen deficiency induced by both surgical menopause from hysterectomy and pharmacological suppression via GnRH agonists—including elevated tau phosphorylation, impaired amyloid-β clearance, and accelerated cognitive decline—require prospective characterization.

### Translational Relevance

The emerging mechanistic framework generates several clinically actionable implications. CGRP receptor antagonism, already approved for migraine prophylaxis, represents a rationally supported therapeutic target for endometriosis-associated

## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interconnected themes: systemic immune dysregulation as a unifying mechanism linking endometriosis to broader comorbidity profiles, chronic pain sensitization and its neurological underpinnings, hormonal disruption from endogenous and exogenous sources, and pervasive health disparities across racial, socioeconomic, and demographic groups. A recurring mechanistic framework implicates persistent inflammatory signaling—particularly involving cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8—alongside hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, gut–brain axis dysfunction, and aberrant immune activation in driving multisystemic disease beyond the reproductive tract.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

Endometriosis emerges repeatedly as a condition with far-reaching systemic consequences rather than isolated pelvic pathology. Most notably, women with endometriosis demonstrate a significantly elevated risk of venous thromboembolism (hazard ratio 1.79; incidence 5.2 per 10,000 woman-years), further amplified by oral contraceptive use, alongside increased prevalence of cardiovascular disease, autoimmune disorders, depression, hypertension, stroke, and cerebrovascular events. The condition also confers approximately a five-fold increased odds of developing long-COVID compared to controls, a finding attributed to shared immunopathological substrates including macrophage activation, cytokine dysregulation, and gut microbiota alterations that characterize both endometriosis and post-COVID syndrome. COVID-19 vaccination was independently associated with elevated endometriosis incidence (odds ratio 1.63) and menstrual disorders, with proposed mechanisms involving spike protein-induced cytokine storms disrupting folliculogenesis and hormonal axes, though whether this reflects true causation or detection bias remains unresolved.

Chronic pelvic pain associated with endometriosis consistently implicates impaired conditioned pain modulation, indicating dysfunction in descending inhibitory pain pathways, compounded by elevated pain catastrophizing and anxiety. Visceral hypersensitivity mediated by ion channels including TRPV1, TRPA1, and TRPV4, along with serotonergic and dopaminergic dysregulation, appears to mechanistically link endometriosis-associated pain to the disproportionately elevated rates of irritable bowel syndrome observed in women, where female sex hormones—estrogen and progesterone—modulate gut motility and sensory thresholds. Similarly, disorders of gut–brain interaction affect premenopausal women at substantially elevated rates, with menstrual cycle-correlated symptom exacerbation underscoring the hormonal sensitivity of visceral pain systems relevant to endometriosis comorbidity.

Endometriosis also emerged as the leading cause of surgical intervention for pregnancy achievement among cancer survivors, highlighting its intersection with infertility, which itself carries substantial psychosocial burden including elevated rates of depression, anxiety, and relationship dysfunction—disproportionately affecting women. Endocrine disruption through environmental exposures, exemplified by in-utero polybrominated biphenyl exposure elevating luteal phase progesterone metabolites, represents an additional mechanistic pathway potentially relevant to endometriosis pathogenesis and associated hormonal dysregulation.

Racial and ethnic disparities are consistently documented across conditions comorbid with endometriosis: Black patients are diagnosed with uterine fibroids at more than three times the rate of White patients, with proposed mechanisms spanning genetic predisposition, vitamin D deficiency, chronic psychosocial stress, and structural racism. Indigenous populations experience disproportionate burdens of chronic pelvic pain conditions including endometriosis and dysmenorrhea, driven by colonization-related barriers to healthcare access. Dysmenorrhea severity is robustly associated with trauma exposure—including sexual assault, domestic violence, and childhood abuse—across multiple study designs, situating menstrual pain firmly within a biopsychosocial framework shaped by social determinants.

Maternal autoimmune conditions, including endometriosis, are associated with increased offspring risk of neurodevelopmental disorders such as tics and Tourette's disorder (1.26-fold elevated risk), with proposed mechanisms involving maternal immune activation, excessive cytokine production, and neuroinflammatory disruption of fetal

## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: Synthesis of the Literature

The papers reviewed converge on several dominant themes: the systemic, multimorbid nature of endometriosis; shared immunogenetic and inflammatory mechanisms linking endometriosis to a broad spectrum of co-occurring conditions; pronounced sex- and hormone-mediated pathophysiology; and substantial patient-level burdens encompassing pain, reproductive impairment, mental health, and healthcare access.

**Shared immunogenetic and inflammatory pathways** emerge as the most consistently reported mechanistic framework. Endometriosis co-occurs with celiac disease, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, and autoimmune conditions at rates substantially exceeding population prevalence, with HLA-DQ2/DQ8 haplotypes (present in 43.3% of endometriosis patients in one cohort), intestinal barrier dysfunction, and dysbiosis identified as convergent vulnerabilities. Systemic inflammation mediated by pro-inflammatory cytokines—including interleukin-6 and interferon-gamma—links endometriosis to cardiovascular risk, with one large cohort study documenting elevated major adverse cardiovascular events, particularly stroke and coronary heart disease, in women under 40. Notably, conventional anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective interventions including aspirin and statins failed to attenuate this elevated risk, suggesting that endometriosis-specific inflammatory mechanisms may operate through pathways not adequately targeted by standard preventive pharmacology.

**Reproductive and hormonal dysregulation** constitute a second dominant theme. Coeliac disease conferred a 2.53-fold increased endometriosis risk alongside markedly elevated odds of dysmenorrhea (2.76-fold), irregular menstruation (3.65-fold), and premature menopause (3.13-fold) in a nationwide cohort, implicating anti-transglutaminase antibody-mediated impairment of endometrial angiogenesis and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis dysfunction. Uterine fibroids demonstrated the strongest phenotypic associations with endometriosis among gynecological conditions (odds ratio 7.89 for co-occurrence in a phenome-wide study), sharing dysmenorrhea, abnormal bleeding, and inflammatory-hormonal drivers including estrogen excess, vitamin D deficiency, and elevated BMI. The UGT2B17 and UGT2B28 glucuronidation pathway, critical for sex steroid and bile acid homeostasis, showed sexually dimorphic metabolomic consequences upon genetic knockout, with female-specific alterations in androgens and fatty acid profiles potentially contributing to endometriosis-related hormonal dysregulation. Obstructive sleep apnea in women—but not men—independently predicted insulin resistance and adrenal axis hyperactivation through nocturnal hypoxia-mediated mechanisms, identifying a metabolic-endocrine comorbidity axis particularly relevant to endometriosis patients with overlapping hormonal dysfunction.

**Chronic pain comorbidity** appears across multiple papers as both a diagnostic and a management challenge. Endometriosis co-occurred with fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and migraine across several datasets. Interstitial cystitis patients with normal or high bladder capacity—a phenotype associated with greater non-bladder symptom burden—showed significantly higher endometriosis prevalence, reinforcing the concept of a central sensitization or systemic inflammatory phenotype. Migraine was independently associated with uterine leiomyoma development, with vitamin D deficiency proposed as a mechanistic link through inhibition of leiomyoma cell proliferation and reduction of neurogenic pro-inflammatory cytokines. Ketamine, acting via NMDA receptor antagonism, demonstrated utility for chronic pain and comorbid depression in endometriosis-relevant conditions, though women experienced higher adverse event rates attributable to sex-differentiated CYP2B6 and CYP3A4 metabolism of ketamine and its active metabolites.

**Mental health comorbidities** are pervasively documented. Depression, anxiety, and chronic pain formed a tightly clustered comorbidity triad, with treated perinatal mental health conditions associated with higher endometriosis prevalence (documented as a pre-existing comorbidity in that cohort) and worse postpartum outcomes.
