## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across these papers converges on several interrelated themes: the systemic consequences of endometriosis extending well beyond the reproductive tract, the role of shared inflammatory and hormonal pathways in generating multisystem comorbidity, and the pervasive influence of structural inequities on diagnosis, treatment access, and health outcomes. Endometriosis emerges repeatedly not as an isolated pelvic condition but as a disease embedded within broader networks of chronic overlapping pain conditions (COPCs), autonomic dysfunction, cardiovascular risk, and immune dysregulation. Hormonal dysregulation—particularly involving estrogen, progesterone, and androgen signaling—constitutes a unifying mechanistic thread, with fluctuations across the menstrual cycle amplifying symptoms in conditions ranging from postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and ADHD to premenstrual dysphoric disorder and long COVID-associated menstrual disturbances.

Inflammatory pathway dysregulation is consistently implicated across multiple disease contexts. The cyclooxygenase-2/thromboxane A2 axis, identified within endometriotic lesions, contributes to attenuated cardiovascular reflex responses and endothelial dysfunction, providing a mechanistic explanation for the substantially elevated myocardial infarction and stroke risk observed in endometriosis patients. Shared Th1/Th17-mediated immune activation and upregulation of interleukin-17 and interleukin-23 appear across endometriosis, psoriasis, and autoimmune thyroid disease, suggesting convergent immunopathological pathways. Similarly, dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis links reproductive conditions to psychological comorbidities including anxiety, depression, and disordered eating, with estrogen modulating both dopaminergic neurotransmission and cortisol responsiveness in ways that amplify mood vulnerability across hormonal transitions.

The iatrogenic dimension of endometriosis receives increasing attention. Laparoscopic cystectomy for endometriomas is now recognized as a significant contributor to premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), with contemporary cohorts showing substantially higher iatrogenic POI rates compared to historical series, accompanied by reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels and downstream risks including osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline. POI itself carries a markedly elevated autoimmune comorbidity burden, most prominently autoimmune hypothyroidism (~20% prevalence), vitiligo (15-fold elevated risk), celiac disease (7.6-fold), and rheumatoid arthritis (5.7-fold), mediated in part through autoimmune polyglandular syndromes and antibodies targeting steroidogenic theca cells. These findings collectively position surgical management of endometriosis as a double-edged intervention requiring careful weighing of reproductive reserve consequences.

Several findings appear with notable consistency across this literature. First, endometriosis clusters with other COPCs—including irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS), fibromyalgia, vulvodynia, and migraine—through shared nociplastic pain mechanisms involving central sensitization, visceral hypersensitivity, and altered gut-brain signaling. IBS and endometriosis co-occur at elevated rates, with psychological stress activating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, producing neuroimaging-detectable changes including reduced cortical thickness in pain-regulating regions and altered resting-state activity in somatosensory and cingulate cortices. Second, cardiovascular risk is robustly elevated in endometriosis, supported by evidence of autonomic dysregulation demonstrated through blunted pressor responses to cold pressor and handgrip testing, endothelial dysfunction with impaired nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation, and epidemiological data showing 50% increased myocardial infarction risk and 39% increased stroke risk. ## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: A Synthesis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interconnected themes: systemic health inequities in endometriosis diagnosis and management, the extensive comorbidity burden associated with endometriosis, shared neurobiological and inflammatory mechanisms linking endometriosis to multisystem disease, and the downstream consequences of hormonal dysregulation. A particularly prominent thread concerns how diagnostic delays—themselves patterned by race, ethnicity, obesity, and socioeconomic status—compound downstream clinical inequities, creating cascading disadvantages in treatment access and outcomes.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

**Health disparities and diagnostic inequity** emerge as the most robustly replicated theme. Multiple independent studies document that Black, Hispanic, and American Indian/Alaska Native patients experience substantially longer delays to endometriosis diagnosis, receive fewer appropriate pain medications including both opioid and non-opioid analgesics, face higher rates of postoperative complications, and are more likely to experience treatment delays for endometriosis-associated malignancies such as endometrioid ovarian carcinoma. These disparities appear attributable to converging factors including implicit clinician bias, false stereotypes regarding pain reporting and drug-seeking behavior, reduced cultural competency, and structural barriers to specialist care. Socioeconomic disadvantage, measured variously by income, deprivation indices, and insurance status, independently predicts poor reproductive health outcomes across multiple national datasets from England, the United States, and China.

**Central sensitization and visceral pain crosstalk** represent a mechanistically coherent second theme. Endometriosis-associated dysmenorrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, painful bladder syndrome, and migraine share overlapping pathophysiology involving peripheral and central sensitization, with bidirectional therapeutic relationships documented such that effective treatment of one visceral pain condition measurably reduces pain frequency and somatic hyperalgesia in comorbid conditions. Calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) signaling, particularly in peptidergic nociceptors, has been identified as a female-predominant mechanism amplifying neuropathic and inflammatory pain, with CGRP receptor antagonism reversing established pain in female experimental models. Interoceptive dysregulation—impaired perception and integration of internal bodily signals—appears as a shared mechanism across endometriosis-related pain, disorders of gut-brain interaction, and anxiety, with specific interoceptive traits such as heightened worrying and reduced trust independently modulating pain interference and emotional regulation in endometriosis patients.

**Hormonal pathways linking endometriosis to cardiovascular and metabolic comorbidity** are consistently implicated. Hysterectomy, frequently performed for endometriosis-related indications, causally elevates blood pressure through estrogen deficiency mechanisms confirmed by Mendelian randomization, with younger age at hysterectomy conferring particularly elevated cardiovascular risk. Multiple studies identify endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome as emerging non-traditional cardiovascular risk factors in young women, with female acute myocardial infarction patients demonstrating higher comorbidity clustering and worse outcomes compared to males. Estrogen's vasodilatory, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective roles appear central to these associations, with estrogen deficiency—whether surgical, pharmacological, or age-related—accelerating vascular smooth muscle proliferation, insulin resistance, and dyslipidemia. The serotonergic system similarly responds to endogenous and exogenous hormonal variation: hormonal contraceptives, widely prescribed for endometriosis management, suppress central serotonergic activity through progestin-dominant mechanisms involving elevated monoamine oxidase-A activity, potentially explaining the mood disturbances and anxiety exacerbations observed in this population.

**Multimorbidity and systemic inflammatory burden** in women with endometriosis extend substantially beyond the reproductive system. Endometriosis co-occurs with thyroid autoimmunity (notably Hashimoto's disease), gastrointestinal disorders including irritable bowel syndrome and functional bloating, anxiety and depressive disorders, migraine, and recurrent urinary tract infections. Shared inflammatory mechanisms—involving TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and dysregulated innate immune activation—appear to underpin many of these associations. Obesity and progressive weight gain during reproductive years are independently associated with endometriosis, ute

## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: Synthesis of Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 papers, several interconnected themes emerge with remarkable consistency. First, endometriosis functions not as an isolated gynecological disorder but as a condition embedded within a broader landscape of systemic comorbidity, encompassing chronic pain syndromes, sleep disturbance, psychiatric conditions, autoimmune dysregulation, and reproductive dysfunction. Second, a pervasive theme of sex- and gender-based health disparities runs through virtually every paper, with female sex independently predicting worse outcomes across pain trajectories, sleep quality, fatigue burden, treatment persistence, and diagnostic delay. Third, central sensitization and neuroinflammatory amplification emerge as mechanistic threads linking endometriosis to comorbid conditions including fibromyalgia, temporomandibular disorders, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and chronic pelvic pain syndrome.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The co-occurrence of endometriosis with fibromyalgia, IBS, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, and chronic pelvic pain is reported across multiple independent studies, suggesting shared pathophysiological substrates rather than coincidental clustering. Central sensitization—characterized by impaired conditioned pain modulation, enhanced temporal summation, and compromised descending inhibitory pathways—is repeatedly implicated as the mechanistic bridge connecting these conditions. Studies examining temporomandibular disorders, fibromyalgia, and chronic pelvic pain syndrome converge on the finding that increasing comorbidity burden progressively erodes pain inhibitory capacity, producing a self-reinforcing cycle of amplified nociception.

Hormonal dysregulation constitutes a second major mechanistic axis. Estrogen and progesterone signaling disruption appears not only in endometriosis pathophysiology but also in associated sleep disturbance, peripartum depression, Alzheimer's disease risk, and menopausal health outcomes. Specifically, declining 17β-estradiol is linked to reduced neuroprotection, elevated tau phosphorylation, impaired amyloid-β clearance, and disrupted descending pain modulation. The allopregnanolone pathway is identified in the context of peripartum depression and hormonal sensitivity, with women exhibiting mood changes on oral contraceptives showing elevated endometriosis and premenstrual syndrome prevalence—suggesting a shared neuroendocrine vulnerability phenotype. Follicle-stimulating hormone elevation during menopause and perimenopause is consistently associated with amyloidogenic processes and sleep disruption, indicating that reproductive aging constitutes a systemic inflammatory and neurodegenerative transition extending well beyond the reproductive tract.

Psychiatric comorbidity, particularly depression and anxiety, is among the most consistently reported findings across papers addressing pain, sleep, multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and fertility treatment. Depression and anxiety independently predict worse pain trajectories, reduced treatment persistence for disease-modifying therapies, impaired quality of life in fertility patients, and exacerbated fatigue in post-COVID syndrome and IBD. The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, with its documented sex-differential reactivity, is proposed as a convergent pathway mediating stress-immune dysregulation across these conditions.

Socioeconomic and structural disparities are identified as fundamental determinants of clinical trajectory rather than merely contextual factors. Female sex combined with lower educational attainment, lower wealth, and racial minority status consistently predicts worse pain outcomes, delayed diagnosis, greater comorbidity burden, and reduced access to minimally invasive surgical approaches. Black women specifically are documented to face higher rates of hysterectomy complications, reproductive health disparities, maternal mortality, and dismissal of symptoms, with structural racism and implicit provider bias identified as independent drivers. Geographic disparities compound these inequities, with rural and low-socioeconomic-status populations experiencing more severe endometriosis-related educational disruption and reduced access to evidence-based care.

Bladder hypersensitivity emerges as a particularly salient, potentially under-recognized comorbidity in endometriosis. A dedicated study using progressive bladder filling paradigms demonstrates that patients with comorbid pelvic pain and bladder symptoms exhibit significantly elevated pain scores at all filling timepoints compared to pain-only patients, with concurrent elevations in gastrointestinal symptom burden and state anxiety. This finding positions bladder dysfunction within a systemic hypersensitivity

## Endometriosis Comorbidities and Patient Outcomes: Synthesis

The papers in this cluster converge on a central theme: endometriosis rarely presents as an isolated condition but is embedded within broader constellations of systemic, immunological, and psychosocial comorbidities that collectively amplify patient burden and complicate clinical management.

**Dominant Themes and Shared Mechanisms**

A consistently prominent mechanism across multiple papers is chronic low-grade inflammation mediated by dysregulated cytokine profiles, particularly elevated IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α. This shared immunopathological milieu links endometriosis to periodontal disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and uterine fibroids, suggesting that systemic inflammatory dysregulation—rather than purely local pelvic pathology—underlies many observed comorbidities. Connective tissue dysfunction emerges as a second recurring theme, with endometriosis co-occurring at elevated rates in patients with hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and POTS, populations already characterized by collagen disorganization and autonomic dysfunction. Psychosocial burden constitutes a third dominant thread: women with endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain exhibit elevated anxiety, depression, sleep disturbance, and pain catastrophizing, and latent profile analysis identifies heterogeneous pain phenotypes driven more by psychological than physiological measures, underscoring the nociplastic dimensions of the condition.

**Consistently Reported Findings**

Diagnostic delay is repeatedly identified as an independent amplifier of disease severity, with delays exceeding six years significantly worsening both systemic and oral manifestations. Racial and socioeconomic disparities in access to appropriate surgical and contraceptive management—including minimally invasive hysterectomy and hormonal IUDs—are consistently documented, with structural barriers compounding biological vulnerability. Sigmoid elongation is identified as a clinically important comorbidity in bowel endometriosis, potentially explaining refractory gastrointestinal symptoms postoperatively. Endometriosis association with ovarian carcinosarcoma (33% of cases) highlights malignant transformation risk warranting surveillance.

**Contradictions and Conflicts**

Some tension exists regarding the relative contribution of physiological versus psychological factors to pain phenotyping: while one study finds questionnaire-based stratification superior to physiological measures, others emphasize objectively measurable inflammatory and structural pathways. Additionally, hormonal therapies intended to treat endometriosis paradoxically worsen oral health outcomes, creating therapeutic conflicts not yet resolved in the literature.

# PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis: Mechanistic Overlap and Emerging Therapeutic Targets

## Overview

A central question animating this body of literature is whether polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), insulin resistance, and endometriosis constitute mechanistically distinct disorders that happen to co-occur, or whether they share sufficient pathophysiological terrain to be understood as interrelated expressions of a common metabolic-inflammatory-reproductive dysfunction. With PCOS affecting an estimated 8–13% of reproductive-age women and insulin resistance present in 35–95% of affected individuals regardless of body mass index, and with endometriosis independently affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, the clinical significance of their overlap—documented at substantially elevated comorbidity rates with associated infertility rates reaching 25.6% in dual-diagnosis cohorts versus 15.4% in PCOS alone—is considerable. The literature reviewed here, encompassing 247 articles, systematically interrogates the molecular, genetic, epigenetic, and environmental determinants of this overlap.

## Consensus Findings

Hyperinsulinemia-driven hyperandrogenism constitutes the most reproducibly described mechanistic axis across independent study cohorts and methodologies. Excess insulin directly stimulates ovarian theca cell androgen biosynthesis through insulin receptor and IGF-1 receptor signaling, upregulates CYP17A1 expression, suppresses aromatase (CYP19A1) activity, and reduces hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin synthesis, thereby amplifying free androgen bioavailability and perpetuating follicular arrest and anovulation. Insulin receptor substrate-1 serine phosphorylation, mediated by JNK and IKKβ pathways, disrupts downstream PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling in granulosa cells and endometrial tissue simultaneously, impeding both folliculogenesis and endometrial receptivity. Elevated androgens reciprocally worsen insulin resistance through NADPH oxidase-driven reactive oxygen species production, mitochondrial membrane depolarization, and PGC-1α/AMPK pathway dysregulation, creating a self-amplifying endocrine-metabolic loop.

Chronic low-grade inflammation, mediated principally by TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and NF-κB signaling, is consistently documented as both a consequence and an independent perpetuator of insulin resistance, operating through NLRP3 inflammasome activation in adipose, hepatic, and ovarian compartments. This inflammatory milieu drives granulosa cell apoptosis via BAX and caspase-3 upregulation, impairs oocyte maturation, and reduces endometrial implantation markers including leukemia inhibitory factor, HOXA10, and progestagen-associated endometrial protein. The leptin-to-adiponectin ratio emerges as a reproducible integrative marker of metabolic severity: elevated leptin directly inhibits ovulation while reduced adiponectin removes tonic suppression of androgen synthesis, with visceral adiposity—rather than BMI alone—more accurately predicting reproductive risk. Gut microbiota dysbiosis, characterized by depletion of short-chain fatty acid-producing organisms including Lachnospiraceae and Blautia alongside expansion of Escherichia-Shigella, creates a bidirectional relationship with insulin resistance through impaired mucosal barrier integrity and systemic endotoxemia. Genetic architecture studies consistently implicate regulatory variants at DENND1A, THADA, LHCGR, INSR, and the IRX3-FTO locus, with transcription factor binding disruption affecting NR4A1/2, NR5A1, and FOXA1—factors governing steroidogenesis across reproductive and metabolic tissues. At the intersection of PCOS and endometriosis specifically, insulin resistance enhances local aromatase activity and estrogen bioavailability, promoting ectopic endometrial lesion survival, while endometriotic lesions reciprocally secrete TNF-α and IL-6 that perpetuate systemic insulin resistance.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive tensions remain unresolved. The relative contributions of central hypothalamic dysfunction versus peripheral ovarian pathology to PCOS initiation are contested: mouse genetic models implicating brain-specific GATA4 dysregulation support a neuroendocrine-first hypothesis, whereas the predominance of gonadal steroidogenic abnormalities in clinical human data supports an ovarian-primary model. These frameworks may not be mutually exclusive, but their resolution carries implications for whether treatment should target central LH pulsatility or peripheral androgen synthesis preferentially. GnRH agonist pretreatment in programmed frozen embryo transfer improves outcomes in specific PCOS subgroups but shows no consistent benefit across the broader population, underscoring unresolved patient stratification criteria. Furthermore, while controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in PCOS reliably produces greater oocyte yields, this quantitative advantage does not consistently translate into improved fertilization rates, blastocyst development, or live birth rates, suggesting that follicular environment quality rather than quantity is the limiting variable. The causal directionality of gut dysbiosis relative to insulin resistance—whether microbial compositional changes precede or follow metabolic deterioration—similarly remains unclear from cross-sectional data.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Despite extensive characterization of the hyperinsulinemia-hyperandrogenism-inflammation axis, several mechanistic domains remain understudied. Glycosylation dysregulation, altered bile acid homeostasis, and glutamine metabolism disruption in the endometrial microenvironment are identified as potentially disease-relevant processes, but causal mechanistic evidence connecting these metabolic alterations to endometriotic lesion establishment or PCOS endometrial dysfunction is limited. The specific molecular crosstalk between endometriotic lesions and systemic insulin resistance—beyond reciprocal cytokine secretion—has not been mechanistically dissected in prospective human models. Follicular fluid metabolomics reveals condition-specific signatures distinguishing PCOS from endometriosis, including opposing patterns of ceramide and phosphatidylcholine abundance, yet the functional consequences of these microenvironmental differences for oocyte epigenetic programming and embryo developmental competence are largely unexplored. Cellular senescence mediated by p53/p21 signaling and the senescence-associated secretory phenotype represent emerging but insufficiently characterized contributors to follicular atresia and endometrial dysfunction that warrant dedicated investigation.

## PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis Overlap: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The corpus of reviewed literature converges on several dominant mechanistic themes underlying polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), its metabolic comorbidities, and its overlap with endometriosis. Across studies, insulin resistance emerges as the central metabolic driver, present in 75–95% of PCOS patients, operating through hyperinsulinemia-mediated stimulation of ovarian androgen synthesis, suppression of sex hormone-binding globulin, and impairment of folliculogenesis. This insulin-androgen axis is consistently reported as a self-reinforcing pathological loop: elevated androgens worsen insulin resistance through NADPH oxidase-driven reactive oxygen species production, mitochondrial dysfunction via PGC-1α/AMPK dysregulation, and visceral lipid accumulation, while hyperinsulinemia reciprocally amplifies androgen secretion from theca cells.

Oxidative stress and chronic low-grade inflammation represent the most consistently reported downstream consequences of this hormonal-metabolic dysregulation. Elevated inflammatory cytokines—including TNF-α, IL-6, MCP-1, and MIP-1α/β—are documented through both observational and Mendelian randomization approaches, with dyslipidemia further compounding follicular inflammation and impairing oocyte maturation. Mitochondrial DNA copy number alterations and telomere shortening are identified as molecular markers of cumulative oxidative damage in both PCOS and ovarian endometriosis, with Mendelian randomization evidence supporting a directional causal relationship from elevated mtDNA copy number to disease risk. Follicular fluid metabolomics further corroborates these findings, revealing disease-specific signatures: PCOS follicular fluid demonstrates depleted amino acids, reduced lipid species including ceramides and phosphatidylcholines, and elevated glucose, whereas endometriosis presents with opposing lipid elevations, suggesting mechanistically distinct but partially overlapping microenvironmental pathologies.

Genetic architecture studies consistently implicate loci at *THADA*, *DENND1A*, *LHCGR*, *INSR*, and *FTO*, with regulatory variants predominantly disrupting transcription factor binding sites for NR4A1/2, NR5A1, and FOXA1—factors governing steroidogenesis and folliculogenesis across reproductive and metabolic tissues including the pancreas, adipocytes, and fetal brain. Epigenetic dysregulation, including hypomethylation of metabolic genes such as *LEP*, *LEPR*, and *ADIPOR2*, further links obesity-associated insulin resistance to PCOS pathogenesis. The HIF-1 signaling pathway and its downstream targets NFKB1, INSR, and PRKCA are identified as a novel convergence point between hemoglobin-related hypoxia and hyperandrogenism.

Environmental contributors are increasingly recognized, with prenatal and postnatal exposure to perfluoroalkyl substances—particularly PFOS and EtFOSAA—associated with PCOS risk and hyperandrogenism through disruption of HPO axis steroidogenesis and thyroid hormone synthesis. Dietary glycemic index, trace element imbalances including molybdenum deficiency and copper excess, vitamin D deficiency, and circadian rhythm disruption via BMAL1 downregulation represent additional modifiable environmental and nutritional determinants that interact with the core insulin resistance–androgen excess axis.

At the molecular regulatory level, multiple microRNA species—including miR-34a-5p, miR-93-3p, and miR-152-3p—are dysregulated in PCOS follicular fluid extracellular vesicles, targeting PI3K/Akt, MAPK, NOTCH, and JAG1 pathways to impair granulosa cell proliferation and promote apoptosis. The PD-L1/PI3K/AKT axis and BMAL1/BMP6 signaling are identified as additional regulators of granulosa cell survival and oocyte quality, offering candidate therapeutic targets.

Clinically, PCOS patients consistently demonstrate elevated LH/FSH ratios, elevated AMH, hyperandrogenemia, dyslipidemia with elevated triglycerides and LDL alongside reduced HD

## PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis: Synthesis of Mechanistic and Clinical Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interrelated pathophysiological axes linking polycystic ovary syndrome, insulin resistance, obesity, and reproductive dysfunction. Insulin resistance and its downstream consequence, hyperinsulinemia, emerge as central mediators that amplify ovarian androgen synthesis, suppress sex hormone-binding globulin, impair endometrial receptivity, and drive chronic low-grade inflammation. This metabolic foundation is consistently coupled with mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and disrupted energy metabolism in granulosa cells, collectively impairing folliculogenesis, oocyte quality, and implantation. Epigenetic dysregulation—encompassing aberrant DNA methylation, histone acetylation, m6A RNA modification, and non-coding RNA activity—represents an additional mechanistic layer linking hyperandrogenism to reproductive and metabolic failure. Circadian rhythm disruption, gut dysbiosis, and environmental exposures including endocrine-disrupting chemicals further compound HPO axis dysfunction.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Elevated luteinizing hormone, testosterone, and anti-Müllerian hormone alongside reduced FSH-mediated follicular recruitment are uniformly reported as hallmark hormonal disturbances. Hyperinsulinemia-driven upregulation of CYP17A1 and suppression of aromatase (CYP19A1) activity are repeatedly implicated in sustaining hyperandrogenism. Inflammatory cytokines—particularly IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β—consistently appear in follicular fluid and systemic circulation, correlating with granulosa cell apoptosis, impaired oocyte maturation, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including gestational diabetes and miscarriage. Metformin is the most consistently endorsed pharmacological intervention, addressing insulin resistance, reducing androgen levels, and improving endometrial function. Dyslipidemia and cholesterol metabolism disruption appear as shared mechanistic features connecting PCOS and endometriosis, with Mendelian randomization evidence supporting statin and PCSK9 inhibitor protective effects against both conditions. Obesity synergistically exacerbates all these pathways, with visceral fat distribution—rather than BMI alone—more accurately predicting reproductive risk.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several tensions exist within the literature. GnRH agonist pretreatment in programmed frozen embryo transfer improved outcomes in specific PCOS subgroups but demonstrated no significant benefit across the broader PCOS population, reflecting unresolved uncertainty about patient selection criteria. While weight loss is universally endorsed as first-line intervention, one study noted that lifestyle and dietary interventions paradoxically risk precipitating eating disorders in PCOS patients, particularly when weight-related stigma accompanies treatment. Additionally, controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in PCOS produced greater oocyte yields but did not consistently translate into improved fertilization, blastocyst development, or live birth rates, suggesting quantitative ovarian response and qualitative reproductive outcomes are dissociable. The relative contributions of central hypothalamic versus peripheral ovarian dysfunction to PCOS pathogenesis also remain incompletely resolved, with mouse genetic data implicating brain-specific GATA4 dysregulation independently of ovarian mechanisms, contrasting with predominantly gonadal frameworks in other reports.

## PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis Overlap: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 articles, insulin resistance emerges unambiguously as the central pathophysiological axis of polycystic ovary syndrome, with evidence spanning genetic, molecular, cellular, and clinical levels. Insulin resistance is estimated to affect 35–80% of PCOS patients regardless of body mass index, and its downstream consequences—hyperinsulinemia, compensatory androgen excess, chronic low-grade inflammation, and metabolic dysregulation—propagate dysfunction across ovarian, endometrial, vascular, and systemic compartments. A second dominant theme is the convergence of multiple amplifying mechanisms on this insulin-resistant core: adipokine dysregulation, oxidative stress, epigenetic remodeling, gut microbiota dysbiosis, immune activation, and neuroendocrine disturbance are consistently invoked as interacting reinforcing loops rather than independent processes.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The hyperinsulinemia–hyperandrogenism axis is the most reproducibly described mechanism. Excess insulin directly stimulates theca cell androgen biosynthesis via insulin receptor and IGF-1 receptor signaling while suppressing hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin production, thereby amplifying free androgen bioavailability. This drives anovulation, follicular arrest, and impaired oocyte competence. Multiple studies converge on the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway as a critical node where insulin resistance and reproductive dysfunction intersect; dysregulation of this pathway in granulosa cells and endometrial tissue impairs both folliculogenesis and endometrial receptivity. Inflammatory mediators—particularly TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and NF-κB—are consistently elevated and mechanistically linked to insulin signaling impairment through serine phosphorylation of insulin receptor substrate proteins, establishing a bidirectional relationship between metabolic and inflammatory dysfunction.

Adipokine imbalance, specifically elevated leptin and reduced adiponectin, is repeatedly reported as an intermediary amplifying both insulin resistance and androgen excess, with leptin directly inhibiting ovulation and adiponectin promoting follicle growth. The leptin-to-adiponectin ratio thus serves as an integrative marker of metabolic severity and reproductive prognosis. Several studies identify the AMPK and SIRT1 pathways as therapeutic targets—activated by resveratrol, metformin, and L-carnitine—that converge on restoring insulin sensitivity and normalizing androgen production.

Endometrial dysfunction in PCOS is characterized with notable consistency across multiple studies. The endometrium demonstrates elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17, IL-18, TNF-α), aberrant PI3K/AKT/MAPK and Wnt/β-catenin signaling, dysregulated expression of implantation-critical genes including leukemia inhibitory factor, HOXA10, and progestagen-associated endometrial protein, and impaired decidualization. These abnormalities collectively reduce endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation rates while increasing miscarriage risk, with PCOS patients showing approximately 1.47-fold higher abortion risk and 5-fold higher early pregnancy loss rates than controls. Chronic endometritis, found in 44.4% of PCOS patients compared to 22.3% of controls in one study, is mechanistically linked to HIF-1α-mediated hypoxia activating endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways (PERK/ATF4/CHOP), which in turn amplify local inflammatory cascades.

The overlap with endometriosis, though less extensively characterized within this cluster, is contextually supported by shared mechanistic substrates. Vitamin D deficiency is consistently associated with both PCOS and endometriosis, impairing granulosa cell proliferation, reducing endometrial receptivity, and worsening inflammatory tone through VDR-mediated regulation of over 1,000 target genes. Inflammatory pathway activation, oxidative stress, and impaired progesterone signaling—central to endometriosis pathogenesis—are also hallmarks of PCOS endometrial dysfunction, suggesting common molecular terrain.

## PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis Overlap: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across these 50 papers, four interconnected mechanistic themes dominate: insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism as co-drivers of reproductive dysfunction; chronic low-grade inflammation mediated by cytokines and inflammasome activation; mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress as unifying pathophysiological mechanisms; and impaired endometrial receptivity as a downstream consequence shared by PCOS and endometriosis. Obesity consistently emerges as a critical amplifier of these processes, operating through visceral adipose-derived free fatty acids, dysregulated adipokines, and inflammatory mediator release that collectively perpetuate the hyperandrogenic, insulin-resistant phenotype.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MAPK signaling cascades are repeatedly identified as central mediators linking insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism to impaired granulosa cell function, follicular arrest, and anovulation. Elevated androgens, acting through androgen receptor signaling, suppress sex hormone-binding globulin, promote visceral adiposity, and dysregulate CYP11A1 and CYP19A1 steroidogenic enzymes—findings reported across multiple independent studies. Anti-Müllerian hormone elevation is consistently documented as both a diagnostic marker and a functional contributor to follicular developmental arrest. The NLRP3 inflammasome and its downstream cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α are recurrently implicated in sustaining the chronic inflammatory milieu that impairs oocyte quality, endometrial receptivity, and implantation. Vitamin D deficiency is independently and reproducibly associated with worsened insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, reduced endometrial HOXA10 expression, and poorer IVF outcomes, with supplementation demonstrating improvements across metabolic and reproductive parameters. Mitochondrial dysfunction—characterized by reduced ATP production, elevated reactive oxygen species, and mtDNA deletions—is consistently linked to granulosa cell apoptosis, insulin resistance severity, and diminished ovarian reserve. Metformin's therapeutic efficacy is attributed not only to insulin sensitization but also to microRNA-mediated suppression of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, specifically through upregulation of miR-223 and miR-132.

At the intersection of PCOS and endometriosis, shared pathogenic mechanisms include IL-6-driven MAPK signaling, dysregulated autophagy and ubiquitination, compensatory miR-146a-5p upregulation targeting TRAF6 and IRAK1, and convergent p53/FoxO signaling governing granulosa cell apoptosis and follicular loss. FSHR polymorphisms (rs6165, rs6166) demonstrate differential associations with PCOS, endometriosis susceptibility, and ovarian cancer risk, indicating shared genetic architecture. Follicular fluid metabolomic profiling identifies condition-specific signatures—including dysregulated citrate, pyruvate, ceramides, and branched-chain amino acids—that distinguish PCOS from endometriosis while reflecting shared carbohydrate and amino acid metabolic disruption.

Glycolytic reprogramming represents an emerging mechanistic theme, with enhanced aerobic glycolysis in granulosa cells and endometrium—driven by hyperinsulinemia activating IGF-1/PI3K-Akt-mTOR and by androgen-induced mitochondrial damage—contributing to lactic acid accumulation, reduced intracellular pH, and accelerated apoptosis. Cellular senescence mediated by p53/p21 signaling and senescence-associated secretory phenotype components including IL-6, IL-1β, and GDF-15 further connects oxidative stress to follicular atresia and endometrial dysfunction in PCOS. Central adiposity, quantified by waist-to-hip ratio, demonstrates causal Mendelian randomization-supported associations with anovulatory infertility, with PCOS representing the most prevalent underlying etiology.

The global disease burden literature consistently documents escalating PCOS prevalence, particularly in high-sociodemographic-index regions and among adolescents, with strong correlations to rising obesity rates. Endometriosis burden, by contrast, peaks in women aged

## PCOS, Insulin Resistance, and Endometriosis: Mechanistic Overlap and Shared Pathophysiology

The 47 papers in this cluster converge on insulin resistance, chronic low-grade inflammation, and hormonal dysregulation as the dominant interconnected mechanisms driving polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis and its overlap with endometriosis. Across studies, hyperinsulinemia consistently emerges as a central amplifier of ovarian androgen production, operating through suppression of hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin synthesis, direct stimulation of ovarian theca cell steroidogenesis, and dysregulation of gonadotropin secretion producing abnormal LH:FSH ratios. Multiple papers independently confirm that PCOS-associated insulin resistance impairs downstream insulin signaling through IRS-1 serine phosphorylation mediated by JNK/IKKβ pathways, with NLRP3 inflammasome activation and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) perpetuating this resistance cycle across adipose, hepatic, and ovarian compartments. The kynurenine pathway, immune dysregulation involving altered CD4+/CD8+ T cell ratios and NK cell dysfunction, and epigenetic mechanisms including m6A RNA methylation and TET2-mediated 5-hydroxymethylcytosine modifications further contribute to the pathogenic cascade.

Granulosa cell dysfunction emerges as a particularly consistent cellular locus of PCOS pathology. Impaired steroidogenic enzyme expression (CYP11A1, CYP19A1, STAR), reduced glycolytic capacity through downregulation of GLUT1, HK2, and LDHA, mitochondrial dysfunction with compromised membrane potential and maximal respiration, and elevated apoptotic signaling via BAX and caspase-3 are reported across mechanistically independent studies. PLAU-mediated NF-κB activation, BPA-induced epigenetic disruption, and NLRP3 pyroptosis all converge on granulosa cell compromise as a final common pathway linking endocrine, metabolic, and environmental insults to follicular arrest and anovulation. The AMPK-LONP1-steroidogenic protein axis identified in exercise studies and the USP45-PRKAA1 autophagy axis described in epigenetic research represent complementary regulatory nodes controlling the same downstream dysfunction.

Gut microbiota dysbiosis represents another consistently reported mechanism, with specific taxa—including Lachnospiraceae, Blautia, Escherichia-Shigella, and reduced glucose-utilizing organisms such as Alicyclobacillus—correlating with elevated fasting glucose, BMI, and androgen levels across independent cohorts studying both systemic circulation and follicular fluid microenvironments. Short-chain fatty acid depletion consequent to dysbiosis further impairs insulin sensitivity and promotes inflammation, creating a bidirectional relationship between metabolic dysfunction and microbial community composition. Follicular fluid metabolomics consistently demonstrates elevated D-glucose and disrupted amino acid metabolism alongside these microbial alterations, with AMH and AFC significantly associated with the degree of metabolic-microbial perturbation.

The endometriosis-PCOS comorbidity is mechanistically explicable through several shared pathways highlighted across multiple papers. Insulin resistance in PCOS enhances local aromatase activity, increasing estrogen bioavailability that promotes ectopic endometrial lesion growth, while endometriotic lesions reciprocally secrete TNF-α and IL-6 that perpetuate systemic inflammation and worsen insulin resistance. The cross-sectional study directly examining this comorbidity confirms substantially worse infertility rates (25.6% versus 15.4%) and quality of life in women carrying both diagnoses, consistent with additive or synergistic pathological interaction. Glycosylation dysregulation, altered bile acid homeostasis dependent on glycemic status, and glutamine metabolism disruption in the endometrial microenvironment represent additional molecular mechanisms linking the two conditions and offering potential therapeutic targets.

Genetic and transcriptomic analyses across the cluster identify several recurrently implicated loci and regulatory factors. DENND1A, THADA, FSHB, and the IRX3-FTO locus appear across independent genomic studies, with regulatory

# Endometriosis: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis

## Overview

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder defined by the ectopic growth of endometrial-like tissue, yet a central and recurring question in the contemporary literature concerns why its clinical expression is so profoundly heterogeneous and why accurate, timely diagnosis remains elusive despite decades of research. The core mechanistic framework integrates retrograde menstruation as an initiating event with sustained downstream pathology driven by estrogen-aromatase positive feedback loops, progesterone resistance, peritoneal immune dysregulation, fibrogenesis, and neuroinflammation. The literature frames endometriosis not merely as a pelvic disorder but as a systemic condition whose manifestations span reproductive, gastrointestinal, urological, neurological, and psychiatric domains, demanding a diagnostic approach commensurate with this complexity.

## Consensus Findings

The literature converges robustly on a canonical symptom cluster—dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and infertility—present across virtually all clinical series and geographic settings. Dysmenorrhea, particularly when progressive, is the single strongest symptom-based predictor of surgically confirmed disease and has been associated with an approximately 19-fold increased risk of adult endometriosis when onset occurs in adolescence. The three principal disease phenotypes—superficial peritoneal lesions, ovarian endometriomas, and deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE)—demonstrate distinct inflammatory and immune profiles: ovarian endometriomas associate with elevated IL-8, CA19-9, and monocytosis, while DIE additionally features galectin-1, elevated CEA, and greater macrophage activation, with the combined phenotype producing the most severe clinical burden and the greatest post-surgical decline in anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), quantified at up to 62.2% reduction.

The estrogen-prostaglandin E2-aromatase positive feedback loop is universally cited as perpetuating local estrogen excess within lesions, while TGF-β1–mediated fibrogenesis is identified as a central driver of lesion invasiveness and follicular destruction. Neuroangiogenesis, propagated through nerve growth factor–TrkA signaling, and central sensitization—evidenced by decreased gray matter volume in pain-processing regions including the thalamus, cingulate gyrus, and insula—collectively underpin the neuropathic-like pain phenotype observed in approximately one-third of patients and explain its frequent dissociation from anatomical disease burden. The rASRM staging system is consistently criticized for failing to correlate with symptom severity or predict clinical outcomes, while the ENZIAN and AAGL2021 classification systems demonstrate superior anatomical precision and correlation with surgical complexity.

Diagnostically, laparoscopy with histopathological confirmation remains the gold standard, but transvaginal ultrasound incorporating dynamic assessments—the uterine sliding sign, sonovaginography, and the novel "twisting sign"—achieves sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90% for ovarian endometriomas and posterior compartment DIE in experienced hands. MRI, employing T1-weighted fat-saturated and T2-weighted sequences with T2 shading and T2 dark spot sign recognition, provides complementary compartment-specific characterization, with structured endometriosis-specific reporting templates improving documentation completeness up to 13-fold over free-text approaches. Serum CA-125, the most extensively studied biomarker, offers diagnostic value primarily in combination with inflammatory indices such as the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, achieving combined AUC values of up to 0.903, though it remains insufficient as a standalone diagnostic marker. Emerging machine learning algorithms integrating genetic, imaging, clinical, and liquid biopsy data—including microRNA signatures from urine and saliva targeting miR-10b, miR-21, miR-145, and miR-183-3p—report AUC values of 0.979–0.983, substantially outperforming individual conventional modalities. Diagnostic delay averaging 3.8–11 years from symptom onset is consistently documented as a major clinical failure, attributable to symptom normalization and the absence of validated non-invasive biomarkers.

The systemic comorbidity burden is strongly and repeatedly supported: endometriosis associates with irritable bowel syndrome at up to 18-fold elevated risk, interstitial cystitis and bladder pain syndrome, fibromyalgia, pure menstrual migraine (OR 11.27), myalgic encephalomyelitis and chronic fatigue syndrome (OR 2.79), depression, anxiety, and autoimmune disorders, reflecting shared mechanisms of central sensitization, hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, and systemic immune dysregulation.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several fundamental areas of conflict persist in the literature. The management of ovarian endometriomas in the context of infertility represents the most consistently documented clinical paradox: laparoscopic cystectomy improves spontaneous pregnancy rates and reduces recurrence but systematically reduces AMH and antral follicle counts, paradoxically worsening the ovarian reserve damage attributed to the endometrioma itself, and pre-treatment cystectomy does not improve live birth rates in IVF/ICSI cycles. Studies remain genuinely divided on whether surgical excision or direct progression to assisted reproduction should be prioritized as first-line management. For colorectal DIE, segmental resection is associated with lower pregnancy rates and higher complication rates in some series, while rectal shaving demonstrates reduced morbidity but may be technically limited by lesion size, with no consensus on optimal surgical strategy. Regarding pain phenotype, some studies characterize ovarian endometriomas as a relatively low-pain phenotype with preserved quality of life, while others document substantial pain burden specifically attributable to endometrioma-associated inflammation, reflecting genuine heterogeneity that current classification systems inadequately capture.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Despite the volume of evidence, critical mechanistic questions remain unresolved. The molecular determinants governing why ectopic endometrial tissue establishes, persists, and progresses in some women but not others—despite universal exposure to retrograde menstruation—are incompletely understood, with genetic susceptibility loci in HOXA10, MEIS1, EFHB, and HGFAC identified but their functional roles in lesion biology remaining poorly characterized. The precise mechanisms by which central sensitization becomes self-sustaining independently of peripheral lesion burden

## Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Recent Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across these 50 articles, several dominant themes emerge consistently. Endometriosis is overwhelmingly characterized as a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease whose clinical burden extends substantially beyond its classically recognized pelvic manifestations. The cardinal symptom triad of dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility appears in virtually every study, yet a strong cross-cutting theme is the systemic and multiorgan nature of the disease. Endometriosis is consistently framed as a heterogeneous syndrome whose phenotypic expression—superficial peritoneal, ovarian endometrioma, and deep infiltrating forms—determines both symptom profile and therapeutic considerations. A second dominant theme concerns the mechanisms underlying infertility, with studies repeatedly implicating intraperitoneal inflammation driven by activated macrophages, elevated prostaglandins (PGE2, PGF2α), interleukins (IL-6, IL-8), and reactive oxygen species as primary mediators of oocyte damage, impaired tubal function, and endometrial receptivity. A third theme is diagnostic inadequacy: laparoscopy remains the gold standard but is increasingly supplemented by transvaginal ultrasound with structured protocols (including the uterine sliding sign), pelvic MRI, and emerging biomarker-based approaches including infrared spectroscopy, serum CA-125, anti-Müllerian hormone, microRNAs, and urinary proteomics.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Certain findings recur with notable consistency. Reduced anti-Müllerian hormone is reported across multiple independent studies as both a consequence of endometriosis-associated ovarian damage and a potential disease-related biomarker, with ovarian endometriomas specifically implicated in iron-mediated oxidative stress and follicular destruction. The negative sliding sign on transvaginal ultrasound emerged repeatedly as a high-value predictor of posterior compartment disease and pelvic adhesion formation. Dysmenorrhea severity, particularly when progressive over time, is consistently identified as the strongest symptom-based predictor of endometriosis or adenomyosis. Deep infiltrating endometriosis of the rectosigmoid and rectovaginal compartments is consistently associated with the most severe pain phenotypes, bowel symptoms (dyschezia), and greatest reproductive impairment. Adenomyosis co-occurrence is reported across multiple studies as an independent contributor to pain, infertility, and adverse surgical outcomes, including reduced response to rectovaginal endometriosis surgery and increased preeclampsia risk. The association between endometriosis and a broad array of comorbidities—including irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, fibromyalgia, migraines, depression, anxiety, and autoimmune disorders—is strongly and repeatedly supported, suggesting shared mechanisms of central sensitization and systemic immune dysregulation. Fibrosis, mediated prominently by TGF-β1 signaling, is identified as a central pathogenic driver correlating with lesion invasiveness, pain severity, and ovarian follicle density reduction. The estrogen-prostaglandin E2-aromatase positive feedback loop is consistently cited as perpetuating local inflammatory estrogen excess within endometriotic lesions.

Several areas of genuine tension or conflicting evidence are apparent. The management of ovarian endometriomas presents a fundamental paradox documented across multiple studies: laparoscopic cystectomy improves spontaneous pregnancy rates and reduces disease recurrence, yet consistently reduces AMH levels and antral follicle counts, potentially worsening the ovarian reserve damage attributed to the endometrioma itself. ## Clinical Manifestations, Diagnosis, and Disease Characterization in Endometriosis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 articles, several dominant themes emerge: the multi-dimensional pain phenotype of endometriosis, the relationship between lesion anatomy and symptom expression, diagnostic challenges and emerging non-invasive modalities, and the systemic nature of the disease beyond pelvic involvement. The pathophysiological mechanisms most consistently invoked include chronic estrogen-dependent inflammation, neuroangiogenesis driven by nerve growth factor (NGF)–TrkA signaling, retrograde menstruation, immune dysregulation, and oxidative stress. Neuroinflammatory mechanisms—including peripheral sensitization, central sensitization, and cross-organ sensitization—are repeatedly cited as contributors to pain heterogeneity, while epithelial-mesenchymal transition, matrix metalloproteinase activity, and decreased apoptosis are implicated in lesion establishment and progression.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Dysmenorrhea emerges as the most prevalent and reliable symptom across virtually all studies, present in up to 98.5% of surgically confirmed patients and independently associated with advanced staging, elevated CA-125 and CA-199 levels, and adenomyosis co-occurrence. The classic "4 Ds"—dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria—are consistently mapped to specific anatomical compartments: uterosacral ligament and parametrial involvement predicts dyspareunia, rectosigmoid lesions predict dyschezia, and bladder endometriosis predicts dysuria. The ENZIAN classification system is repeatedly endorsed over the older rASRM system for its superior correlation with deep infiltrating endometriosis symptom profiles and surgical complexity. Left-sided pelvic predominance of lesions is noted in multiple cohort studies, particularly for ovarian endometriomas and compartment B involvement. CA-125, the most studied biomarker, consistently demonstrates diagnostic value—particularly when combined with inflammatory markers such as the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (combined AUC 0.903)—though alone it remains insufficient for definitive diagnosis. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI achieve sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90% for ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis but perform poorly for superficial peritoneal lesions. Laparoscopy with histopathological confirmation remains the diagnostic gold standard across all studies. Diagnostically, symptom-based screening tools such as ENDOPAIN-4D (AUC 0.95) and machine learning algorithms show promise for earlier detection in primary and tertiary care settings, respectively. Pain severity correlates with lesion depth and multi-compartment involvement, and BMI modulates symptom expression through divergent adipokine pathways, with lean individuals exhibiting greater pelvic pain through enhanced neural sensitization while obesity impairs fertility through hormonal dysregulation. Long-term medical therapy with progestins such as dienogest effectively reduces symptom severity—improving dysmenorrhea in 85% and dyschezia in 82% of patients—and maintains disease suppression, with amenorrhea representing the most protective factor against lesion progression. Adolescent endometriosis, characterized predominantly by superficial peritoneal lesions that evolve toward deep infiltrating disease with age, is strongly associated with obstructive Müllerian anomalies and secondary dysmenorrhea, with prolonged dysmenorrhea duration conferring approximately 19-fold increased risk of adult endometriosis.

Endometriosis is consistently characterized as a systemic condition with comorbidities extending beyond the pelvis, including autoimmune diseases, migraines, psychiatric conditions (anxiety and depression), irritable bowel syndrome, and bladder pain syndrome, reflecting shared neuroinflammatory and hormonal-immune pathways. Extra-pelvic manifestations—including sciatic nerve compression, thoracic endometriosis causing catamenial hemothorax, bladder endometriosis, ureteral involvement, and rare decidualized anterior peritoneal lesions—underscore the diagnostic challenge of atypical presentations. Differential diagnosis must include abdominal tuberculosis, pelvic venous insufficiency, and pelvic inflammatory disease, all of which share overlapping clinical features with endometriosis.

## Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Recent Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature reviewed converges on several overarching themes: endometriosis as a systemic chronic inflammatory disease extending well beyond its pelvic origins, the persistent challenge of diagnostic delay and the search for non-invasive diagnostic alternatives, and the complex multidimensional symptom burden encompassing pain, infertility, psychological morbidity, and extra-pelvic organ involvement. A central pathophysiological narrative threads through nearly all papers: ectopic endometrial tissue drives sustained pro-inflammatory signaling—primarily through IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and galectin-1—creating a peritoneal and systemic inflammatory milieu that promotes neoangiogenesis, fibrosis, neuroinflammation, and immune dysregulation. Altered Th1/Th2 ratios, reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and activated macrophage phenotypes characterize the immune landscape particularly in deep infiltrating endometriosis. Estrogen dependence and progesterone resistance remain foundational to lesion establishment and proliferation, with elevated estrogen receptor expression and Bcl-2/Ki-67 upregulation documented in ectopic tissue.

Central sensitization emerges as a critically important and recurrent mechanistic concept, with chronic nociceptive input from lesion-nerve fiber interactions promoting neuroplastic changes that amplify and perpetuate pain independently of lesion burden. This mechanism underpins the neuropathic-like pain phenotype identified in approximately 36% of endometriosis patients, which shows no correlation with disease stage or surgical complexity, as well as the strong epidemiological association with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (OR 2.79). Autonomic dysfunction, hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation with cortisol abnormalities, and mitochondrial dysfunction further link endometriosis to systemic conditions including chronic fatigue, cognitive impairment, and mood disorders.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Across the reviewed papers, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and infertility represent the canonical symptom cluster, reported with high prevalence in virtually all clinical series regardless of disease phenotype. Importantly, multiple studies concur that symptom severity does not reliably correlate with anatomical disease stage as assessed by rASRM classification, a finding with direct implications for diagnostic and treatment paradigms. Deep infiltrating endometriosis and ovarian endometriomas each demonstrate distinct inflammatory and immune profiles: ovarian endometriomas associate with elevated IL-8 and CA19-9 alongside monocytosis, whereas deep infiltrating endometriosis additionally exhibits elevated galectin-1 and CEA with greater macrophage activation. The Enzian classification system consistently demonstrates superior correlation with clinical outcomes—particularly sexual dysfunction and surgical complexity—compared to rASRM staging, appearing across multiple imaging and surgical studies.

Regarding diagnosis, transvaginal ultrasound is consistently identified as a reliable first-line modality, particularly for posterior compartment disease, with dynamic assessment techniques including the sliding sign and sonovaginography substantially improving detection sensitivity. MRI provides complementary compartment-specific diagnostic information using T1-weighted fat-saturated and T2-weighted sequences, with characteristic findings including T2 shading, T2 dark spot sign, and junctional zone thickening in adenomyosis. Novel ultrasonographic markers such as the "twisting sign"—uterine rotation of 15°–90° on fundal transverse section—show promise as independent predictors of rectosigmoid nodules and posterior compartment disease. The biomarker landscape beyond CA-125 is expanding, with urinary urocortin showing preliminary specificity for ovarian endometriosis, though larger validation studies are required.

Ovarian reserve depletion is a consistently documented finding across papers addressing endometrioma and deep infiltrating endometriosis, with both the disease itself and surgical intervention independently reducing anti-Müllerian hormone levels. The combined ovarian endometrioma plus deep infiltrating endometriosis phenotype produces the greatest post-surgical decline (62.2% reduction), and paradoxically, pre-treatment endometrioma excision does not improve live birth rates in IVF/ICSI cycles. ## Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Recent Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across these 50 articles converges on several dominant themes: the heterogeneous clinical presentation of endometriosis and related conditions, the inadequacy of current diagnostic pathways, the central role of inflammation and hormonal dysregulation in pathogenesis, and the emerging utility of non-invasive diagnostic technologies. Mechanistically, papers consistently implicate retrograde menstruation as a primary initiating event, with subsequent lesion establishment driven by estrogen-dependent proliferation, aromatase upregulation, progesterone resistance, and a chronic peritoneal inflammatory milieu characterized by elevated cytokines including IL-1β, TNF-alpha, and prostaglandins. Oxidative stress, iron accumulation from recurrent hemorrhage, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, fibrogenesis, and local neurogenesis collectively contribute to tissue invasion and pain amplification. At the molecular level, dysregulated microRNAs—particularly miR-10b, miR-21, miR-30c-5p, miR-145, and miR-183-3p—are implicated in regulating these pathological cascades, while genetic variants in HOXA10, MEIS1, EFHB, and HGFAC contribute to disease susceptibility and severity.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility emerge as the cardinal symptoms across virtually all studies, regardless of geographic setting or disease subtype. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) consistently associates with the most severe symptom burden, particularly dyschezia, neuropathic pain, and voiding dysfunction, reflecting infiltration of the rectovaginal septum, uterosacral ligaments, rectum, and bladder. Adenomyosis co-occurs in 15–91% of endometriosis cases across multiple studies and independently amplifies pain severity, particularly dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia. Elevated serum CA-125 is the most consistently reported biomarker, with AUC values of 0.75–0.84 when combined with hematological parameters such as the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio and HALP index; however, CA-125 alone lacks sufficient specificity for diagnostic deployment. The revised ASRM staging system is repeatedly criticized for failing to correlate with symptom severity or predict clinical outcomes, with the Enzian and AAGL2021 classifications demonstrating superior anatomical precision and correlation with surgical complexity and operative time.

Central sensitization and nociplastic pain mechanisms are highlighted across multiple papers as critical contributors to the chronic pain experience, with endometriosis patients demonstrating significantly elevated rates of comorbid conditions including irritable bowel syndrome (up to 18-fold elevated risk), interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, fibromyalgia, migraine (particularly pure menstrual migraine, OR 11.27), high-tone pelvic floor dysfunction, depression, and anxiety. This comorbidity clustering suggests shared neuroinflammatory and estrogen-dependent pathophysiological pathways. Diagnostically, transvaginal ultrasound performed by experienced operators achieves accuracy comparable to MRI for deep endometriosis detection, while MRI demonstrates superior sensitivity (100%) for endometriomas but substantially reduced sensitivity (75.6%) for DIE in non-specialized centers. Structured, endometriosis-specific MRI reporting templates improve documentation completeness up to 13-fold compared to free-text reporting across critical anatomical compartments. Machine learning approaches integrating genetic, imaging, clinical, and liquid biopsy data—particularly microRNA signatures from urine and saliva—achieve AUC values of 0.979–0.983 and outperform individual conventional modalities, with ensemble algorithms including LightGBM and XGBoost demonstrating particular promise.

Diagnostic delay averaging 3.8–6 years from symptom onset is consistently reported as a major clinical problem, attributable to symptom normalization, absence of validated non-invasive biomarkers, and frequent misdiagnosis. Second opinions modify initial diagnoses in approximately 51% of cases, underscoring the scope of diagnostic inaccuracy. ## Clinical Manifestations, Diagnostic Approaches, and Disease Complexity in Endometriosis

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across the 38 articles reviewed, several dominant themes emerge regarding the clinical presentation, pathophysiology, and diagnostic challenges of endometriosis and related conditions. Retrograde menstruation remains the foundational pathogenic mechanism, but papers consistently extend this framework to incorporate chronic inflammatory signaling, estrogen dependence, progesterone resistance, oxidative stress, and immune dysregulation as co-operative drivers of ectopic tissue establishment and persistence. Inflammatory cytokines—particularly IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and MCP-1—and angiogenic mediators including VEGF are repeatedly implicated in sustaining lesion viability and propagating pain. Several studies highlight neurogenic and central sensitization mechanisms as critical but underappreciated contributors to pain burden, with evidence of decreased gray matter volume in pain-processing brain regions (thalamus, cingulate gyrus, putamen, insula), cross-organ sensitization via shared peripheral neural pathways, and elevated Fibromyalgia Survey scores in a substantial proportion of endometriosis patients, collectively suggesting a nociplastic pain phenotype that current therapies inadequately address.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The clinical triad of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain is reported with remarkable consistency across virtually all studies, alongside infertility affecting 30–50% of patients and diagnostic delays averaging 5–11 years from symptom onset. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is identified across multiple papers as the most symptomatically severe phenotype, with anatomical involvement of the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, bowel, bladder, and ureters producing organ-specific manifestations including dyschezia, dysuria, hematuria, and ureteral obstruction, the last carrying risk of silent progressive renal dysfunction. Ovarian endometriomas are consistently associated with diminished ovarian reserve, quantified through reduced anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle count, with bilateral lesions and larger cyst diameters (>50–70 mm) demonstrating the most pronounced adverse effects on folliculogenesis and fertility. CA-125 emerges as the most consistently reported serum biomarker, correlating with disease stage, inflammatory burden, and endometrioma size, while C-reactive protein provides complementary diagnostic value particularly in DIE. Transvaginal ultrasound is uniformly endorsed as the first-line imaging modality, with MRI providing superior characterization of deep disease; multiparametric MRI demonstrated 76–96% sensitivity for simultaneously detecting DIE and pelvic venous insufficiency in women with chronic pelvic pain, a clinically important co-occurrence identified in 61.5% of cases in one cohort. Surgical excision, particularly nerve-sparing laparoscopic approaches, achieves 50–80% symptom reduction, though recurrence remains problematic, especially in younger patients, those with larger cysts, and those with elevated preoperative CA-125 or CA-199.

The association between endometriosis and adenomyosis is repeatedly documented, with extrinsic adenomyosis showing particular co-occurrence with severe endometriosis phenotypes including DIE and ovarian endometriomas. Adolescent endometriosis, characterized predominantly by peritoneal red lesions rather than fibrotic disease, presents distinct risk factors including genital tract malformations (adjusted odds ratio 16.15), hyperfibrinogenemia, and dyslipidemia, underscoring that pathophysiological heterogeneity exists across the life course. Multiple studies report that endometriosis confers increased obstetric risks—placenta previa, preterm delivery, postpartum hemorrhage, pre-eclampsia—and a 4.2-fold elevated risk of ovarian cancer, with mechanistic links to chronic estrogen stimulation and oxidative DNA damage. The co-occurrence of endometriosis with pelvic congestion syndrome, characterized by shared inflammatory mediators including IL-6 and CRP, further illustrates the complexity of chronic pelvic pain as a multietiological syndrome requiring integrated diagnostic evaluation.

Regarding treatment, the literature converges on the principle that therapeutic decisions must balance disease eradication against iatrogenic harm, particularly ovarian reserve depletion. Surgical cystectomy consistently reduces

# Biomarkers for Ovarian Cancer in Endometriosis: Current Evidence, Controversies, and Translational Horizons

## Overview

A central challenge in gynecologic oncology is the accurate discrimination of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC) from benign endometriotic disease—a distinction rendered particularly difficult because endometriosis simultaneously elevates non-specific diagnostic markers, confers genuine malignant risk, and shares molecular features with the carcinomas it precedes. The literature across this field addresses a tripartite research question: which biomarkers reliably distinguish malignant from benign ovarian pathology in the presence of endometriosis, what molecular mechanisms underlie the endometriosis-to-carcinoma transition, and how can multi-modal and combinatorial strategies overcome the inherent limitations of single-analyte approaches. Mechanistically, converging evidence implicates chronic pelvic inflammation, oxidative stress–mediated DNA damage, and pathway dysregulation—particularly PI3K/AKT/mTOR, ARID1A/PTEN, Notch, and Wnt/CTNNB1 signaling—as the molecular bridges between endometriotic lesions and malignant transformation, predominantly toward endometrioid and clear cell carcinoma histotypes.

## Consensus Findings

CA-125 (MUC16) and HE4 (WFDC2) remain the most extensively validated biomarkers in this domain, with consistent cross-study agreement that their combination substantially outperforms either marker alone. CA-125 achieves sensitivity of approximately 80–90% for advanced epithelial ovarian cancer but suffers fundamental specificity limitations arising from its systematic elevation in endometriosis, adenomyosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, heart failure, peritoneal tuberculosis, and preeclampsia. HE4 demonstrates critically superior specificity—it is notably absent in endometriosis—making it particularly advantageous for differential diagnosis in patients with known endometriotic disease. The ROMA algorithm integrating both markers with menopausal status consistently achieves AUC values of 0.86–0.90, while advanced multimarker frameworks such as EarlySEEK—incorporating CA-125, HE4, ctDNA, prolactin, IL-6, and CA19-9—achieve AUC values approaching 0.97.

At the subtype level, the literature consistently associates endometriosis with a three- to eightfold elevated risk of endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas, but not high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSOC). HGSOC is instead driven by TP53 mutations and BRCA1/2-mediated homologous recombination deficiency, supporting a molecularly distinct pathogenesis. For endometriosis-associated subtypes, shared somatic mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, KRAS, and CTNNB1 mechanistically link endometriotic lesions to their malignant derivatives. Mendelian randomization data confirm a causal relationship between endometriosis and clear cell carcinoma specifically, with a 1.87-fold risk elevation. Tissue factor pathway inhibitor 2 (TFPI2) emerges as a promising clear cell carcinoma–specific biomarker largely unaffected by endometriosis or menstrual cycle variation, addressing a histotype-specific diagnostic gap.

Liquid biopsy platforms represent a domain of robust convergence. Circulating tumor DNA carrying TP53 mutations is detectable in 75–100% of HGSOC cases; methylated ARHGAP40 ctDNA is present in approximately 80% of HGSOC plasma samples while absent in benign and healthy controls. Cell-free DNA methylation patterns at RASSF1A, BRCA1, and OPCML loci provide complementary specificity. Exosomal microRNA panels—particularly the miR-200 family, the miR-183/182/96 cluster, and miR-21—consistently discriminate malignant from benign ovarian pathology, with hsa-miR-183-5p achieving near-perfect discrimination in at least one validation cohort. Inflammatory indices including the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII) provide diagnostically useful adjunctive information, particularly for early-stage disease where CA-125 specificity is most compromised. Machine learning ensemble models integrating CA-125, HE4, radiomics features, and clinical variables reproducibly achieve AUC values exceeding 0.95, substantially outperforming individual modalities.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive tensions persist within this literature. CA-125 performance varies markedly by menopausal status—demonstrating superior sensitivity postmenopausally while HE4 offers greater specificity premenopausally—yet individual studies weight these trade-offs differently when proposing clinical algorithms, precluding uniform implementation guidance. One large epidemiological study reported uterine fibroids as a stronger independent ovarian cancer risk factor than endometriosis (HR 1.65 versus a non-significant HR 1.16), directly contradicting the broader literature's consistent designation of endometriosis as a major independent risk factor. Regarding the IOTA ADNEX model, certain studies endorse its superiority over the Risk of Malignancy Index for adnexal mass characterization, yet others document reduced accuracy specifically in endometriosis patients due to overlapping vascular morphology, with approximately 27% misclassification of endometrioma as malignancy. Candidate biomarkers with strong tissue-level mechanistic rationale—including osteopontin and galectin-7—have shown no significant serum-level differences between malignant and benign cases in case-control validation, illustrating a recurrent disconnect between biomarker biology and circulating diagnostic utility. An additional paradox concerns ESR1 polymorphisms: the same variants (rs2234693 CC and rs9340799 GG genotypes) that increase ovarian cancer risk appear inversely associated with endometriosis susceptibility, suggesting divergent hormonal mechanisms depending on tissue context. The protective effect of oral contraceptives is epidemiologically consistent, yet simultaneously documented paradoxical elevations in CRP with prolonged use raise unresolved questions regarding inflammatory chemoprevention mechanisms.
