# Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Tumors: Molecular Pathogenesis, Diagnostic Characterization, and Therapeutic Implications

## Overview

A central and enduring question in gynecologic oncology concerns the mechanisms by which endometriosis—a benign, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women—undergoes malignant transformation. Although this transformation is estimated to occur in only 0.5–1% of endometriosis cases, the resulting endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers (EAOCs) and extraovarian malignancies constitute a clinically and molecularly distinct disease spectrum from other gynecologic tumors. The literature in this domain seeks to delineate the somatic mutational landscape, tumor immune microenvironment dynamics, histotype-specific oncogenic drivers, and translatable therapeutic vulnerabilities of these neoplasms, with particular emphasis on ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) and endometrioid carcinoma as the principal EAOC subtypes.

## Consensus Findings

The most consistently reported molecular alterations across independent cohorts are mutations in *ARID1A* (reported in 40–100% of OCCC series depending on methodology), *PIK3CA* (35–55%), and *PTEN* (up to 78% in some Asian cohorts), collectively implicating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis as the dominant oncogenic pathway in endometriosis-associated clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas. Functional modeling corroborates that concurrent *ARID1A* loss and *PIK3CA* activation—potentially accompanied by *KRAS* overexpression and c-Myc activation—are sufficient to drive malignant transformation of endometriotic epithelial cells, positioning these co-occurring alterations as early, potentially initiating pathogenic events rather than late progression markers. *CTNNB1* mutations characterize a distinct molecular subset of endometrioid carcinomas with aberrant Wnt/β-catenin pathway activation, neuroendocrine co-expression, and notably favorable organ-confined prognosis. *TP53* mutations, in contrast, approach 90–98% prevalence in high-grade serous carcinoma while remaining largely absent in endometrioid and clear cell subtypes, reinforcing the molecular distinctiveness of these lineages. Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma arising from extrauterine endometriosis is consistently characterized by *JAZF1::SUZ12* fusion genes, strong estrogen and progesterone receptor co-expression, and sensitivity to hormone-based therapeutic strategies. *KRAS* mutations are reproducibly identified across multiple rarer EAOC subtypes, including mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA), mucinous carcinoma, and rectal endometriosis-associated adenocarcinoma, reflecting broad MAPK pathway involvement beyond the OCCC/endometrioid core. Immunohistochemically, MLA is consistently distinguished from mimics by PAX8, GATA3, TTF1, and CD10 co-positivity with absent hormone receptor expression and wild-type p53, while OCCC is reliably identified by NapsinA, CK7, HNF-1β, and AMACR positivity. Mismatch repair deficiency (MMR-D) and elevated tumor mutational burden, identified across 10–66% of cases depending on series, consistently stratify tumors with potential sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibition, and *POLE*-mutated endometrioid ovarian carcinomas demonstrate near-complete disease-free survival in early studies.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive conflicts pervade this literature. The prognostic significance of *ARID1A* deficiency is inconsistent across studies: while some reports associate ARID1A loss with elevated HDAC6 expression, increased *TP53* co-mutation incidence, and worse progression-free survival, others emphasize its value as a predictive biomarker for gemcitabine sensitivity in platinum-resistant OCCC, suggesting that prognostic interpretation is highly context- and treatment-dependent. The immune microenvironment of OCCC presents a particularly unresolved paradox: transcriptomically defined immune-hot tumors associate with poor prognosis, whereas histologically quantified intraepithelial CD8+ T-cell infiltration predicts favorable outcomes, revealing a critical methodological divergence between gene expression profiling and morphological assessment that remains unexplained. MMR-D status generates similarly contradictory signals—paradoxically conferring substantially worse survival outcomes (hazard ratios approaching 19.58 for progression-free survival in some series) while simultaneously predicting immunotherapy responsiveness. Whether concurrent endometriosis at the time of OCCC diagnosis confers a survival advantage through earlier disease detection or is prognostically neutral remains incompletely resolved, with at least one cohort study reporting a paradoxical survival benefit attributable to symptom-driven earlier presentation. PD-L1 expression, *ARID1A* loss, and p53 status have not reliably predicted response to pembrolizumab in the PEACOCC trial, underscoring the inadequacy of currently proposed predictive biomarkers for checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Significant mechanistic uncertainty persists regarding the precise sequence and timing of somatic mutation acquisition during the endometriosis-to-carcinoma continuum, particularly within atypical endometriosis as an intermediate precursor lesion. The specific contributions of iron-mediated oxidative stress, chronic estrogenic stimulation, and inflammatory cytokine signaling to the initiation versus progression of transformation remain incompletely partitioned. How immune microenvironment context—specifically the balance between FOXP3+ regulatory T-cells, M2-polarized macrophages, and CD8+ effector cells—determines whether identical oncogenic drivers produce CCC versus endometrioid phenotypes is not established. The molecular basis for the divergent immunotherapy response signals from histological versus transcriptomic immune profiling requires resolution, as does the biological mechanism underlying MMR-D-associated poor survival paradoxically coexisting with checkpoint inhibitor sensitivity. Lynch syndrome—particularly *MSH6* pathogenic variants—is recognized as an underappreciated contributor to EAOC and extraovarian endometriosis-associated adenocarcinoma, but systematic prevalence estimates and surveillance recommendations remain underdeveloped.

## Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Tumors: Synthesis of Research Findings

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The body of literature across these studies converges on several interconnected themes: the molecular pathogenesis of endometriosis-to-malignancy transformation, histological subtype specificity, tumor microenvironment characterization, and therapeutic vulnerabilities. Endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers (EAOCs), principally clear cell carcinoma (CCC) and endometrioid carcinoma, are consistently distinguished from high-grade serous carcinomas by distinct etiological and molecular pathways. Iron-mediated oxidative stress within endometriotic cysts, chronic estrogenic stimulation, and inflammatory microenvironments are recurrently implicated as mechanistic drivers of malignant transformation, which occurs in approximately 0.5–1% of endometriosis cases.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

The PI3K/AKT/mTOR and RAS/MAPK signaling axes emerge as the most frequently implicated oncogenic pathways across histological subtypes. **ARID1A** mutations (reported in 48.8–100% of CCC cohorts) and **PIK3CA** mutations constitute the most consistently identified somatic alterations in endometriosis-associated CCC and endometrioid carcinomas, with multiple independent studies confirming that concurrent ARID1A loss and PIK3CA activation represent early, potentially initiating pathogenic events. Functional modeling confirms that ARID1A loss combined with KRAS overexpression and c-Myc activation is sufficient to drive malignant transformation of endometriotic epithelial cells, producing tumors with either CCC or endometrioid phenotypes depending on immune microenvironment context. **PTEN** loss is additionally reported in up to 78% of Asian CCC cohorts, driving PI3K/AKT pathway hyperactivation and correlating with chemotherapy resistance and worse progression-free survival. **KRAS** mutations are prominent across multiple endometriosis-associated malignancy subtypes, including mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA), mucinous carcinoma, and rectal endometriosis-associated adenocarcinoma, reflecting broad MAPK pathway involvement.

Molecularly distinct subtypes include **CTNNB1-mutant** endometrioid carcinomas with aberrant Wnt/β-catenin activation, which display neuroendocrine marker co-expression and sex cord-like morphological variants, both associated with favorable prognosis when organ-confined. **JAZF1-SUZ12** gene fusions characterize low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) arising from endometriosis, consistently co-expressed with estrogen and progesterone receptors, reinforcing the role of hormonal dependency in these tumors. Mesonephric-like adenocarcinomas are distinguished by near-ubiquitous **KRAS** mutations alongside MAPK pathway dominance, GATA3/TTF1 co-expression, and aggressive clinical behavior with high recurrence rates.

The tumor immune microenvironment of CCC is characterized by a paradoxical distribution of CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells, which accumulate in nonneoplastic adjacent regions rather than within tumor tissue, and PD-L1 upregulation—particularly in PTEN-deficient tumors—contributes to immune evasion. Immune checkpoint blockade with **pembrolizumab** demonstrated a 42% response rate in clear cell gynecologic cancers in the PEACOCC trial, though neither PD-L1 expression, ARID1A loss, nor p53 status reliably predicted response. Mismatch repair deficiency (MMR-D), identified in 10–66% of cases across CCC and dedifferentiated endometrioid carcinomas, consistently correlates with immune checkpoint inhibitor sensitivity and elevated tumor mutational burden. **Homologous recombination deficiency** and **BRCA1/2** alterations, prominent in high-grade serous and a subset of endometrioid carcinomas, predict sensitivity to platinum-based therapy and PARP inhibitors, with molecular subtyping enabling stratification of therapeutic vulnerabilities.

Histological subtype-specific associations with endometriosis

## Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Tumors: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected themes: the malignant transformation potential of endometriosis through defined molecular pathways, the diagnostic challenges of rare endometriosis-associated neoplasms, and the therapeutic implications of molecular subtyping. Endometriosis serves as a precursor lesion principally through chronic inflammation, reactive oxygen species accumulation, hormone dysregulation, and somatic mutations accumulating in ectopic endometrial tissue. The most commonly implicated transformation pathway involves mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, and KRAS, with TP53 alterations characterizing more aggressive phenotypes. The SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex—particularly through ARID1A and SMARCA4 dysfunction—emerges as a central oncogenic axis across multiple histotypes, including ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), endometrioid carcinoma, gastric-type cervical adenocarcinoma, and small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Clear cell carcinoma is the most consistently cited endometriosis-associated malignancy, with ARID1A loss (reported in approximately 54% of cases) and PIK3CA mutations constituting its molecular hallmarks. Diagnostic confirmation relies on HNF-1β, Napsin A, PAX8, and CK7 positivity with WT1 and hormone receptor negativity—a pattern corroborated across multiple independent reports. Endometrioid carcinomas, both ovarian and endometrial, similarly demonstrate PTEN, PIK3CA, and CTNNB1 mutations, with ARID1A deficiency shared across both subtypes. Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma is characterized by JAZF1::SUZ12 fusion genes alongside strong ER/PR and CD10 expression, supporting hormone-based therapeutic strategies. A recurring finding across OCCC, endometrioid carcinoma, and mismatch repair-deficient tumors is elevated tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, PDL1 expression, and microsatellite instability, positioning immune checkpoint inhibitors—particularly nivolumab, ipilimumab, and pembrolizumab—as viable treatment options. BRCA1/2-associated high-grade serous carcinoma consistently demonstrates fallopian tube epithelial origin via serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma precursors sharing clonal TP53 mutations, with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy superior to salpingectomy alone for prevention.

Several prognostic molecular markers emerge repeatedly: TP53 mutations consistently associate with advanced disease, chemoresistance, and poor survival across endometrial, ovarian, and cervical carcinomas, while TERT promoter -124C>T mutations unexpectedly confer favorable prognosis in OCCC. ARID1A deficiency predicts gemcitabine sensitivity in platinum-resistant OCCC while simultaneously correlating with elevated HDAC6 expression and worse progression-free survival, illustrating its complex prognostic role. NLR as a systemic inflammatory biomarker independently predicts survival specifically in clear cell carcinoma but not endometrioid histotype, underscoring tumor immune microenvironment heterogeneity. Cell-free DNA liquid biopsy from Pap smears demonstrates superior sensitivity (77.4%) over plasma (42.9%) for endometrial cancer detection, with PTEN, PIK3CA, and CTNNB1 mutations most frequently identified.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several areas of genuine tension exist within this literature. The prognostic significance of ARID1A deficiency is inconsistent: while some reports associate it with poor outcomes through increased TP53 mutation incidence and HDAC6 upregulation, others emphasize its role as a predictive biomarker for gemcitabine sensitivity, suggesting context-dependent rather than uniformly adverse effects. The question of whether endometriosis-associated OCCC carries better or worse prognosis than non-endometriosis-associated OCCC is incompletely resolved; one study reports that OCCC arising directly from endometriosis has superior 5

## Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Tumors: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature surveyed across these fifty articles converges on several interconnected themes: the molecular pathways mediating malignant transformation of endometriosis, the histopathological and immunohistochemical characterization of endometriosis-associated cancers, and the clinical implications of molecular subtyping for prognosis and treatment. A unifying biological concept is that chronic inflammation and unopposed estrogen exposure create a permissive microenvironment in which somatic mutations accumulate, driving neoplastic progression. This inflammatory-hormonal axis operates across diverse anatomical sites—ovarian, extraovarian, retroperitoneal, abdominal wall, and peritoneal—underscoring that endometriosis-associated malignant transformation is not restricted to the adnexa.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

The most reproducibly identified molecular alterations in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers are mutations in *ARID1A*, *PIK3CA*, *PTEN*, *CTNNB1*, and *KRAS*, with the specific combination varying by histotype. Clear cell carcinomas are most consistently characterized by *ARID1A* mutations (40–67%) and *PIK3CA* alterations (35–55%), activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, while endometrioid carcinomas additionally harbor *CTNNB1* and *PTEN* mutations engaging Wnt/β-catenin signaling. These two histotypes share a common endometriotic precursor and together represent the core of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer, distinguished from high-grade serous carcinoma, which is dominated by near-universal *TP53* mutations (90–98%) and homologous recombination deficiency through *BRCA1/2* and related gene alterations. Mismatch repair deficiency and *POLE* mutations, though less prevalent, carry prognostic significance: *POLE*-mutated endometrioid ovarian carcinomas demonstrate near-complete disease-free survival, while mismatch repair-deficient tumors may benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition. Lynch syndrome, specifically *MSH6* pathogenic variants, was documented as an underappreciated contributor to endometriosis-associated adenocarcinoma, including in extraovarian sites.

Immunohistochemically, several marker combinations recur across multiple tumor types. Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA)—a rare malignancy documented in multiple independent case reports as arising from endometriotic foci in the ovary, peritoneum, and fallopian tube—is consistently characterized by PAX8, GATA3, TTF1, and CD10 positivity with absent estrogen and progesterone receptor expression and wild-type p53, alongside *KRAS* mutations in the majority of cases. This immunoprofile reliably distinguishes MLA from high-grade serous carcinoma, with which it can be morphologically confused. Clear cell carcinomas, whether ovarian, retroperitoneal, or abdominal wall in origin, are characterized by NapsinA, PAX8, CK7, and AMACR positivity, wild-type p53, and absent hormone receptor expression, with elevated Ki-67 reflecting aggressive proliferative biology. Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma arising from extrauterine endometriosis consistently expresses CD10, estrogen and progesterone receptors, and WT1, with recurrent *JAZF1*-containing gene fusions that dysregulate the PRC2 histone modification complex.

Progesterone receptor expression emerges as a particularly consistent prognostic stratifier across multiple tumor contexts. In endometrioid ovarian carcinoma, high progesterone receptor expression associates with *CTNNB1* mutations and favorable outcomes, whereas low expression accompanies *TP53* alterations and poor survival. In endometrial carcinoma broadly, loss of both estrogen and progesterone receptors correlates with higher tumor grade, lymphovascular invasion, and reduced survival. Lymphovascular invasion itself is the most consistently identified independent prognostic factor in endometrial carcinoma, correlating with myometrial invasion depth, lymph node involvement, and advanced FIGO stage.

## Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Tumors: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on several interrelated themes: the molecular pathways driving malignant transformation of endometriotic tissue, the immunological microenvironmental changes enabling tumor progression, and the diagnostic challenges posed by rare endometriosis-associated histotypes. A central mechanistic framework positions endometriosis as a premalignant precursor through the stepwise accumulation of somatic mutations, with atypical endometriosis serving as an intermediate lesion. Progressive loss of tumor suppressor function, particularly PTEN, ARID1A, and p53, combined with activating mutations in PIK3CA, KRAS, and CTNNB1, appears to initiate and sustain malignant transformation across multiple histotypes including ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), ovarian endometrioid carcinoma (OEC), and endometriosis-associated intestinal tumors. Hormonal dysregulation, particularly estrogen dominance and progesterone resistance, provides a permissive biological environment, as exemplified by adenosarcomas demonstrating rapid growth during pregnancy and sensitivity to anti-estrogen therapy postpartum.

### Consistently Reported Findings

ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations emerge as the most frequently cited molecular alterations across endometriosis-associated malignancies, particularly OCCC and OEC, with ARID1A loss additionally implicated in chromatin remodeling disruption and SWI/SNF complex dysfunction. Mismatch repair deficiency and microsatellite instability-high status constitute a reproducibly identified molecular subtype with distinct prognostic implications: MMRd tumors paradoxically demonstrate significantly worse outcomes in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (hazard ratios of 19.58 for progression-free survival) yet confer responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibition. TP53 mutations are consistently enriched in high-grade serous carcinomas approaching 100% prevalence, while largely absent in endometrioid and clear cell subtypes, reinforcing the molecular distinction between these lineages. Immunohistochemically, PAX8, CK7, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor expression repeatedly characterizes müllerian-origin tumors, providing reliable diagnostic anchors across case series. PD-L1 upregulation, driven by PIK3CA pathway activation and ARID1A deficiency, is consistently linked to platinum resistance and adverse survival outcomes in epithelial ovarian cancers. Immunotherapy combinations, particularly dual CTLA-4/PD-1 blockade with ipilimumab plus nivolumab, demonstrate promising efficacy in gynecologic clear cell carcinomas, with tumor mutational burden emerging as a predictive biomarker.

The immune microenvironment undergoes consistent remodeling during endometriosis-to-carcinoma progression, with accumulation of FOXP3+ regulatory T cells, M2-polarized macrophages marked by CD163 elevation, and progressive decline in estrogen and progesterone receptor expression reflecting increasing hormonal autonomy and immunosuppression. Cell cycle regulators cyclin D1, CDK4, and P16 show progressive overexpression across atypical endometriosis and carcinoma, mechanistically linking G1-S phase dysregulation to malignant progression.

Molecular subtyping frameworks are consistently applied across endometrial and ovarian malignancies, distinguishing POLEmut, MMRd, p53-abnormal, and p53 wild-type categories with differential prognostic and therapeutic implications. Several studies independently validate that clear cell histotype, advanced FIGO stage, bilateral ovarian involvement, and MMRd status function as independent adverse prognostic factors amenable to nomogram-based risk stratification.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Notable discordances exist within the literature. Most strikingly, the immune microenvironment of OCCC presents conflicting prognostic signals depending on the analytical methodology: transcriptomically defined immune-hot tumors associate with significantly poor prognosis, whereas morphologically defined intraepithelial CD8+ T-cell infiltration predicts favorable outcomes, revealing critical divergence between gene expression profiling and histological assessment that remains unresolved. Regarding MMRd tumors, studies variously report improved immunotherapy responsiveness yet substantially worse survival outcomes

## Endometriosis-Associated Malignant Transformation: Mechanisms, Diagnostics, and Therapeutic Implications

The four studies collectively illuminate the intersection of Müllerian-derived tissue biology, malignant transformation, and diagnostic challenges in endometriosis-associated and gynecologic malignancies. A dominant theme across papers is the critical role of immunohistochemistry in establishing tissue origin and distinguishing benign from malignant lesions. PAX8 and ER positivity consistently identify Müllerian lineage across both the cervical clear cell adenocarcinoma and colonic endometriosis cases, while organ-specific markers such as CDX2/CK20 exclusion and napsin-A expression refine differential diagnoses. This immunophenotypic framework emerges as the most reproducible diagnostic approach across contexts.

Endometriosis as a precursor or coexistent condition receives significant attention. The ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) cohort study notably demonstrates a paradoxical survival benefit associated with concurrent endometriosis, attributed to earlier disease detection driven by symptomatic endometriosis prompting timely medical evaluation. This finding introduces a nuanced perspective: while endometriosis confers malignant risk, its clinical detectability may afford a diagnostic window that improves stage distribution and outcomes. The colonic endometriosis case reinforces that ectopic endometrial tissue can convincingly mimic adenocarcinoma histologically, underscoring the malignant transformation diagnostic pitfall.

Therapeutically, immune checkpoint inhibitor utility is addressed across two studies. In OCCC, nivolumab and ipilimumab are highlighted for advanced disease, with CD8+ T cell infiltration as a potential predictive biomarker. Conversely, the anal gland adenocarcinoma case emphasizes microsatellite stability as a limiting factor for single-agent immunotherapy efficacy, favoring combinatorial approaches such as durvalumab plus tremelimumab.

No direct contradictions emerge across papers; however, the immunotherapy evidence remains largely observational or case-level, limiting generalizable conclusions about checkpoint inhibitor efficacy across these histologically distinct malignancies.

# Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

## Overview

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, has emerged as a significant independent risk factor for a broad spectrum of adverse obstetric outcomes. The central research question animating this literature concerns whether endometriosis causally disrupts the molecular and structural requirements for successful placentation, or whether the associations observed in epidemiological studies are substantially confounded by comorbid uterine pathology, assisted reproductive technology (ART) use, and shared inflammatory diatheses. Mechanistically, investigations have focused on how the ectopic endometrial microenvironment—characterized by estrogen dominance, progesterone resistance, and chronic inflammatory signaling—propagates defects in decidualization, trophoblast invasion, and spiral artery remodeling that ultimately manifest as placental dysfunction and obstetric complications.

## Consensus Findings

Across observational cohort studies, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses, the literature consistently identifies placenta previa, preterm birth, postpartum hemorrhage, low birth weight, cesarean delivery, and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy as the most robustly reported adverse outcomes in women with endometriosis. One meta-analysis reported a relative risk of 2.246 for placenta previa and 1.277 for preterm birth in endometriosis patients undergoing ART, while population-level analyses suggest approximately 20% of pregnant women with endometriosis experience preterm birth compared to roughly 11% of controls. Spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP), though rare, is disproportionately associated with endometriosis in over 55% of reported cases, representing one of the more disease-specific pregnancy complications in this literature.

The mechanistic convergence of these outcomes centers on defective spiral artery remodeling driven by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines—including interleukin-6, interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, cyclooxygenase-2, and prostaglandin E2—alongside impaired trophoblast invasion mediated by progesterone resistance and altered decidualization. At the molecular level, the angiogenic imbalance defined by reduced placental growth factor and vascular endothelial growth factor expression alongside elevated soluble Fms-like tyrosine kinase-1 (sFLT-1) recurrently links endometriosis and artificial cycle frozen embryo transfer to preeclampsia and placental insufficiency. The absence of corpus luteum-derived relaxin and pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A in programmed frozen embryo transfer cycles further exacerbates this angiogenic dysregulation, with natural cycle protocols demonstrating substantially lower rates of threatened abortion and recurrent pregnancy loss in endometriosis patients. Adenomyosis, sharing myometrial infiltration and chronic inflammatory pathways with endometriosis, demonstrates similarly robust associations with miscarriage, placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, and preeclampsia, reinforcing the concept that ectopic endometrial tissue broadly impairs placentation regardless of anatomical compartment. Immune dysregulation—including elevated endometrial CD200S expression on uterine natural killer cells, reduced regulatory T cell populations, and antiphospholipid-related antibody activity—constitutes a consistently identified contributory mechanism to implantation failure and recurrent pregnancy loss.

## Controversies and Contradictions

The most substantive conflict within this literature arises from Mendelian randomization analyses that find limited evidence for causal relationships between endometriosis and several outcomes—including preterm birth, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and postpartum hemorrhage—attributing previously reported associations to confounding by comorbidities including uterine fibroids, Müllerian anomalies, and PCOS, with significant causal effects confirmed only for intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy in advanced-stage disease. This stands in direct tension with multiple large observational cohorts and meta-analyses that identify these same outcomes as significantly and independently elevated in endometriosis patients. The discrepancy likely reflects the methodological limitations inherent to both approaches: observational studies cannot fully adjust for endometriosis staging, comorbid uterine pathology, and ART use, while Mendelian randomization instruments for endometriosis may insufficiently capture disease heterogeneity across superficial peritoneal, ovarian, and deep infiltrating subtypes. Indeed, deep infiltrating endometriosis is consistently identified as conferring the highest placental complication risk, suggesting that pooled analyses obscure clinically meaningful subgroup-level effects. The impact of surgical treatment on pregnancy outcomes remains similarly contested; one review found that prior surgical intervention does not consistently reduce obstetric complications and may introduce additional risk, while observational studies have reported dramatically lower placenta previa rates in untreated versus surgically managed cohorts.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Several critical mechanistic questions remain inadequately addressed. The degree to which inflammatory pathophysiology operates predominantly at the local endometrial and decidual level versus as a detectable systemic process remains unresolved; peripheral inflammatory indices including the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio do not differ significantly between endometriosis patients who do and do not experience preterm birth near delivery, implying that systemic biomarker strategies may be insufficiently sensitive. The specific epigenetic mechanisms through which endometriosis-associated progesterone resistance propagates across successive pregnancies, and whether these are reversible with targeted intervention, have not been characterized. The causal architecture linking endometriosis to long-term neonatal outcomes—including emerging data on refractive errors in offspring—is essentially unexplored, as are the mechanisms by which iatrogenic endometriosis following cesarean delivery contributes to subsequent reproductive complications. The interaction between endometriosis and comorbid metabolic and endocrine dysregulation, including subclinical hypothyroidism and vitamin D deficiency, requires causal dissection through appropriately powered longitudinal studies.

## Translational Relevance

The identification of the sFLT-1/VEGF axis and corpus luteum-derived angiogenic factors as mechanistic mediators of placental dysfunction in endometriosis patients has direct implications for endometrial preparation protocol selection in ART, strongly supporting the preferential use of natural or modified natural cycle frozen embryo transfer where feasible. Restoration of physiological luteal-phase angiogenic signaling represents a tractable therapeutic target. The consistent implication of progesterone resistance and deficient decidualization positions selective progesterone receptor modulators and decidualization-enhancing agents as rational candidates for peri

## Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on several interconnected mechanistic themes linking reproductive pathology—most prominently endometriosis—to adverse pregnancy outcomes. Chronic inflammation emerges as the central unifying mechanism, operating through elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6, interleukin-1β, TNF-α, IFN-γ, and IL-12) that impair endometrial receptivity, decidualization, and trophoblast invasion, thereby disrupting implantation and placentation. Abnormal uterine contractility, driven by hormonal dysregulation including progesterone resistance and altered estradiol signaling, represents a second dominant theme, with endometriosis-associated retrograde contractions facilitating ectopic implantation while simultaneously compromising embryo placement and contributing to preterm labor. Placental pathology—including deficient spiral artery remodeling, aberrant trophoblast invasion, placenta previa, placental abruption, and placenta accreta spectrum—recurs across multiple papers as a downstream consequence of these inflammatory and structural derangements.

Assisted reproductive technology (ART) procedures, particularly programmed frozen embryo transfer with exogenous hormone endometrial preparation, are consistently identified as amplifying these risks. The proposed pathway involves suboptimal decidualization secondary to absence of corpus luteal factors such as relaxin, producing paradoxically excessive trophoblast invasion, larger placentas, fetal overgrowth, late-onset preeclampsia, and postterm birth. Nordic and other population-based data corroborate that endometriosis patients undergoing ART face compounded placentation risks, including a 9.35-fold elevation in placenta previa risk with fresh blastocyst transfer.

Congenital uterine anomalies, including septate uterus and didelphys uterus, represent a parallel structural pathway to adverse outcomes. Reduced endometrial vascularity, diminished uterine capacity, and compromised receptivity in these conditions increase miscarriage, preterm delivery, fetal growth restriction, and malpresentation. Hysteroscopic correction substantially improves outcomes, though residual risk of preterm birth and miscarriage persists, indicating that anatomical repair does not fully resolve underlying endometrial dysfunction.

Systemic autoimmune and inflammatory conditions including systemic lupus erythematosus and Sjögren syndrome recapitulate the inflammatory mechanisms seen in endometriosis, impairing trophoblast growth, endometrial receptivity, and decidualization through autoantibody-mediated pathways. Notably, SLE patients demonstrate a 30% preeclampsia rate versus approximately 3% in the general population, and share with endometriosis patients the features of antiphospholipid-mediated vascular dysfunction and diminished ovarian reserve. A genetic architecture study further identifies negative genetic correlations between gestational duration and both endometriosis and preeclampsia, suggesting shared biological pathways—potentially involving endometrial function, sex hormone regulation, and immune tolerance—that simultaneously predispose to shorter gestation and these maternal conditions.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

Preeclampsia and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy represent the most consistently reported adverse outcomes, appearing across endometriosis, ART, autoimmune disease, and assisted reproductive modality contexts. The sFLT-1/VEGF axis is implicated in preeclampsia pathogenesis, with circulating VEGFR-3 levels markedly reduced in preeclampsia and inversely correlating with disease severity, neutrophil counts, and markers of anemia, positioning this receptor as both a mechanistic mediator and potential biomarker. Placenta accreta spectrum, retained placenta, and placental abruption are recurrently reported complications of endometriosis and ART, with the shared final pathway of impaired spiral artery remodeling and abnormal trophoblast invasion. Preterm birth is universally elevated across virtually all pathological conditions examined, including endometriosis, uterine anomalies, uterine fibroids, autoimmune disease, ART conception, and sociodemographic adversity, with inflammatory cytokine-mediated uterine contractility and cervical remodeling representing the most frequently invoked mechanistic explanation.

## Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes: Synthesis of the Literature

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across this cluster of 50 articles, several interconnected themes emerge regarding endometriosis and its relationship to adverse pregnancy outcomes. The dominant mechanistic framework centers on impaired placentation arising from chronic pelvic inflammation, aberrant hormonal signaling, and structural uterine dysfunction. Elevated estrogen levels, progesterone resistance, and inflammatory mediators—particularly cyclooxygenase-2, prostaglandin E2, and interleukin-8—are repeatedly implicated in disrupting trophoblast invasion, spiral artery remodeling, and endometrial receptivity. These pathological processes collectively predispose women with endometriosis to a characteristic spectrum of obstetric complications.

A second prominent theme involves the intersection of endometriosis with assisted reproductive technology (ART). Multiple papers document that ART itself independently increases risks of placenta previa, velamentous cord insertion, vasa previa, preterm birth, and low birth weight through mechanisms including trophectoderm manipulation, blastocyst malrotation, and altered endometrial receptivity from controlled ovarian stimulation. Disentangling endometriosis-attributable risk from ART-attributable risk thus represents a persistent methodological challenge across the literature.

Adenomyosis, representing myometrial invasion by ectopic endometrial tissue, is consistently treated as a closely related condition sharing mechanistic pathways with endometriosis, including myometrial hyperperistalsis, impaired placental implantation, and increased risk of preterm birth, preeclampsia, and postpartum hemorrhage.

### Most Consistently Reported Findings

The most consistently reported adverse outcomes associated with endometriosis across these papers are placenta previa, preterm birth, postpartum hemorrhage, small-for-gestational-age neonates, and stillbirth. Placenta previa occupies a particularly prominent position, appearing across multiple independent studies as a strongly endometriosis-associated complication, with one case-control study reporting placenta previa incidence of 36% in surgically treated endometriosis patients versus 7% in controls. The mechanistic pathway consistently invoked involves pelvic adhesions and chronic inflammation impairing normal placental migration and trophoblast invasion of the lower uterine segment.

Spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP) is identified across two independent studies as a rare but disproportionately endometriosis-associated complication, occurring with endometriosis in over 55% of reported cases, driven by decidualization of ectopic implants, vascular proliferation, and chronic inflammation predisposing to hemorrhage. This represents one of the more specific and consistently reported endometriosis-pregnancy associations in the cluster.

Several papers converge on deficient endometrial decidualization as a shared mechanistic pathway linking endometriosis, advanced maternal age, and progesterone resistance to adverse implantation and placental outcomes. One study demonstrating FOXC1 upregulation and epigenetic remodeling in aging uteri explicitly parallels the progesterone-resistant decidualization deficits observed in endometriosis-associated reproductive failure, suggesting overlapping molecular mechanisms.

The association between endometriosis and postpartum depression is reported in one large cohort study, positioning endometriosis within a broader landscape of gynecological risk factors for maternal mental health complications. Similarly, thyroid autoimmunity is noted to be epidemiologically co-associated with endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, with shared inflammatory immune mechanisms potentially mediating reproductive dysfunction across these conditions.

Metabolic syndrome and its components—insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and visceral adiposity—emerge as causally linked to multiple reproductive complications including polycystic ovary syndrome, gestational diabetes mellitus, and eclampsia via Mendelian randomization analysis, with specific plasma metabolite signatures identified for each condition. While endometriosis is situated within this metabolic-reproductive disease framework, the causal directionality between metabolic dysfunction and endometriosis-specific pregnancy complications requires further elucidation.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable contradictions and inconsistencies are apparent across this literature. First, the relationship between endometriosis treatment modality and pregnancy outcomes is conflicting. One case-control study reports that surgical treatment prior to pregnancy dramatically

## Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes: A Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Research across this cluster converges on several interconnected themes linking endometriosis to adverse pregnancy outcomes through chronic inflammatory dysregulation, impaired placentation, and uterine microenvironmental disruption. Endometriosis is consistently framed as a condition of estrogen predominance, progesterone resistance, and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines—particularly interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha—that collectively compromise decidualization, trophoblast invasion, and spiral artery remodeling. These defects in early placental development appear to be the proximate mechanism underlying the broad spectrum of obstetric complications associated with the disease. A second major theme concerns the compounding effect of assisted reproductive technology (ART), which independently elevates risks for multiple adverse outcomes through supraphysiological hormone exposure, epigenetic alterations from in vitro culture, and placental implantation abnormalities, with endometriosis further amplifying these risks. A third theme involves endometrial preparation protocol selection in frozen embryo transfer cycles, where the presence or absence of a functional corpus luteum emerges as a critical determinant of placental angiogenic balance and maternal cardiovascular adaptation.

### Consistently Reported Findings

The most consistently reported adverse outcomes associated with endometriosis across multiple study designs include preterm birth, placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, cesarean delivery, low birth weight, and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. One systematic review and meta-analysis reported a relative risk of 1.277 for preterm birth and 2.246 for placenta previa in women with endometriosis undergoing ART, while a separate population-level analysis found that approximately 20% of pregnant women with endometriosis experience preterm birth compared to 11.4% of controls. The mechanistic basis for these placental complications is attributed to defective spiral artery remodeling secondary to chronic pelvic inflammation and altered angiogenic signaling, with deep infiltrating endometriosis consistently identified as conferring the highest risk across placental complications and miscarriage compared to superficial or ovarian disease. The impairment of pro-angiogenic pathways—specifically reduced placental growth factor and vascular endothelial growth factor expression alongside elevated anti-angiogenic soluble Fms-like tyrosine kinase-1—is a repeatedly identified molecular mechanism connecting endometriosis and artificial cycle endometrial preparation to hypertensive disorders and placental dysfunction.

Artificial cycle frozen embryo transfer, which lacks corpus luteum-derived relaxin and pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A, is consistently associated with elevated risks of hypertensive disorders, placenta previa, and postpartum hemorrhage in both endometriosis and non-endometriosis populations, with natural cycle protocols demonstrating substantially reduced rates of threatened abortion (21.0% versus 35.1%) and recurrent pregnancy loss (5.7% versus 9.4%) specifically in endometriosis patients. These findings are corroborated by mechanistic data from oocyte donation studies demonstrating that corpus luteum preservation enhances placental vascular development through pro-angiogenic factor upregulation. Systemic inflammatory indices measured peripherally near delivery—including the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio and systemic immune-inflammation index—do not differ significantly between endometriosis patients who do and do not experience preterm birth, suggesting that the inflammatory pathophysiology operates predominantly at the local uterine tissue level rather than as a detectable systemic process, a finding with important implications for biomarker development.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several substantive conflicts emerge across this literature. A Mendelian randomization analysis found limited causal evidence for endometriosis causing preterm birth, gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, or postpartum hemorrhage, attributing previously reported associations to confounding, and demonstrated significant causal effects only for intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy in advanced-stage disease. This stands in direct contrast to multiple observational cohort studies and meta-analyses reporting significant associations between endometriosis and precisely these outcomes, raising unresolved questions about the degree to which endometriosis-related comorbidities, ART use, and disease staging mediate or confound observed risks. ## Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes: A Synthesis

The body of literature represented in this cluster collectively establishes that endometriosis and related gynecological conditions—including adenomyosis, Müllerian duct anomalies, and polycystic ovary syndrome—confer substantial risks across the pregnancy continuum, from implantation failure through postpartum complications. Several dominant mechanistic themes emerge consistently: chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, immune dysregulation particularly involving uterine natural killer cells and endometrial CD200S expression, epigenetic alterations, and structural distortion of the myometrium and endometrium impairing placentation and uterine distensibility.

The most consistently reported adverse outcomes associated with endometriosis include miscarriage, preterm birth, placenta previa, preeclampsia, and low birth weight. Notably, the Mendelian randomization analysis (PMC12998892) provides important nuance: while endometriosis demonstrates a significant causal relationship with recurrent pregnancy loss—plausibly mediated through immune dysregulation at the endometrial level—genetic analysis failed to confirm causal links between endometriosis and preterm birth, placental abruption, or low birth weight, suggesting that several widely reported observational associations may reflect confounding by comorbid conditions such as uterine fibroids, Müllerian anomalies, or PCOS. This finding stands in partial contradiction to the large meta-analytic data on adenomyosis (PMC13076185), which robustly demonstrates elevated risks across multiple adverse outcomes including preterm birth and placental complications, and to the South Korean cohort study (PMC12990608) identifying endometriosis as an independent preterm birth risk factor.

Adenomyosis, which shares pathophysiologic features with endometriosis including ectopic endometrial tissue and myometrial infiltration, demonstrates particularly strong and consistent associations with reduced live birth rates, miscarriage, placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, and preeclampsia. The identification of focal adenomyosis as a risk factor for asymptomatic uterine torsion during pregnancy (PMC12948917) represents an emerging structural complication with direct surgical implications.

Immune dysregulation emerges as a central mechanistic pathway across multiple papers. Elevated CD200S expression on endometrial natural killer cells in endometriosis may mediate embryo rejection and recurrent loss (PMC12998892), while advanced maternal age independently reduces regulatory T cell populations critical for decidual vascular remodeling (PMC12979401). Chronic endometritis, endometrial dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus crispatus dominance, and antiphospholipid-related antibodies (anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR) together constitute a triad of pathologies substantially contributing to unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss (PMC13029616), underscoring the multifactorial immune landscape of implantation failure.

Comorbid conditions including subclinical hypothyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, and PCOS appear across multiple studies as conditions sharing pathophysiologic overlap with endometriosis, each independently amplifying adverse pregnancy risks. Subclinical hypothyroidism, for instance, is overrepresented in women with endometriosis and PCOS and independently elevates risks of eclampsia and preterm delivery (PMC12942046). Vitamin D deficiency correlates with both endometriosis susceptibility and adverse pregnancy outcomes including hypertensive disorders and miscarriage, potentially through shared immune-modulatory and anti-inflammatory pathways (PMC12949526).

Structural uterine anomalies, including uterine didelphys and unicornuate uterus (PMC12974349; PMC12974371), share with endometriosis an association with retrograde menstruation, infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and abnormal placentation, reinforcing the concept that disrupted uterine architecture—whether developmental or acquired—broadly compromises reproductive outcomes through overlapping mechanisms. Iatrogenic endometriosis is also noted as a recognized complication of cesarean delivery in the context of failed trial of labor management (PMC13036918), extending considerations of endometriosis risk into the intrapartum setting.

# Androgens and IVF Outcomes in Endometriosis

## Overview

The intersection of androgen biology and assisted reproductive technology in women with endometriosis represents an increasingly investigated domain within reproductive medicine. The central mechanistic question concerns whether exogenous androgen supplementation — principally via transdermal testosterone or the adrenal precursor dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) — can rescue diminished follicular competence in endometriosis patients by augmenting androgen receptor-mediated signaling within granulosa cells, thereby restoring sensitivity to gonadotrophin stimulation and improving oocyte yield, embryo quality, and ultimately live birth rates. Endometriosis poses particular challenges for ovarian stimulation: the disease is associated with reduced ovarian reserve, accelerated follicular depletion, and a disproportionate representation of poor ovarian response phenotypes among women pursuing in vitro fertilization (IVF), making androgen priming a biologically plausible and clinically appealing intervention.

## Consensus Findings

The most robustly supported finding in the available literature derives from a Cochrane systematic review (PMC11152211) demonstrating that transdermal testosterone pre-treatment significantly improves live birth rates (odds ratio 2.53) and clinical pregnancy rates (odds ratio 2.17) in women with poor ovarian response undergoing assisted reproduction, relative to no pretreatment. The proposed mechanism centers on testosterone acting as a direct ligand for the androgen receptor in granulosa and theca cells, upregulating follicle-stimulating hormone receptor expression, promoting preantral-to-antral follicle transition, and amplifying gonadotrophin responsiveness during controlled ovarian stimulation. This receptor-mediated sensitization is thought to increase the number of dominant follicles recruited per stimulation cycle and to improve oocyte competence at retrieval. The evidence thus converges on transdermal testosterone as an effective adjunct in the poor ovarian response setting, with a mechanistic basis grounded in androgen receptor signaling and folliculogenesis.

## Controversies and Contradictions

Despite the signal favoring testosterone, DHEA supplementation — which has been widely adopted in clinical practice based on earlier observational data — did not demonstrate comparable improvements in live birth rate or clinical pregnancy rate in the same systematic review, challenging a degree of existing enthusiasm for its use. This dissociation between testosterone and DHEA effects is mechanistically significant: DHEA must undergo peripheral conversion to testosterone and then to estradiol via the aromatase pathway, and inter-individual variability in steroidogenic enzyme activity may render its androgenic potency inconsistent across patients. Whether women with endometriosis-associated poor ovarian response exhibit distinct steroidogenic microenvironments in follicular fluid — potentially including altered aromatase expression in ectopic versus eutopic tissue — that could explain differential responses to the two compounds remains unresolved. Furthermore, whether miscarriage rates are meaningfully altered by either intervention remains uncertain, with available data showing no significant effect, though this endpoint may be underpowered in existing trial designs.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Substantial mechanistic questions remain unanswered, particularly as they pertain specifically to the endometriosis population rather than to poor ovarian response patients broadly. The degree to which endometriosis-associated oxidative stress within the peritoneal and follicular microenvironment modifies androgen receptor expression or post-receptor signaling in granulosa cells has not been systematically characterized. The relationship between endometrioma-induced cortical fibrosis and local androgen bioavailability — including the extent to which follicles adjacent to endometriomas exhibit altered intrafollicular testosterone concentrations — remains poorly defined. Additionally, the optimal timing, dose, and route of testosterone administration relative to stimulation onset have not been established through adequately powered dose-finding trials in endometriosis-specific cohorts. The contribution of anti-Müllerian hormone dynamics and antral follicle count trajectories to androgen priming responsiveness in this population also warrants dedicated investigation.

## Translational Relevance

The available evidence carries meaningful clinical implications for the management of endometriosis patients who are identified as poor ovarian responders prior to IVF. Transdermal testosterone pre-treatment emerges as the androgen intervention with the most current evidentiary support for improving live birth and clinical pregnancy rates, and its adoption in structured stimulation protocols for this subgroup merits serious consideration. Androgen receptor agonism thus represents a tractable pharmacological target for improving reproductive outcomes without the systemic risks associated with higher-dose hormonal manipulations. Clinically, the dissociation between testosterone and DHEA efficacy suggests that presuming equivalence between these agents is unwarranted, and that protocol selection should be guided by the specific compound's documented benefit profile. Future translational work should prioritize endometriosis-stratified trial designs, integration of follicular fluid androgen profiling as a predictive biomarker, and exploration of whether adjunctive interventions targeting intraovarian inflammation could synergize with androgen priming to further optimize IVF outcomes in this population.

The 50 article summaries provided for the cluster "Androgens and IVF Outcomes in Endometriosis" do not constitute a coherent body of literature on this topic. The overwhelming majority of the submitted summaries describe content wholly unrelated to androgens, IVF, or endometriosis, including case reports of bowel obstruction and phytobezoar formation in myotonic dystrophy, veterinary surgical procedures, pediatric urology, global health workforce training, qualitative studies on domestic violence, bibliometric analyses of ovarian cancer machine learning literature, and educational curriculum development. A substantial proportion of summaries explicitly note that no relationship triples were extracted from the underlying articles, precluding any mechanistic synthesis.

Only one summary (PMC11152211) bears direct relevance to the designated cluster topic. That Cochrane systematic review reported that testosterone pre-treatment significantly improved live birth rates (OR 2.53) and clinical pregnancy rates (OR 2.17) in women with poor ovarian response undergoing assisted reproduction, putatively through enhanced follicular sensitivity to gonadotrophin stimulation and increased oocyte yield, whereas DHEA pre-treatment showed no comparable benefit on these endpoints. Neither androgen intervention meaningfully reduced miscarriage rates.

Given that 49 of 50 summaries are either topically irrelevant or contain no extractable findings, a valid synthesis of dominant themes, consistent mechanistic findings, or contradictions across the cluster cannot be responsibly constructed. Doing so would misrepresent the available evidence. A meaningful synthesis of androgens and IVF outcomes in endometriosis requires re-curation of the article set to include primary studies and reviews directly addressing androgen receptor signaling, folliculogenesis, ovarian reserve, and assisted reproductive outcomes in endometriosis patients.

The 50 article summaries provided for the cluster "Androgens and IVF Outcomes in Endometriosis" do not, in aggregate, constitute a coherent body of literature on this topic. The overwhelming majority of summaries describe articles entirely unrelated to androgens, IVF outcomes, or endometriosis, including studies on preterm infant neurodevelopment, aquaculture feed alternatives, dental pain, work-life balance among Iranian women, colonic volvulus, social media profiling in oncology, and environmental justice in China, among others. A substantial proportion of summaries explicitly report that no relationship triples were extracted from their respective articles, rendering them uninformative for synthesis purposes. The small number of articles with partial relevance to reproductive medicine address topics such as endometrioma surgical management before assisted reproduction, machine learning prediction of IVF outcomes in endometriosis, uric acid levels and IVF success, and comorbidity between endometriosis and irritable bowel syndrome or systemic lupus erythematosus, but none specifically examine androgen biology, androgen priming protocols, testosterone supplementation, or dehydroepiandrosterone administration in the context of IVF for endometriosis patients. Consequently, no synthesis of dominant themes, consistently reported mechanistic findings, or contradictions pertaining to androgens and IVF outcomes in endometriosis can be responsibly constructed from this article set. A meaningful review section on this topic would require articles directly investigating androgen receptor signaling, follicular androgen environments, DHEA or testosterone pretreatment protocols, and their effects on ovarian reserve, oocyte quality, or live birth rates in endometriosis populations.

The 50 articles assigned to this cluster—nominally focused on "Androgens and IVF Outcomes in Endometriosis"—do not, in aggregate, constitute a coherent body of mechanistic or clinical evidence on this topic. The overwhelming majority of submissions either failed to yield extractable relationship triples or were fundamentally misaligned with the stated cluster theme. Articles in this set include retraction and correction notices, expressions of concern, case reports on unrelated conditions (pericardial mesothelioma in hyenas, zoonotic bacteria in turtles), qualitative social science investigations, health services research, digital health implementation studies, and methodological commentaries spanning neonatology, cardiology, and colorectal surgery. None of the processed documents contains extractable data on androgen signaling, androgen receptor activity, testosterone or dehydroepiandrosterone supplementation, ovarian reserve, controlled ovarian stimulation protocols, or IVF outcome metrics in the context of endometriosis.

Where endometriosis-relevant content was nominally present—such as references to CA125 as a pain biomarker, curcumin's putative anti-angiogenic effects in ectopic tissue, or outcomes following endometriotic cystectomy prior to assisted reproduction—the source documents were editorial notices (expressions of concern, corrections, retractions) rather than primary data sources, precluding mechanistic synthesis. No consistent findings, dominant pathways, or reproducible entity-level associations can be reported across this article set. No contradictions can be meaningfully characterized in the absence of primary data.

This cluster as constituted does not support a scientifically valid synthesis on androgens and IVF outcomes in endometriosis; reprocessing with verified, topically appropriate primary literature is required before a meaningful review section can be written.

The eight article summaries provided to this synthesis task do not contain extractable biomedical content relevant to androgens, IVF outcomes, or endometriosis. None of the summaries include relationship triples, mechanistic data, molecular pathway information, or clinical findings pertaining to androgen signaling, ovarian stimulation protocols, or reproductive outcomes in endometriosis patients. The articles represented in this cluster appear to span unrelated domains including trauma-informed digital health design, bacteriophage research, robotic gynecological surgery, COVID-19 socioeconomic impacts, cancer family communication, ovarian cancer epidemiology, endometriosis diagnostics, and sexuality education pedagogy. While one article (PMC13073272) nominally references endometriosis diagnostics and another (PMC13031215) touches on gynecological surgery, neither provides structured data amenable to mechanistic synthesis.

Consequently, a meaningful scientific synthesis addressing dominant themes in androgen biology, consistently reported findings regarding testosterone or dehydroepiandrosterone supplementation, follicular microenvironment modulation, or IVF outcome metrics cannot be responsibly constructed from the materials provided. Doing so would require fabricating conclusions unsupported by the supplied evidence, which would compromise scientific integrity.

To generate a valid synthesis on androgens and IVF outcomes in endometriosis, the underlying full-text articles with extracted relationship triples documenting androgen receptor activity, ovarian reserve markers, stimulation response, embryo quality, and clinical pregnancy rates should be provided for analysis.

label: Ferroptosis and Oxidative Stress in Endometriosis

# Ferroptosis and Oxidative Stress in Endometriosis: Mechanisms, Contradictions, and Therapeutic Horizons

## Overview

The intersection of iron biology, lipid peroxidation, and regulated cell death has emerged as a mechanistically central framework for understanding endometriosis pathogenesis. Ferroptosis—an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death driven by the lethal accumulation of phospholipid hydroperoxides—occupies a paradoxical position in endometriosis: it operates simultaneously as a destructive force within the peritoneal microenvironment and as a fate that ectopic lesion cells have evolved to resist. This duality raises a fundamental research question: how do ectopic endometrial stromal cells survive and proliferate within an iron-replete, oxidatively stressed niche that is lethal to surrounding immune and stromal cells? Resolving this question carries direct implications for the development of ferroptosis-targeted therapeutics aimed at dismantling the lesion's resistance architecture while preserving host tissue integrity.

## Consensus Findings

The literature converges with remarkable consistency on the System Xc⁻–glutathione–GPX4 axis as the principal ferroptosis-regulatory module in endometriosis. Retrograde menstruation introduces erythrocytes into the peritoneal cavity, where hemolysis liberates hemoglobin and heme that catalyze reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation via Fenton chemistry, establishing chronic iron overload as the upstream initiating insult. This iron excess drives lipid peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acid-containing phospholipids through both enzymatic pathways—mediated by ACSL4, LPCAT3, and ALOX family lipoxygenases—and non-enzymatic mechanisms, collectively compromising membrane integrity and triggering ferroptotic death in susceptible cells. An inverse relationship between ferritin accumulation and GPX4 expression in endometriotic tissue is broadly reported, supporting the co-occurrence of iron loading and antioxidant depletion as cooperating pathogenic events. The transcription factor NRF2 constitutes the most consistently identified cytoprotective regulator, transcriptionally activating GPX4, SLC7A11, HO-1, NQO1, and SOD2; upstream activators including SIRT1 and SESN2, as well as the KEAP1–NRF2 axis, modulate this response across endometrial and ovarian contexts. Progesterone-driven transcription factors FOXO1 and NR2F2 further upregulate antioxidant enzymes including GPX3/4 and SOD2 during decidualization, and impaired progesterone signaling in endometriosis is explicitly linked to defective oxidative stress resistance and heightened ferroptotic susceptibility. Beyond the canonical GPX4 axis, iron-induced ferroptosis in lesion-infiltrating CD8⁺ T cells occurs through a p53-dependent mechanism that suppresses xCT and GPX4 expression, generating an immunosuppressive peritoneal microenvironment. Concurrently, ferroptosis in peritoneal macrophages impairs phagocytic clearance and stimulates pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory mediators—including VEGF-A and interleukin-8—thereby accelerating lesion establishment and vascularization.

## Controversies and Contradictions

A fundamental tension pervades the ferroptosis literature in endometriosis: while the iron-rich peritoneal environment promotes ferroptotic destruction of immune effector cells and normal stromal elements, ectopic endometrial cells paradoxically acquire multilayered ferroptosis resistance. Several mechanistically distinct resistance pathways have been identified, and their relative contributions remain contested. Aerobic glycolysis-driven lactate production promotes histone lactylation—specifically H3K18 lactylation—which epigenetically suppresses ACSL4 while upregulating SLC7A11 and GPX4, a mechanism further elaborated through METTL3-regulated m6A modification and HIF1A/HMOX1 signaling. FZD7-mediated Wnt/β-catenin activation similarly suppresses GPX4 and SLC7A11, yet the net consequence appears to be lesion viability rather than ferroptotic death, suggesting that the signaling context determines the directional outcome of these regulatory interactions. PDLIM3 additionally protects ectopic stromal cells from ferroptosis via Gli1/Hedgehog signaling stabilization. The role of heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) remains particularly unresolved: cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory in normal physiology, HO-1 associates with tumor aggressiveness and therapy resistance in malignant contexts, complicating its therapeutic targeting in endometriosis-to-malignancy transitions. Whether ferroptosis induction in ectopic cells represents a therapeutic opportunity or whether it inadvertently worsens the immunosuppressive and pro-angiogenic peritoneal environment by killing effector immune cells first is a contradiction that current models do not adequately address.

## Mechanistic Gaps

Several critical mechanistic questions remain inadequately explored. The precise threshold at which peritoneal iron accumulation shifts from driving lesion ferroptosis resistance to overwhelming ectopic cell defenses is undefined, and longitudinal studies tracking ferroptotic marker dynamics across disease stages are absent. The contribution of GPX4-independent suppression systems—specifically the FSP1–CoQ10H₂ axis, the GCH1–BH4 pathway, and DHODH—to ferroptosis resistance in ectopic endometrial stromal cells has not been systematically characterized, despite evidence from cancer models that these parallel pathways confer redundant protection. The ferritinophagy axis mediated by NCOA4–FTH1 has been described in ovarian and hepatic ferroptosis but remains largely unexplored in endometriosis lesions. Furthermore, mitochondrial contributions—including cristae remodeling, impaired β-oxidation, and PINK1-PARKIN-mediated mitophagy—are documented in adjacent reproductive contexts but have not been rigorously interrogated within endometriotic stroma. The interplay between epigenetic ferroptosis resistance (lactylation, m6A modification) and the somatic mutation landscape of endometriosis-associated malignancies such as clear cell ovarian carcinoma represents an unstudied interface of particular importance.

## Ferroptosis and Oxidative Stress in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across this cluster converges on ferroptosis—an iron-dependent, regulated form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation—as a mechanistically central process in endometriosis pathogenesis and a therapeutically exploitable vulnerability. Retrograde menstruation introduces erythrocytes into the peritoneal cavity, where hemolysis liberates hemoglobin and heme that catalyze reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation via the Fenton reaction, establishing iron overload as the initiating insult. This iron excess drives lipid peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acid-containing phospholipids, ultimately compromising cellular membrane integrity and triggering ferroptotic death. Simultaneously, the antioxidant axis centered on glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) and the cystine transporter SLC7A11 is repeatedly implicated as the principal defense system whose dysregulation determines ferroptotic susceptibility across endometriosis-relevant cell types.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Multiple studies converge on a significant inverse relationship between ferritin and GPX4 expression in endometriotic tissue, supporting iron accumulation and antioxidant depletion as co-occurring pathogenic events. The GPX4/SLC7A11/glutathione axis emerges as the most consistently reported ferroptosis-regulatory module; its suppression, whether through iron overload, androgen excess (as in PCOS-associated endometrial pathology), or upstream signaling disruption, uniformly promotes lipid peroxidation and cell death. Several papers additionally identify ACSL4 as a pro-ferroptotic executioner that amplifies phospholipid peroxidation, counterbalancing the protective GPX4 axis. Beyond canonical ferroptosis regulators, disease-specific upstream mechanisms are identified: PDLIM3 protects ectopic endometrial stromal cells from ferroptosis via Gli1/Hedgehog signaling stabilization, while FZD7-mediated Wnt/β-catenin activation suppresses GPX4 and SLC7A11 to confer ferroptosis resistance, paradoxically maintaining lesion viability despite the iron-rich microenvironment. Macrophage ferroptosis, induced by peritoneal iron excess, impairs phagocytic function and stimulates pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory mediators including VEGF-A and interleukin-8, thereby accelerating lesion establishment. The transsulfuration enzyme CTH, highly expressed in endometriosis-derived clear cell ovarian cancer, links the chronically oxidative endometriotic niche to malignant progression through HIF1α-dependent mechanisms that preserve redox homeostasis and promote metastasis.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

A notable tension pervades the literature regarding the net effect of ferroptosis in endometriosis: while iron-driven ferroptosis destroys normal peritoneal cells and impairs immune surveillance, ectopic endometrial cells paradoxically acquire ferroptosis resistance through upregulation of protective mechanisms including DMT-1-mediated iron storage, FZD7/Wnt signaling, and PDLIM3-Gli1 stabilization. This duality—ferroptosis as simultaneously destructive to the host environment and evaded by lesion cells—creates an apparent paradox that is incompletely resolved across the surveyed papers. Additionally, heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) presents conflicting roles: in normal physiology HO-1 is cytoprotective and anti-carcinogenic, yet in cancer contexts its expression associates with tumor aggressiveness and resistance, complicating its therapeutic targeting in endometriosis-to-malignancy transitions. The relationship between ferroptosis induction and disease suppression versus progression therefore remains context-dependent, and the precise threshold at which iron accumulation shifts from promoting lesion ferroptosis resistance to overwhelming cellular defenses requires further delineation.

## Ferroptosis and Oxidative Stress in Endometriosis: A Synthesis of Recent Research

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on ferroptosis—an iron-dependent, regulated form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation—as a mechanistically central process across reproductive, inflammatory, and oncological contexts. The dominant regulatory framework involves the System Xc⁻/GSH/GPX4 axis, wherein inhibition of the cystine-glutamate antiporter SLC7A11 depletes intracellular glutathione, inactivates GPX4, and permits accumulation of toxic phospholipid hydroperoxides. Iron overload amplifies this process through Fenton chemistry, generating reactive oxygen species that propagate lipid peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids via ACSL4/LPCAT3/lipoxygenase-mediated mechanisms.

Within endometriosis-specific literature, several papers identify a paradoxical ferroptosis resistance in ectopic endometrial stromal cells. One study demonstrates that elevated aerobic glycolysis increases lactate production, driving histone lactylation—particularly H3K18 lactylation—that epigenetically suppresses ACSL4 while upregulating SLC7A11 and GPX4, thereby maintaining ferroptosis resistance. A complementary paper elaborates that METTL3-regulated m6A modification and HIF1A/HMOX1 signaling underlie this lactylation-mediated resistance, and that glycolysis inhibitors (2-deoxy-D-glucose, oxamate) sensitize ectopic cells to erastin-induced ferroptosis in vivo. Lysine lactylation more broadly emerges as a multi-target suppressor of ferroptosis, additionally activating GCH1, FTH1, and NFS1 to maintain iron homeostasis and antioxidant defenses in endometriotic tissue.

Iron overload in endometriotic lesions, arising from retrograde menstruation and hemoglobin catabolism by peritoneal macrophages, operates as a primary upstream driver. One paper reports that this iron excess induces ferroptosis in lesion-infiltrating CD8⁺ T cells through a p53-dependent mechanism that suppresses xCT and GPX4, creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment that paradoxically promotes lesion survival despite ferroptotic cell death. The same iron-rich milieu that destroys immune effector cells simultaneously drives angiogenesis through ferroptosis-induced VEGFA and IL-8 secretion from ectopic stromal cells, with some of these cells exhibiting resistance via ATF4-xCT and Fibulin-1 pathways. The compound 4-hydroxyindole, identified as a biomarker depleted in endometriosis patients, functions as a radical-trapping antioxidant ferroptosis inhibitor, suggesting that its reduced levels may contribute to endometriotic pathology. Additionally, NRF2/GCL axis deregulation has been documented in endometriotic lesions, linking the master antioxidant transcription factor to pathological growth and fibrosis through mechanisms shared with female malignancies.

Extending beyond endometriosis, ferroptosis dysregulation recurs across related gynecological and reproductive contexts. In adenomyosis, a METTL3-m6A-YTHDF1-GPX4 axis is disrupted in myometrial lesions, reducing GPX4 translation and promoting ferroptosis, with GPX4 levels inversely correlating with disease severity markers including CA125, uterine size, and dysmenorrhea. In polycystic ovary syndrome, DPP4-mediated ferroptosis impairs endometrial receptivity and decidualization, reversible by sitagliptin treatment. Ovarian ferroptosis driven by GPX4/ACSL4 dysregulation contributes to follicular atresia, oocyte aging, granulosa cell death, and PCOS-related infertility. Maternal sleep deprivation induces ferroptosis in offspring ovaries through dysregulation of GPX4, NRF2, ACSL4, and SLC7A11, reducing the primordial follicle reserve.
