## Ferroptosis and Oxidative Stress in Endometriosis: Synthesis of Current Evidence

The assembled literature converges on ferroptosis—an iron-dependent, lipid peroxidation-driven form of regulated cell death—as a mechanistically central process across a broad spectrum of pathological conditions, with particular relevance to reproductive and endometrial biology. The dominant regulatory axis consistently described is the System Xc⁻–glutathione–GPX4 pathway, wherein disruption of cystine import via SLC7A11 depletes cellular glutathione and inactivates GPX4, permitting accumulation of toxic phospholipid hydroperoxides. This mechanism is reported across virtually all surveyed conditions, from ischemia-reperfusion injury and cancer to ovarian dysfunction and endometriosis-related complications.

Iron homeostasis emerges as a second dominant theme. Elevated labile iron drives Fenton chemistry, generating reactive oxygen species that initiate lipid peroxidation through both enzymatic mediators (ACSL4, ALOX family enzymes) and non-enzymatic mechanisms. Ferritinophagy via the NCOA4–FTH1 axis, transferrin receptor upregulation, and impaired ferroportin-mediated export collectively amplify intracellular iron in pathological states. In the context of endometriosis specifically, recurrent intrapelvic hemorrhage establishes chronic iron overload in the peritoneal microenvironment, which several papers identify as a driver of ferroptosis in granulosa cells, osteoblasts, and endometrial stromal cells, linking the disease to downstream complications including premature ovarian insufficiency, implantation failure, and osteoporosis.

Multiple upstream regulatory nodes modulate ferroptosis sensitivity across tissues. The Nrf2 transcription factor is the most consistently cited cytoprotective regulator, transcriptionally activating GPX4, SLC7A11, HO-1, NQO1, and SOD2. Upstream activators of Nrf2 reported across papers include SIRT1, RORα, SESN2, and C-Phycocyanin, while the KEAP1–NRF2 axis also governs iron homeostasis through ABCG2 and other iron-handling proteins. Progesterone-driven transcription factors FOXO1 and NR2F2 similarly upregulate antioxidant enzymes including GPX3/4 and SOD2 during decidualization, and impaired progesterone signaling in endometriosis is explicitly linked to defective oxidative stress resistance and ferroptosis susceptibility. The TP53–SLC7A11 axis constitutes a complementary pro-ferroptotic regulatory node: p53 transcriptionally represses SLC7A11 and activates ALOX12, amplifying lipid peroxidation, a mechanism documented in hepatic, ovarian, and endometrial contexts.

Mitochondrial integrity is repeatedly implicated as both a target and mediator of ferroptosis. Mitochondrial dysfunction—characterized by reduced membrane potential, cristae loss, and impaired β-oxidation—precedes or accompanies ferroptotic cell death across multiple models. Mitophagy, regulated by AMBRA1 and the PINK1-PARKIN pathway, functions as a protective mechanism by clearing damaged mitochondria and maintaining redox homeostasis; its suppression by mycotoxins or other stressors exacerbates ferroptosis. The SIRT1–FOXO3–PGC-1α axis supports mitochondrial biogenesis as an additional layer of ferroptosis resistance in granulosa cells, particularly relevant to ovarian aging and PCOS pathophysiology.

Supplementary ferroptosis suppression mechanisms beyond GPX4 are documented with increasing frequency. The FSP1–CoQ10H₂ axis and the GCH1–BH4 pathway independently limit lipid peroxidation without relying on glutathione, and their suppression is proposed as a strategy to sensitize cancer cells to ferroptosis-based therapies. DHODH represents a third GPX4-independent suppressor noted in cancer contexts. These parallel systems suggest a layered, redundant architecture of ferroptosis resistance that may be differentially active across tissue types.

label: Ectopic Pregnancy and Endometriosis Management
l3:
## Ectopic Pregnancy and Endometriosis: Pathophysiology, Management, and Emerging Directions

### Overview

The central research questions animating this literature cluster concern how endometriosis and ascending genital tract infection cooperatively and independently disrupt fallopian tube structure and function to predispose women to ectopic implantation, and how advances in diagnostics, pharmacotherapy, and minimally invasive surgery can reduce the attendant morbidity and mortality. Ectopic pregnancy, occurring in approximately 95% of cases within the fallopian tube but increasingly documented at rare anatomical sites including the cesarean scar, ovary, cervix, retroperitoneum, and peritoneal surfaces, represents a paradigmatic intersection of reproductive pathology and clinical emergency medicine. Endometriosis occupies a dual position in this literature: it is simultaneously a mechanistic contributor to ectopic implantation and a complicating factor that constrains surgical and medical management options.

### Consensus Findings

The literature converges with notable consistency on the pathogenic mechanisms by which endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) promote ectopic pregnancy. Endometriosis impairs fallopian tube function through at least three complementary mechanisms: disruption of ciliary beat frequency and smooth muscle peristalsis, peritoneal adhesion formation that distorts normal tubal architecture, and chronic inflammatory signaling that alters the local hormonal milieu, with estradiol and progesterone exerting opposing modulatory effects on tubal contractility. Machine learning models integrating clinical, hormonal, and obstetric variables consistently identify endometriosis as an independent predictor of ectopic pregnancy, with reported odds ratios of approximately 2.6. PID, principally driven by *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, is uniformly identified as the leading modifiable risk factor, operating through epithelial damage, cytokine-mediated fibrosis involving IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α, and progressive luminal scarring. Emerging pathogens—*Mycoplasma genitalium*, *Ureaplasma urealyticum*, and anaerobes associated with bacterial vaginosis—are increasingly recognized as contributors to this fibroinflammatory cascade, and antimicrobial resistance data documenting 100% ciprofloxacin resistance among *N. gonorrhoeae* isolates from infertile women further complicate the prevention of tubal damage upstream of ectopic implantation.

Diagnostically, serial serum β-hCG measurement combined with transvaginal ultrasonography constitutes the established standard, with MRI demonstrating superior localizing capability for atypical implantation sites where ultrasound is insufficient. Methotrexate, functioning through inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase and consequent suppression of trophoblast DNA synthesis, remains the dominant first-line medical therapy for hemodynamically stable patients, with treatment success most reliably predicted by baseline β-hCG below approximately 4,340 mIU/mL, absence of fetal cardiac activity, and small ectopic mass diameter. Importantly, prospective data indicate that methotrexate does not significantly impair ovarian reserve as measured by anti-Müllerian hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, or antral follicle count. Laparoscopic salpingectomy remains the surgical standard for ruptured or hemodynamically unstable presentations, with vNOTES demonstrating measurable advantages in operative time, hemoperitoneum drainage, and postoperative pain in appropriately selected patients.

### Controversies and Contradictions

Several substantive contradictions persist within the evidence base. Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor that suppresses placental estrogen and VEGF-mediated trophoblast proliferation, has emerged as a candidate alternative to methotrexate, yet a meta-analysis reported comparable success rates with superior hepatic and hematological safety profiles, while a randomized trial found modestly lower success rates—approximately 70% versus 85%—despite more rapid β-hCG decline, leaving the comparative efficacy question unresolved. Competing clinical prediction algorithms for managing pregnancies of unknown location, including the M4 model and the NICE algorithm, demonstrate non-inferior but not superior sensitivity relative to each other, with opposing trade-offs in specificity and false-positive rates. Expectant management of tubal ectopic pregnancy has been associated with recurrence rates comparable to surgical intervention in at least one retrospective cohort, contradicting broader guidance favoring intervention, though this finding is likely confounded by selection bias inherent to retrospective design. The protective effect of frozen-thawed versus fresh embryo transfer in reducing ART-associated ectopic pregnancy risk requires further prospective validation, and the paradoxical observation that bilateral salpingectomy reduces tubal ectopic recurrence while elevating non-tubal ectopic implantation rates and compromising ovarian blood supply remains incompletely reconciled in clinical counseling frameworks.

### Mechanistic Gaps

Despite substantial progress, critical mechanistic gaps remain. The precise molecular pathways through which endometriosis-associated inflammatory signaling reprograms trophoblast invasion dynamics at ectopic implantation sites are poorly characterized. The contributions of emerging pathogens such as *Mycoplasma genitalium* to tubal dysfunction independent of classical PID pathogens remain inadequately quantified. Furthermore, the downstream mechanisms by which elevated serum estradiol at ART trigger and multiple fresh embryo transfer elevate ectopic implantation risk have not been fully delineated at the level of endometrial receptivity, tubal contractility, and embryo-epithelial signaling. The global burden paradox—wherein age-standardized ectopic pregnancy incidence declined between 1990 and 2021 while absolute disability-adjusted life years increased—has not been mechanistically explained and likely reflects heterogeneous healthcare access rather than biological variation, but this hypothesis remains underexplored.

### Translational Relevance

Several translational directions emerge with particular promise. Nanomedicine platforms, including glutathione-responsive polymersomes enabling a six-fold reduction in methotrexate dose with preserved trophoblastic efficacy and absent hepatic or renal toxicity in murine models, offer a compelling strategy for expanding the therapeutic window of the current standard of care. Circulating microRNA signatures, particularly hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-411-5p ratios, represent candidate non-invasive biomarkers for early differential diagnosis of pregnancies of unknown location

## Synthesis: Ectopic Pregnancy and Endometriosis Management

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The literature across these 50 articles converges on three principal themes: the pathophysiology and clinical management of ectopic pregnancy across its diverse anatomical presentations, the role of ascending genital tract infections in promoting reproductive pathology, and emerging diagnostic and therapeutic innovations aimed at improving patient outcomes. Ectopic pregnancy—occurring in the fallopian tube in approximately 95% of cases but also documented at rare sites including the ovary, cervix, cesarean scar, diaphragm, and omentum—emerges as the central clinical entity, consistently framed as a life-threatening emergency requiring prompt diagnosis and individualized management. Endometriosis appears recurrently not as a primary subject but as a significant contributing risk factor, implicated in tubal dysfunction, pelvic adhesion formation, altered ovum transport, and abnormal implantation, thereby increasing susceptibility to both tubal and non-tubal ectopic gestations.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Several findings appear with high consistency across the corpus. Pelvic inflammatory disease, driven primarily by *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, is uniformly identified as a leading modifiable risk factor for ectopic pregnancy through mechanisms of tubal epithelial damage, ciliary loss, scar tissue formation, and adhesion development. Endometriosis is similarly cited across multiple studies as independently increasing ectopic pregnancy risk by disrupting normal tubal architecture and promoting peritoneal adhesions. Diagnosis relies consistently on the combination of serial serum β-hCG measurement and transvaginal ultrasonography, with laparoscopy providing definitive confirmation and treatment. Methotrexate—a folate antagonist inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase and thereby suppressing rapidly proliferating trophoblast tissue—is consistently endorsed as the first-line medical therapy for hemodynamically stable patients with unruptured ectopic pregnancies, with gestational sac diameter greater than 20 mm and elevated β-hCG levels identified as the most reliable predictors of treatment failure. Surgical management via laparoscopic salpingectomy remains the standard for unstable or ruptured cases. Assisted reproductive technologies, particularly IVF and ovarian stimulation protocols, consistently emerge as independent risk factors substantially elevating heterotopic and non-tubal ectopic pregnancy incidence, likely through mechanisms involving multiple embryo transfer, compromised endometrial receptivity, and ovulatory dysfunction. One prospective cohort study demonstrated that methotrexate administration—whether as single or multiple-dose regimens—does not significantly impair ovarian reserve as measured by anti-Müllerian hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, or antral follicle count, supporting its reproductive safety profile. Emerging nanomedicine approaches, including glutathione-responsive polymersomes enabling a six-fold methotrexate dose reduction with preserved efficacy and absent hepatic or renal toxicity in murine models, represent a promising therapeutic direction, as do microRNA biomarkers such as hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-411-5p ratios for early differential diagnosis of pregnancies of unknown location. Minimally invasive surgical techniques including single-incision laparoscopy and vNOTES demonstrated comparable safety to conventional laparoscopy with measurable advantages in blood loss, postoperative pain, and cosmetic outcomes. Bilateral salpingectomy, while reducing tubal ectopic recurrence, is reported to paradoxically increase non-tubal ectopic implantation rates and compromise ovarian blood supply, diminishing IVF success rates—a finding with direct clinical implications for patient counseling.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several areas of apparent conflict warrant attention. The M4 prediction model and NICE algorithm for managing pregnancies of unknown location demonstrated non-inferior but not superior sensitivity for ectopic pregnancy detection compared to each other, with opposing trade-offs in specificity and false-positive rates, leaving the optimal algorithmic approach unresolved. Expectant management of tubal ectopic pregnancy was associated with recurrence rates comparable to surgical management in one retrospective cohort, which contrasts with broader literature emphasizing surgical intervention as the standard of care and may reflect selection bias inherent to retrospective design. The protective effect of blastocyst transfer in reducing IVF-related ectopic pregnancy risk was

## Synthesis: Ectopic Pregnancy and Endometriosis Management

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

Across these fifty articles, several converging themes emerge. First, ectopic pregnancy is consistently framed as a multifactorial condition arising from structural and functional compromise of fallopian tube transport, with endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), prior tubal surgery, and assisted reproductive technology (ART) constituting the most frequently cited predisposing factors. Second, the literature emphasizes a diagnostic triad—serial serum β-hCG measurement, transvaginal ultrasonography, and cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI)—as foundational to identifying both typical and atypical implantation sites. Third, management strategies span a spectrum from expectant care and medical therapy to minimally invasive and open surgery, with patient-specific risk stratification increasingly guided by biochemical and imaging criteria.

### Consistently Reported Findings

Endometriosis appears as a risk factor across virtually all ectopic pregnancy subtypes, including tubal, ovarian, interstitial, retroperitoneal, cervical, intramural, and abdominal pregnancies, operating through adhesion formation, ciliary dysfunction, altered tubal contractility, and chronic pelvic inflammation. Multiple studies confirm that endometriosis impairs fallopian tube function by disrupting ciliary beat frequency and smooth muscle peristalsis, with estradiol and progesterone exerting opposing modulatory effects on these parameters. Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are the most consistently implicated infectious agents driving tubal factor pathology through epithelial damage, cytokine-mediated fibrosis (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), and progressive scarring; emerging pathogens including Mycoplasma genitalium, Ureaplasma urealyticum, and anaerobes associated with bacterial vaginosis are increasingly recognised as contributors. Methotrexate, functioning through inhibition of tetrahydrofolate and consequent blockade of trophoblast DNA synthesis, remains the dominant medical intervention, with treatment success most reliably predicted by baseline β-hCG levels below approximately 4,340 mIU/mL, absence of fetal cardiac activity, and small ectopic mass size. Day-4 β-hCG trajectory, lymphocyte counts, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio further refine methotrexate failure prediction. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the predominant surgical approach across tubal, ovarian, and heterotopic presentations, with robotic-assisted techniques offering enhanced precision for reconstructive procedures such as fimbrioplasty and neosalpingostomy. ART, particularly multiple fresh embryo transfers, blastocyst-stage transfer, and elevated serum estradiol at trigger, significantly elevates heterotopic and ectopic pregnancy incidence, while frozen-thawed embryo transfers appear comparatively protective. Machine learning models, including Random Forest and CatBoost algorithms, achieve diagnostic AUCs of 0.87–0.93 by integrating clinical, hormonal, and obstetric variables, with endometriosis consistently emerging as an independent predictor (odds ratio approximately 2.6).

Atypical ectopic sites—retroperitoneal, hepatic, peritoneal, and intramural—are uniformly characterised by diagnostic delay attributable to nonspecific presentations and limitations of standard ultrasonography, with MRI proving superior for localisation. The global burden of ectopic pregnancy demonstrates a paradox: age-standardised incidence rates declined between 1990 and 2021, yet absolute disability-adjusted life years increased substantially, reflecting population growth and disproportionate burden in sub-Saharan Africa and among women aged 20–24 years.

Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor that suppresses placental estrogen and VEGF-mediated trophoblast proliferation, emerges across two comparative studies as a safer alternative to methotrexate, with one meta-analysis reporting comparable success rates and more favourable hepatic and haematological safety profiles, while a randomised trial reported modestly lower success rates (70% versus 85%) despite more rapid β-hCG decline. This discrepancy represents the most notable contradiction within the corpus. Similarly, salpingectomy versus salpingostomy comparisons

## Ectopic Pregnancy and Pelvic Infection: Mechanisms, Management, and Complicating Factors

The five papers reviewed collectively address the pathophysiology, surgical management, and infectious etiologies underlying ectopic pregnancy and related pelvic pathology, with endometriosis emerging as a recurrent, cross-cutting risk factor.

A dominant theme across this cluster is the multifactorial etiology of ectopic pregnancy. Endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), prior pelvic surgery, and assisted reproductive technologies are consistently identified as predisposing conditions across the tubal, ovarian, and recurrent ectopic pregnancy literature. Mechanistically, these conditions share a common pathway of disrupted tubal motility and anatomical distortion that impairs normal embryo transport. N. gonorrhoeae-mediated ascending infection represents a particularly important upstream driver of PID-associated ectopic risk, with the antimicrobial resistance data highlighting 100% ciprofloxacin resistance and 78.3% multidrug resistance in isolates from infertile women—an alarming finding with direct implications for preventing the tubal damage that predisposes to ectopic implantation. Similarly, the Candida glabrata tubo-ovarian abscess case illustrates how immunocompromised states can precipitate severe adnexal disease through non-bacterial mechanisms that standard empiric regimens fail to address.

Regarding surgical management, minimally invasive approaches—specifically laparoscopy and vNOTES—are consistently favored, with vNOTES demonstrating additional advantages in operative time reduction and hemoperitoneum drainage via direct pouch of Douglas access. Importantly, advanced-stage endometriosis obliterating the pouch of Douglas is explicitly contraindicated for vNOTES, representing a clinically significant limitation. The case supporting complete over partial salpingectomy to minimize recurrence complements this surgical literature.

No direct contradictions were identified across papers; however, the tension between fertility preservation through conservative surgery and recurrence risk minimization through complete salpingectomy remains an unresolved clinical consideration requiring individualized decision-making.

## Women's Reproductive Health and Cancer Risk: Endometriosis in the Context of Oncogenic and Inflammatory Mechanisms

### Overview

A central question animating this body of literature is how chronic inflammatory and hormonal dysregulation in gynecological conditions—particularly endometriosis—intersects with malignant transformation, immune dysfunction, and broader reproductive morbidity. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent condition defined by the ectopic implantation of endometrial-like tissue, has emerged not merely as a cause of infertility but as a pathological state with mechanistic overlaps shared by oncogenic processes. The research cluster situates endometriosis within a wider framework of immune-mediated reproductive dysfunction, asking whether the condition represents an independent cancer risk modifier, a marker of systemic immune dysregulation, or a direct precursor to specific malignancies—particularly clear-cell and endometrioid subtypes of epithelial ovarian cancer.

### Consensus Findings

The literature consistently characterizes endometriosis as an estrogen-dependent, pro-inflammatory condition whose incidence is rising across diverse global populations, including sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia, populations previously underrepresented in epidemiological datasets. Across multiple study designs, endometriosis is repeatedly implicated as a contributor to the global infertility burden, operating through tubal distortion, impaired endometrial receptivity, and immune-mediated disruption of implantation tolerance. The inflammatory milieu associated with endometriotic lesions—characterized by elevated peritoneal concentrations of IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α—mirrors the cytokine profile associated with vaginal dysbiosis and HPV-driven cervical carcinogenesis, suggesting shared upstream mechanisms of chronic immune activation. Nulliparity and hormonal dysregulation consistently appear as shared epidemiological risk factors linking endometriosis to endometrial and ovarian cancers, reinforcing the hypothesis that prolonged unopposed estrogen exposure and disrupted progesterone signaling create a permissive oncogenic environment. Mendelian randomization analyses add causal nuance, implicating dietary modulators—including coffee and shellfish consumption—as gene-environment interactors that modify endometriosis susceptibility, though the specific metabolic intermediaries remain under investigation.

### Controversies and Contradictions

Despite broad consensus on the inflammatory character of endometriosis, the magnitude and specificity of its association with cancer risk remain contested. While epidemiological studies consistently document elevated relative risks for clear-cell and endometrioid ovarian carcinoma in women with endometriosis, absolute risk elevation remains modest, and the degree to which confounding by shared hormonal or immune factors explains these associations is incompletely resolved. A parallel controversy concerns the directionality of immune dysregulation: it remains unclear whether the immunosuppressive microenvironment in endometriosis—featuring regulatory T cell accumulation and reduced NK cell cytotoxicity analogous to tumor immune evasion—drives malignant progression or instead reflects systemic immune tolerance mechanisms that are epiphenomenal to the condition. Additionally, findings on hormone replacement therapy in perimenopausal women with prior endometriosis carry contradictory risk signals; one meta-analysis reports opposing effects for squamous and adenocarcinoma subtypes (OR=0.51 versus OR=1.82), complicating clinical guidance in women who also carry a history of estrogen-sensitive endometriotic disease. The relationship between vaginal microbiome dysbiosis and endometriosis-associated infertility is similarly unresolved, as a randomized trial restoring Lactobacillus-dominant communities failed to improve live-birth rates, suggesting that dysbiosis may be a correlated marker rather than an independent, causally modifiable determinant of implantation failure.

### Mechanistic Gaps

Substantial mechanistic gaps persist. Foremost among these is the incomplete characterization of the molecular transition from endometriotic epithelium to frank malignancy. The roles of PIK3CA and KMT2D mutations—each observed at approximately 27% prevalence in cervical cancer genomics datasets—and APOBEC-associated mutagenesis signatures have not been systematically interrogated in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers, leaving open the question of whether endometriotic lesions accumulate analogous mutational landscapes under chronic inflammatory selection pressure. The contribution of miRNA dysregulation, including altered expression of hsa-miR-125b-5p and hsa-miR-23b-3p documented in HPV-driven carcinogenesis, has not been formally mapped onto endometriosis-to-cancer transition models. Furthermore, the interaction between endometriosis and co-existing infectious burden—including HPV, Chlamydia trachomatis, and Mycoplasma genitalium—on cumulative cancer risk is entirely understudied, despite the mechanistic plausibility of synergistic immune suppression. The role of vitamin D deficiency, prevalent in over 70% of women with cervical lesions in high HIV-prevalence settings, as a potential modulator of endometriosis-associated immune dysregulation also lacks dedicated investigation.

### Translational Relevance

The translational implications of this research are multifold. Recognition of endometriosis as an immune-inflammatory condition with oncogenic potential positions anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory strategies—including IL-6 and TNF-α pathway inhibition—as rational candidates for lesion suppression and potentially for cancer chemoprevention in high-risk individuals. The documented overlap between endometriosis-associated immune exhaustion markers (FOXP3+ regulatory T cells, PD-L1 upregulation) and those operative in established gynecological malignancies raises the prospect that checkpoint immunotherapy agents currently employed in cervical cancer could be evaluated in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer subtypes. From a screening perspective, integrating endometriosis history into stratified ovarian cancer surveillance protocols is supported by the epidemiological evidence, even if absolute risk thresholds remain debated. Finally, the genetic susceptibility data—including Lynch syndrome MMR gene mutations and FOXP3 polymorphisms—suggest that molecular profiling of women with endometriosis could refine individual cancer risk estimates, enabling more targeted surveillance and preventive intervention in a condition whose clinical management remains predominantly symptomatic.

## Women's Reproductive Health and Cancer Risk: Synthesis of Recent Literature

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The preponderance of papers in this cluster converges on human papillomavirus as the central etiological agent across a spectrum of gynecological and anogenital malignancies. HPV-driven carcinogenesis is consistently framed through the oncoproteins E6 and E7, which respectively degrade p53 and inactivate the retinoblastoma protein, thereby abrogating apoptosis and enabling uncontrolled cell cycle progression. Beyond this canonical mechanism, multiple papers extend the carcinogenic framework to encompass immune evasion, tumor microenvironment remodeling, and microbial co-factors. A secondary but prominent theme concerns the broader epidemiology of gynecological disease burden—including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and polycystic ovary syndrome—and its intersection with cancer risk, reproductive dysfunction, and socioeconomic disparities.

### Consistently Reported Findings

HPV-16 and HPV-18 emerge as the dominant oncogenic genotypes across cervical, vaginal, vulvar, anal, and oropharyngeal cancers, with HPV-16 predominantly associated with squamous cell carcinoma and HPV-18 with adenocarcinoma and neuroendocrine subtypes. The immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment is consistently characterized by PD-L1 upregulation, CD8+ T cell exhaustion bearing markers including HAVCR2 and TIGIT, FOXP3+ regulatory T cell accumulation, and impaired dendritic and Langerhans cell function. Vaginal microbiome composition, particularly Lactobacillus crispatus-dominant communities, is repeatedly identified as protective against HPV persistence and cervical disease progression, operating through suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α and direct downregulation of E6/E7 oncoproteins. Dysbiosis involving Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, and Porphyromonas gingivalis associates with enhanced cervical cancer risk and metastatic potential, with the latter activating the JNK/paxillin pathway via FimA–CD151–integrin β1 interactions. Across reproductive outcomes, seminal HPV infection—detected in 8.9–23% of samples—correlates with elevated sperm DNA fragmentation, increased miscarriage rates, and reduced live-birth rates, implicating post-fertilization mechanisms rather than impaired fertilization per se. Genetic susceptibility factors including FOXP3 polymorphisms (rs3761548, rs2232365), RAD18 variants (rs250403, rs615967), and Lynch syndrome MMR gene mutations (particularly MSH2) independently modify cervical and gynecological cancer risk. Obesity, nulliparity, and hormonal dysregulation consistently appear as shared risk factors for endometrial and ovarian cancers, while cervical cancer burden inversely correlates with socioeconomic development, remaining concentrated in low-income settings where HPV vaccination and screening coverage are limited.

### Contradictions and Conflicting Results

Several notable discordances emerge across papers. Regarding seminal HPV and assisted reproduction, two studies report conflicting associations with sperm motility: one cohort found high-risk HPV positivity correlated with increased progressive motility in non-infertile men, while others consistently document reduced motility and morphological defects, suggesting that population heterogeneity or HPV genotype composition may explain divergent results. With respect to hormone replacement therapy, one meta-analysis reports paradoxically opposing effects—a protective association with squamous cell carcinoma (OR=0.51) contrasted by increased adenocarcinoma risk (OR=1.82)—complicating clinical guidance for perimenopausal women. Regarding vaginal microbiome intervention, a randomized controlled trial found that clindamycin with or without Lactobacillus crispatus (LACTIN-V) successfully restored normobiosis but failed to improve IVF pregnancy or live-birth rates, contradicting observational evidence linking dysbiosis causally to implantation failure and suggesting that dysbiosis may be a marker rather than a modifiable determinant of poor reproductive outcomes. Finally, while iron adequacy is associated with reduced HPV infection risk, iron excess is

## Synthesis: Women's Reproductive Health, Infection, and Cancer Risk

### Dominant Themes and Mechanisms

The papers in this cluster converge on three interconnected themes: (1) the central role of infectious agents—particularly human papillomavirus, sexually transmitted bacterial pathogens, and dysbiotic microbiota—in driving reproductive dysfunction and malignant transformation; (2) immune-mediated mechanisms linking chronic inflammation to both infertility and oncogenesis; and (3) the modifiable nature of many risk factors, emphasizing prevention and early detection.

### Consistently Reported Findings

High-risk HPV, especially types 16 and 18, emerges as the most consistently implicated oncogenic driver, causally linked to cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, vaginal malignancy, and multizonal anogenital neoplasia. HPV oncoproteins E6 and E7 disrupt p53 and Rb tumor suppressor pathways, while downstream miRNA dysregulation—including consistent downregulation of hsa-miR-125b-5p, hsa-miR-106b-5p, hsa-miR-23b-3p, and hsa-miR-30d-5p—amplifies carcinogenic signaling through SOX2/SERPINE1 and ERBB4/GLI1 hubs. HPV vaccination consistently demonstrates efficacy in reducing high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions and cancer incidence across multiple study designs.

Vaginal dysbiosis, characterized by loss of Lactobacillus dominance and expansion of anaerobic communities (community state type IV, including Gardnerella-dominated subtypes), repeatedly associates with impaired reproductive outcomes including reduced IVF pregnancy rates, miscarriage risk, and increased STI susceptibility. Machine learning models incorporating vaginal microbiome data achieved up to 85% accuracy in predicting miscarriage, with dysbiotic CST-II and CST-IVB conferring 6.5-fold and 4-fold elevated risk, respectively. The proposed mechanism involves Th1/Th17 pro-inflammatory cytokine induction that compromises implantation tolerance.

Sexually transmitted pathogens—principally Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Mycoplasma genitalium, and Ureaplasma urealyticum—consistently appear as causes of pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal factor infertility, and endometritis across epidemiological, clinical, and mechanistic studies. PID and its sequelae disproportionately burden women in low sociodemographic index regions, particularly sub-Saharan Africa. Antiphospholipid antibodies and anti-sperm antibodies additionally contribute to implantation failure through autoimmune pathways. Global burden analyses confirm that approximately 30% of female infertility is attributable to STIs, with chlamydia-related secondary infertility increasing in high-income settings despite declining gonorrhea-related burden.

Immunosuppression represents a recurrent amplifying factor across multiple pathologies. HIV co-infection markedly elevates HR-HPV risk (6.52-fold with concurrent HBV co-infection), accelerates cervical dysplasia progression, and correlates with vitamin D deficiency—itself prevalent in over 70% of women with cervical lesions in high HIV-prevalence settings. PD-L1 overexpression in cervical squamous cell carcinoma suppresses CD8+ tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte activity and independently predicts adverse outcomes, while the inverse immunophenotype (PD-L1-negative, high CD8+ TILs) confers superior prognosis.

Endometriosis appears across multiple analyses as an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition contributing substantially to infertility burden, with rising incidence documented in sub-Saharan Africa and among Asian women. Mendelian randomization data suggest dietary factors, including coffee and shellfish intake, modulate endometriosis risk through gene-environment interactions, though the mechanistic pathways remain incompletely characterized.

Cancer genomics studies highlight PIK3CA and KMT2D mutations (each ~27% prevalence) alongside APOBEC mutagenesis signatures in cervical cancer, with chemoradiation-resistant tumors displaying EGFR amplification
